Guidance for ITAAC closure under 10 CFR 52.99 is described as acceptable for compliance, while the containment structural integrity evaluation for internal pressure loadings above design-basis pressure is noted as conforming with no exceptions. SRP 14.3 (March 2007) addresses the acceptability of ITAAC scope, with partial conformity for some acceptance criteria applicable to COL applicants, and full conformity for specific acceptance criteria in SRP Section 14.3. Methodologies for developing ITAAC for various systems—including structural and systems engineering (SRP 14.3.2), piping systems and components (SRP 14.3.3), reactor systems (SRP 14.3.4), electrical systems (SRP 14.3.6), plant systems (SRP 14.3.7), human factors engineering (SRP 14.3.9), and emergency planning (SRP 14.3.10)—are all referenced as being provided in SRP 14.3, with no additional details or exceptions noted.

The reviewed sections indicate conformance with Standard Review Plan (SRP) requirements. For SRP 1.0, Rev 2 (Introduction and Interfaces), both the acceptance criteria associated with referenced SRP sections (II.2) and the performance of new safety features and design qualification testing requirements (II.3) are met, with no exceptions noted. Regarding SRP 9.3.2, Rev 3 (Process and Post-Accident Sampling Systems), technical specifications (II.2) are not applicable as they were addressed in NRC-approved TSTF 366-A. The process sampling system functional design (II.3) and seismic design and quality group classification (II.4) both conform to requirements, with no exceptions identified.

The provided passages summarize the applicability and status of various Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and appendices related to nuclear plant licensing and security. SRP 2.1.2, Rev 3 (Exclusion Area Authority and Control) is site-specific and not applicable. SRP Appendix 8-A, Rev 1 governs NRC staff visits to plant sites during operating or COL licensing reviews. SRP 13.5.2.2 (Maintenance and Other Operating Procedures) was never finalized; relevant guidance was moved to SRP 17.5. SRP 13.6.1, Rev 1 (Physical Security for COL and Operating Reactors) is not applicable, while SRP 13.6.2, Rev 2 (Physical Security for Design Certification) applies to physical security elements within the NuScale plant’s certified design boundary. SRP 13.6.3, Rev 1 (Physical Security for Early Site Permit) is not applicable, and SRP 13.6.4, Rev 1 (Access Authorization, referencing 10 CFR 73.56) applies to COL applicants. SRP 14.3.12, Rev 1 (Physical Security Hardware ITAAC) is partially conforming, with the COL applicant addressing ITAAC for physical security hardware outside the nuclear island and structures.

Specific acceptance criteria for SRP Chapter 7 are detailed in its individual sections and summarized in SRP Section 7.1, Table 7-1, and Appendix 7.1-A. DSRS Appendix 7.0-A, Rev 0 addresses I&C Hazard Analysis, which conforms with no exceptions noted. SRP 14.3.8, Rev 0, concerning Radiation Protection Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC), is not applicable, with the methodology for developing ITAAC provided in SRP 14.3. Provisions related to nonradiological analyses in SRP Section 15.1.5, Appendix A, and SRP Section 15.4.8, Appendix A, remain applicable to the DCA.

The provided passages summarize the applicability of various Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections for Combined License (COL) applicants. SRP 13.1.1 (Rev 5) and 13.1.2–13.1.3 (Rev 6) address management, technical support, and operating organization requirements, but are noted as not applicable to COL applicants. SRP 13.2.1 (Rev 3) and 13.2.2 (Rev 3) cover reactor operator requalification, operator training, and non-licensed plant staff training, and are also not applicable to COL applicants. SRP 13.3 (Rev 3) and 13.4 (Rev 3) pertain to emergency planning and operational programs, with no specific requirements for COL applicants in these sections. SRP 13.5.1.1 (Rev 1) and 13.5.1.2 (Draft Rev 0) relate to administrative procedures, with the latter never finalized and its content incorporated into SRP Section 14.2. SRP 13.6.6 (Rev 0) addresses the Cyber Security Plan, and SRP 13.7.1 (Rev 0) covers Fitness for Duty (Operational); both are also not applicable to COL applicants. No technical requirements or details are specified for COL applicants in these SRP sections.

The passages reference SRP 13.3, Rev 3: Emergency Planning, covering sections II.1 through II.14, II.29, and II.30, which address requirements such as meeting 10 CFR 50.47(b) standards, conducting full participation exercises per 10 CFR 50 Appendix E, onsite and offsite emergency response plans, emergency classification and action level schemes, meteorological criteria, upgrading emergency response facilities, alerting and notifications, protective action recommendations, state, tribal, and local government planning and preparedness, emergency planning zones, acceptability of emergency plans, and offsite emergency planning when local governments decline to participate. For all sections except II.5, the responsibility is assigned to the COL applicant, with no further details provided. For II.5, it is specifically noted that there are no proposed changes to existing emergency response facilities. Sections II.29 and II.30, covering NRC notifications, communications, and generic communications or Commission orders pertaining to emergency planning, are also assigned to the COL applicant.

The NuScale design partially conforms to certain emergency planning requirements outlined in SRP 13.3, Rev 3. Specifically, it includes an emergency response data system and a technical support center; however, site-specific aspects such as evacuation time estimates, the operational support center, and the emergency operations facility are the responsibility of the COL (Combined License) applicant referencing the NuScale certified design. Emergency planning ITAAC (Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria) are not included in the NuScale Design Certification Application (DCA). Additionally, guidance from NSIR/DPR-ISG-01 on emergency planning for nuclear power plants applies to site-specific programmatic and design aspects, which are also the responsibility of the COL applicant.

Sections II.15 through II.21 of SRP 13.3, Rev 3: Emergency Planning, address criteria and requirements related to early site permit (ESP) applications, including physical characteristics unique to the proposed site, preliminary analysis of evacuation times, copies of letters of agreement or certifications, emergency preparedness information and plans, complete and integrated emergency plans, and ITAAC (Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria) associated with ESP applications. However, all these sections are marked as "Not Applicable" for the current context, with guidance specified as applying only to early site permit applicants. No technical details or requirements are provided for other application types in these passages.

SRP Section 14.2.1 (August 2006) provides generic guidelines for extended power uprate testing programs and is applicable only to extended power uprate license amendment requests. Since the issuance of SRP Section 17.1, the NRC has released SRP Section 17.5, which is based on NQA-1, for the review of Quality Assurance Program Descriptions (QAPDs) for new reactor applicants, including those seeking design certification under 10 CFR 52. Therefore, SRP Section 17.5, rather than SRP Section 17.1 or 17.3, is the appropriate guidance for reviewing the NuScale QAPD and the QAPD incorporated into the Design Certification Application (DCA).

Section 1.8 of the document addresses interfaces with the certified design and details Combined License (COL) Information Items, as summarized in Table 1.8-2. This table lists item numbers, descriptions of each COL information item, and the corresponding section references, with continued entries spanning sections 3.6, 3.7, 3.13, 6.6, and 12.3. The COL applicant is required to identify the group within the operating organization responsible for maintaining relevant procedures, as noted in section 13.5. Additionally, COL Items 20.1-1 through 20.1-6 are marked as "Not used" in section 20.1. Table 1.9-1 provides a conformance status legend for reference.

A Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must identify the site-specific plant location and address known issues related to reactor vessel internals reliability, including aging degradation mechanisms, through programs such as the Comprehensive Vibration Assessment Program and steam generator programs. The applicant is responsible for developing and implementing a Boric Acid Control Program, which includes inspection elements to ensure the integrity of reactor coolant pressure boundary components, monitoring the containment atmosphere for reactor coolant system leakage, specifying inspection types (visual or nondestructive), and defining inspection frequency. The applicant must also describe a reactor vessel material surveillance program consistent with NUREG 0800, Section 5.3.1.5.3, and, if operational surveillance indicates non-compliance, ensure adherence to 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, and RG 1.161. Additionally, the applicant must verify that the final containment vessel design maintains flange contact pressure at accident temperature and peak accident pressure, and perform an analysis demonstrating that the as-built containment internal free volume provides sufficient margin for containment design pressure and temperature, accounting for complex internal structures and manufacturing processes. Furthermore, the applicant must provide a certified Overpressure Protection Report in compliance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section III, Subarticles NB-7200 and NC-7200, to demonstrate adequate overpressure protection features for both the reactor coolant pressure boundary and secondary system, including low temperature overpressure protection. All plant-specific programmatic information, such as the reactor vessel surveillance program, is the responsibility of the COL applicant.

The NuScale Power Plant design certification includes specific Combined License (COL) information items that must be addressed by COL applicants referencing the design. The design power incorporates an additional 2 percent margin for measurement uncertainty. Applicants are required to provide site-specific schedules for construction and commercial operation of each power module (COL Item 1.1-2), identify the prime agents or contractors for plant construction and operation (COL Item 1.4-1), and submit a list of departures from the certified design (COL Item 1.8-1). Additionally, applicants must review and address conformance with regulatory criteria effective six months prior to the COL application docket date for site-specific and operational aspects (COL Item 1.9-1), and identify the implementation milestone for the seismic monitoring program (COL Item 3.7-8). The NuScale FSAR distinguishes between Conceptual Design Information (CDI), which provides context for interface requirements outside the certified design, and COL items, which pertain to site-specific design elements under the applicant’s responsibility. Table 1.8-2 in the FSAR lists all COL information items and their corresponding sections. Fluor Corporation provided the balance of plant design for the application. The COL applicant must address each COL information item in the relevant FSAR section.

Applicants for a Combined Operating License (COL) referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must provide site-specific diagrams and legends for electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and piping systems, as indicated in Figures 1.7-1a, 1.7-1b, and 1.7-2. They must also list any additional site-specific piping and instrumentation diagrams and legends as applicable (COL Items 1.7-1 and 1.7-2). The COL applicant must demonstrate that site-specific characteristics are bounded by the parameters in Table 2.0-1, or otherwise justify the acceptability of any deviations (COL Item 2.0-1). For electrical systems, the applicant must describe the site-specific switchyard layout and design, including offsite power connections, control and indication systems, circuit breaker and bus characteristics, protective relaying, power supplies, lightning and grounding protection, and compliance with General Design Criteria (GDC) 5 (COL Item 8.2-1). They must also provide details of offsite power connection and grid stability studies, addressing grid contingencies such as the loss of the largest operating unit, one NuScale Power Module, or all 12 modules, in accordance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, North American Electric Reliability Corporation, and transmission operator requirements (COL Item 8.2-2). The applicant must describe switchyard and offsite power system testing, including initial testing under degraded power conditions, consistent with Regulatory Guide 1.68, Revision 4 (COL Item 8.2-3). Additionally, the applicant must detail the site-specific auxiliary AC power system, electrical heat tracing system, and the design of the plant grounding grid and lightning protection network (COL Items 8.3-1, 8.3-2, and 8.3-3).

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must evaluate potential hazards from construction activities to safety-related and risk-significant structures, systems, and components (SSCs) of both existing and newly constructed operating units at a co-located site, in accordance with 10 CFR 52.79(a)(31). This evaluation includes identifying management and administrative controls to mitigate hazards and ensuring that limiting conditions for operation are not exceeded; however, this requirement does not apply to construction activities at an individual NuScale Power Plant with operating NuScale Power Modules. Additionally, the applicant must identify and describe the use of probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) and risk-informed applications in support of licensee programs during the COL application, construction (from COL issuance to initial fuel loading), and operational phases (from initial fuel loading through commercial operation). The applicant must also evaluate site-specific external event hazards (such as liquefaction and slope failure), screen them for risk significance, and assess risks from external hazards not bounded by the design certification. Furthermore, the applicant is required to confirm and, if necessary, modify the key assumptions and data used in the design certification PRA for applicability to the as-built, as-operated PRA. Finally, the applicant must develop severe accident management guidelines and administrative controls to address beyond-design-basis events.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must provide detailed site-specific information, including geographic and demographic characteristics (COL 2.1-1), and describe nearby industrial, transportation, and military facilities, demonstrating design acceptability for associated hazards or proposing site-specific alternatives (COL 2.2-1). The applicant must also describe site-specific meteorological (Sections 2.3.1–2.3.5; COL 2.3-1), hydrologic (Sections 2.4.1–2.4.14, except 2.4.8 and 2.4.10; COL 2.4-1), and geologic, seismologic, and geotechnical characteristics (Sections 2.5.1–2.5.5; COL 2.5-1). Table 3.2-1 must be updated to classify site-specific structures, systems, and components (COL 3.2-1). The applicant must confirm the final location of structures, systems, and components subject to flood protection and the final routing of piping (COL 3.4-1), develop an inspection and maintenance program for water-tight doors, penetration seals, and other degradable measures (COL 3.4-3), and ensure site-specific tanks or water sources cannot cause flooding in the Reactor or Control Buildings (COL 3.4-4). The extent of waterproofing and dampproofing for underground portions of the Reactor and Control Buildings must be determined based on site conditions, with specified design life for waterstops, waterproofing, dampproofing, and watertight seals; if the design life is less than the plant’s operating life, continued protection measures must be described (COL 3.4-5). Finally, the applicant must confirm that nearby structures exposed to external flooding will not collapse and adversely affect the Reactor Building or Seismic Category I portion of the Control Building (COL 3.4-6).

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design must confirm that nearby structures exposed to severe and extreme wind loads, such as tornadoes and hurricanes, will not collapse and adversely affect the Reactor Building or the Seismic Category I portion of the Control Building. The applicant must also demonstrate that site-specific turbine missile parameters are bounded by the design certification analysis or provide a site-specific missile analysis to show that barriers adequately protect essential structures, systems, and components from turbine missiles. Additionally, the applicant must address the effects of turbine missiles from nearby or co-located facilities and confirm that automobile missiles cannot be generated within a 0.5-mile radius of safety-related and risk-significant structures, systems, and components that would result in impacts higher than 30 feet above plant grade. If such impacts are possible, the applicant must evaluate and demonstrate that these missiles will not compromise safety-related and risk-significant structures, systems, and components. Furthermore, the applicant is required to evaluate site-specific hazards for external events that may produce more energetic missiles than those defined in the design basis (Tier 2, Section 3.5.1.4). Confirmation of protection against extreme wind events and missiles is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must complete the routing of piping systems outside the containment vessel and bioshield area, identify high- and moderate-energy line locations, and update Table 3.6-1 as needed. If changes occur, the applicant will update the pipe rupture hazards analysis, implement additional protection features, and revise Table 3.6-2. The applicant must perform a pipe rupture hazards analysis—including dynamic and environmental effects—for high- and moderate-energy lines outside the reactor pool bay in the Reactor Building (RXB), update Table 3.6-2, evaluate multi-module impacts in common pipe galleries, and assess subcompartment pressurization. The analysis must show that RXB piping bounds rupture effects for lines routed outside the RXB, or else analyze those lines separately. The applicant will confirm that site-specific seismic demands for critical structures, systems, and components (as listed in Appendix 3B) are bounded by the certified design’s seismic demands; if not, the design must be shown to have sufficient margin or be modified accordingly. Seismic demands to be investigated include forces, moments, deformations, in-structure response spectra, and seismic stability. The applicant will perform a structure-soil-structure interaction analysis for the Reactor Building, Control Building, Radioactive Waste Building, and both Turbine Generator Buildings, ensuring site-specific seismic demands are bounded or that adequate margin exists. A site-specific configuration analysis will be performed for the Reactor Building and NuScale Power Module layout, confirming that in-structure response spectra at the foundation and roof are bounded by certified seismic demands. The applicant must also demonstrate that site-specific seismic demand is bounded by the FSAR capacity for an empty dry dock condition, determine the appropriate number of interaction planes for soil-structure interaction, and establish means and methods for lifting the bioshield, demonstrating that bioshield components and connections can withstand the required loads and load factors. COL Item 3.6-4 is not used.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must develop a site-specific, strain-compatible soil profile, confirm that the minimum required response spectrum criterion is met, and verify that seismic site characteristics—such as soil layering, soil parameter ranges, shear wave velocity, and minimum soil bearing capacity—fall within the certified design parameters. The applicant must also confirm that nearby structures exposed to a site-specific safe shutdown earthquake will not collapse and adversely affect the Reactor Building or Seismic Category I portion of the Control Building. A soil-structure interaction analysis of the Reactor Building and Control Building must be performed using NuScale SASSI2010 models. The applicant is required to provide a seismic monitoring system and program compliant with Regulatory Guide 1.12 (Rev. 2 or later) and Regulatory Guide 1.166 (Rev. 0 or later), and to prepare site-specific post-earthquake procedures using seismic instrumentation data to determine if shutdown criteria are exceeded. Additionally, the applicant must include an analysis of performance-based response spectra at the surface and intermediate depths, accounting for subsurface layer complexities and justifying vertical to horizontal (V/H) spectral ratios for site-specific foundation input and vertical response spectra. Site-specific in-structure response spectra for the NuScale Power Module at the skirt support and lug restraints must be shown to be bounded by the certified spectra in Figures 3.7.2-156, 3.7.2-157, and 3.7.2-158 through 3.7.2-163, respectively. Maximum forces in the NuScale Power Module lug restraints and skirts are provided in Table 3B-28, and maximum forces and moments in the west wing wall and pool wall are in Tables 3B-23a and 3B-23b. Site-specific in-structure response spectra for various Reactor Building and Control Building locations must be shown to be bounded by their corresponding certified spectra (Figures 3.7.2-110, 3.7.2-112, 3.7.2-114, 3.7.2-119a, 3.7.2-119b, 3.7.2-121a, and 3.7.2-121b); if not, the standard design must demonstrate appropriate margin or be modified to accommodate site-specific demands.

A Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must fulfill several requirements: provide applicable test procedures and submit test and inspection results from the comprehensive vibration assessment program for the NuScale Power Module per Regulatory Guide 1.20; submit a summary of reactor core support structure ASME service level stresses, deformation, and cumulative usage factor values for each component and operating condition in accordance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section III, Subsection NG; submit a Preservice Testing program for valves as required by 10 CFR 50.55a; establish an Inservice Testing program per ASME Operation and Maintenance (OM) Code and 10 CFR 50.55a; identify site-specific valves, implementation milestones, and applicable ASME OM Code (and Code Cases) for preservice and inservice testing; develop test procedures for monitoring power-operated valve and emergency core cooling system valve assembly performance to satisfy periodic verification of design basis capability requirements; implement a control rod drive system Operability Assurance Program per Section 3.9.4.4 and provide a summary of the testing program and results; provide an inservice inspection program for ASME Class 1, 2, and 3 threaded fasteners, identifying the applicable edition and addenda of ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section XI and ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50.55a; and develop site-specific preservice examination, inservice inspection, and inservice testing program plans in accordance with ASME Section XI, establishing implementation milestones.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must verify that detailed design evaluations of the main steam lines are performed using acoustic resonance screening criteria and calculations such as the Strouhal number to identify potential concerns. The methodology in the “NuScale Comprehensive Vibration Assessment Program Technical Report,” TR-0716-50439, is acceptable for this purpose, and Section 3.9.2.1.1.3 will be updated with evaluation results. The applicant may select specific piping portions for vibration testing based on system design, analysis, and the Comprehensive Vibration Assessment Program’s scope. For steam generator design, the applicant will develop a methodology to analyze secondary-side instabilities, addressing identification and qualification of density wave oscillations and reverse fluid flows in the steam generator tubes and reactor coolant system. Additionally, the applicant must develop and maintain a site-specific seismic and dynamic qualification program, create and maintain an equipment qualification database and record files for seismic and environmental qualification, and submit an implementation program for NRC approval before installing seismically qualified equipment. The applicant will also provide a full description of the environmental qualification program, including milestones and completion dates, and implement an operational program for environmental qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment, updating Table 3.11-1 as needed. The environmental qualification program must describe how equipment in harsh environments will be monitored and managed throughout plant life, including methodologies to ensure continued qualification if measured doses exceed calculated values.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must provide a certified Overpressure Protection Report in accordance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section III Subarticles NB-7200 and NC-7200, demonstrating that the reactor coolant pressure boundary and secondary system have adequate overpressure protection, including low temperature overpressure protection features. The applicant must also establish plant-specific procedures for operator actions to identify, monitor, and trend reactor coolant system leakage when leakage rates are above normal but below technical specification limits, ensuring operators have sufficient time to act before limits are reached. Additionally, measures must be established to control onsite cleaning of the reactor pressure vessel during construction per Regulatory Guide 1.28, and operating procedures must be developed to prevent operational transients more severe than those for which the reactor design has been demonstrated adequate. Finally, the applicant must develop a containment leakage rate testing program specifying whether the prescriptive-based (Option A) or performance-based (Option B) approach under 10 CFR 50, Appendix J will be implemented.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must develop and implement a Strategic Water Chemistry Plan, establish procedures to evaluate neutron attenuation uncertainty due to material lot variability, and inspect as-manufactured materials for contamination and defects. The applicant will select appropriate chemicals for the reactor component cooling water system based on site-specific water quality and materials, describe potable water sources and pre-treatment methods (including pumps and storage tanks), and detail sanitary waste storage and disposal methods with associated treatment facilities. The applicant must also provide measures for preventing long-term corrosion and organic fouling in the site cooling water system, identify the site-specific water source, and ensure the water treatment system meets plant chemistry requirements. Additionally, a site-specific chemistry control program must be provided, adhering to the latest Electric Power Research Institute Pressurized Water Reactor Secondary Water Chemistry Guidelines and NEI 97-06. The applicant will describe a flow-accelerated corrosion monitoring program for steam and power conversion systems per Generic Letter 89-08 and EPRI NSAC-202L, determine the size and number of new and spent resin tanks in the condensate polishing system, specify the auxiliary boiler fuel supply, and provide a secondary water chemistry analysis demonstrating that the feedwater treatment system’s size, materials, and capacity meet the secondary water chemistry program requirements and are compatible with the chemicals used.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must implement several programs and provide specific documentation. For containment cleanliness (COL 6.3-1), the applicant must describe a program limiting debris within containment, including foreign material exclusion controls. Regarding control room habitability, the applicant must comply with Regulatory Guide 1.78 Revision 1 (COL 6.4-1) and specify testing and inspection requirements for the control room habitability system and envelope integrity as per Table 6.4-4 (COL 6.4-5). For fuel handling and storage, the applicant must develop programs and procedures for safe operations, including criticality control (COL 9.1-1), demonstrate that an NRC-licensed cask can be used for spent fuel removal (COL 9.1-2), and develop procedures for transferring spent fuel to a transfer cask (COL 9.1-3). The applicant must also provide a periodic testing plan for fuel handling equipment (COL 9.1-4), a design for a spent fuel cask and handling equipment with associated procedures and programs (COL 9.1-6), and a program for heavy loads handling addressing operating and maintenance procedures, inspection and test plans, personnel qualifications, operator training, and safe load paths (COL 9.1-7). Additionally, a structural evaluation of spent fuel storage racks and assemblies must be provided, confirming the validity of thermal-hydraulic, criticality, and material analyses, dependent on the vendor-specific rack design (COL 9.1-8). A neutron absorber material qualification report demonstrating compliance with Technical Report TR-0816-49833 is also required (COL 9.1-9). Finally, the applicant must submit a leakage control program for systems outside containment that may contain accident source term radioactive materials, including those used for post-accident hydrogen and oxygen monitoring (COL 9.3-1).

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification is responsible for implementing life cycle processes for the operation, maintenance, and retirement phases of instrumentation and controls (I&C) systems, as defined in IEEE Std 1074-2006 and IEEE Std 1012-2004. The NuScale Digital I&C Software Configuration Management Plan provides guidance for the retirement and removal of software products. For mobile equipment connected to plant systems, compliance with ANSI/ANS-40.37, RG 1.143, 10 CFR 20.1406, NRC IE Bulletin 80-10, and 10 CFR 50.34a is required. The applicant must also develop a site-specific process control program per NEI 07-10A, and describe site-specific process and effluent monitoring and sampling system components in accordance with ANSI N13.1-2011, ANSI N42.18-2004, and Regulatory Guides 1.21, 1.33, and 4.15. Processes and programs supporting Objectives 5 and 6 must be developed to demonstrate compliance with 10 CFR 20.1406 and RG 4.21. Construction, testing, and repairs are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. The NuScale design conforms to RG 1.141 through adherence to ANS N271-1976 for containment isolation provisions for fluid systems. For electric cables, the COL holder determines applicability of condition monitoring per RG 1.218 during the development of the maintenance rule (10 CFR 50.65) program; only cables meeting the criteria are subject to this guidance.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must provide detailed descriptions and plans in several areas: the offsite communication system and its interface with onsite systems, ensuring continuous communications for effective command and control during normal and emergency situations (COL 9.5-1); the location of security power equipment within a vital area per 10 CFR 73.55(e)(9)(vi)(B) (COL 9.5-2); the corporate or home office management and technical support organization, including qualification requirements for technical support and management positions (COL 13.1-1); the structure, functions, and responsibilities of the onsite operating organization, consistent with minimum licensed operator staffing requirements in Section 18.5 (COL 13.1-2); qualification requirements for all management, operating, technical, and maintenance positions (COL 13.1-3); initial and requalification training programs for reactor operators and senior reactor operators (COL 13.2-1); training programs for non-licensed plant staff, including initial, periodic retraining, and qualification requirements (COL 13.2-2); a description of the onsite operational support center (OSC) and its direct communication systems with the control room (COL 13.3-1); a description of an emergency operations facility for overall emergency response management (COL 13.3-2); a comprehensive emergency plan in accordance with 10 CFR 50.47, 10 CFR 50 Appendix E, 10 CFR 52.48, and 10 CFR 52.79(a)(21) (COL 13.3-3); security plans covering physical security, security training and qualification, safeguards contingency, site security provisions during construction and operation, and physical security system components outside the nuclear island (COL 13.6-1); and compliance with requirements in Table 5-1 of TR-0416-48929, “NuScale Design of Physical Security Systems” (COL 13.6-2).

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must perform several site-specific evaluations and develop operational programs in compliance with regulatory requirements. For liquid effluents, the applicant will calculate public doses using site-specific parameters, compare results to the numerical design objectives of 10 CFR 50, Appendix I, and comply with 10 CFR 20.1302 and 40 CFR 190. They must also evaluate the consequences of accidental radioactive liquid releases from the pool surge control system storage tank per NRC Branch Technical Position 11-6, and perform dilution flow evaluations using site-specific data. For gaseous effluents, similar dose calculations and regulatory comparisons are required. The applicant will develop an Offsite Dose Calculation Manual (ODCM) following NEI 07-09A guidance, and establish a Radiological Environmental Monitoring Program (REMP) consistent with NUREG-1301 and NUREG-0133, considering local land use census data and all potential radiation pathways. Additionally, the applicant must describe an ALARA operational program, identify any additional site-specific contained radiation sources exceeding 100 millicuries not previously listed, and develop administrative controls for access to high and very high radiation areas in accordance with Regulatory Guide 8.38.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must perform a cost-benefit analysis per 10 CFR 50.34a, 10 CFR 50 Appendix I, and Regulatory Guide 1.110, as well as a site-specific cost-benefit analysis. The applicant must provide descriptions of their 10 CFR 26 compliant fitness-for-duty (FFD) programs for both plant operations and construction. Additionally, the applicant is required to submit a schedule for the Initial Test Program, test abstracts for pre-operational testing of the potable water system and seismic monitoring system, and select the plant configuration (number of NuScale Power Modules in service) for the Island Mode Test. Site-specific selection methodologies, inspections, tests, analyses, and acceptance criteria must be provided for emergency planning and for structures, systems, and components within the applicant’s scope. The applicant must also supply final plant-specific information identified in the generic Technical Specifications and Bases, and prepare and maintain an owner-controlled requirements manual that includes owner-controlled limits and requirements as described in the Technical Specifications Bases or the FSAR.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must provide detailed descriptions of site-specific procedures used by operators both in the main control room and locally, including normal, abnormal, and emergency operating procedures, as well as their classification systems and general format and content. The applicant must also describe site-specific maintenance and other operating procedures, their classification, and format. Required procedure categories include plant radiation protection, emergency preparedness, calibration and test, chemical-radiochemical control, radioactive waste management, maintenance and modification, material control, and plant security procedures. The applicant must submit plans for the development, implementation, and control of administrative, emergency operating, and maintenance procedures, including preliminary preparation schedules and target completion dates. For emergency operating procedures (EOPs), the submittal must include a Procedures Generation Package with plant-specific technical guidelines, a writer’s guide, verification and validation programs, operator training programs, and identification of the responsible organizational group. Similarly, the applicant must identify the groups responsible for maintaining and following maintenance and other operating procedures. The responsibility for establishing a plant procedure improvement program, as required by 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(ii), including emergency procedures, reliability analyses, human factors engineering, crisis management, operator training, and industry coordination, lies with the COL applicant.

NuScale’s application for standard design certification applies relevant guidance only to the extent necessary to provide reasonable assurance that a Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the certified design can meet these requirements. While NuScale, as the design certification applicant, considers the guidance applicable to its application as needed, the responsibility for fully implementing this guidance—including aspects related to site-specific design, fabrication, erection, construction, testing, inspection of structures, systems, and components (SSC), and maintenance of records throughout the facility’s life—rests with the COL applicant. The guidance is thus applicable only as necessary to ensure that the COL applicant referencing the certified design can satisfy the requirements.

The design of the new and spent fuel storage facility partially conforms to Regulatory Position C.8 regarding Makeup Water by utilizing the large water inventory within Seismic Category I structures as the ultimate heat sink (UHS) and a separate Quality Group C, Seismic Category I makeup line. The facility fully conforms to SRP 9.1.1, Rev 3, meeting the specific criteria for GDC 62 on criticality safety of fresh and spent fuel storage and handling. It also conforms to DSRS 9.1.2, Rev 0, meeting the specific criteria for GDC 2 (design bases for protection against natural phenomena), GDC 4 (environmental and dynamic effects), GDC 5 (sharing of structures, systems, and components), GDC 61 (fuel storage and handling), and GDC 63 (monitoring of spent fuel and storage conditions). An ESF ventilation system is not required per RG 1.52. The facility also meets the requirements of 10 CFR 20.1101(b) and conforms to criteria for criticality monitors and subcriticality margin. No exceptions or additional requirements are noted except for the partial conformance regarding makeup water.

The Ultimate Heat Sink (UHS) pool structures, which contain the credited inventory of makeup water for spent fuel cooling during accident conditions, meet Seismic Category I requirements but are not designed to Quality Group C standards. According to DSRS 9.1.3, Rev 0, the Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup System partially conforms to the specific criteria for GDC 4, as the normal makeup water supply system and its source are not designed to accommodate the effects of postulated accidents. The system fully conforms to the criteria for GDC 5, GDC 61, GDC 63, and 10 CFR 20.1101(b), with no exceptions noted. The ITAAC acceptance criterion is not applicable, as it pertains only to combined license (COL) applicants.

The NuScale control room habitability system conforms to SRP 6.4, Rev 3 criteria for ventilation systems (II.2) and pressurization systems (II.3), and to SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3 criteria for control room area ventilation (II.4) and control of radioactive material releases (II.5), except for aspects related to ESF atmosphere cleanup systems. The system does not rely on or use emergency standby atmosphere filtration for operator protection during accident conditions, making SRP 6.4 II.4 not applicable. For toxic gas hazards (SRP 6.4.1 II.7), the system partially conforms, with programmatic requirements assigned to the COL applicant. In the event of a station blackout (SBO), the control room habitability system uses compressed air tanks to pressurize the control room envelope, and the main control room’s walls, ceiling, and structure serve as a passive heat sink to maintain acceptable environmental conditions.

The passages indicate that the Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity Design (SRP 9.1.4, Rev 4) conform to the specific criteria required to meet General Design Criteria (GDC) 2, 5, 61, and 62, with no exceptions or additional notes. Similarly, the Overhead Heavy Load Handling Systems (SRP 9.1.5, Rev 1) conform to the specific criteria for GDC 1, 2, 4, and 5, also with no exceptions or additional notes. All referenced systems meet the applicable regulatory requirements as specified.

The reviewed sections address compliance with Standard Review Plans (SRPs) for various nuclear plant systems. For SRP 9.2.6 (Condensate Storage Facilities), the design basis for environmental and dynamic effects, sharing of structures, systems, and components (SSCs), control of radioactive releases, and compliance with 10 CFR 20.1406 all conform to requirements, with no impairments to safety-related or risk-significant SSCs and no additional details provided. SRP 9.3.3 (Equipment and Floor Drainage System) confirms that no portions of the NuScale drain system penetrate the containment barrier, ensuring control of radioactive material releases. SRP 10.4.1 (Main Condensers) and SRP 10.4.2 (Main Condenser Evacuation System) both conform to GDC 60 requirements to prevent excessive releases of radioactivity to the environment, with no further details. For SRP 13.3 (Emergency Planning), containment monitoring and continuous sampling from potential accident release points only partially conform, as programmatic aspects of containment and effluent monitoring are assigned to the COL applicant.

The NuScale compressed air systems, as described in SRP 9.3.1, Rev 2, are classified as non-safety and non-risk-significant systems, and therefore do not need to meet the specific criteria for GDC 1 and GDC 2. The system conforms to the requirements of GDC 5. Regarding 10 CFR 50.63, the system partially conforms, as the intent of the acceptance criterion—to maintain the ability to withstand and recover from a station blackout (SBO) of a specified minimum duration—is applicable.

The Equipment and Floor Drainage System (SRP 9.3.3, Rev 3) conforms to requirements for protection against natural phenomena (II.1) and environmental and dynamic effects (II.2), with no exceptions noted. The Control Room Area Ventilation System (SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3) also conforms to protection against natural phenomena (II.1) and environmental and dynamic effects (II.2), with no exceptions. Regarding sharing of structures, systems, and components (II.3), the Control Room Ventilation System (CRVS) operates as part of normal plant operations, and up to 12 modules share the same control room. The Lighting Systems (SRP 9.5.3, Rev 3) conform to lighting level requirements (II.3), with no exceptions.

The Spent Fuel Pool Area Ventilation System, as reviewed under SRP 9.4.2, Revision 3, conforms to General Design Criteria (GDC) 2 and GDC 5, with no exceptions noted. It also conforms to GDC 60, except for aspects related to Engineered Safety Feature (ESF) atmosphere cleanup systems, which are addressed by Regulatory Guide 1.52. The Auxiliary and Radwaste Area Ventilation System, reviewed under SRP 9.4.3, Revision 3, also conforms to GDC 2 and GDC 5 with no exceptions. However, GDC 60 is not applicable to the Radwaste Building Ventilation (RWBV) system because it does not filter exhaust; instead, exhaust filtration is performed by the Reactor Building Ventilation (RBV) system.

The review of DSRS 15.0, Rev 0 and SRP 15.0.2 (March 2007) sections on transient and accident analyses indicates general conformance with regulatory guidance for categorization of transients and accidents, classification by type, analysis acceptance criteria for anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs), assumed protection and safety system actions, evaluation of individual initiating events, identification of causes and frequency classification, and accident scenario identification processes. Some events with initiating event (IE) frequencies are also deterministically classified as AOOs. For analysis acceptance criteria for IEs and postulated accidents, partial conformance is noted: critical heat flux (CHF), rather than departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR), is used to determine thermal margin for fuel cladding, and guidance for items 4.B.ii and 4.B.iv is not fully met. Sequence of events and systems operation criteria are also partially met, with Item B.vi applicable only to COL applicants. For uncertainty analysis, non-LOCA methods employ sensitivity analyses or bounding values for input parameters. Quality assurance plan requirements are met. No additional exceptions or comments are noted.

The Strategic Water Chemistry Plan establishes an optimization strategy for maintaining primary coolant chemistry in accordance with the latest Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI) Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines (e.g., EPRI Technical Report 3002000505) and fuel vendor recommendations. The plan defines requirements for sampling and analysis frequencies, corrective actions, and detailed procedures for monitoring and controlling chemical additives and reactor coolant impurities, as listed in Table 5.2-5. Objectives include ensuring the integrity of reactor pressure boundary materials, fuel cladding, and fuel performance by limiting cladding corrosion and minimizing radiation fields. Alkaline-reducing water chemistry is maintained during power operation, with pH and lithium concentrations controlled per plant-specific programs, and dissolved hydrogen added to sustain a reducing environment. Zinc is added to reduce radiation levels and mitigate primary water stress-corrosion cracking (PWSCC) initiation rates. Chemistry parameters and impurity limits conform to EPRI PWR Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines and Regulatory Guide 1.44, Revision 1. Sampling frequencies vary (continuous, daily, weekly, or as needed) based on operating conditions, and increased sampling is performed following corrective actions to verify effectiveness. During refueling, the NuScale Power Module is isolated and pool water is purified to meet impurity limits (chloride, fluoride, sulfate) specified for cold shutdown. A Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must develop and implement this Strategic Water Chemistry Plan.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report confirms that the reactor vessel materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy values well above the 50 ft-lb threshold throughout the vessel's life, with initial values at a minimum of 75 ft-lb, rendering the guidance for lower energies inapplicable. Reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated to ensure a minimum end-of-life Charpy upper shelf energy of 50 ft-lb. The ΔRTNDT at the 1/4-T location and the methodology for pressure and temperature limits are detailed in NuScale Technical Report TR-1015-18177. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening follows 10 CFR 50.61, using RG 1.99 equations for fluence and temperature factor calculations. At both 32 and 57 effective full-power years (EFPY), the predicted RTPTS values for the lower RPV shell, weld, and bottom head remain below the regulatory screening criteria (270°F for shell and head, 300°F for weld), with maximum adjusted reference temperatures at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head). The material specimen program matrix meets ASTM E185-82 requirements, with specified numbers of Charpy and tension specimens for both unirradiated and irradiated conditions. Operational restrictions are in place, such as prohibiting reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transient conditions and below 185°F during steady-state conditions. The data tables provide detailed fluence, RTNDT, and margin values for key vessel locations, confirming compliance with regulatory safety margins over the 60-year design life.

The provided passages outline the structure and content of Section 5.2 of a nuclear engineering document, focusing on the integrity of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). Key topics include compliance with applicable codes and code cases (5.2.1), detailed descriptions of RCPB materials (5.2.3), and requirements for inservice inspection and testing (5.2.4). Leakage detection methods for the RCPB are addressed in section 5.2.5, with supporting references in 5.2.6. The section includes several technical tables: Table 5.2-2 lists design parameters for reactor safety valves; Table 5.2-4 details the specifications, alloy designations, grades, classes, or types for RCPB components, including the reactor vessel, attachments, and appurtenances; Table 5.2-5 covers reactor coolant water chemistry controls; and Table 5.2-6 outlines reactor pressure vessel inspection elements. Additional tables in Section 5.3 provide the chemical composition of reactor pressure vessel beltline materials (Table 5.3-2) and the 1/4-T adjusted reference temperature at 57 effective full-power years fluence (Table 5.3-3). Figures include containment leakage detection acceptability (Figure 5.2-3) and variable LTOP (Low Temperature Overpressure Protection) setpoints (Figure 5.2-4). The pressurizer is discussed in Section 5.4.5.

Section 5.2.2 addresses overpressure protection, with additional details on testing and inspection of overpressure protection components found in Sections 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6. Table 5.2-10 presents the Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) pressure setpoint as a function of cold temperature. Section 5.3.2 details the analyses and data supporting the establishment of pressure-temperature limits, pressurized thermal shock (PTS) evaluations, and Charpy upper-shelf energy requirements, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, and 10 CFR 50.61 for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) over a 60-year plant life. Table 5.3-1 lists reactor vessel parameters, while Table 5.3-5 provides the surveillance capsule withdrawal schedule and Table 5.3-6 and Table 5.3-7 specify pressure-temperature limits for normal heatup, cooldown, inservice leak, and hydrostatic tests. Table 5.3-8 presents the PTS screening result. Figures 5.3-2 through 5.3-5 illustrate the location of surveillance capsules and graphical pressure-temperature limits for various operational and test conditions.

The provided passages reference several sections and figures related to the reactor vessel and associated systems. Section 5.3 covers the reactor vessel, with Section 5.3.1 detailing reactor vessel materials, and Section 5.3.3.2 specifying that these materials are described in Sections 5.2.3 and 5.3.1. Section 5.3.3.3 states that fabrication methods for the reactor vessel are also detailed in Sections 5.2.3 and 5.3.1. Table 5.2-7 lists reactor vessel internals inspection elements. Section 5.4.2 addresses reactor coolant system piping. Figures include 5.2-1 (Reactor Safety Valve Simplified Diagram), 5.2-2 (Reactor Safety Valve Pilot Valve Assembly Simplified Diagram), 5.3-1 (Reactor Vessel), and 5.4-17 (Pressurizer Region of Reactor Vessel). These references collectively outline the materials, fabrication, inspection, and key components of the reactor vessel and its associated systems.

Section 5.4 of the document details the design of Reactor Coolant System components and subsystems, focusing on steam generators. Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates, while Table 5.2-1 lists relevant ASME Code Cases. Table 5.4-1 provides best estimate full-load thermal-hydraulic operating conditions for the steam generator. Table 5.4-2 summarizes steam generator design data, and Table 5.4-3 lists system component materials. Figure 5.4-2 illustrates the configuration of steam generators within the upper reactor pressure vessel section. Figures 5.4-3 and 5.4-4 depict the main steam and feedwater plena and the integral steam plenum, respectively. Figure 5.4-5 shows the feedwater plenum access port. Figures 5.4-6 and 5.4-7 detail the steam generator tube supports and support tabs. Figure 5.4-8 presents the steam generator flow restrictor assembly, and Figure 5.4-9 provides a simplified diagram of the steam generator. All technical details, component names, and referenced figures and tables are included as presented in the passages.

The inspection, testing, and maintenance strategy for the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) involves designing components to facilitate required inspections and developing an Inservice Inspection (ISI) program that specifies inspection intervals, frequencies, component and weld selection, and expansion criteria. Table 5.2-8 and Table 5.2-9 detail the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) Class 1 Piping and Support Inspection Elements, respectively. Key references include ASME NQA-1-2008/1a-2009 Addenda for quality assurance, ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code 2013 Edition Section III for construction rules, and ASME B16.11-2009 for forged fittings. Additional guidelines are provided by the Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI) documents, such as the Steam Generator Management Program (EPRI #1013706, Rev.7, 2007), Materials Reliability Program (MRP-227-A, EPRI #1022863, 2011), and the Advanced Light Water Reactors Utility Requirements Document (EPRI #3002003129, Rev. 13, 2014). Relevant research includes Wambsganss' study on tube vibration in helical tube steam generators (Journal of Sound and Vibration, 1983).

The NuScale Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) Material Surveillance Program follows ASTM E185-82 requirements, as detailed in Table 5.3-4, using archived materials identical to those used for baseline and capsule specimens, but maintained as full-thickness sections for flexibility in future specimen fabrication. Archive materials are sourced from actual production forgings and weldments using the same weld wire heat and flux lot as production welds. The NuScale RPV is designed for a 60-year life, with the surveillance capsule withdrawal schedule for the first 40 years based on 32 effective full-power years (EFPY), per ASTM E185-82 Table 1. Three capsules suffice for the initial 40-year period, with a fourth capsule included for potential regulatory requirements at license extension. Capsules are housed in holders attached to the core barrel at mid-core height, positioned approximately 90 degrees apart to achieve a lead factor of ~2.5. The withdrawal schedule (Table 5.3-5) specifies: the 1st capsule at the first refuel outage (~2 EFPY or 1.55E+18 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the 2nd at ~13 EFPY (when capsule fluence matches peak 32-EFPY RPV inside surface fluence), and the 3rd between ~13 and ~26 EFPY. The 4th capsule's withdrawal will be determined by regulations at license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT of 50°F for the limiting weld metal is reached at 4.27E+17 n/cm² (E > 1 MeV, 0.55 EFPY), but 28°F of this shift is a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F, likely causing overcompensation at low fluence. Therefore, the first capsule may be withdrawn at the end of the first fuel cycle rather than mid-cycle.

The NuScale reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated for Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) in accordance with Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.99, Rev. 2, to ensure compliance with the 10 CFR 50, Appendix G requirement of a minimum end-of-life USE of 50 ft-lb at 57 effective full power years (EFPY) fluence, which bounds the 60-year design life. The initial Charpy USE is 75 ft-lb. Table 5.3-9 summarizes the predicted 1/4-T Charpy USE decrease at 57-EFPY fluence: for the lower RPV shell beltline (0.10 wt% Cu, 1.37E+19 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the USE decreases by 20.5% to 59.6 ft-lb; for the lower RPV weld (0.06 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 15.3% to 63.5 ft-lb; and for the RPV bottom head (0.10 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 14.5% to 64.1 ft-lb. Table 5.3-10 provides the final 1/4-T Charpy USE values after adjusting for NuScale reactor pressure vessel irradiation temperature, applying a -1.2 ft-lb correction: lower RPV shell beltline, 58.4 ft-lb; lower RPV weld, 62.3 ft-lb; and RPV bottom head, 62.9 ft-lb. These values meet the regulatory minimum, and the Material Surveillance program monitors changes in fracture toughness properties throughout the vessel’s service life.

The analytical model for the overpressure protection system analysis is detailed in NuScale Topical Reports TR-0516-49416, "Non-Loss-of-Coolant Accident Analysis Methodology," and TR-0516-49422, "Loss-of-Coolant Accident Evaluation Model" (Revision 2). Pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 effective full power years (EFPY) are calculated using the methodology from ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects due to neutron fluence per Regulatory Guide 1.99. Additional references include ASTM E185-82 for surveillance testing of light-water reactor vessels, NuScale's "Fluence Calculational Methodology and Results" (TR-0116-20781-P, Revision 1), and "Pressure and Temperature Limits Methodology" (TR-1015-18177-P, Revision 2).

The NRC has developed a NuScale Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS) as a supplement to NUREG-0800, “Standard Review Plan for the Review of Safety Analysis Reports for Nuclear Power Plants: LWR Edition” (SRP), to guide the review of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. Table 1.9-3 documents the conformance of the NuScale design with the applicable sections of NUREG-0800 (SRP) and the DSRS, listing for each section the title, acceptance criteria (AC), AC title/description, conformance status, and comments. The improved standard technical specification guidance for light water reactors (LWRs), as specified in DSRS NUREGs-1430 through -1434 and NUREG-2194, was utilized to the extent appropriate and practicable in the development and review of the NuScale design.

Section pages are numbered sequentially using the "X.Y" format, except for chapter appendices, which use the chapter number and appendix letter followed by a sequential number (e.g., 3A-1 for the first page of Appendix A to Chapter 3). The substantive content and intent of RG 1.30 are incorporated in Subpart 2.4 of NQA-1-2008, NQA-1a-2009, and IEEE Std.336-1985, all applicable to the NuScale design per NQA-2008 and NQA-1a-2009.
