It includes details on the plant location (1.1.1), power output (1.1.4), project schedule (1.1.5), and the format and content of the report (1.1.6). The general plant description (1.2) covers principal site characteristics (1.2.1), the general arrangement of major structures and equipment (1.2.2), and plant features of special interest (1.2.3). There is also a comparison with other facilities (1.3), identification of agents and contractors (1.4), division of responsibility (1.4.2), and principal consultants and other participants (1.4.3). Section 1.7 references drawings and other detailed information. Section 1.4.1 is noted as "Not Used."

Section 1.7 covers Electrical and Instrumentation and Control (I&C) Drawings, including Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams, with system drawings summarized in Table 1.7-2. Electrical symbols are detailed in Figures 1.7-1a and 1.7-1b, while the Instrumentation and Controls Symbol Legend is provided in Figure 1.7-2. Piping and Instrumentation Diagram Legends are presented across Figures 1.7-3a through 1.7-3f. Tables and figures, such as Table 1.1-1 and Figure 1.1-1, are sequentially numbered within each section and placed at the end of the relevant "X.Y" section. The I&C architectural design emphasizes clear interconnection interfaces, separation of safety and nonsafety systems, and functional simplification, with the primary I&C systems described in Section 7.0.

The provided passages from the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, Table 1.1-1, present an extensive list of acronyms and abbreviations used throughout the document, each with its corresponding description. These include terms related to power sources (AAC: alternate AC power, AAPS: auxiliary AC power source), reactor systems and components (ABWR: Advanced Boiling Water Reactor, AFWS: auxiliary feedwater system, MCR: main control room, RXC: reactor core, SFP: spent fuel pool, SG: steam generator), safety and accident terminology (AOO: anticipated operational occurrence, ATWS: anticipated transient without scram, BDBE: beyond design basis event, SBO: station blackout, SBLOCA: small-break loss-of-coolant accident), organizations and standards (ACI: American Concrete Institute, ASME: American Society of Mechanical Engineers, ANSI: American National Standards Institute), instrumentation and monitoring (ARM: area radiation monitor, MEMS: meteorological and environmental monitoring system, SDIS: safety display and indication system), waste management (LWMS: liquid waste management system, RWMS: radioactive waste management system, SWMS: solid waste management system), and various building and system designations (ANB: Annex Building, RWB: Radioactive Waste Building, TGB: Turbine Generator Building). The list also covers technical concepts such as ALARA (as low as reasonably achievable), MCHFR (minimum critical heat flux ratio), MWe/MWt (megawatt electric/thermal), and UHS (ultimate heat sink), as well as abbreviations for safety analysis, seismic considerations, and control systems. This comprehensive table ensures clarity and consistency in terminology throughout the NuScale safety documentation.

The provided passages from the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, Tier 2, Introduction, contain an extensive list of acronyms and abbreviations used throughout the document, along with their definitions. These include technical systems, components, organizations, and regulatory terms relevant to the NuScale Power Module (NPM) and NuScale Power Plant (NPP) design and operation. Examples include safety and control systems such as the backup diesel generator (BDG), emergency core cooling system (ECCS), decay heat removal system (DHRS), reactor protection system (RPS), and containment vessel (CNV). The list also covers regulatory and industry standards like the Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), and General Design Criteria (GDC), as well as analytical and operational terms such as core damage frequency (CDF), loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA), and probabilistic risk assessment (PRA). Additionally, it includes references to organizations (NRC, DOE, INL), testing and qualification procedures (inservice inspection (ISI), integrated leak rate testing (ILRT)), and various plant subsystems (chemical and volume control system (CVCS), feedwater system (FWS), reactor coolant system (RCS)). This comprehensive table ensures clarity and consistency in terminology for all technical, operational, and regulatory aspects discussed in the NuScale FSAR.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, Section 1.7, provides detailed information and legends for interpreting instrumentation and controls (I&C), electrical, and mechanical drawings, which are included as figures to visually clarify design information. Figure 1.7-1a presents the single-line diagram legend for electrical symbols, detailing components such as 3-winding and 2-winding power transformers (with high and low side windings, MVA/kVA ratings, cooling methods, and impedance ratings), current and potential transformers (with quantity, transformer ratio, and accuracy class), and high voltage (>100 kV) circuit breakers (with ampere rating and normally open status). Figure 1.7-1b expands on electrical symbols, including motor generator fields, control power transformers, indicating lights (with color codes: A=Amber, B=Blue, C=Clear, G=Green, R=Red, W=White), various switches (pressure, temperature, level, flow, limit, speed), push buttons (normally closed/open, 2-position), resistors (fixed/variable), diodes, SCRs, thyristors, Zener diodes, arc suppressors, terminal boards, solenoids, coil relays, RTDs (resistance temperature detectors), horns, welding receptacles, and push-to-test indicating lights. Abbreviations such as DCS (Distributed Control System), GTO (Gate Turn Off), NO (Normally Open), NC (Normally Closed), PLC (Programmable Logic Controller), and SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier) are defined. Figure 1.7-2 provides logic gate symbol definitions for OR, AND, and NOT gates, specifying their output conditions. Figure 1.7-3a describes piping and instrumentation diagram (P&ID) legends, noting that all vents and drains are represented by a 0.75" normally closed valve unless otherwise specified. Figure 1.7-3b details valve positions on P&IDs, indicating that all inline valves are normally open (except butterfly valves), with specific symbols for fail-open, fail-locked-as-is, drift-open, control valve fail positions, and fail-closed positions, and that open status during normal operation is standard for all valves except butterfly valves.

The passages provide detailed conventions and symbol definitions for electrical schematics in nuclear engineering, covering medium voltage (1kV–100kV) and low voltage (<1kV) circuit breakers, with ampere ratings indicated on the right and contact status (N.O. = normally open, N.C. = normally closed) on the left. Device types include inverters (with DC input and AC output voltages), multicell batteries (voltage rating, amp-hour rating, number of cells), fuses (right: ampere rating, left: quantity), safety disconnect switches (top right: ampere rating), transfer switches (ATS = auto transfer, STATIC = static switch), cable bus, surge capacitors, connection stabs, electrical heaters (top: kW rating), synchronous generators (operating frequency, RPM, % power factor), lightning arresters (right: voltage rating, top left: quantity), relays/devices (referenced by NP-ES-0303-2923), and various transformer types (delta, wye, delta-wye with resistance ground, potential transformer with double secondary, transformer ratio, and quantity). Additional components include chargers (AC input, DC output), fused safety switches, VFDs, selector switches, monitoring devices (BM = battery monitor), local controllers, phasor indicators, meters (ammeter, frequency, synchroscope, voltmeter, VAR, watt, temperature, digital multimeter), power bus, rotary switches, and disconnect switches with interlocked grounding. Terminal numbering conventions are specified for different compartments (starter, field terminal box, local control panel, DCS/PLC panel, MCC, low/medium voltage switchgear). Abbreviations and ratings are provided for transformers (primary-secondary voltage, MVA/kVA, phases), loads (electrical load in kW, motors in HP), generators (Hz, RPM, % PF), conductors/cables (rated voltage, current, short circuit rating, ID tags), and protection devices (thermal overload, circuit breakers). Interlocks are denoted as K (key), M (mechanical), E (electrical), and line types reference conductor/cable specifications.

The passages detail conventions and specifications for instrumentation and equipment identification in nuclear engineering documentation. Instrument connections are labeled using codes such as NP12-YY-DYY-E-SL-YYY-SYY, with connections to/from designated equipment (ZZZ) and referenced by connecting drawing identifiers. Instrumentation tagging includes component type, function identifier (per ANSI/ISA 5.1-2009), and unique identifiers, with location and accessibility specified (e.g., field-mounted, panel-mounted, operator-accessible). Temperature elements are thermocouples unless otherwise noted. Communication links are defined for fieldbus systems, intelligent and smart devices, and mechanical connections. Instrumentation line symbols distinguish between process and equipment connections, impulse lines, analyzer sample lines, and heat/cool traced lines, with tracing types indicated (ET for electrical, ST for steam, CW for chilled water). Equipment specifications include capacity (gallons, tons), duty cycle (%), motor nameplate (HP), air/water flow (CFM, GPM), entering/leaving water temperatures (°F), power (kW), and heating/cooling capacities (BTU/hr, kW). Numbering formats for lines, components, and drawings are standardized, incorporating system abbreviations, module numbers, unique identifiers, and discipline codes. Abbreviations from function identifier tables must be used consistently, especially when multiple element types appear on a drawing. Pressure values (psig) are listed in tabular form, ranging from 40 to 3459 psig, corresponding to various equipment or process points. Recommendations for equipment specifications are provided, but required specifications are at the designer’s discretion.

The system utilizes bistable outputs that provide a logic “1” when the measured variable exceeds or falls below the setpoint value, depending on configuration. An AND gate produces a true output only if at least 2 out of 4 inputs are true. Main Control Room (MCR) indications include system trip/actuation status, operational bypass permissive status, and a first out annunciator for system trip/actuation. Annunciators in the MCR display system trip/actuation status, and the system is organized into four redundant separation groups (SGs) labeled A, B, C, and D. Control interfaces include momentary and maintained-position hand switches in the MCR, as well as locally mounted switches for maintenance and testing. A momentary hand switch in the MCR can initiate functions with a preset time delay; if the input signal returns to false before the delay elapses, the output remains false. The MCS momentary manual control interface in the MCR allows nonsafety control of MPS-actuated equipment. Some outputs are defined as nonlinear or unspecified functions of the input, as detailed in accompanying notes, while others are the algebraic sum of the inputs.

The provided passages from the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report detail the legends and identification conventions used in Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs). System vents and drains are labeled as "OPEN VENT OR DRAIN" or "CLOSED VENT OR DRAIN," with "XXXX" representing the system abbreviation. Instrumentation identification uses a structured letter code: the first letter indicates the measured or initiating variable (e.g., F for flow, P for pressure, T for temperature), followed by modifiers for function, readout, output, and other attributes. Common combinations include controllers (e.g., FIC for flow indicating controller), readout devices (e.g., PI for pressure indicator), switches and alarms (e.g., PSL for pressure switch low), transmitters, solenoids, relays, computing devices, primary elements, test points, viewing devices, safety devices, and final elements. Additional function identifiers specify analysis methods or sensor types, such as FR (flame rod), UV (ultraviolet), IR (infrared), n (neutron radiation), and various chemical and physical property sensors (e.g., pH, DO for dissolved oxygen, TDS for total dissolved solids). Flow measurement devices are also coded (e.g., VENT for venturi tube, TUR for turbine, DP for differential pressure). Temperature sensors include thermocouples (TC, TCE, TCJ, TCK, TCT), thermistors (THRM), and resistance temperature detectors (RTD). Miscellaneous equipment and functions are identified with abbreviations such as HOA (hand-off-auto), PB (pushbutton), ALM (alarm), and ANN (annunciator). The legends also clarify diagrammatic conventions for signal types and supply pressures, and note that the tables are not all-inclusive, allowing for user-defined combinations as needed. Arrows in diagrams indicate failure or unactuated flow paths.

The provided passages list and categorize a comprehensive range of components, symbols, and devices used in nuclear plant piping and instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs), as referenced in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. The document details numerous valve types—including gate, globe, ball, butterfly, plug, diaphragm, check, blowdown, and specialty valves (e.g., backflow preventers, fire hydrants, and safety showers)—along with their operational statuses (NO, NC, FO, FC, FL, LO, LC) and actuator types (manual, pneumatic, hydraulic, solenoid, rotary/linear piston, spring/weight actuated, pilot actuated, and electrohydraulic). It also covers specialty items such as flame and hammer arrestors, expansion joints, strainers, vents, filters, sample coolers, desuperheaters, oil separators, sight glasses, and various fittings (reducers, flanges, unions, hose connections). The legend includes tanks, vessels, hoppers, pumps (centrifugal, positive displacement, diaphragm, submersible, metering, screw, turbine), compressors, blowers, and heat exchangers (shell & tube, plate & frame, double pipe, helical coil, steam generator, evaporator, condenser, cooling tower). HVAC components (filters, dampers, fans, coils, humidifiers, dehumidifiers), drivers (manual, electric motor, steam turbine, diesel engine, pneumatic, hydraulic), and sample analysis accessories are also specified. Instrumentation elements for flow, pressure, temperature, and level measurement are detailed, including orifice plates, venturi tubes, flow nozzles, pitot tubes, various flowmeters (thermal mass, magnetic, vortex, turbine, wedge, ultrasonic, Coriolis), and level sensors (float, displacer, radar, probe). The document provides standardized symbols and notes for each component, ensuring clarity and consistency in P&ID drawings for nuclear facility systems.

Exceptions must be noted on the P&ID. Line types and symbols include instrument electric power supply (indicate voltage and type, e.g., ES-220 VAC, which may be replaced by 24 VDC, 120 VAC, etc.), hydraulic signal, filled thermal element capillary tube, filled sensing line between pressure seal and instrument, instrument hydraulic power supply (indicate pressure, e.g., HS-70 PSIG), guided electromagnetic signal, guided sonic signal, fiber optic signal, and communication link/system bus for shared display and control systems. Line symbols include primary line, secondary line, steam trace line, electrical trace line, and pneumatic signal. System components and parameters to be specified include air cooler (tube DP/DT and OP/OT in PSIG/°F, design duty in BTU/HR, configuration, motor name plate HP), compressor (DP/DT and OP/OT in PSIG/°F, capacity in FT³/MIN), filters (DP/DT and OP/OT in PSIG/°F, size ID x T-T, particle size in micron), pressure vessel (DP/DT and OP/OT in PSIG/°F, size ID x T-T), heat exchanger (design duty in BTU/HR, duty cycle %, shell and tube DP/DT and OP/OT in PSIG/°F), pump (capacity in GPM, duty cycle %, design head in FT, motor name plate HP, DP/DT in PSIG/°F), and tank (size ID x height in FT, capacity in GAL, DP/DT and OP/OT in PSIG/°F). Boundary identification includes limits of upstream and downstream pipe class or line number, area identification (Area A, Area B), and drawing connector ID. Operating parameters to be indicated are fluid temperature (°F) and pressure (psig) at various points.

The 2006 version of ISA-67.04.01 updates guidance for evaluating instrument channel operability by comparing as-found and as-left values from previous instrument calibrations for instrument setpoints. Compliance with Clause 5.5 of IEEE 7-4.3.2-2003 depends on the use of field programmable gate array technology, which renders some tests, such as calculation and watchdog timer tests, inapplicable. Digital Instrumentation and Control (I&C) safety systems conform to various sections of IEEE Std 7-4.3.2-2003, as endorsed by RG 1.152 (with exceptions and clarifications), including Section 5.4 for equipment qualification, Sections 5.5 and 5.15 for reliability, integrity, and completion of protective action, and guidance for computer-based interlocks. Additionally, these systems adhere to Section 5.11 for identification, Sections 5.7, 5.5.2, and 5.5.3 for test and calibration capability, and partially conform to RG 1.118 for test and calibration as referenced in Table 1.9-2. These criteria are applicable to safety systems, including the Engineered Safety Feature Digital System (EDSS).

The provided passages summarize conformance of the Instrumentation and Control (I&C) system to various regulatory guidance documents. The I&C system architecture and simplicity conform to DSRS Appendices 7.0-B and 7.0-C, Rev 0, with no exceptions noted. Independence requirements conform to DSRS 7.1.2, Rev 0, ensuring compliance with RG 1.75 and RG 1.152. Redundancy is addressed in DSRS 7.1.3, Rev 0, conforming to RG 1.53. Predictability and repeatability requirements also conform per DSRS 7.1.4, Rev 0. Quality requirements conform to DSRS 7.2.1, Rev 0, and RG 1.28, with reference to Table 1.9-2. Displays and monitoring requirements in DSRS 7.2.13, Rev 0, partially conform to RG 1.97 (see Table 1.9-2.7.2.13) and fully conform to RG 1.47, with no exceptions noted.

The NuScale Instrumentation and Control (I&C) design's Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) assessment conforms to the guidelines in NUREG/CR-6303, as described in DSRS 7.1.5 and referenced in Table 1.9-3. Methods for performing D3 analyses of reactor protection systems are consistent with regulatory expectations. Several staff positions and guidance documents, including Digital I&C-ISG-02 (covering BTP 7-19 Position 4 Challenges, Effects of Common Cause Failure, Echelons of Defense, and Single Failure) and Digital I&C-ISG-03 (Risk-Informed Digital Instrumentation and Controls), are deemed not applicable to the NuScale Design Certification Application (DCA). The position on defense against common-mode failures in digital I&C systems, specifically regarding the use of defense-in-depth and diversity for advanced reactor design approval, is also noted as conforming, with no additional comments.

Section 7.1.5 of DSRS Rev 0 addresses Diversity and Defense in Depth, confirming conformance with regulatory guidance from SECY-93-087 (summarized in Section 7.1.5), GL 85-06 and 10 CFR 50.62 (summarized in Section 7.1.6), and the quality assurance program described in Section 17.5. The design also conforms to RG 1.53 and RG 1.62, with references provided in Table 1.9-2. Internal diversity within the MPS (Module Protection System) is identified as a simpler approach that meets the intent of the diverse scram elements of the ATWS Rule and satisfies acceptance criteria II.3.A.ii and II.3.C(2) for evolutionary plants.

The document details conformance to various Regulatory Guides (RGs) and Generic Letters (GLs) as referenced in DSRS sections. Full conformance is noted for RG 1.152 (see Table 1.9-2.7.2.1), RG 1.209 (no exceptions), and GL 91-04, with the latter applied to setpoint methodology per TR-616-49121. Partial conformance is reported for RGs 1.168, 1.169, 1.170, 1.171, 1.173, 1.151, 1.204, and 1.105, with specific references to Table 1.9-2 or Table 1.9-2.7.2.1 for details. Sections on derivation of system inputs (DSRS 7.2.6), auxiliary features (DSRS 7.2.8), and interaction between sense and command features and other systems (DSRS 7.2.10) state full conformance, noting the absence of specific DSRS acceptance criteria, though relevant guidance is used for review.

The review of DSRS sections indicates the following conformance to regulatory guides: DSRS 7.2.2 (Equipment Qualification) partially conforms to RG 1.180, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2. DSRS 7.2.4 (Operating and Maintenance Bypasses) fully conforms to RG 1.47 with no exceptions. DSRS 7.2.9 (Control of Access, Identification, and Repair) fully conforms to RG 1.75 with no exceptions. DSRS 7.2.11 (Multi-Unit Stations) fully conforms to RG 1.53 with no exceptions. DSRS 7.2.12 (Automatic and Manual Controls) fully conforms to RG 1.62, with additional information referenced in Table 1.9-2. All technical details and referenced regulatory guides are maintained as specified in the passages.

SRP Appendix 18-A, Revision 0, titled "Crediting Manual Operator Actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) Analyses," supersedes DI&C ISG-05, Section 3, for all phases of review criteria, including Phase 1 (Analysis), Phase 2 (Preliminary Validation), Phase 3 (Integrated System Validation), and Phase 3 (Maintaining Long-Term Integrity of Credited Manual Actions in the D3 Analysis). The appendix is referenced in sections 7.1.5, 18.4, 18.6, and 18.7, and is confirmed as conforming for each review phase. Additionally, the acceptance criteria in Digital I&C-ISG-05, Section 3 (pages 13–21), are now superseded by Chapter 18, Appendix 18-A.

Digital I&C-ISG-02, which addresses Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3), is not applicable to the NuScale Design Certification Application (DCA), and thus its staff position on adequate diversity and manual operator actions does not apply. Similarly, Digital I&C-ISG-04, concerning Highly Integrated Control Rooms and Digital Communication Systems, is also not applicable to the NuScale DCA. However, the topics covered by ISG-04, specifically interdivisional communications, are addressed in NuScale Power, LLC's topical report TR-1015-18653-P-A, "Design of the Highly Integrated Protection System Platform Topical Report." This information is summarized in Table 1.9-4, which documents conformance with Interim Staff Guidance (ISG).

The acceptance criterion for Minimum Inventory in Digital I&C-ISG-05 (pages 9–11) is partially conformed to, except for certain subtier guidance. The acceptance criteria for Phase 1: Analysis (Section 3.1.B, pages 15–16), Phase 2: Preliminary Validation (Section 3.2.B, page 18), and Phase 3: Integrated System Validation (Section 3.3.B, pages 19–20) of Digital I&C-ISG-05 are not applicable, as they are superseded by Chapter 18 Appendix 18-A Criteria 1.B, 2.B, and 3.B, respectively. Digital I&C-ISG-06 guidance is not applicable, as it pertains to the review of licensing basis change requests from existing licensees for digital I&C upgrades. Similarly, Digital I&C-ISG-07 guidance is not applicable, as it addresses the review of proposed measures to protect digital I&C equipment used as items relied on for safety (IROFS) at fuel cycle facilities from unintentional digital events.

The enhanced firefighting capabilities must address the expectation elements specified in Section 4.1.3 of Technical Report TR-0816-50796 (Reference 20.2-1). Several acceptance criteria from SRP 9.5.1.1, Rev 0, including those related to Fire Protection Probabilistic Risk Assessment (II.1), Fire Protection Program Considerations for License Renewal (II.2), Fire Protection for Permanently Shutdown and Decommissioning Reactor Plants (II.4), and Operational Program and Proposed Implementation Milestones (II.7), are not applicable, as they pertain to COL applicants referencing the NuScale design, license renewal applicants, or reactors undergoing decommissioning. The development and implementation of a risk-informed, performance-based fire protection program, as outlined in SRP 9.5.1.2, Rev 0, is also the responsibility of COL applicants electing to implement 10 CFR 50.48(c). NuScale partially conforms to NRC staff positions and guidelines on fire protection (II.3), using current year subtier documents, and to enhanced fire protection criteria for new reactor designs (II.6), which specify passive separation of redundant trains as the preferred approach to ensure safe-shutdown capability.

The provided passages summarize the applicability of various regulatory guides (RGs), safety guides, and requirements for evaluating radiological consequences and other safety aspects in nuclear power reactors. RG 1.4 and RG 1.183 pertain to loss of coolant accident assumptions for pressurized water reactors (PWRs), with RG 1.183 specified for new reactors in SRP Section 15.0.3. Safety Guide 5 addresses steam line break accident assumptions for boiling water reactors (BWRs) only. RG 1.24 covers assumptions for PWR radioactive gas storage tank failures, with site-specific guidance required from the COL applicant or licensee. RG 1.25 applies to fuel handling accidents in both BWRs and PWRs using TID14844 source terms, unless the alternative source term under 10 CFR 50.67 is authorized. RG 1.98 addresses radioactive offgas system failures in BWRs only. RG 1.195 provides methods and assumptions for evaluating design basis accident radiological consequences at light-water reactors, also limited to TID14844 source terms for existing reactors not using the alternative source term. DC/COL-ISG-10 guidance on adverse flow effects applies except for BWR-specific aspects. 10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(xii) regarding alternative hydrogen control systems is excluded from design certification applications per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f). 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(vii) on management plans for design and construction applies only to reactor facility license applicants and holders. Issue 191, concerning assessment of debris accumulation on PWR sump performance, is confirmed as conforming.

The evaluation of control room habitability for the NuScale SMR design references several regulatory guides and standards. Aspects of RG 1.78 related to control room habitability design are partially applicable to NuScale. Inleakage testing per RG 1.197 is not applicable, as these activities are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee referencing the certified design. SRP 6.4, Rev 3, II.6.A dose guidelines apply only to currently operating reactors and are not applicable, while II.6.B dose guidelines for new reactors conform, with RG 1.183 being partially applicable. DSRS 15.0.3, II.2 requirements for control room radiological habitability fully conform, and II.3 requirements for Technical Support Center (TSC) radiological habitability partially conform, with the dose acceptance criterion met for the TSC when AC power is available. SRP 18.0, Rev 2, II.B on human factors engineering aspects of control room modifications is not applicable, as it pertains to existing reactor licensees seeking NRC approval for modifications. Analytical methods for control room habitability, including dose limits and accident duration per III.E, conform to regulatory standards.

Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.111 addresses methods for estimating atmospheric transport and dispersion of gaseous effluents from routine releases at light-water-cooled reactors, and is applicable except for the requirement of site-specific dispersion data, which must be provided by the COL applicant or licensee. RG 1.112 covers the calculation of releases of radioactive materials in gaseous and liquid effluents from light-water-cooled nuclear power reactors, and is also applicable except for the need for site-specific information such as meteorological data, which is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. RG 1.113, which provides guidance for analyzing the aquatic dispersion of radioactive liquid effluents from component failures in accordance with BTP 11-6 for the purpose of implementing Appendix I, is not applicable in this context.

The design guidance for radioactive waste management systems, structures, and components installed in light-water-cooled nuclear power plants (RG 1.143) is partially conformed to, with the exception that aspects related to steam generator blowdown systems are not applicable; however, the radioactive waste management system design criteria specified in the RG are applicable. For BTP 11-3, Rev 4, regarding solid radioactive waste management systems, the guidance for processing requirements (B.1) is conformed to, except for aspects related to Process Control Program (PCP) development and implementation, which apply to COL applicants. The guidance for waste storage (B.3) is fully conformed to with no exceptions. For portable solid waste systems (B.4), the guidance is partially conformed to, except for aspects related to the control and use of portable solid radwaste processing equipment applicable to COL applicants. Similarly, for additional design features (B.5), the guidance is partially conformed to, with exceptions for PCP development and implementation aspects relevant to COL applicants.

BTP 11-5, Rev 4, Section B.1 (Waste Gas System Leak or Failure Analysis) is partially applicable, with exceptions for BWR-specific or site-specific aspects. Section B.2 (Staff Method for Analysis) fully conforms with no exceptions. For BTP 11-6, Rev 4, Section B.4 (Calculations of Transport Capabilities in Groundwater or Surface Water) and Section B.7 (Specifications on Tank Waste Radioactivity Concentration Levels), the criteria are not applicable, as the development of representative site parameters and compliance with tank waste radioactivity concentration guidance are site-specific and the responsibility of the COL applicant.

The passages address BTP 11-6, Rev 4, concerning postulated radioactive releases due to liquid-containing tank failures. Section B.2 (Mitigating Design Features) is not applicable and is the responsibility of the COL applicant. Section B.3 (Radioactive Source Term) partially conforms to the acceptance criterion, except for BWR-specific or site-specific aspects, which are also the COL applicant's responsibility. Section B.5 (Exposure Scenarios and Acceptance Criteria) is not applicable, as the development of representative site parameters is site-specific and assigned to the COL applicant. Section B.6 (SRP Dose Acceptance Criteria) is similarly not applicable and is the responsibility of the COL applicant.

Heat from the circulating water loop, serving up to six condensers in one Turbine Generator Building (TGB), is rejected to the atmosphere via a set of evaporative mechanical-draft cooling towers. Circulating water pumps draw water from a common basin to supply cooling water to the condensers. After absorbing heat, the water exits the condensers and flows by gravity to the mechanical-draft cooling towers, where excess heat is removed before the cooled water returns to the common basin. Similarly, heated site cooling water from individual system heat exchangers is directed to dedicated mechanical-draft cooling towers for heat removal, with the cooled water also returning by gravity to the common basin.

The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive system that supplies clean, compressed, breathable air to the Main Control Room (MCR) during a radioactive release or loss of AC power, maintaining positive pressure relative to adjacent areas. The CRHS has sufficient compressed air storage to provide clean air to the MCR for at least 72 hours after an initiating event, with further design and operational details in Section 6.4. The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) provides secondary side reactor cooling for non-LOCA events when normal feedwater or secondary-side cooling is unavailable. The DHRS is a passive, safety-related system with redundant, single active failure-proof capability to cool the reactor core and coolant to safe shutdown conditions, without relying on actuation of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS). The DHRS operates as a two-phase natural circulation system, designed to prevent flow disruption from gas accumulation and to account for the heat transfer limiting effects of maximum noncondensible gas accumulation. Long-term core cooling methodology is detailed in NuScale Technical Report TR-0916-51299.

The NuScale Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) serves a function similar to the Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system in large pressurized water reactors (PWRs), but operates via passive natural circulation without pumps. While the DHRS performs some AFW functions, it is specifically designed for NuScale reactor transients and system characteristics, with tailored actuation and indication features. Key monitored parameters include DHRS pressure, valve position indication, and reactor coolant system pressure and temperature. Regulatory requirements such as 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xii) and 10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(ii) are not considered applicable to the NuScale DHRS due to differences in design intent and system operation.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes the operation and design of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and its integration with the secondary system. During wet layup, feedwater is recirculated through the DHRS heat exchangers, and secondary water chemistry is controlled before the steam generators (SGs) are isolated from the main steam and feedwater systems. The DHRS connects to the secondary system without direct interface to the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), with SGs detailed in Section 5.4.1 and the containment system piping coupling the DHRS to the SGs described in Section 6.2.4. To ensure DHRS safety function, the accumulation of noncondensible gases is evaluated, assuming a gas temperature of 60°F based on a minimum pool temperature of 65°F. Analyses include the effects of heat transfer surface fouling, SG tube plugging, and noncondensible gas accumulation on DHRS performance across the DHRS loop inventory range. The report also calculates the maximum mass of noncondensible gas that may accumulate within the secondary system before, during, and after DHRS actuation.

The Reactor Building structure, NPM operating bays, and the placement of the NPM within these bays provide protection against both external and internal missiles, with no credible sources of internally generated missiles above the NPM due to the absence of nearby rotating equipment. The DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) is protected from pipe whip, as detailed in Section 3.6. The DHRS does not share any active or passive components between individual NPMs, in accordance with GDC 5, ensuring independent safety function performance. While the NPMs share the reactor pool as the ultimate heat sink for DHRS passive condensers, shared power supplies do not impact DHRS safety functions since DHRS active components fail-safe upon loss of power. During NPM movement and refueling, the DHRS is not functional; instead, decay heat removal is achieved via conduction through the RPV and containment shell with RVVs and RRVs open, or by direct contact with reactor pool water during refueling. The DHRS is not affected by potential LOCA loads, as it is not connected to the RCS and its critical components are located outside the CNV. Protection during NPM movement is ensured by a single failure-proof Reactor Building crane, as described in Section 9.1, and the DHRS remains unavailable during refueling and maintenance operations.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the design and failure modes of key Reactor Coolant System components, focusing on the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS). DHRS actuation valves (DHR-HOV-0101A/B, DHR-HOV-0201A/B) are normally closed and fail open upon actuation signal or loss of control power, ensuring DHRS operation without requiring added inventory. Failure modes include spurious opening, which creates an open flow path to the steam generator, and failure to open, which is mitigated by redundant valves and the unaffected DHRS train. Main steam isolation valves (MS-HOV-0101, MS-HOV-0201) and feedwater isolation valves (FW-HOV-0137, FW-HOV-0237) are normally open and fail closed to establish a closed DHRS cooling loop; failure to close results in continued inventory maintenance by downstream valves or temporary addition of DHRS inventory until feedwater flow ceases. The SG thermal relief valves (SG-RV-0102, SG-RV-0202) provide pressure boundary protection, but a spurious opening with failure to close leads to loss of DHRS train inventory and inoperability. All failure modes are analyzed for mechanical and electrical/I&C causes, with system effects and detection methods specified.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes the actuation of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) as being automatically controlled by the Module Protection System (MPS), with manual initiation possible from the control room or a remote location outside the main control room; however, for physical and cyber security, the MPS does not permit remote access to operate safety-related systems. The DHRS can be actuated without reducing Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure or temperature, as it uses the normally operating steam generators (SGs) as the interface with the RCS. Part of the DHRS is submerged in the reactor pool and shielded from internally generated missiles by the NuScale Power Module (NPM) operating bay walls. Figures 5.4-12 through 5.4-15 illustrate RCS cooldown responses during transients with one DHRS train operating under both low and high DHRS inventory conditions. In the event of an extended DHRS cooldown following a reactor trip, if the RCS has low boron concentration and the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) is unavailable to add boron, there is a possibility that the DHRS could cool the core enough to reestablish some level of critical neutron power, particularly if the most reactive control rod remains stuck out.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report evaluates four condensation-induced water hammer mechanisms—pressure drop, water cannon, counter flow, and steam pocket collapse—in two-phase piping systems to assess their impact on the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS). Low-pressure discharge is deemed not applicable to the DHRS flowpath due to insufficient natural circulation flow rate and lack of significant restrictions. Analysis confirms that flow-induced vibration is either not expected or its effects are acceptable for the DHRS components' design life. Preservice and inservice inspection requirements per Section XI for Class 2 components of the BPVC are applied to DHRS components, including steam piping, actuation valves, condensers, and condensate piping.

The off-normal transient cases analyzed assume an initial reactor pool temperature of 100°F and reduced heat removal capability due to fouling, steam generator (SG) tube plugging, and accumulation of noncondensible gases. Figures 5.4-12 and 5.4-14 present the reactor coolant system (RCS) cooldown over 4 hours, while Figures 5.4-13 and 5.4-15 show the RCS cooldown over 36 hours. Results in Figure 5.4-16 indicate that increasing the limiting reactor pool temperature to 140°F has only a small effect on the RCS temperature after 36 hours compared to the 100°F initial condition.

The NuScale certified design includes the Reactor Building (RXB), which is situated both above and below grade and houses key facilities such as the ultimate heat sink (comprising the reactor pool, refuel pool, and spent fuel pool), fuel handling areas, a remote shutdown station, and primary systems. The fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas are located in the west end of the RXB and provide space for the spent fuel pool (SFP), refueling pool, and dry dock, as depicted in Figure 1.2-16. The ultimate heat sink is a large, stainless steel-lined, reinforced concrete pool located below plant grade within the RXB, and it consists of the reactor pool area, refueling pool area, and spent fuel pool area. The plant cooling water systems, which are essential for supporting plant operation, are also included. Table 3.2-1 details the location and classification of all systems, structures, and components.

The Control Building (CRB) is located approximately 30 ft east of the Reactor Building (RXB), with a rectangular footprint of about 120 ft by 80 ft at the 100'-0" elevation, and spans both above and below grade. It houses the main control room (MCR) below grade, which contains the equipment, controls, and indications for operation of the Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs), and the technical support center above the MCR, outside the radiological controlled area, for emergency operations and personnel support. Portions of the CRB above the 120'-0" elevation, inside the elevator shaft and stairwells (full building height), and the east-side fire protection vestibule are nonsafety-related and Seismic Category II, constructed with structural steel and metal siding. The remainder of the CRB below the 120'-0" elevation is a safety-related, Seismic Category I concrete structure, with the lowest elevation primarily housing electrical equipment and CRHS air bottles. A tunnel with two levels connects the RXB to the CRB. The Annex Building (ANB), a nonsafety-related structure, controls access to the radiologically controlled area (RCA) and provides space for health physics facilities, servicing of potentially radioactive and non-radioactive tooling, fixtures, and instrumentation, security services, and various personnel services.

The Reactor Building (RXB) is centrally located on the site and is primarily a rectangular, reinforced concrete structure measuring approximately 350 ft long, 150 ft wide, and extending 81 ft above nominal plant grade, with its foundation 86 ft below grade (92 ft below grade under the elevator pit and refueling pool). The RXB is classified as Seismic Category I and is designed to withstand aircraft impact, environmental conditions, postulated design basis accidents (internal and external), and design basis threats, while providing radiation protection for plant operations and maintenance personnel. It houses the Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs) and all systems and components required for plant operation and shutdown, with each NPM located in its own three-walled bay within the common reactor pool. The adjacent Radioactive Waste Building (RWB), located both above and below grade, contains equipment and systems for processing radioactive gaseous, liquid, and solid waste, preparing waste for off-site shipment, compacting low-level radioactive waste to reduce volume, and providing temporary storage. The RWB also houses HVAC equipment, including high-efficiency particulate air filtration for both the RXB and RWB, and is designed to maintain radiation exposures to operators and maintenance personnel as low as reasonably achievable.

The Security Buildings (SCBs) are nonsafety-related structures, including the primary access control building, main security building, and vehicle barrier system, that provide controlled access into the Site Outer Controlled Area (SOCA) and the Protected Area (PA) of the plant, controlling both personnel and vehicle entry and screening for unescorted access. The Central Utility Building (CUB) houses equipment for the chilled water system and other ancillary balance of plant systems. The Main Control Room (MCR) is supported by associated spaces such as a conference room for shift turnover, open office area (auxiliary operator room), two offices, storage room, janitor closet, three air locks, viewing area, shift manager’s office, reference room, emergency equipment room, lavatories, break room, and telecommunication room. The Technical Support Center (TSC) and its associated spaces—including records storage, three offices, two conference rooms, data equipment room, lavatories, data maintenance room, and break room—are located at grade level in the Control Room Building (CRB), which also contains the control room HVAC system (CRVS) equipment, chilled water system equipment supporting the CRVS, and an elevator machine room. The Access and Nonradiological Building (ANB) controls access to both radiologically-controlled and nonradiologically-controlled areas of the Reactor Building (RXB), and houses personnel support services (locker rooms, showers, toilets, lunch and conference rooms, first aid), space for decontamination equipment and dosimeter processing, and plant security support facilities (secondary alarm station, security briefing room, armory, security manager's office). If AC power is unavailable, TSC functions are transferred to the main control room.

The NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) are partially immersed in a below-grade reactor pool and protected by passive safety systems, including dedicated emergency core cooling systems (ECCS) and decay heat removal systems (DHRS) for each module. Bioshields are designed for removal to access the NPMs, with the capability to stack bioshields for NPM movement during refueling. The reactor pool, spent fuel pool (SFP), most primary systems, and some radioactive waste equipment are all located below grade, with the reactor pool water surface approximately 6 feet below grade. During normal operations, heat is removed from the pool via the reactor pool cooling system and rejected to the atmosphere through a cooling tower or other external heat sink, while the SFP has an independent cooling system. In a design basis accident with sustained loss of all AC power, decay heat is passively transferred from the NPMs to the pool, causing pool heat-up and boiling. The reactor pool water inventory is sufficient to cool the NPMs for at least 72 hours without water addition and provides an additional barrier for fission product retention beyond the fuel, cladding, reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and containment. During shutdown, NPM movement, or transfer to the refueling area, residual and decay heat are removed by convection and conduction from the reactor to the pool via the reactor coolant system (RCS), flooded containment, and the RPV and containment vessel (CNV) walls. Protection from adverse interactions with other equipment is achieved by submerging passive condensers in the reactor pool and using module bay walls between operating NPMs. Further design and operational details are provided in Section 9.2.5, with references to GDC 45, 46, and PDC 44.

The NPM (NuScale Power Module) is designed for normal power maneuvers, allowing electric power adjustment via turbine bypass to the condenser, and core power maneuvering through control rods, soluble boron concentration changes, or a combination of both, as detailed in Sections 4.2 and 4.3. In multi-module plants, individual NPMs can be incrementally placed into service to match construction schedules and grid demand, and can be taken offline individually for refueling or maintenance. The refueling pool houses bolting tools for NPM disassembly and reassembly, with the reactor core remaining in the lower head of the RPV during refueling. A fuel handling machine transfers new and used fuel between the refueling pool and spent fuel pool (SFP) via a weir. The dry dock area, separated from the refueling pool by a gate, contains the module inspection rack and allows the water level to be lowered for maintenance on the upper NPM. Inspection and testing equipment are moved to the dry dock during refueling, which also serves for introducing new NPM components and preparing them for assembly. During refueling, the RBC (Reactor Building Crane) moves the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool, where it is set into the containment flange tool and the CNV flange is unbolted. The crane then separates the lower CNV from the upper CNV (with RPV attached), moves the upper CNV and RPV to the reactor vessel flange tool, and unbolts the RPV flange. The upper and lower RPV are separated, leaving the lower RPV with the reactor core in the flange tool, while the upper NPM (upper CNV with attached upper RPV) is moved to the module inspection rack in the dry dock for inspection, testing, and maintenance. After these activities and refueling, the upper NPM is returned to the refueling pool and reassembled in reverse order using the flange tools. The reassembled NPM is then moved back to its operating bay by the RBC, where startup tests are performed before the reactor is brought online for steam and power production.

The module control system (MCS) is a distributed control system enabling monitoring and control of module-specific plant components, while the plant control system provides nonsafety inputs to human system interfaces in the main control room (MCR), remote shutdown station, and other necessary locations. The fixed area radiation monitoring system continuously tracks in-plant radiation and airborne radioactivity levels. The safety display and indication system delivers visual displays and indications in the MCR from the module protection system (MPS) and plant protection system, with the latter monitoring and controlling systems common to all NuScale Power Modules (NPMs). The health physics network supplies the permanent communications infrastructure for the radiation protection program. The MCR contains control panels for all installed NPMs, allowing reactor operators to monitor and control multiple NPMs from dedicated panels, with the control room supervisor station providing an overview of all NPMs via multiple monitors. All displays are designed using human factors analysis and graphical representations to enhance simplicity. Typical MCR functions include initiating NPM startup and shutdown, adjusting set points, and taking corrective actions if systems do not operate as intended. Alarm annunciation is provided on the plant group-view overview display monitor as part of the alarm management system, integrating information from individual NPMs via the MPS, MCS, and shared I&C systems. A Technical Support Center (TSC) is provided in compliance with NUREG-0696. The reactor coolant system (RCS) is a subsystem of the NPM.

The reactor pool features two rows of six bays each, aligned along the north and south walls at the east end of the pool, with a central channel for moving Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs) between bays and the refueling pool. Each bay measures approximately 20 ft wide by 20 ft long by 98 ft deep, with a normal water depth of about 69 ft (elevation ~94'). Bays are equipped with a concrete bioshield, consisting of a two-foot-thick reinforced concrete horizontal slab with a stainless steel surface and a vertical assembly of square stainless steel tube framing and nine radiation panel assemblies per side, providing radiation shielding and ventilation. The horizontal slab is bolted to the bay top. At the west end (100'-0" elevation) of the Reactor Building (RXB), operating areas support fuel handling and access to the upper NPM during refueling, with the refueling pool directly connected to the reactor pool for NPM transport via the Reactor Building crane (RBC). A weir between the refueling pool and spent fuel pool (SFP) allows underwater fuel assembly transport during refueling. The fuel handling and maintenance areas are designed for radiation protection, with the area west of the SFP containing a fuel receiving area, a jib crane for loading new fuel assemblies into the new fuel elevator, and pallet jack access for fuel receiving. New fuel assemblies are inspected and temporarily stored in racks in the SFP before core placement. The SFP provides storage for both spent and new fuel assemblies, with spent fuel placed in storage racks prior to removal for dry storage.

The passages summarize the conformance of a reactor design to DSRS 4.4, Rev 0 (Thermal and Hydraulic Design) and DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0 (Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review Responsibilities) criteria. Subchannel hydraulic analysis codes, core oscillations and thermal-hydraulic instabilities, preoperational and initial test programs, critical heat flux (CHF) calculations and process monitoring, and instrumentation/procedures for detection and recovery from inadequate core cooling all conform to requirements, with no exceptions noted. The loose parts monitoring system requirement is marked as a departure, as low primary system flow precludes damage from loose parts, eliminating the need for such a system. Core stability performance during anticipated transients without scram (ATWS) is not applicable due to diverse reactor trip system (RTS) signals that prevent ATWS and associated flow instabilities. For DSRS 15.9.A, there are no requirements under II.1, and the design conforms to GDC 12 under II.2. The criteria for detecting and suppressing system instability consequences (II.3) and for trip before CHF violation are not applicable, as reactor trip signals act before unstable flow oscillations or CHF violations can develop. For II.5, the design partially conforms, as existing reactor trip signals provide an exclusion zone that is already more limiting than what is required to preclude flow instabilities and prevent SAFDL (Specified Acceptable Fuel Design Limits) violations.

Consistent with Commission policy, the coping capability for loss of nonemergency AC power to station auxiliaries eliminates the safety benefit typically provided to large LWRs by an alternate AC power source, such as a gas turbine generator, during station blackout. For DSRS 15.2.6, Rev 0, NuScale's approach conforms to requirements for fuel cladding integrity, incidents of moderate frequency (with events categorized as Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOO) and Initiating Events (IE)), General Design Criteria (GDC) 10 and 15, the most limiting plant system single failure, and the analysis of loss of AC power using analytical models, conservative assumptions, and Regulatory Guide 1.105. No exceptions or additional details are noted for these criteria.

The passages summarize the evaluation of NuScale’s conformance with DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0, which addresses events such as decrease in feedwater temperature, increase in feedwater flow, increase in steam flow, inadvertent opening of the turbine bypass system, and inadvertent operation of the decay heat removal system. The basic objectives are to identify limiting heat removal events and verify that fuel damage and system pressure criteria are met for these events. Specific criteria include maintaining the Minimum Critical Heat Flux Ratio (MCHFR) above the 95/95 limit, with NuScale noting that critical heat flux is a more accurate descriptor than departure from nucleate boiling. Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs) should not escalate to more serious conditions without independent faults, and NuScale’s event classification aligns with SRP requirements. Instrument spans and setpoints conform to RG 1.105. Analytical parameters include an initial power level of 102%, use of conservative scram assumptions, and consideration of core burnup. For DSRS 15.2.7, Rev 0 (Loss of Normal Feedwater Flow), NuScale meets fuel and system pressure parameters and categorizes events as AOOs and Initiating Events (IEs), conforming to regulatory expectations.

The reviewed DSRS sections address NuScale’s conformance with specific criteria and assumptions for various accident scenarios, including steam system piping failures (inside and outside containment), loss of normal feedwater flow, feedwater system pipe breaks, and loss-of-coolant accidents. For steam system piping failures (DSRS 15.1.5), NuScale meets criteria for reactor coolant and main steam system pressure, evaluation of core damage potential—using critical heat flux as a more accurate descriptor than departure from nucleate boiling—safety-related classification and auto-initiation of the decay heat removal system, initial power level and plant operating mode, and core burnup, with no exceptions noted. For loss of normal feedwater flow (DSRS 15.2.7) and feedwater system pipe breaks (DSRS 15.2.8), NuScale conforms to requirements for reactor coolant and main steam system pressures, evaluation of core damage potential (using a heat generation rate limit for slower reactivity insertions to prevent fuel centerline melting), calculated site boundary doses, and assumptions for initial plant conditions and postulated failures. For loss-of-coolant accidents (DSRS 15.6.5), NuScale meets TMI Action Plan requirements. No deviations or exceptions are identified in any section unless specifically noted.

DSRS 15.1.5, Rev 0 addresses steam system piping failures both inside and outside of containment, with specific criteria and assumptions evaluated for conformance. The radiological criteria for steam line breaks are met, with no exceptions noted. Assumptions considered include loss of offsite power, postulated steam line break effects, and the worst-case failure of a single active component, all of which conform to requirements. For initial core flow, NuScale has determined that critical heat flux more accurately represents plant phenomena than departure from nucleate boiling. Both postulated failures of non-seismic and seismic main steam lines are addressed and conform to criteria. Limiting consequence assessment when operator action is credited is not applicable, as operator action is not required to mitigate the consequences of a steam line break.

The review of DSRS 15.1.6, Rev 0, "Loss of Containment Vacuum," confirms conformance with all specified criteria and analytical parameters. For specific criteria, the reactor coolant pressure and cladding integrity requirements are met, anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs) do not generate more serious conditions, instrument spans and setpoints are established per RG 1.105, and the limiting single failure is identified. For analytical parameters, a conservative scram is used, core burnup is considered, heat transfer from the reactor coolant system (RCS) to containment and reactor pool is maximized, and setpoint inaccuracies are addressed using guidance from RG 1.105. No exceptions or additional comments are noted for any criterion or parameter.

The NuScale design was evaluated against DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0, which covers initiating events such as Loss of External Load, Turbine Trip, Loss of Condenser Vacuum, Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV), and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed). For the basic objectives regarding initiating events (II.1), the design conforms fully. For specific criteria for events of moderate frequency (II.2), the design partially conforms because NuScale does not have a steam pressure regulator. The design conforms to criteria for Reactor Coolant System and Main Steam System Pressures (II.2.A), Incidents of Moderate Frequency (II.2.C), Instrument Setpoints and their impact on plant response (II.2.D), Most Limiting Plant System Single Failure (II.2.E), and Performance of Nonsafety-Related Systems and Single Failures of Active and Passive Systems (II.2.F). The analyzed Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs) that could lead to Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) overpressure include loss of load, turbine trip (with and without bypass), loss of condenser vacuum, inadvertent MSIV closure, steam pressure regulator failure closed, loss of normal AC power, loss of feedwater, and inadvertent operation of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS). These AOOs are detailed in DCD Chapter 15, including initial conditions, system parameters, analysis assumptions, systems and equipment assumed to operate, reactor trip signals, and sensitivity analyses.

The passages summarize the evaluation of DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0, which addresses the following events: Loss of External Load, Turbine Trip, Loss of Condenser Vacuum, Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve, and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed). For each event, the analytical model parameters were reviewed in four areas: initial power level and modes of operation (II.3.A), scram characteristics (II.3.B), core burnup (II.3.C), and instrumentation setpoints for mitigating system actuation (II.3.D). In all cases, the values of parameters used in the analytical model were found to conform to requirements, with no exceptions noted.

The review of DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2, Rev 0, addressing a Chemical and Volume Control System malfunction that increases reactor coolant inventory, confirms conformance in several key areas: (1) the event is classified as an Anticipated Operational Occurrence (AOO); (2) the sequence of events from initiation to stabilization, including assumptions regarding equipment operation, failure, and required operator actions, is adequately described; (3) the evaluation model used is either an approved model or is properly justified; (4) input parameters and initial conditions related to scram characteristics are appropriate; and (5) input parameters and initial conditions regarding core burnup are suitable. No exceptions or deficiencies are noted in any of these areas.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features two collocated steam generators (SGs) within the reactor pressure vessel, which transfer heat from the primary system to generate steam from feedwater via natural circulation under both normal and off-normal conditions. Thermal-hydraulic performance testing of the SGs was conducted in an electrically heated facility using Joule heating, where a known electrical current was applied to the tube walls to create a constant heat flux boundary condition. Three distinct heating zones were used to simulate subcooled, boiling, and superheat regions. Each SG includes a total heat transfer area with a 10% tube plugging margin, and a fouling factor (as specified in Table 5.4-2) is applied to account for typical end-of-cycle performance based on secondary water chemistry control. The SGs utilize seamless helical tubing, eliminating the need for traditional tube support plates; instead, austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies provide full-circumferential, though not continuous, support to minimize crevice formation and corrosion product buildup. Eight sets of tube support assemblies are positioned between each column of helical tubes, limiting unsupported tube lengths and ensuring sufficiently high modal frequencies to prevent unacceptable flow-induced vibration. The lower SG supports accommodate thermal growth and provide lateral support for the tube supports.

The steam generator (SG) tube program encompasses assessment of degradation, tube inspection requirements, tube integrity assessment, tube plugging, primary-to-secondary leakage monitoring, shell side integrity assessment, primary and secondary side water chemistry control, foreign material exclusion, loose parts management, contractor oversight, self-assessment, and reporting. The internal surface of SG tubes is fully accessible for nondestructive examination methods capable of detecting all relevant degradation types throughout the tube's service life. Individual tubes may be plugged or stabilized to prevent adverse interactions with non-plugged tubes. The SG tube design lacks U-bends, allowing volumetric examination along the entire tube length as specified in Item B16.10 of Table IWB-2500-1 (B-Q). Preservice examinations are conducted per BPVC Section III, Paragraph NB-5280, and Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2200, using appropriate examination methods. The tube length is defined from the tube-to-tubesheet weld at the inlet to the weld at the outlet, with the welds themselves not considered part of the tube. Preservice inspection is performed after tube installation and hydrostatic testing, but before initial power operation, to establish a baseline for future inservice inspections. Tubes exhibiting flaws exceeding 40% of the nominal wall thickness, or flaws that could compromise integrity before the first inservice inspection, or indications affecting future inspectability, must be plugged. The volumetric preservice examination technique must detect all relevant preservice flaws and allow comparison with future inservice inspection results to meet plant technical specification requirements.

The Steam Generator (SG) Program, as described in Section 5.5.4 of the technical specifications, is based on NEI 97-06, Regulatory Guide 1.121, and implements ASME Code Sections III and XI for SG tubes, as well as applicable portions of Section XI of the BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a(b)(2)(iii). The program ensures reasonable assurance of structural and leakage integrity of SG tubes by permitting access to SG components without excessive removal of plates, shields, covers, or piping, thereby minimizing worker dose risk. It provides monitoring and management of tube degradation and precursors, enabling timely preventative and corrective actions, and is integrated into the overall Inservice Inspection (ISI) program. The NuScale SG Program also follows EPRI guidance and applies established commercial SG program requirements, justified by historical causes of SG tube degradation and specific NuScale SG design features. Prototypic testing of SG tube supports is conducted to validate acceptable performance, including wear, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism based on assessments and operating PWR fleet experience. The program supports implementation of tube plugging criteria and inspection frequencies consistent with the operating PWR SG fleet. Appendix B to 10 CFR 50 applies to SG program implementation, and a Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale design must develop and implement a Steam Generator Program for periodic monitoring to ensure SG tube integrity is maintained.

The NuScale steam generator (SG) design minimizes crevices between SG tubes, tube supports, and tubesheets to limit corrosion product buildup, with tube-to-tubesheet contact fully within the primary coolant environment. Full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore eliminates crevices at the tube-to-tubesheet face, and tubes are expanded into both steam and feed plenum tubesheets. The once-through SG design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet plena, preventing accumulation or concentration of materials and making traditional SG blowdown systems unnecessary and ineffective for removing corrosion products or impurities. Instead, any secondary coolant impurities or corrosion products may deposit as scale or film on interior tube surfaces or be removed by carryover, with low concentrations ensured by material selection and chemistry control. Periodic cleaning during outages, using proven chemical or mechanical methods, removes unacceptable corrosion product films. Secondary side SG surfaces are constructed of corrosion-resistant materials such as nickel alloy, stainless steel, or stainless steel cladding, eliminating concerns about degradation from cleaning solutions and negating the need for invasive sludge removal techniques. The design provides access to both internal (secondary) and external (reactor coolant) sides of tubesheets for inspection and foreign object removal. The tube support structure is located within the primary coolant environment, preventing secondary system corrosion products from depositing on the SG shell. Chemistry control for both primary and secondary water is maintained per industry guidelines, with modifications for the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, ensuring corrosion protection for stainless steels and nickel alloys in SG components, as detailed in Sections 5.2.3, 6.1, and 10.3.5, with the secondary water quality control program following NEI 97-06.

Feedwater is supplied to the steam generators (SGs) via piping from the feedwater and condensate system located outside the Reactor Building, with feedwater lines routed through the containment vessel (CNV) and into the lower SG plena, penetrating the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) wall. The SG design incorporates features to minimize tube damage from loose parts wear and allows for periodic inspection and testing of critical areas during refueling to assess structural and pressure boundary integrity. SG tubes are supported against vibration and seismic loads by vertical bars extending through the tube bundle from the feed to the steam plena, reducing the likelihood of tube failure and eliminating the potential for pipe whip due to tube-side jetting; feedwater enters the SG tubes at their lowest point. A fluid elastic instability analysis was conducted for the tube bundle across 50 vibration modes, comparing effective cross-flow velocity to a critical velocity determined by Connor's criteria. In operating PWR fleets with A690TT SG tubing, observed degradation has been limited to wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects. Key SG system component materials include SA-312 Type 304/304L for Class 2 piping, SA-182 Grade F304/F304L for reducers and elbows, SA-479 Type 304/304L for piping supports, SB-166 Alloy 690 for flow restrictor mounting plate spacers, SB-637 Alloy 718 for stud bolts, nuts, and washers, SA-508 Grade 3 Class 2 for steam plenum access ports, and various stainless steel and nickel-based alloy weld filler materials, with dual certification for Type or Grade 304/304L stainless steel.

The steam generator (SG) tube fouling factor is applied to the tube inner surface, where deposits from steam plant corrosion products accumulate; the thin oxide layer on the tube outer surface in the primary coolant is not considered in fouling calculations. SG tubes are designed with a nominal wall thickness of 0.050 inch, which includes a lifetime degradation allowance of 0.010 inch to account for inservice degradation mechanisms such as general corrosion, erosion, wear, and defects from assembly. SG degradation evaluations confirm that tubing maintains acceptable integrity with up to 50 percent thickness loss under all loading conditions. Minimum wall thickness is evaluated under combined design basis pipe break and safe shutdown earthquake loads, assuming worst-case uniform thinning on the tube inner diameter. The 0.010-inch wall thinning allowance ensures the reduced wall thickness remains below the ASME Code Level D Service condition allowable stress limit, providing a safety margin. SGs are tested and inspected per Section 5.2.4, with equipment designed for accessibility to minimize time and radiation exposure during maintenance. Additional design, operational, and radiological details are referenced in Sections 3.9.1, 3.9.3, 11.1.2, and 15.0.3.8.2, and SG classification and risk categories are listed in Table 3.2-1.

The evaluation of secondary side instabilities in the steam generator (SG) design confirms that in-phase oscillation of secondary flow does not occur, and out-of-phase oscillatory flows in individual tubes cancel out, resulting in no net oscillating secondary flow. Acceptable instabilities are limited to tube mass flow fluctuations that do not induce reactor power oscillations exceeding fuel design limits and that comply with ASME Code criteria. The comprehensive vibration assessment program, conforming to Regulatory Guide 1.20, Revision 3, includes the SGs, main steam piping up to and including the main steam isolation valve (MSIV), SG supports, and SG tube supports, all located within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) fluid volume. Flow-induced vibration (FIV) screening criteria were developed, and under normal operating conditions, the low primary coolant flow rates in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design result in low flow energy available to excite tube vibration. The analysis identified fluid elastic instability (affecting SG tubes), vortex shedding (affecting SG tubes and lower SG supports), and turbulent buffeting (affecting SG tubes, SG tube supports, lower SG supports, and SG inlet flow restrictors) as mechanisms requiring evaluation. Both beam and lower frequency helical "breathing" vibration modes were considered. Testing indicates that the onset of fluid elastic instability in helical tube arrays is governed by the same parameters as straight tubes, and Connor's constants were used accordingly. Effective cross-flow velocities provide a minimum 33% margin to the onset of fluid elastic instability. Vortex shedding is precluded for downstream tubes due to turbulence disrupting coherent vortex formation. Turbulent flow analysis shows a 20.8% fatigue usage factor and a maximum fretting wear thickness ratio of 34.2% over the 60-year design life, below the 40% allowable tube wall wear depth. The comprehensive vibration analysis demonstrates that FIV is either not predicted or is acceptable for the SG pressure retaining components, including main steam piping, MSIVs, and SG tube support assemblies. SG reliability and risk significance are evaluated per Sections 17.4 and Chapter 19, respectively. The low flow rate reduces FIV wear degradation risk, and the additional tube wall and FIV turbulent buffeting wear margins support the application of existing PWR SG Program requirements to the NuScale design.

Alloy 690 tubing in the NuScale steam generator (SG) design exhibits little-to-no corrosion from either primary or secondary-side fluids, with primary and secondary chemistries equivalent to existing PWR SG designs. However, secondary-side flow conditions, including boiling, differ from traditional PWRs, leading to the assumption of uniform thinning on the tube inner diameter. The NuScale design incorporates improvements to restrict SG tube degradation, addressing historical issues in PWRs such as wastage and stress corrosion cracking. Key enhancements include thicker SG tube walls, providing a substantial degradation allowance beyond minimum design requirements, and lower reactor coolant flowrates compared to PWR recirculating SGs. The risk of foreign object introduction is comparable to existing designs, requiring no changes to SG program guidelines. Notable design differences are the helical shape of the SG tubing—whose minimum bend radius remains within the operational experience of PWRs—and a novel tube support structure that retains essential attributes of traditional plate supports. Preliminary assessments identified potential secondary-side corrosion mechanisms, such as under deposit pitting and intergranular attack, due to the once-through design and internal tube boiling. However, estimated defect growth rates are low, and the SG tube plugging criterion remains at a 40 percent through-wall defect, consistent with the existing PWR SG fleet.

The Human Factors Engineering (HFE) program, detailed in Chapter 18, fulfills regulatory requirements and guidance by incorporating human performance insights and operational experience from both nuclear and non-nuclear industries. For new plant design certification, per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(22), applicants must describe how operational experience, including that from BWR, Ice Condenser Containment, or other nuclear facility designs, is integrated into the design process. The design represents an evolution of previously operated U.S. nuclear power plants, as referenced in 10 CFR 52.41(b)(1), and NRC guidance on operational experience is addressed in the relevant FSAR sections. The development and implementation of a risk-informed, performance-based fire protection program, as permitted by 10 CFR 50.48(c), is the responsibility of COL applicants or licensees referencing the NuScale design. Regulatory Guide 1.206 serves as the FSAR layout template, with some exceptions, and its design-specific guidance (such as Position C.2) applies to the NuScale Design Certification Application (DCA).

The program addresses internal flooding mitigation procedures, lists the structures, systems, and components (SSCs) requiring flood protection in each plant area, and ensures reliable mitigation for these SSCs. The NuScale nuclear power plant (NPP) design assumes the facility is sited above the probable maximum flood height, including wind-induced wave run-up, rendering certain flood protection requirements not applicable. For NRC guidance 1.102 and SRP 3.4.2, the design is considered applicable or conforming, as the plant is above the maximum flood level, including considerations for hydrostatic effects, wave action, and dynamic loads. For safety-related instrumentation setpoints (1.105), the design partially conforms, with Chapter 15 analyses using setpoints from Chapter 7. Overall, the NuScale Certified design consistently assumes the NPP is above the most severe flood and groundwater levels, conforming or partially conforming to relevant regulatory criteria for external flooding protection.

The NuScale design utilizes a steel containment vessel and does not incorporate concrete or prestressed tendons in its design. Therefore, regulatory guides and requirements related to inservice inspection of ungrouted or grouted tendons in prestressed concrete containments, determination of prestressing forces, and qualification for cement grouting of tendons are not applicable. These regulations pertain only to LWR designs with prestressed concrete containment structures, which are not present in the NuScale design.

The NuScale nuclear facility's seismic design conforms to RG 1.61, with an alternative damping value determined for the NPM substructure. Manual initiation of protective actions also conforms, with no additional requirements. The facility includes a metal-lined concrete dike around the PSCS storage tank, eliminating the need for further mitigative analysis. Guidance to operators at the controls and to senior operators in the control room partially conforms, as site-specific guidance is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. The electrical system design meets the passive plant guidance of SECY-94-084, Section G. The facility conforms to positions on the use of generic bounding seismic hazard curves, seismic PRA, and leak-before-break concepts. Consideration of Severe Accident Mitigation Design Alternatives (SAMDAs) is included as part of the final design approval, and generic rulemaking related to design certification is noted as information only.

The provided passages summarize the applicability of various regulatory guidance documents to the NuScale nuclear power plant design. Guidance on initial test programs for water-cooled nuclear power plants (sections 3.13, 5.3, 1.68) is partially conforming, with exceptions for BWR-specific or certain PWR SSC design features not present in NuScale, and site-specific implementation activities are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. The initial startup test program to demonstrate remote shutdown capability (sections 14.2, 1.68.2) also partially conforms, except for site-specific aspects such as test performance, report preparation, and records retention, which are assigned to the COL applicant or licensee. Guidance on service limits and loading combinations for Class 1 linear-type supports (sections 3.7, 3.12, 1.124) fully conforms with no exceptions.
