Guidance on physical models for design and operation of hydraulic structures (sections 3.9, 1.125) is not applicable, as the NuScale design does not require hydraulic structures. Similarly, guidance on inspection of water-control structures (sections 4.2, 1.127) is not applicable, since such structures and their inspection programs are site-specific and not part of the NuScale design.

The provided passages summarize NuScale's conformance with various NRC Regulatory Guides (RGs) as detailed in Table 1.9-2. For RG 1.68.1, "Initial Test Program of Condensate and Feedwater Systems for Light-Water Reactors" (Rev. 2), NuScale partially conforms, as the RG is applicable except for BWR-specific aspects or PWR design features not present in the NuScale design. RG 1.70, "Standard Format and Content of Safety Analysis Reports for Nuclear Power Plants (LWR Edition)" (Rev. 3), is not applicable since RG 1.206 and NuScale Design Specific Review Standards (DSRS) are used instead. For RG 1.79, "Preoperational Testing of Emergency Core Cooling Systems for Pressurized Water Reactors" (Rev. 2), NuScale partially conforms; the intent is applicable, but the literal language refers to SSC design features not in the NuScale design. RG 1.190, "Calculational and Dosimetry Methods for Determining Pressure Vessel Neutron Fluence," is also partially conformed to, with NuScale's neutron flux and fluence calculation methods consistent with RG 1.190 guidance except as described in Technical Report TR-0116-20781. RG 1.191, "Fire Protection Program for Nuclear Power Plants During Decommissioning and Permanent Shutdown," is not applicable, as it pertains only to license holders that have permanently ceased power operations.

The NuScale containment vessel is constructed entirely of steel, without the use of concrete or grouted tendons, and therefore guidance related to concrete containments (such as SRP 1.136 and SRP 3.8.3) is not applicable. Site-specific evaluations for explosions near transportation routes are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. For anchoring components and structural supports, NuScale partially conforms to guidance that endorses Appendix B of ACI 349-2001, but instead uses the 2006 version of the ACI 349 standard. In terms of barrier design procedures for overall damage prediction, NuScale partially conforms to SRP 3.5.3, using the 2012 version of ANSI/AISC N690 rather than the referenced 1994 version with Supplement 2 (2004). The NuScale containment does not include internal structures, making related guidance not applicable, while it fully conforms to requirements for the description of other Seismic Category I structures.

The provided passages summarize regulatory guidance and positions related to nuclear power plant design and licensing. For plugging degraded PWR steam generator tubes (Bases for Plugging Degraded PWR Steam Generator Tubes, August 1976), the guidance fully conforms with no exceptions. The development of floor design response spectra for seismic design of floor-supported equipment or components (1.122) partially conforms, with site-specific guidance being the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. The content requirements for the Updated Final Safety Analysis Report per 10 CFR 50.71(e) (1.181) are not applicable, as these are site-specific reporting activities for the COL applicant or licensee. Guidance for assessing beyond-design-basis aircraft impacts (1.217) fully conforms with no exceptions. The requirement to analyze multiple steam generator tube failures (2 to 5 tubes) in passive ALWR design certification applications (II.R) fully conforms. The position on converting the design certification PRA into a plant-specific PRA (II.S) also fully conforms. Recommendations for additional ALWR alarm system requirements to improve control room annunciator reliability (II.T) fully conform. The regulatory treatment of active nonsafety systems in passive designs (III.A) and the redefinition of some passive failures as active failures for more stringent evaluation (III.B) both fully conform. The requirement for thermal-hydraulic stability of the SBWR (III.C) is not applicable, as it is a BWR-specific requirement.

The review of SRP 3.2.2, Rev 2: System Quality Group Classification indicates that the NuScale design conforms to the Quality Group Classification requirements to meet GDC 1 and 10 CFR 50.55a, with no exceptions noted. For the summary of construction codes and standards for components of water-cooled nuclear power plants by the NRC Quality Classification System (Table 3.2.21), the design partially conforms; the only exception is the reference to RG 1.85, which was withdrawn in 2004 after its guidance was incorporated into RG 1.84. Additional guidance from App.A and Table A-1 is also partially applicable, as some language refers to SSCs not included in the NuScale design. Foundations testing and inservice surveillance requirements (II.7) conform fully, as does the specification of transients for mechanical components per SRP 3.9.1, Rev 3 (II.1), with no exceptions noted.

The NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application (Revision 5, July 2020) provides a comprehensive overview of the plant’s principal design criteria, operating characteristics, and safety considerations, including engineered safety features (ESFs), emergency systems, instrumentation, controls, electrical systems, power conversion system, fuel handling and storage, plant cooling water systems, radioactive waste management, and auxiliary systems such as compressed air and non-radioactive drains. Each Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale design must develop a Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) that incorporates the NuScale FSAR by reference and provide site-specific information and implementation schedules for operational programs, including inservice inspection and testing, environmental qualification, pre-service inspection and testing, reactor vessel material surveillance, containment leakage rate testing, fire protection, process and effluent monitoring, radiation protection, staff and operator training, emergency planning, process control, security, quality assurance, maintenance, motor-operated valve testing, and the initial test program. Administrative controls for safe operation must align with Regulatory Guide 1.33, Revision 3. Additional requirements for COL applicants include developing procedures, training, and qualification programs for ultimate heat sink level instrumentation per NEI 12-02, Revision 1, and enhancing firefighting capabilities in accordance with 10 CFR 50.155(b)(2). For multi-unit sites, COL applicants must evaluate hazards from construction activities to safety-related and risk-significant structures, systems, and components of both existing and new units as required by 10 CFR 52.79(a)(31). The application also details the reactor coolant system and connecting systems, with all information subject to NuScale Power LLC’s copyright restrictions. The NRC inspected the test program using Inspection Procedures IP 35034, 35017, and 36100.

The NuScale inspection program is developed through several key steps: identifying the applicable in-service inspection (ISI) or testing requirements for the NuScale design, including the relevant code version and the overall inspections and tests required; determining the structures, systems, and components (SSC) subject to inspection and testing, as well as the specific inspection or test elements for each SSC; and assessing each inspection and test element. A comprehensive ISI and testing plan is then developed. For Class 1 systems and components, the ISI schedule and requirements adhere to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), Section XI. The documentation also references NuScale testing programs, test and inspection plans, and topical reports, and addresses requirements for nuclear power plants operated on multi-unit sites.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant certified design and the site-specific design to be provided in the combined license (COL) application. Table 1.8-1 summarizes the interfaces between the certified design and the remainder of the plant, while Section 1.2 lists the structures, systems, and components included in the certified design. Table 1.8-2 identifies COL information items, such as COL Item 1.1-1, which requires the COL applicant to specify the site-specific plant location. COL Item 11.4-1 requires the applicant to describe mobile equipment used and connected to plant systems, in accordance with ANSI/ANS 40.37, Regulatory Guide 1.143, 10 CFR 20.1406, NRC IE Bulletin 80-10, and 10 CFR 50.34a. Additionally, in compliance with 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(iv), the NuScale safety display and indication system is integrated into the control room human-system interface design, rather than utilizing a separate safety parameter display console.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report delineates the Certified Design Interface (CDI) using double brackets ([[]]), with security-related information marked by double braces {{ }} and withheld per 10 CFR 2.390(d)(1). Figures 1.2-1 and 1.2-2 illustrate the overall facility and the boundaries between the certified and site-specific designs, respectively, while Figure 1.2-3 details the basic power generation systems. Although some system components are physically located within CDI buildings, only the clouded portions—specifically the CDI cooling towers and certain circulating water systems—are included in the CDI. Table 1.8-1 summarizes the interfaces between the NuScale certified design and the remainder of the plant, listing systems, structures, and components such as turbine generator buildings, annex building, cooling towers and associated systems (e.g., cooling tower basin, circulating water pumps, cooling tower fans, chemical treatment building), security buildings, central utility building, diesel generator buildings, offsite power transmission system, main switchyard, transformer area, auxiliary AC power system, site cooling water system, circulating water system, grounding and lightning protection system, and plant exhaust stack as CDI. Other systems, including potable and sanitary water systems, resin tanks for the condensate polishing system, site drainage system, raw water system, site parameters (e.g., geographic, demographic, meteorological, hydrologic, geologic, seismologic, and geotechnical characteristics), site-specific communications, turbine generators, and the Operational Support Center, are designated as site-specific (COL). Relevant FSAR sections are referenced for each interface.

NuScale Power, LLC holds overall design responsibility for the NuScale certified design. For Combined License (COL) applicants referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification, several responsibilities are specified: development of an on-site flood mitigation program (COL Item 3.4-2); justification of fuel performance codes and methods for non-baseload operations (COL Item 4.2-1); demonstration of NuScale Power Module stability during normal and power maneuvering operations for closed-loop module control systems using reactor power as a control input (COL Item 7.0-1); description of processes for handling and receipt of critical loads, including NuScale Power Modules (COL Item 9.1-5); provision of a plan for developing, implementing, and controlling operating procedures, including preliminary schedules, target completion dates, and identification of the responsible operating organization group (COL Item 13.5-5); development of a Startup Administration Manual detailing administrative procedures and requirements for the Initial Test Program (COL Item 14.2-2); and description of a human performance monitoring program in accordance with NUREG-0711 or equivalent criteria (COL Item 18.12-1). Certain items, such as 1.4.1 and 5.2-1, are noted as not used.

Section 1.5 of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) describes the verification and confirmation tests for unique design features supporting the plant's safety analysis, with a testing program developed to provide supporting data. Section 1.6 lists topical and technical reports incorporated by reference in the NuScale Design Certification Application, detailed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2. Referenced topical reports include those on Quality Assurance Program Description (NP-TR-1010-859-NP-A, Rev. 5), Risk Significance Determination (TR-0515-13952-NP-A, Rev. 0), Safety Classification of Passive Electrical Systems (TR-0815-16497-P-A, Rev. 1), Highly Integrated Protection System Platform (TR-1015-18653-P-A, Rev. 2), Accident Source Term Methodology (TR-0915-17565-P-A, Rev. 4), AREVA Fuel Methodology Applicability (TR-0116-20825-P-A, Rev. 1), Nuclear Analysis Codes and Methods Qualification (TR-0616-48793-P-A, Rev. 1), Stability Analysis Methodology (TR-0516-49417-P-A, Rev. 1), Loss-Of-Coolant Accident Evaluation Model (TR-0516-49422-P-A, Rev. 2), Subchannel Analysis Methodology (TR-0915-17564-P-A, Rev. 2), Non-Loss-of-Coolant Accident Analysis (TR-0516-49416-P-A, Rev. 3), Critical Heat Flux Correlations (TR-0116-21012-P-A, Rev. 1), Rod Ejection Accident Methodology (TR-0716-50350-P-A, Rev. 1), and Fuel Assembly Structural Response (TR-0716-50351-P-A, Rev. 1). Technical reports referenced include Fluence Calculation Methodology (TR-0116-20781-P, Rev. 1), Advanced Sensor Technical Report (TR-0316-22048-P, Rev. 3), Physical Security Systems (TR-0416-48929-P, Rev. 1), Containment Response Analysis (TR-0516-49084-P, Rev. 3), Instrument Setpoint Methodology (TR-0616-49121-P, Rev. 3), Combustible Gas Control (TR-0716-50424-P, Rev. 1), Vibration Assessment Program (TR-0716-50439-P, Rev. 2), Fuel Storage Rack Analysis (TR-0816-49833-P, Rev. 1), Loss of Large Areas Assessment (TR-0816-50796-P, Rev. 1), Mitigation Strategies for Loss of All AC Power (TR-0816-50797-NP, Rev. 3),

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, specifies several Combined License (COL) applicant actions for those referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification. Applicants must: (1) verify the applicability of the pipe rupture hazards analysis, including dynamic and environmental effects, for high- and moderate-energy lines outside the containment vessel under the bioshield (COL Item 3.6-2); (2) perform site-specific analyses using soil and time histories to confirm the adequacy of the fluid-structure interaction correction factor (COL Item 3.7-12); (3) assess piping systems inside the reactor building to determine which portions require vibration and thermal expansion testing (COL Item 3.9-13); (4) develop and implement a Steam Generator Program for periodic monitoring to ensure steam generator tube integrity (COL Item 5.4-1); (5) specify periodic testing and inspection requirements for the Turbine Building heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) system (COL Item 9.4-4); and (6) describe radiation shielding design measures to compensate for major shield wall penetrations, ensuring protection of equipment and minimizing exposures to workers and the public, in accordance with FSAR Sections 12.1.2.3.2, 12.3.1.2.3, and 12.3.2.2 (COL Item 12.3-8).

The conformance assessment regarding operational experience is detailed in Table 1.9-6, "Evaluation of Operating Experience (Generic Letters and Bulletins)." As part of NuScale’s QA supplier evaluation program, all 10 CFR 21 notifications are reviewed for their impact on the NuScale design before approving any Nuclear Safety Related supplier for safety-related items or services. Additionally, evaluation of 10 CFR 21 notifications is conducted during periodic annual reviews to monitor supplier performance. To date, there have been no 10 CFR 21 notifications affecting nuclear safety-related work performed by NuScale-approved safety-related suppliers for the NuScale Design, confirming that all applicable 10 CFR 21 notifications have been evaluated.

The applicant will confirm that site-specific ratios of peak ground acceleration (A), ground velocity (V), and ground displacement (D)—specifically V/A and AD/V²—are consistent with characteristic values for the magnitude and distance of the controlling seismic events that define the site-specific uniform hazard response spectra. The seismic design parameters, including design ground motion and percentage of critical damping values, conform to DSRS 3.7.1, Rev. 0. Seismic system analysis, covering the development of in-structure response spectra and the use of three components of design ground motion, conforms to DSRS 3.7.2, Rev. 0. Seismic subsystem analysis, including the analysis procedure for damping and the use of three components of design ground motion, conforms to DSRS 3.7.3, Rev. 0. No exceptions or deviations are noted in any of these areas.

The seismic design classification for nuclear power plants, as described in the referenced passages, partially conforms to regulatory requirements. Structures, systems, and components (SSCs) identified in Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.a through C.1.h are designated as Seismic Category I, in accordance with Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.29. However, SSCs that meet the criteria of Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i are classified as Seismic Category II instead of Seismic Category I. The specific seismic classification for each component, as determined by RG 1.29, is detailed throughout the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR). This approach is consistent with the acceptance criteria of SRP 3.2.1, Rev 2, which implements the requirements of GDC 2, 10 CFR 100 Appendix A, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix S, except for the noted deviation regarding SSCs under C.1.i.

The Certified Seismic Design Response Spectra (CSDRS) for the nuclear power plant was not developed using Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.60, but it is demonstrated to envelope the RG 1.60 spectra anchored to 0.1g. The seismic system analysis, as described in DSRS 3.7.2, Rev 0, conforms to requirements for seismic analysis methods, natural frequencies and responses, effects of parameter variations on floor response spectra, use of equivalent vertical static factors, and methods used to account for torsional effects. However, NuScale does not perform both time history analysis and response spectrum analysis for structural analysis, making the comparison of responses not applicable.

The provided passages summarize compliance with various sections of the Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) related to seismic system analysis for nuclear facilities. The analysis covers procedures for analytical modeling (DSRS 3.7.2, II.3), soil-structure interaction (DSRS 3.7.2, II.4), combination of modal responses (DSRS 3.7.2, II.7), and the interaction of non-seismic Category I structures with seismic Category I structures, systems, and components (SSCs) (DSRS 3.7.2, II.8), all of which are noted as conforming with no exceptions. Additionally, the determination of overturning moments, sliding forces, structure-to-soil pressures, and frictional forces for seismic Category I structures (DSRS 3.7.2, II.14) is also confirmed as conforming. The passages also note that Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.93, concerning the availability of electric power sources, is not applicable as it is not identified in DSRS Section 8.1. No deviations or non-conformances are reported in any of the referenced sections.

The provided passages address seismic design and ground motion considerations for nuclear facilities. Site-specific earthquake ground motion response spectra development is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee, as outlined in Section 3.8, 3.9, 3.12, and 1.208, and is not directly applicable to the DCA. Section 3.7 and DC/COL-ISG-1 confirm that ground motion definitions and staff guidance on Safe-Shutdown Earthquake (SSE), Operating-Basis Earthquake (OBE), and seismic instrumentation are consistent and conformant. The Certified Seismic Design Response Spectrum (CSDRS), including its high-frequency (CSDRS-HF) component, serves as the SSE for the DCA. The OBE is defined as one-third of the CSDRS, eliminating the need for separate OBE analysis in the DCA. However, COL applicants must ensure the Ground Motion Response Spectrum (GMRS) is enveloped and implement a seismic monitoring program with appropriate responses if OBE exceedance occurs. The design approach decouples the OBE from the safety system design evaluation by setting it at one-third of the SSE.

The provided passages summarize the seismic design and structural analysis considerations for Seismic Category I structures in accordance with DSRS (Design-Specific Review Standard) sections 3.7.1, 3.8.4, and 3.8.5, all Revision 0. The design parameters, supporting media, and review considerations for DC (Design Certification) and COL (Combined License) applications conform to the specified DSRS requirements, with no exceptions noted. The design and analysis procedures, structural acceptance criteria, materials, quality control, special construction techniques, and testing and inservice surveillance requirements for other Seismic Category I structures also conform to DSRS 3.8.4, with no deviations. Masonry walls are not used in the NuScale design, making DSRS 3.8.4, Section II.8 not applicable. The description of the foundation for these structures conforms to DSRS 3.8.5, with no exceptions.

The seismic subsystem analysis for the NuScale design conforms to the requirements of DSRS 3.7.3, Rev. 0, across multiple technical areas. These include seismic analysis methods, determination of the number of earthquake cycles, procedures for analytical modeling, and the basis for selection of frequencies. The analysis also addresses the combination of modal responses, interaction of non-seismic Category I subsystems with seismic Category I SSCs, multiply-supported equipment and components with distinct inputs, and the use of equivalent vertical static factors. Additional considerations include torsional effects of eccentric masses and the seismic analysis of Category I buried piping, conduits, and tunnels. The methods for seismic analysis of above-ground tanks are also addressed and conform to the standard. However, methods for seismic analysis of Category I concrete dams are not applicable, as the NuScale design does not utilize dams.

SRP 3.7.4, Rev 2 (Seismic Instrumentation) section II.2 states that comparison with RG 1.166 is not applicable and refers to Table 1.9-2 for RG 1.166. For SRP 3.10, Rev 3 (Seismic and Dynamic Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical Equipment), section II.1 (Qualification of Electrical Equipment and Associated Supports) is in conformance with requirements, with no exceptions noted. Section II.2 (Testing of Instrumentation Described in RG 1.97) partially conforms, with reference to RG 1.97 in Table 1.9-2. Section II.3 (Experience-Based Qualification) is not applicable, as experience-based seismic qualification is not used. Section II.4 (Records) conforms, with the NuScale design requiring a records program and including a COL item to maintain it. Section II.6 (Documentation of Qualification Program) also conforms, with no exceptions noted.

The passages confirm that the design conforms to DSRS 3.8.4, Rev. 0 requirements for Other Seismic Category I Structures, specifically regarding applicable codes, standards, specifications, loads and load combinations, and concrete structures, with no exceptions noted. For foundations, compliance with DSRS 3.8.5, Rev. 0 is affirmed for applicable codes, standards, specifications, loads and load combinations, design and analysis procedures, structural acceptance criteria, and materials, quality control, and special construction techniques, again with no exceptions. Additionally, DC/COL-ISG-7 requirements for assessing normal and extreme winter precipitation loads on the roofs of Seismic Category I Structures are met, with Section 3.4 specifying the parameters for these events, which are used in the structural analysis in Section 3.8.

Programs must comply with the latest edition and addenda of the ASME OM Code incorporated by reference in 10 CFR 50.55a, within the timeframe specified before initial fuel load, or use optional ASME Code Cases listed in Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.192. The 2013 Edition of the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), as endorsed by ASME and referenced in the 2015 rulemaking to amend 10 CFR 50.55a (80 FR 56820), satisfies the requirements of 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(1) and RGs 1.84 (Revision 36) and 1.147 (Revision 17). Operational and maintenance inservice testing codes for NPMs are based on ASME OM-2012, "Operation and Maintenance of Nuclear Power Plants," as permitted by 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(1). ASME OM-2012, as endorsed and referenced in the 2015 rulemaking, meets the requirements of 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(1) and RG 1.192, Revision 1. Use of ASME OM Code editions and addenda issued after design certification is allowed or required per the design certification provisions. If a later ASME OM Code edition or addenda is used, it must be approved for incorporation by reference under 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(1) or authorized by the NRC under 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(3). ASME OM code cases for preservice and inservice testing are identified by the plant owner and must be those listed in the applicable ASME OM Code Edition specified in 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(1)(iv) or Tables 1 and 2 of RG 1.192, in accordance with 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(3)(iii) and subject to 10 CFR 50.55a(b).

The piping systems subject to testing include ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section III, Class 1, 2, and 3 piping, other high-energy piping within Seismic Category I structures or those whose failure would compromise any Seismic Category I plant feature, and Seismic Category I portions of moderate-energy piping outside containment. Breaches of this piping system outside containment are not considered credible due to the welded design, system design pressure equivalent to the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), compliance with Class 2 requirements per ASME BPVC Section III, and adherence to NRC Branch Technical Position 3-4, Revision 2. Reduced inspection requirements for small primary system pipe welds (less than four NPS) are not applied to welds between containment and Containment Isolation Valves (CIVs), as a break here would cause a Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) leak outside containment. Consequently, ASME Class 1 welds between containment and CIVs undergo volumetric and surface examinations each interval per ASME BPVC Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2500. Additionally, RPV flanges feature dual O-rings with a leak port tube between them for leakage testing.

Typical reactor pressure vessel load combinations for Class 1 vessels are more applicable to the containment vessel than those specified in RG 1.57, due to the higher fabrication, inspection, and testing standards required by ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section III, Subsection NB for Class 1 vessels. Overpressure protection equipment is tested and inspected per industry standards, including ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, ASME OM Code Mandatory Appendix I, and 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(x) (Three Mile Island action plan Item II.D.1). Preservice and inservice inspection and testing of ASME BPVC Class 1 pressure-retaining components within the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB)—such as vessels, pumps, valves, bolting, and supports—are performed in accordance with ASME BPVC Section XI and its mandatory appendices, as required by 10 CFR 50.55a(g). Inservice examinations and system pressure tests during each 120-month inspection interval must comply with the latest Code edition and addenda referenced in 10 CFR 50.55a(b), subject to its conditions. The inspection program specifies the Code edition and addenda for each inspection interval, with system pressure and functional tests for active components defined in ASME BPVC Section XI, Articles IWA-5000 and IWB-5000, to verify pressure boundary integrity. ECCS valve actuators and actuator pressure sensing lines, as part of the ASME BPVC Section III Class 1 boundary, are subject to Section XI testing. The inspection and testing program addresses unique requirements for each Nuclear Power Module (NPM) to ensure plant safety throughout its operating life, including core support components, internal structures, and steam generator supports. Due to the containment’s high pressure and safety function, it receives enhanced inspection requirements exceeding ASME BPVC Section XI, Subsection IWE Class MC standards. The containment, classified as Class MC per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NE, is designed, fabricated, and stamped as a Class 1 pressure vessel per Section III, Subsection NB, with overpressure protection per Article NE-7000. Preservice examinations and documentation follow ASME BPVC Section III, Paragraph NB-5281, with volumetric and surface exams per NB-5282, and exemptions as listed in NB-5283. Preservice examinations for ASME Code Class 1 pressure boundary and attachment welds conform to Section III, Paragraph NB-5280, and Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2200. Additional details on containment compliance, preservice inspection, inservice inspection (ISI), and testing are provided in Sections 3.8.2 and 6.2.

The use of load combinations for Class 1 vessels, as required by ASME Code Section III, Subsection NB, is considered more appropriate for containment vessels than the load combinations specified in RG 1.57, due to the higher standards of fabrication, inspection, and testing. Compliance with applicable ASME BPVC Section III code cases for design, fabrication, and construction is determined by those listed in the ASME BPVC Edition specified in 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(1)(i) or Tables 1 and 2 of RG 1.84, pursuant to 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(3)(i), and subject to 10 CFR 50.55a(b). For preservice inspection and inservice inspection (ISI), the applicable ASME BPVC Section XI code cases are identified by the plant owner and determined by those listed in the ASME BPVC Edition specified in 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(1)(ii) or Tables 1 and 2 of RG 1.147, pursuant to 10 CFR 50.55a(a)(3)(ii), and subject to 10 CFR 50.55a(b). Code cases listed in Table 2 of RG 1.192 are also acceptable if they meet the conditions established in the RG. Table 5.2-1, "ASME Code Cases," consolidates specific code cases used in the NPM RCPB design that are not addressed in the ASME BPVC 2013 Edition. Conditionally acceptable ASME code cases in Table 5.2-1 are subject to the conditions in Table 2 of RG 1.84, and other acceptable or conditionally acceptable code cases in RGs 1.84, 1.147, and 1.192, effective at the time of Design Certification or in subsequent RG revisions, may be used for RCPB components. The initial ISI Program incorporates the latest ASME BPVC edition and addenda approved in 10 CFR 50.55a(b) prior to initial fuel load, subject to the conditions in 10 CFR 50.55a(b).

The RPV bolting material is not subject to the concerns addressed by RG 1.65 Positions 1(a)(i) and 2(b), and thus these positions do not apply. Section 3.9 outlines the design transients, loading combinations, stress limits, and evaluation methods used in the design and fatigue analyses of Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components, confirming that RCPB integrity is maintained. RCPB components are designed, constructed, and maintained to quality standards ensuring overpressure protection of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS). The RPV and pressure-retaining components associated with the RCPB are designed, fabricated, and tested as Class 1 to the highest quality standards per the Quality Assurance Program in Chapter 17, in compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 1 and 30. Section 5.3 provides details of materials conformance for the RPV, confirming compliance with GDC 1, 4, 14, 30, and 31, ensuring compatibility with environmental conditions, non-brittle behavior, and minimized probability of rapidly propagating fracture or gross rupture. Material specifications for Class 1 components and supports, including the RPV and steam generators (SGs), are listed in Table 5.2-4, which also covers RPV attachments and appurtenances. Cleaning of RCPB components meets ASME NQA-1 requirements, with internal surfaces achieving "Class B" and external surfaces "Class C" cleanness per Subpart 2.1. Handling, storage, and shipping of RCPB components comply with ASME NQA-1-2008, Part I, Requirement 13, and ASME NQA-1a-2009, Part II, Subpart 2.2.

Systems other than the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) that connect to the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) require isolation but are not classified as part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). Table 3.2-1 lists the RCPB pressure-retaining components and their Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) quality group classifications. For the NuScale design certification, the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), 2013 Edition with no Addenda, is the code of record. RCPB components subject to inspection as Class 1 are classified as Quality Group A, as detailed in Section 3.2.2.1, and comply with ASME BPVC requirements per Section 5.2.1. Figure 6.6-1 delineates the ASME BPVC Section III, Class 1 boundary for RCS piping and the Steam Generator System (SGS). The feedwater (FW) plenum, main steam (MS) plenum, SGS subcomponents, and steam generator tubes that form parts of the RCPB are also classified as Quality Group A and are designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 1 in accordance with BPVC Section III. Steam and feedwater piping between the Containment Nuclear Transport System (CNTS) and Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), including portions supporting the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), are designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 2 per BPVC Section III. The SGS and connected components up to the CNTS are designated as Seismic Category I. Materials used in the SGS that form the RCPB, including weld materials, are classified as Quality Group A and meet the fabrication, construction, and testing requirements of BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, as well as the applicable conditions of 10 CFR 50.55a(b).

The ASME BPVC Section XI inservice inspection (ISI) requirements for Quality Group A systems and components (ASME BPVC Class 1) are detailed in Section 5.2.4, which covers the RCPB but excludes Quality Group B or C components. ISI requirements for Quality Groups B and C (ASME BPVC Class 2 and 3) are addressed in Section 6.6, which discusses Class 2 and 3 component examinations. Section 5.2.4.1 outlines the inspection and testing process for ASME BPVC Class 1 components, excluding steam generator (SG) tubes, for which ISI requirements are described in Section 5.4.1. The steam generator system (SGS) components are designed to allow ISI per ASME BPVC Section XI, including preservice inspections per ASME Section III. The integral steam plenum, integral steam plenum caps, and feed plenum access ports are classified as Quality Group A and are designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 1 in accordance with the BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a(b). Steam plenum access ports and associated covers, as well as feed plenum access port covers, are classified as Quality Group B but are also designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 1 per ASME BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a(b). All steam and feed plenum components meet the fabrication, construction, and testing requirements of ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, with materials and specifications for MS and FW piping, reducers, elbows, steam and feed plenum components, and fasteners provided in Table 5.4-3.

A position verification test is conducted for each valve every 24 months during refueling, following ASME Code OM-2012, Division 1, Subarticle ISTC-3700, and a set pressure test is performed every 5 years per ASME Code OM-2012, Division 1, Mandatory Appendix I, Paragraphs I-1320 and I-7310. Inspection elements and methods for ISI are detailed in Tables 5.2-6 through 5.2-9, covering the reactor pressure vessel, vessel internals, ASME Class 1 piping, and Class 1 supports. Before NPM startup after each refueling outage, a system leakage test and a VT-2 examination of the RPV Class 1 pressure retaining boundary are performed in accordance with ASME BPVC Section XI, Table IWB-2500-1 (B-P), and Articles IWA-5000 and IWB-5000. For valves located in the reactor pool during startup, the required VT-2 examination cannot be performed during the system pressure test; instead, a seal verification test is conducted prior to startup. This test uses the test port to pressurize the space between the inner and outer O-ring to RCS design pressure with gas while the NPM is in the dry dock, meeting Section XI, Table IWB-2500-1 (B-P) requirements. Pressure retaining bolting two inches or less in diameter is inspected per ASME BPVC Section XI, Table IWB-2500-1 (B-G-2), requiring a VT-1 visual examination each interval if removed, with augmentation to VT-1 when bolts are removed.

Measures are established per Criterion XIII of 10 CFR 50, Appendix B to control on-site cleaning of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and pressure-retaining components associated with the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) during construction. The RPV ferritic pressure-retaining and integrally attached materials are tested and meet fracture toughness acceptance criteria per Appendix G to 10 CFR 50. RPV surfaces are designed for examination and conform to ASME BPVC Sections III and XI. Material specifications for the RPV and appurtenances are listed in Table 5.2-4, with materials selected and fabricated to maintain RCPB integrity for the plant design lifetime. The chemical composition of RPV beltline materials is shown in Table 5.3-2. The RPV is fabricated per ASME BPVC Section III, NB-4000, except for steam generator (SG) tube supports (ASME BPVC Section III, NG-4000), RPV supports, and control rod drive mechanism (CRDM) seismic support structure (ASME BPVC Section III, NF-4000). The RPV is designed against non-ductile fracture in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III and XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50, Appendix G. Pressure-temperature limits are developed per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, and RG 1.99, Revision 2. Specimens are periodically removed and tested to monitor fracture toughness changes per ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix H, using material from the actual production forging and weldments. The limiting base metal is expected to be the lower RPV shell forging, but unit-specific limiting base metal is determined by actual forging chemistry and initial RTNDT, and may be the lower RPV shell or bottom head forging. The predicted limiting weld metal is the lower RPV shell to bottom head circumferential weld, and the limiting heat affected zone is adjacent to the limiting base metal. Archive materials fill at least two capsules per ASTM E185-82. RPV design ensures compatibility with inspection requirements. Packaging, shipment, handling, and storage of the RPV comply with ASME BPVC Section III, 2013 edition, subsection NCA-4134.13, and meet Level C requirements per ASME NQA-1-2008/1a-2009 Addenda, subpart 2.2.

For ASME Class 1 piping welds requiring ultrasonic preservice examination, the welds comply with the surface finish and marking requirements of ASME BPV Code Section III, Subparagraph NB-4424.2, with a specified surface finish of 125 in Ra or better and surface flatness less than 0.03125 inches for at least twice the part thickness from the weld centerline. For Class 1 pipe welds with a nominal pipe size (NPS) of 4 or greater, both surface and volumetric examinations are performed, while only surface examinations are required for welds less than 4 NPS, as per Table IWB-2500-1 Category B-J. Class 1 boundary valves (CIVs) are located outside the NPM. Stud welds to cladding require a preservice liquid penetrant examination per ASME BPVC NB-5272 and comply with NB-4435 for welding nonstructural attachments; there are no inservice examination requirements for lock plate stud welds or lock plates. Socket welds are not used on lines ≥ 3/4 inch NPS, and any socket welds on piping < 3/4 inch NPS conform to 10 CFR 50.55a(b)(1)(ii) and ASME B16.11.

Leakage testing is conducted after each installation of O-rings to ensure proper sealing, and the RPV and containment main flange bolts are inspected per ASME BPVC Section XI, Table IWB-2500-1 (B-G-1). Visual (VT-3) and volumetric examinations are performed on various RPV components, including the flow diverter to RPV lower head, interior surfaces, attachment welds (B-N-1), external welds, support plate to gussets, support plate to upper RPV SG shell (F-A), support gussets to upper RPV SG shell, and lateral support lug (B-K). RPV nozzle to shell and head welds, reactor recirc valve flange, feedwater nozzles, and RCS discharge (B-D) undergo volumetric inspection. All RVV and RRV flange threaded fasteners are subject to augmented volumetric examination per B-G-2, following B-G-1 guidance, with no sampling permitted; all are inspected during the interval, and RPV bolting two inches or less in diameter is examined by VT-1 if removed. Assembled RPV leakage is continuously monitored in the containment vessel (CNV) as a VT-2 examination per Section XI IWA-5241(c). RPV pressure-retaining and integrally attached materials are examined per ASME BPVC Section III, using methods from Section V as modified by Section III and additional requirements. Threaded insert fabrication inspections follow ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB-2580, using the outer diameter for sizing, and lock plates are used to hold the RPV flange nut during stud removal or installation. Inservice surveillance and threaded fastener requirements are detailed in Sections 5.2.4, 5.3.1, and 3.13.

The examination requirements for various nuclear plant nozzles and welds are specified primarily by ASME BPVC Section XI, Table IWB-2500-1, with references to specific paragraphs such as IWB-2500-7(a)-(d). Main steam nozzles (B-D) require volumetric examination per IWB-2500-7(d), while RCS injection and PZR spray supply lines (B-D) are not applicable for inside corner welds. Certain nozzles, including PZR heater access ports and I&C channels (B-D), are exempted from inspection as they have similar functionality and consequences as traditional pressurizer nozzles, per Note 1 of Table IWB-2500-1. Feedwater and main steam plenum access ports (B-D) require volumetric examination per IWB-2500-7(b) and (c), respectively, with all welds examined except inside corners. PZR pressure taps, T-Hot thermowells, PZR liquid temp thermowells, and ultrasonic testing sensor nozzles (B-D) require volumetric examination per IWB-2500-7(a) or (b), with shell side exams only and no inside corner welds. Nozzle-to-safe end dissimilar metal welds, including feedwater and main steam nozzle safe ends (B-F), require both surface and volumetric exams, while RCS injection, discharge, and PZR spray supply safe ends (B-F) have varying requirements for surface or volumetric exams depending on location (inner or outer). Open-ended pipes and certain safe ends have no inspection requirement. CRDM nozzle safe ends (B-O) require volumetric or surface exams. Threaded fastener inserts and their welds, including RSV, RVV, and RRV flanges and various access ports, have no inspection requirement except for VT-1 visual testing. Interior surface exams are conducted to check for gaps between reflector blocks. CRDM nozzles (B-J) and various RCS and PZR piping-to-nozzle safe end welds require surface or volumetric exams, while RRV and RVV trip-reset actuator piping and fitting welds (B-J) are exempted from inspection due to small size, as per IWB-1220.

The steam generator (SG) classification designations and their applicability are detailed in Table 3.2-1, with Chapter 3 providing comprehensive information on the design basis and qualification of structures, systems, and components according to these classifications. Figure 6.6-1 illustrates the BPVC Section III, Class 1 and 2 boundaries for the SG system (SGS). Pressure boundary materials for the SGs and associated components are selected and fabricated per BPVC Section III and Section II requirements, as outlined in Section 5.2.3, with specific materials listed in Table 5.2-4. All fabrication materials conform to BPVC Section II specifications and Section III, Article NB-2000 requirements. SG tubes are made from SB-163 Alloy 690 (UNS N06690), and all SGS materials forming the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) comply with BPVC Section II and Section III, Article NB-2000. Feedwater (FW) and main steam (MS) piping from the CNTS to the SGs, including the thermal relief valve, are classified as Quality Group B and designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 2 per BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a(d), with all weld materials meeting BPVC requirements. Welding of RCPB portions of the SGS follows ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-4300 and Section IX, while secondary side welding follows Subarticle NC-4300 and Section IX. SG weld filler metals, listed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.4-3, comply with BPVC Section II, Part C. SG supports and tube supports are designed per BPVC Section III, Subsection NG, except they are not Code stamped as “Core Supports” per NG-8000 and do not require an N-Certificate holder, though a 10 CFR 50 Appendix B quality program is mandatory. SG piping structural supports, including weld materials, meet BPVC Section III, Subsection NF requirements, with materials specified in Table 5.4-3. Additional details on material compatibility, fabrication, process controls, and welding controls for ASME Class 2 components are in Section 6.6, and threaded fasteners are described in Section 3.13. The SG program monitors SG performance and condition to ensure intended function is maintained.

Regulatory Guides 1.166 and 1.167 provide acceptable guidance for developing procedures related to pre-earthquake planning, immediate post-earthquake operator actions, and the restart of nuclear power plants following seismic events. Regulatory Guide 1.132 addresses site investigations for nuclear power plant foundations, but site-specific guidance is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. Guidance in RG 1.137, concerning fuel-oil systems for standby diesel generators, is not applicable as the design does not include safety-related emergency diesel generators. RG 1.138, which covers laboratory investigations of soils and rocks, and RG 1.198, addressing seismic soil liquefaction assessment, both pertain to site-specific activities and are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee referencing the certified design. NSIR/DPR-ISG-01 provides guidance on emergency planning programmatic and design aspects, which are also site-specific and the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the NuScale design. SECY-14-088 discusses options for addressing lessons learned from the NRC’s Force-On-Force Inspection Program, with requirements determined on a site-specific basis.

The facility is designed to meet the requirements of 10 CFR 50.47(b)(8) and Section IV.E, "Emergency Facilities and Equipment," of Appendix E to 10 CFR Part 50. The main control room and remote shutdown station maintain alarm system reliability in accordance with item II.T of SECY-93-087. The NuScale design includes provisions for design-specific emergency facilities and equipment consistent with 10 CFR 50.47(a)(8), though communication systems and equipment supporting emergency response are the responsibility of the COL applicant. The design documents comply with PDC-19, ensuring communication equipment is available in the control room to support all normal and emergency operations, including intra-plant and off-site communications, even in the event of a single subsystem failure or loss of normal power. Control room communications are addressed to maintain connectivity with site and offsite entities during both normal and accident conditions. The design conforms to GDC 3 and GDC 4, but certain acceptance criteria related to 10 CFR 73.45 are not applicable, as they pertain only to specific licensees. Compliance with 10 CFR 73.55(e)(9)(vi)(B) is achieved, with site-specific physical security communication systems being the responsibility of the COL applicant. The design partially conforms to 10 CFR 73.55(j), focusing on requirements for physical protection against radiological sabotage and necessary communication requirements.

The passages provide examples where regulatory requirements or guidance are either only partially applicable, refer to different LWR designs or SSCs not present in the current design, or are specific to BWRs, large PWRs, or COL applicants. Some requirements are applicable only to SSCs not included in the design. For Quality Group Classifications and Standards (RG 1.26), the FSAR describes the applicable quality group classification for each component. For SRP 19.3, Rev 0, concerning the regulatory treatment of non-safety systems in passive advanced LWRs, the guidance is noted as conforming, with no additional comments.

The first operational NuScale Power Module (NPM) is classified as a prototype per RG 1.20, making prototype reactor provisions applicable during preoperational and initial startup vibration assessment. For RG 1.54 on protective coatings, the design partially conforms, excluding operational aspects like maintenance, which are the COL applicant or licensee's responsibility. Regarding RG 1.63, the design partially conforms by applying IEEE-317-1983 for electric penetration assemblies and using IEEE 741-1997 for external circuit protection instead of IEEE 741-1986. Full conformance is noted for RG 1.84 on ASME Section III code case acceptability, with no exceptions. RG 1.86 on license termination is not applicable, as it governs license termination processes. For RG 1.89, partial conformance is claimed, excluding BWR-specific aspects, non-relevant SSCs, and source term references now superseded by RG 1.183. RG 1.96 on main steam isolation valve leakage control is not applicable, being BWR-specific. RG 1.151 on instrument sensing lines is partially conformed to, with installation criteria delegated to the COL applicant or licensee. The design partially conforms to RG 1.155 on station blackout as it pertains to passive plant designs, and fully conforms to RG 1.156 on qualification of connection assemblies. RG 1.167, covering post-seismic event restart, and RG 1.174, on risk-informed licensing basis changes, are not applicable as they pertain to licensee responsibilities.

The provided passages summarize the conformance status of various regulatory guidance and requirements for nuclear power plant systems. Periodic Testing of Protection System Actuation Functions (11.5, 1.22) and Bypassed and Inoperable Status Indication for Nuclear Power Plant Safety Systems (3.6, 5.2, 6.2, 9.3, 11.5, 14.2, 14.3, 1.47) both conform with no exceptions. Control of Preheat Temperature for Welding of Low-Alloy Steel (1.50) also conforms. Meteorological Monitoring Programs (1.23) are not applicable, as they are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. The Initial Startup Test Program to Demonstrate Remote Shutdown Capability (14.2, 1.68.2) and Preoperational Testing of Instrument and Control Air Systems (14.2, 1.68.3) partially conform, with site-specific aspects such as test performance, report preparation, and records retention left to the COL applicant or licensee. Qualification Tests for Safety-Related Actuators (1.73) partially conform, except for portions relevant to high temperature gas-cooled reactor designs. Criteria for Independence of Electrical Safety Systems (1.75), Guidelines for Environmental Qualification of Safety-Related Computer-Based Instrumentation and Control Systems (1.209), and the requirement to ensure the QA list includes all SSC important to safety (50.34(f)(3)(ii)) all conform with no exceptions. Testing of Safety-Related Logic Circuits per Generic Letter 96-01 (8.1.4, 8.3.2) also conforms.

RG 1.183 is specified for use instead of RG 1.25 and RG 1.195 for new reactors and existing reactors authorized to use the alternative source term under 10 CFR 50.67. The general approach and intent of RG 1.77 remain applicable and are used in Section 15.4.8 analyses. Portions of RG 1.97 related to 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xix) are addressed in Section 19.2.3.3.8. Compliance with SRP 5.2.1.1, Rev 3, regarding 10 CFR 50.55a, is achieved through the use of RG 1.26 to meet GDC 1 and 10 CFR 50.55a, as noted in Table 1.9-2. For SRP 5.2.1.2, Rev 3, RG 1.84 is used to meet GDC 1 and 10 CFR 50.55a (conforms), while RG 1.147 and RG 1.192 are partially conforming, with further details in Table 1.9-2. The design and construction of the Main Steam System (MSS) follow RG 1.29, Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i, and C.2. Code cases listed in Table 2 of RG 1.84 meet the established conditions, and Sections 5.2.4 and 6.6 summarize preservice and inservice inspection (ISI) examinations and procedures.

The provided passages summarize the applicability and conformance of various regulatory guides and bulletins to the electrical power systems of a nuclear power plant. RG 1.31 (Control of Ferrite Content in Stainless Steel Weld Metal) fully conforms, while RG 1.32 (Criteria for Power Systems for Nuclear Power Plants) is only partially applicable, as it does not pertain to offsite and onsite AC power systems. RG 1.81 (Shared Emergency and Shutdown Electric Systems for Multi-Unit Nuclear Power Plants) is not relevant to the AC power systems, though the EDSS conforms to some portions as described in Section 8.3. SRP BTP 8-9, Rev. 0 (Open Phase Conditions in Electric Power System) is partially conformed to for electrical system design (B.1) and passive plant design criteria (B.3), but not applicable to active plant design criteria (B.2). Generic Letter 2007-01 (Inaccessible or Underground Power Cable Failures) is partially conformed to, as the plant’s electrical systems do not include power cables serving risk-significant or safety-related equipment. Bulletin 2012-01 (Design Vulnerability in Electric Power System) is also partially conformed to, with compliance demonstrated through adherence to SRP BTP 8-9 as detailed in Section 8.2.3.

The passages summarize compliance with various regulatory guides and codes related to nuclear reactor safety. For radiation embrittlement of reactor vessel materials (RG 1.99) and fuel densification analysis models (RG 1.126), the requirements are met with no exceptions. For inservice inspection code case acceptability (RG 1.147), partial conformity is noted, with the responsibility for ASME Section XI, Division 1 inspections assigned to the COL applicant or licensee. If thermal annealing of reactor pressure vessels becomes necessary (RG 1.162), compliance with 10 CFR 50.66 and RG 1.162 is also the applicant or licensee's responsibility. ASME code cases not approved for use (RG 1.193) are not applied unless specifically authorized by the NRC under 10 CFR 50.55a(z). For atmospheric dispersion factors used in EAB and LPZ assessments, RG 1.194 is referenced, and conformity is indicated for control room radiological habitability assessments, with no exceptions noted.

The ability of the COL applicant to meet NRC guidance, as discussed in RG 1.114 Section B.1, is supported by the control room design and layout, including the designated surveillance area described in Position C.1.3. Simulator capability that accurately models the control room and simulates small-break LOCAs (per 50.34(f)(2)(i), I.A.4.2) is not applicable to the current design, as provisions for simulator capability are the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the certified design. The requirement to provide a control room design reflecting state-of-the-art human factor principles for Commission review prior to fabrication or revision of control room panels and layouts (50.34(f)(2)(iii), I.D.1) is met. The provision of an onsite Technical Support Center and onsite Operational Support Center (50.34(f)(2)(xxv), III.A.1.2) only partially conforms. For passive plant control room designs, sufficient man-in-the-loop testing and evaluation, including a fully functional integrated control room prototype, are required to demonstrate proper integration of functions and tasks into the man/machine interface, and this requirement is met.

The NuScale design addresses various regulatory guides as follows: Site-specific guidance is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. For the installation design and installation of vented lead-acid storage batteries (RG 1.128), the design partially conforms since the EDSS uses VRLA batteries, applying IEEE Std 1187-2013. For maintenance, testing, and replacement of vented lead-acid storage batteries (RG 1.129), partial conformance is achieved by applying IEEE Std 1188-2005 with the 2014 amendment, as the EDSS uses VRLA batteries. Best-estimate calculations of Emergency Core Cooling System performance (RG 1.157) are not applicable, as such calculations are not used. For qualification of safety-related lead storage batteries (RG 1.158), the design conforms, noting that the DC system batteries are non-Class 1E. Qualification of safety-related battery chargers and inverters (RG 1.210) is not applicable because the NuScale design does not use safety-related battery chargers or inverters, and EDSS battery chargers are not located in a harsh environment. Qualification of safety-related cables and field splices (RG 1.211) is met with full conformance. For sizing of large lead-acid storage batteries (RG 1.212), the design partially conforms by following VRLA sizing guidance in IEEE Std.

The responsibility for the design, installation, and testing practices addressing the effects of EMI/RFI and power surges on safety-related instrumentation and control (I&C) systems, as well as the design and implementation of communication systems supporting emergency response facilities, lies with the COL applicant or licensee. Several acceptance criteria in DSRS 9.5.2, Rev 0 (Communication Systems) are designated as the COL applicant’s responsibility, including emergency facilities and equipment, and the preparation of COL-specific ITAAC. Aspects of the acceptance criteria related to the physical design of the power reactor and communication systems within the certified design scope are applicable to the Design Certification Application (DCA). Site-specific compliance with GDC 1 and elements related to the facility physical security plan also fall under the COL applicant’s purview. For lighting systems (SRP 9.5.3, Rev 3), both the integrated design and emergency lighting systems conform to requirements, with no additional notes.

The implementation of inservice testing per the ASME OM Code is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee, with Code Case acceptability not specifically addressed. The acceptance criteria for Technical Specifications in this DSRS section are applicable but largely reference existing LWR or plant-specific technical specifications to be developed by the COL applicant, indicating only partial conformity. For DC/COL-ISG-6, the acceptance criteria for 10 CFR 20.1406 compliance partially conform, as the guidance incorrectly references Attachment C instead of Attachment B. Compliance with GL 2007-01 is limited to power cables within the scope of 10 CFR 50.65, with conformance achieved by the COL holder following RG 1.218 guidance as detailed in Chapter 8. Additionally, the licensee is required to confirm continued compliance with 10 CFR 50.54(hh)(2), which is addressed in Section 20.2.

The passages indicate that for SRP 3.7.4, Rev 2 (Seismic Instrumentation) section II.3, and SRP 12.1, Rev 4 (Assuring That Occupational Radiation Exposures Are As Low As Is Reasonably Achievable) section II.4, the requirements are not applicable, with the comparison to 10 CFR 20.1101 (ALARA) identified as an expectation for COL applicants. For SRP 12.1, Rev 4 section II.1 (Policy Considerations), the requirements partially conform, with site-specific aspects being the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the certified design. Radiation protection considerations in section II.4 are also deemed not applicable, referencing the comment for Acceptance Criterion II.3.

The reviewed passages address the applicability and conformity of various Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) documents and Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections for Combined License (COL) applications under 10 CFR Part 52. For DC/COL-ISG-13 and NUREG-0800 SRP Section 11.2 and Branch Technical Position 11-6, the assessment of accidental radioactive releases from liquid waste tanks partially conforms, with site-specific aspects delegated to the COL applicant. Calculations of transport capabilities in groundwater or surface water, exposure scenarios, and acceptance criteria are not applicable at the design certification stage, as these require site-specific hydrogeological and radiological data and are the responsibility of the COL applicant. SRP dose acceptance criteria and specifications on tank waste radioactivity concentration levels also partially conform, with site-specific implementation (such as the Offsite Dose Calculation Manual, ODCM) left to the COL applicant. DC/COL-ISG-17, concerning seismic input for site response and soil-structure interaction analyses, is not applicable at this stage but is relevant for COL application reviews. DC/COL-ISG-28, which addresses the technical adequacy of Advanced Light-Water Reactor probabilistic risk assessments (PRA), fully conforms and provides guidance for both design certification and COL applicants to meet PRA standards.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features an integral containment system that provides primary containment for the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), as detailed in Section 6.2. The containment design conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0 requirements, including design margin for containment pressure, heat removal capability, protection from external pressure, monitoring instrumentation, internal structures, and evaluation of accidents involving generated hydrogen or other modules. For the limiting event, the peak containment pressure remains below the design pressure. The NuScale design does not utilize an ice condenser containment, as specified in SRP 6.2.1.1.B, Draft Rev 3. Additionally, preliminary design information requirements under 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(v) are not applicable to the design certification application per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f). Further details on containment systems are provided in Section 6.2.

The NuScale design minimizes the amount of oxygen in containment during normal operations and includes hydrogen and oxygen monitoring equipment capable of continuously measuring gas concentrations in the containment atmosphere following significant beyond design-basis accidents for accident management and emergency planning. While the design complies with the intent of Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.7 regarding hydrogen and oxygen monitors, atmosphere mixing, hydrogen gas production, and containment structural integrity, it differs from the system designs addressed by the guidance and does not use active combustible gas control systems. The design partially conforms to DSRS 6.2.5 requirements for analysis of hydrogen and oxygen concentration control and distribution, but fully conforms to requirements for equipment survivability, containment structural integrity, and ensuring a mixed atmosphere. Containment integrity is assured without dedicated systems to control hydrogen and oxygen concentrations, and the design supports an exemption from containment leakage rate testing at design pressure as required by GDC 52 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix J Type A tests.

The leakage control program includes an initial test program, a re-testing schedule, and actions to minimize system leakage to as low as practical. The design of containment penetrations supports local leak rate tests (Type B and Type C) per ANSI/ANS 56.8, Regulatory Guide 1.163, and NEI 94-01, though the performance-based containment leak-test program is noted as not applicable. Hydrogen control systems to accommodate hydrogen from a 100% fuel-clad metal-water reaction (II.B.8) are not applicable per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f), which exclude Paragraph (f)(2)(ix) from design certification applications. The design conforms to positions on acceptable practices for minimizing intersystem LOCA (ISLOCA) probability, hydrogen measurement and mitigation requirements, containment performance analyses to ensure high protection, and the provision of a dedicated containment vent penetration to prevent over-pressurization failure. Containment leak rate testing partially conforms to the position on Type C test duration (prior to rule change), and the post-accident sampling system position addresses the capability to analyze dissolved hydrogen, oxygen, and chloride as required by regulations.

The NuScale ultimate heat sink (UHS) system is a seismic Category I supply system that provides spent fuel cooling, shielding, and containment heat removal, designed to accommodate postulated accident conditions. The UHS makeup supply line is built to Quality Group C and seismic Category I requirements, and meets GDC 4. An engineered safety feature (ESF) ventilation system is not required, as referenced in RG 1.52. The NuScale design uses a passive cooling system to transfer heat to the UHS, making Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.27 not applicable; instead, compliance with GDC 2 is demonstrated by adherence to RG 1.13, Regulatory Positions C.1 and C.2. The UHS is protected from natural phenomena and site-related events by the Seismic Category I RXB structure and a Seismic Category I emergency makeup line. The system conforms to requirements regarding the sharing of structures, systems, and components.

The NuScale design utilizes a steel containment vessel without interior concrete structures, rendering aspects of Regulatory Positions C.1 and C.14 related to concrete structures within containment inapplicable. The 2006 version of the ACI 349 standard has been referenced, but concrete containment requirements (SRP 3.8.1, Rev 4) and internal concrete or steel structures (SRP 3.8.3, Rev 4) are not applicable to this design. For the steel containment, the design conforms to DSRS 3.8.2, Rev. 0 requirements, including applicable codes, standards, and specifications; loads and loading combinations; design and analysis procedures; structural acceptance criteria; materials, quality control, and special construction techniques; and testing and inservice surveillance requirements. There are no internal structures within the containment, and the description of other Seismic Category I structures conforms to DSRS 3.8.4, Rev. 0.

The NuScale design employs the chemical and volume control system as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26 requirements. For containment pressure boundary fracture prevention (SRP 6.2.7, Rev 1) and most Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) criteria (DSRS 6.3, Rev 0), including ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46, single-failure consideration, design of non-safety-related ECCS portions, ECCS interfaces and shared systems, long-term cooling, ECCS outage times and unavailability reporting, and programmatic requirements, the design conforms to regulatory guidance. However, departures are noted for inservice inspection and operability testing, and for combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions; specifically, the NuScale ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, though actuation signal guidance is applied. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) for the emergency zone are met. For inadvertent ECCS operation (DSRS 15.6.6, Rev 0), the reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure remains below 110 percent of design value, and minimum DNBR is maintained. The design provides high point venting capability for noncondensible gases from the RCS and other systems to ensure adequate core cooling (50.34(f)(2)(vi)), and instruments in the control room give unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(xviii), with all relevant requirements conformed to unless otherwise noted.

The NuScale design's Decay Heat Removal (DHR) System, as described in DSRS 5.4.7, Rev 0, generally conforms to applicable requirements, including GDC 5, with no departures noted for responsibilities II.1 through II.4. The design ensures that sharing of the site cooling water system between units does not adversely affect essential SSCs or safety functions. However, departures are identified for GDC 19 (II.6) and GDC 34 (II.7), where the design supports exemptions and instead complies with NuScale-specific principal design criteria as detailed in Sections 3.1.2 and 3.1.4, respectively. For containment heat removal systems (DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0), the design conforms to GDC 39 regarding inspection (II.3) but departs from GDC 40 on testing (II.4), supporting an exemption. Similarly, for combustible gas control in containment (DSRS 6.2.5, Rev 0, II.4), the design supports an exemption from the power provisions of GDC 41, again relying on a NuScale-specific principal design criterion. Equipment survivability is used to assure protection from radiation. Additionally, regarding 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii), the NuScale design departs from the requirement to provide prompt sampling and analysis of reactor coolant and containment for accident source term radioactive materials, as it does not rely on such samples to assess potential core damage.

The reviewed sections of DSRS 6.2.1 (Containment Functional Design) and DSRS 6.2.4 (Containment Isolation System), Rev 0, indicate that there are no specific requirements listed for containment functional design, with applicable acceptance criteria addressed in SRP 2.4.6, 2.4.10, 2.4.12, 3.9.3, 19.0, and DSRS 3.8.2. For the containment isolation system, the design conforms to requirements for instrument line isolation (no instrumentation process lines penetrate containment), isolation and leak detection in engineered safety feature or related systems, and systems needed for safe shutdown (none present), as well as containment isolation valve requirements, use of sealed-closed barriers, classification of essential or nonessential systems, location of isolation valves outside containment, loss of power to automatic isolation valves, isolation reliability, parameter diversity for initiation of containment isolation, use of closed systems inside containment, and specific design criteria for containment isolation components (none present in each case). Relief valves are not used as containment isolation valves (CIVs). In summary, the containment isolation system either conforms to requirements or is not applicable due to the absence of relevant lines, valves, or systems.

The reviewed sections confirm that the containment heat removal capability and design margin for LOCA assumptions (DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0, II.3) conform to requirements, with no exceptions noted. The mass and energy release analysis for postulated LOCAs (DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, II.3) and its associated ITAAC are not applicable, as they pertain only to COL applicants. Containment heat removal systems (DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, II.5 and II.6) are compliant with 10 CFR 50.46(b)(5) regarding long-term cooling and adequate water level margin in the presence of LOCA-generated and latent debris. The LOCA acceptance criteria employ a temperature limit more restrictive than 2,200°F, as referenced in DSRS 15.0, Rev 0, I.5, and this also conforms to requirements. However, there is a departure regarding system operation from the control room: venting of noncondensible gases is deemed unnecessary to ensure long-term core cooling capability, diverging from the expectation that such systems should not increase the probability of a LOCA or challenge containment integrity.

The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) for the PWR, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0 requirements regarding functional performance during adverse environmental phenomena, pumping capacity, and defense-in-depth RCS makeup, as its only safety-related function is to preclude inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The system conforms to single failure criteria and GDC 5, which apply solely to the two safety-related demineralized water isolation valves designed to prevent inadvertent boron dilution. The CVCS also conforms to requirements for minimization of contamination, system design and arrangement, detection of reactor coolant leakage outside containment, and ITAAC, with no additional comments. However, it only partially conforms to criteria for prevention of CVCS holdup tank wall buckling/failure and CVCS venting and draining, as some acceptance criteria reference CVCS designs not applicable to the NuScale design.

The NuScale Main Steam Supply System (MSS) is not classified as safety-related or risk-significant, and the system conforms to protection requirements against natural phenomena (GDC 2), with the portion downstream of the main steam isolation valves (MSIV) inside the reactor building (RXB) including secondary MSIVs that serve as backups. The MSS also conforms to requirements for protection of structures, systems, and components (SSC) important to safety from turbine missile effects (GDC 4), and there are no shared SSCs important to safety that perform required safety functions (GDC 5). The MSS is only partially conformant with the requirement to support core cooling or safe shutdown (non-design basis accident) during a station blackout (SBO) per 10 CFR 50.63, as the acceptance criterion and its guidance are applicable. For the Condensate and Feedwater System, seismic event protection (GDC 2) is confirmed, with no additional details provided.

The NuScale design's approach to coolant source terms, as described in DSRS 11.1, Rev 0, partially conforms to several regulatory guides, including RG 1.110 and RG 1.112 (referenced in Table 1.9-2), and follows methodologies consistent with NUREG-0017, PWR-GALE86, and ANSI/ANS 18.1-1999 for effluent release calculations. The design is similar to large PWRs in the existing fleet regarding effluent release calculations, and decontamination factors for both gaseous and liquid effluent releases are consistent with the NuScale Technical Report, Effluent Release (GALE Replacement) Methodology and Results, Revision 0 (TR-1116-52065). RWMS system augmentations used in cost-benefit calculations are also partially consistent with RG 1.110. The primary and secondary coolant source terms used for characterizing liquid and gaseous effluents fully conform to requirements. The design basis coolant source term is partially based on a failed fuel fraction significantly less than 0.25 percent, as detailed in the referenced NuScale technical report. Compliance with 10 CFR 50.34(b)(3), 10 CFR 50.34a, and 10 CFR 52.79(a)(3) is partial, reflecting the similarities to large PWRs in effluent release calculations. DC/COL-ISG-5 is deemed not applicable due to these similarities.

Section 6.1 provides details on the selection and fabrication methods for metallic and organic materials used in Engineered Safety Feature (ESF) components, ensuring compatibility with fluids encountered during normal, accident, maintenance, and testing conditions. The NuScale design conforms to SRP 6.1.1, Rev 2 guidance on the composition and compatibility of ESF system fluids, except it does not provide a method for post-accident pH control as addressed in BTP 6-1. Regarding component and systems cleaning, the design only partially conforms since NRC Regulatory Guide 1.37 has been withdrawn. The design fully conforms to guidance on thermal insulation. SRP 6.1.2, Rev 3, concerning protective coating systems (paints) for organic materials, is not applicable because the NuScale Power Module does not use protective coatings inside the containment vessel.

The design does not utilize fiberglass-reinforced thermosetting resin piping in spray pond applications or for the Ultimate Heat Sink (UHS) design. For plant design protection against postulated piping failures in fluid systems outside containment, the design conforms to SRP 3.6.1, Rev 3 requirements. Specifically, it ensures separation of high and moderate energy fluid systems from essential systems and components (II.1), encloses high and moderate energy fluid systems (II.2), addresses cases where neither physical separation nor protective enclosures are practical (II.3), incorporates appropriate design features (II.4), and considers the effects of postulated failures (II.5). No exceptions or additional comments are noted for any of these criteria.

The review of SRP 3.6.2, Rev 2, "Determination of Rupture Locations and Dynamic Effects Associated with the Postulated Rupture of Piping," confirms conformance with all evaluated sections, including II.2 (Postulated Pipe Rupture Locations Outside Containment), II.3 (Methods of Analysis), III.1 (Pipe Break Criteria), III.2 (Dynamic Effects), III.3 (Assumptions for Modeling Jet Impingement Forces), and III.4 (Analyses of Pipe Break Dynamic Effects on Mechanical Components and Supports). No exceptions or deviations were noted in any section.

The passages summarize compliance evaluations with various Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections for nuclear plant mechanical components and systems. For SRP 3.6.3, Rev 1, Leak-Before-Break Evaluation Procedures (II.1), compliance with GDC 4 is confirmed. Under SRP 3.9.1, Rev 3, Special Topics for Mechanical Components, the use of computer programs in dynamic and static analyses (II.2) conforms, while the use of experimental stress analysis methods instead of analytical methods (II.3) is not applicable, as experimental methods are not used. For SRP 3.9.2, Rev 3, Dynamic Testing and Analysis of Systems, Structures, and Components, vibration, thermal expansion, and dynamic effects testing (II.1) partially conforms, with test performance and corrective actions being the responsibility of the COL applicant. Compliance with GDC 2 (II.2), analytical solutions for predicting vibrations of reactor internals in prototype plants (II.3), preoperational vibration and stress test programs (II.4), structural design adequacy of reactor internals and reactor coolant piping (II.5), correlation of tests and analyses of reactor internals (II.6), and test specifications for new applications (II.7) all conform with no exceptions noted.

The passages summarize compliance with various sections of the Standard Review Plan (SRP) and ASME Code requirements for nuclear plant components and piping. For SRP 3.9.3, Rev 3, the design and installation of pressure relief devices for ASME Code Class 1, 2, and 3 components, component supports, and core support structures conform to requirements, with no exceptions noted. The component supports section is not applicable, as NRC Bulletin 88-11 pertains only to PWR designs with a separate pressurizer and surge line, which does not apply here. For SRP 3.12, Rev 1, covering ASME Code Class 1, 2, and 3 piping systems, piping components, and their associated supports, the piping analysis methods, modeling techniques, stress analysis criteria, and piping support design all conform to requirements, with no exceptions or additional notes provided.
