The review of SRP 3.9.4, Rev 3 (Control Rod Drive Systems) confirms adequacy of descriptive information, though a typographical error causes confusion by mixing an SRP section reference with a Regulatory Guide (RG) per seismic design requirements of RG 1.29. The codes and standards for construction, as well as load combination sets for design and service conditions defined in ASME Code Section III, NB-3113, are met with no additional comments. For SRP 4.5.1, Rev 3 (Control Rod Drive Structural Materials), material specifications conform, noting that RG 1.85 was withdrawn in 2004 and its guidance incorporated into RG 1.84. Requirements for austenitic stainless steel components and cleaning/cleanliness control are satisfied, with the NuScale QAPD based on ANSI/ASME NQA-1-2008 and NQA-1a-2009 addenda, as endorsed by RG 1.28, Rev. 4. No additional comments are provided for other materials.

The review of NuScale's design against Standard Review Plan (SRP) criteria indicates full conformance for the Control Rod Drive Systems Operability Assurance Program (SRP 3.9.4, Rev 3, II.4), and for Reactor Pressure Vessel Internals regarding loads, loading combinations, and limits for ASME Code Section NG portions (SRP 3.9.5, Rev 3, II.1), as well as the design and construction of core support structures (II.2), and deformation limits for reactor internals (II.4). The design also fully conforms to SRP 4.5.2, Rev 3 requirements for reactor internal and core support structure materials (II.1) and controls on welding (II.2). Partial conformance is noted for the design of reactor internals to accommodate asymmetric blowdown loads from postulated pipe ruptures (SRP 3.9.5, Rev 3, II.5), as the referenced NUREG-0609 language pertains to different LWR and SSC conditions not relevant to NuScale. Similarly, partial conformance is identified for the effects of flow-induced vibration and acoustic resonances (including Appendix A, II.6), with exceptions for BWR-specific aspects. No additional exceptions or clarifications are noted for the fully conforming criteria.

The review of SRP 3.9.6, Rev 3, covering the functional design, qualification, and inservice testing programs for pumps, valves, and dynamic restraints, indicates partial conformance for functional design and qualification of pumps, valves, and dynamic restraints, except for aspects related to safety-related pumps. The inservice testing program for valves also partially conforms, with further details provided in Sections 3.9.6.3.2 and 3.9.6.6 regarding valve and augmented valve testing programs. The inservice testing program for dynamic restraints is not applicable, as the NuScale Power Plant does not utilize pumps or dynamic restraints with functions identified in ASME OM Code Subsection ISTA-1100. Relief requests and proposed alternatives fully conform, with references to Section 3.9.6.5 for details. Operational programs are not applicable, as these activities are the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the certified design. For SRP 3.10, Rev 3, the qualification program for valves that are part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary fully conforms. Additionally, in-service testing of pumps and valves to confirm operability of safety-related components conforms, as does the position on the use of recently developed or modified industry codes and standards in ALWR designs not yet reviewed by the NRC.

The passages indicate that the fuel system and nuclear design aspects of the reactor conform to the requirements outlined in SRP 4.2, Rev 3 and SRP 4.3, Rev 0, as well as DSRS 4.4, Rev 0. Specifically, the fuel system design meets criteria for fuel rod failure (II.1.B), fuel coolability (II.1.C), description and design drawings (II.2), design evaluation (II.3), and testing, inspection, and surveillance plans (II.4). The evaluation of fuel assembly structural response to externally applied forces (App A) and interim acceptance criteria for reactivity initiated accidents (App B) also conform. For nuclear design, the design limits for power densities and power distributions (II.1), reactivity coefficients (II.2), control rod patterns and reactivity worth (II.3), and analytical methods and data (II.4) all conform. Additionally, the thermal and hydraulic design section confirms compliance with fuel design limits, core design, and thermal margin requirements (DSRS 4.4, II.1). No exceptions or deviations are noted in any section.

The NuScale design's functional design of the Control Rod Drive System (CRDS), as reviewed under SRP 4.6, Rev 2, demonstrates conformance with several General Design Criteria (GDC): GDC 4 (Environmental and Dynamic Effects), GDC 23 (Failure Modes and Effects), GDC 25 (Single Malfunction), GDC 28 (Reactivity Limits), and GDC 29 (Protection Against Anticipated Operational Occurrences), with no exceptions noted. For GDC 26 (Operational Control and Reliability), NuScale conforms but clarifies that it does not interpret this criterion as requiring two safety-related means of reactivity control. Regarding GDC 27 (Combined Capability), NuScale departs from the standard GDC and instead adheres to a design-specific Principal Design Criterion (PDC) as detailed in Section 3.1.

For NuScale reactor events involving uncontrolled control rod assembly withdrawal from subcritical or low power startup conditions (SRP 15.4.1) and at power (SRP 15.4.2), as well as control rod misoperation (SRP 15.4.3), the use of critical heat flux (CHF) is considered more appropriate than departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR) for assessing thermal margin limits. NuScale ensures fuel centerline melting limits are met during slower reactivity insertions by applying a heat generation rate limit. The uniform cladding strain criterion, specified in the SRP as applicable to BWRs, is not applicable to NuScale; instead, NuScale employs the 95/95 minimum critical heat flux ratio (MCHFR) approach to ensure no cladding or fuel failures. All relevant criteria for thermal margin limits and fuel centerline temperatures are met, with no additional notes for uniform cladding strain under control rod misoperation scenarios.

The reviewed passages address conformance with Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections related to site characteristics and parameters for nuclear facilities. SRP 2.0 (March 2007) serves as a reference to other SRP sections, with acceptance criteria pointing to related chapters. NuScale partially conforms to Table 1 in Appendix A by providing site parameters where applicable. For SRP 2.4.7 (Rev 3: Ice Effects), all criteria under III.6.B (i–iv)—including postulated site parameters, Tier 1 information, summary tables, and basis for site parameters—are met, with no additional details provided. Similarly, for SRP 2.4.9 (Rev 3: Channel Diversions) and SRP 2.4.14 (Rev 3: Technical Specifications and Emergency Operation Requirements), all criteria under their respective sections (III.8.B and III.5.B) are confirmed as conforming, with no further information required or provided.

The provided passages summarize NuScale's conformance with various Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections related to site characteristics, climatology, meteorology, and wind loadings. For SRP 2.0 (Site Characteristics and Site Parameters), NuScale partially conforms by providing site parameters where applicable. For SRP 2.1.1 (Site Location and Description), and SRP 2.3.1/2.3.2 (Regional and Local Climatology/Meteorology), many requirements are noted as "Not Applicable" due to being site-specific, while sections requiring postulated site parameters, inclusion as Tier 1 information, summary tables, and basis for site parameters are marked as "Conforms" with no exceptions or additional details provided. For wind loadings (SRP 3.3.1), NuScale conforms to requirements for design wind speed, recurrence interval, site-related wind parameters, and procedures for transforming wind speed into equivalent pressure, again with no exceptions noted. Overall, NuScale's documentation aligns with SRP requirements where applicable, with site-specific details to be addressed separately.

The provided passages summarize compliance with several Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections related to site hazards and flooding for nuclear facility licensing. For SRP 2.2.1-2.2.2, Rev 3, the identification of potential hazards in the site vicinity is addressed as site-specific, with no general applicability. SRP 2.2.3, Rev 3 covers evaluation of potential accidents, including event probability and design-basis event analysis, also noted as site-specific. SRP 2.4.2, Rev 4 (Floods) confirms conformance with postulated site parameters (III.11.B.i) and inclusion of site parameters as Tier 1 information (III.11.B.ii), with no exceptions. SRP 2.4.4, Rev 3 (Potential Dam Failures) is addressed as site-specific in general, but conformance is confirmed for postulated site parameters (III.8.B.i), inclusion as Tier 1 information (III.8.B.ii), the site parameters summary table (III.8.B.iii), and the basis for site parameters (III.8.B.iv), all with no exceptions. SRP 2.4.5, Rev 3 (Probable Maximum Surge and Seiche Flooding) is also noted as site-specific, with no general applicability. All referenced sections either conform to requirements or are addressed as site-specific, with no additional exceptions or comments.

The passages summarize compliance with Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections related to meteorological and atmospheric dispersion analyses for nuclear facility licensing. SRP 2.3.3, Rev 3 addresses the onsite meteorological measurements program, which is site-specific and not applicable for generic evaluation. SRP 2.3.4, Rev 3 covers short-term atmospheric dispersion estimates for accident releases, including requirements for site parameters as Tier 1 information (III.6.b.2), a site parameters summary table (III.6.b.3), and the basis for site parameters (III.6.b.4); all are confirmed as conforming with no exceptions. Additionally, applicable short-term (post-accident) site parameters such as Exclusion Area Boundary (EAB), Low Population Zone (LPZ), and control room atmospheric dispersion factors are addressed and conform. SRP 2.3.5, Rev 3 pertains to long-term atmospheric dispersion estimates for routine releases, also site-specific, with postulated site parameters (III.5.b.1), Tier 1 information (III.5.b.2), and a summary table (III.5.b.3) all conforming. The applicable long-term (routine release) site parameter, specifically the maximum annual average site boundary atmospheric dispersion factor, is also confirmed as conforming. No exceptions or deviations are noted in any section.

The passages summarize conformance with various sections of the Standard Review Plan (SRP) revisions related to hydrologic descriptions, floods, cooling water canals and reservoirs, channel diversions, and low water considerations. For SRP 2.4.1 (Hydrologic Description, Rev 3), SRP 2.4.2 (Floods, Rev 4), SRP 2.4.8 (Cooling Water Canals and Reservoirs, Rev 3), SRP 2.4.9 (Channel Diversions, Rev 3), and SRP 2.4.11 (Low Water Considerations, Rev 3), the documentation indicates that all relevant subsections—including postulated site parameters, site parameters included as Tier 1 information, site parameters summary tables, and the basis for site parameters—are addressed and conform to the SRP requirements. No exceptions or deviations are noted, and all site parameters are either site-specific or not applicable, with no additional information provided.

The passages summarize compliance with Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections for external flooding hazards at a nuclear facility. For SRP 2.4.3, Rev 4 (Probable Maximum Flood on Streams and Rivers), the documentation confirms conformance for postulated site parameters (III.4.B.i), inclusion of site parameters as Tier 1 information (III.4.B.ii), and the site parameters summary table (III.4.B.iii), with no exceptions or additional notes. For SRP 2.4.5, Rev 3 (Probable Maximum Surge and Seiche Flooding), conformance is also confirmed for postulated site parameters (III.7.B.i), Tier 1 information (III.7.B.ii), summary table (III.7.B.iii), and basis for site parameters (III.7.B.iv), again with no exceptions. For SRP 2.4.6, Rev 3 (Probable Maximum Tsunami Hazards), the site-specific nature is noted, and conformance is confirmed for postulated site parameters (III.9.B.i), Tier 1 information (III.9.B.ii), and the summary table (III.9.B.iii), with no exceptions. All referenced sections indicate that the site parameters conform to the applicable SRP requirements, and no additional information or deviations are identified.

The passages summarize compliance with various Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections related to flooding and water considerations for nuclear facility sites. For SRP 2.4.10, Rev 3: Flooding Protection Requirements, the site conforms to requirements for postulated site parameters (III.5.B.i), inclusion of site parameters as Tier 1 information (III.5.B.ii), and the basis for site parameters (III.5.B.iv), with no exceptions noted. Flooding protection requirements are identified as site-specific where applicable. SRP 2.4.11, Rev 3: Low Water Considerations is also noted as site-specific and not applicable in general terms. For SRP 3.4.1, Rev 3: Internal Flood Protection for Onsite Equipment Failures, compliance with General Design Criterion (GDC) 4 is confirmed, with no exceptions.

The passages summarize compliance with SRP 2.4.12, Rev 3 (Groundwater) and SRP 2.4.13, Rev 3 (Accidental Releases of Radioactive Liquid Effluents in Ground and Surface Waters), indicating that for each relevant section—such as postulated site parameters (III.6.B.i and III.5.B.i), inclusion of site parameters as Tier 1 information (III.6.B.ii and III.5.B.ii), site parameters summary tables (III.6.B.iii), and basis for site parameters (III.6.B.iv and III.5.B.iv)—the documentation conforms to requirements, with no exceptions noted. Several items are marked as not applicable or site-specific, particularly for sections covering various requirements or technical specifications and emergency operation requirements (SRP 2.4.14, Rev 3). No additional information or exceptions are provided for any of the reviewed sections.

The passages summarize compliance with Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections related to geologic and seismic site characterization for nuclear facilities. For SRP 2.5.1 (Basic Geologic and Seismic Information), SRP 2.5.2 (Vibratory Ground Motion), SRP 2.5.3 (Surface Faulting), SRP 2.5.4 (Stability of Subsurface Materials and Foundations), and SRP 2.5.5 (Stability of Slopes), all relevant subsections—including postulated site parameters, site parameters summary tables, basis for site parameters, and inclusion as Tier 1 information—are addressed. Each section is noted as site-specific where applicable, and all referenced subsections (e.g., III.2.a, III.2.b, III.2.c, III.2.d) are confirmed to conform with requirements, with no exceptions or additional comments. No specific numerical values or component names are provided, and all site parameters are either not applicable or conform to the SRP requirements.

The Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to make as many copies of NuScale Power, LLC reports as needed for its internal use related to generic and plant-specific reviews, approvals, and regulatory actions—including issuance, denial, amendment, transfer, renewal, modification, suspension, revocation, or violation of licenses, permits, orders, or regulations—subject to 10 CFR 2.390 requirements on public disclosure for information identified as proprietary by NuScale Power, LLC, regardless of copyright protection. For nonproprietary versions, the NRC may make additional copies as necessary for public viewing in docket files and public document rooms in Washington, DC, and elsewhere as required by NRC regulations. All NRC copies must include the copyright notice and, if applicable, the proprietary notice. Proprietary or safeguards information required for design certification review is provided separately to the NRC in topical or technical reports, with referenced reports listed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2.

The NuScale Power, LLC design documentation includes the design bases, operational limits, a safety analysis of structures, systems, and components, and all information required by 10 CFR 52.47(a) relevant to the NuScale design. The design also considers conformance with the NRC's Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) for small modular reactors, as detailed in Table 1.9-3, and addresses NRC Interim Staff Guidance in Table 1.9-4. The NuScale system accommodates 10 CFR 50.54(o) and the test method frequencies of 10 CFR 50, Appendix J, Option A or B, with responsibility for Regulatory Guide (RG) compliance assigned to the COL applicant or licensee implementing Option B. Some referenced documents are not fully relevant to the NuScale design, but technically relevant features of Appendix K requirements are included in the analysis of small break LOCAs. While discussions influenced the development of NuScale Technical Specifications (TS), their specific content is not applicable to new generic TS in a Design Certification Application (DCA). Compliance with and applicability of 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, General Design Criteria (GDC) 55 and 57 for the NuScale design are addressed in Section 3.1.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, details the validation and regulatory conformance of its new designs, emphasizing performance, reliability, and repeatability, with additional testing for misalignment limits described in Section 1.5.1.7. The design partially conforms to several regulatory criteria: for accident monitoring instrumentation (IEEE Std 497-2002), NuScale uses highly reliable power for Type B and C variables instead of Class 1E; for the use of computers in safety systems (RG 1.152), the I&C development lifecycle differs from the conceptual waterfall model, with applicable tasks mapped accordingly; for verification, validation, reviews, and audits of digital computer software (RG 1.152, Rev. 3 and IEEE Std.7-4.3.2-2003), NuScale applies the endorsed standards; and for software life cycle processes (IEEE 1074-2006), requirements are tailored to the NuScale I&C lifecycle, which also differs from the RG 1.152 waterfall model. The qualification of safety-related motor control centers is not applicable, as the NuScale electrical system does not use them. The design fully conforms to response strategies for potential aircraft threats. Development and implementation of a risk-informed, performance-based inservice testing program (SRP 3.9.7, Rev 0) is not applicable to NuScale, as this is the responsibility of COL applicants referencing the certified design.

Test results verified the operability of NuScale's control rod drive shafts under various component conditions and distortions, confirming both control rod assembly (CRA) drop time and impact force at the end of drop. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) documents partial conformance with regulatory guidance for control rod ejection accident radiological consequence analysis (RG 1.77), primary containment fission product control systems (SRP 6.5.3), emergency coolant water pH requirements (BTP 6-1), potable and sanitary water system interfaces (SRP 9.2.4), and process and post-accident sampling systems (SRP 9.3.2), noting that some criteria are either specific to other reactor types or reference systems not present in the NuScale design. Full conformance is achieved for fuel system design bases and fuel system damage (SRP 4.2), protection of condensate storage facilities against natural phenomena (SRP 9.2.6), fuel centerline temperature limits during uncontrolled CRA withdrawal at power (SRP 15.4.2), and requirements for instrumentation to detect fuel loading errors (SRP 15.4.7). The condensate storage system is neither safety-related nor risk-significant, and the potable and sanitary water systems do not interface with radioactive systems. For slower reactivity insertions, a heat generation rate limit is used to ensure fuel centerline melting limits are not exceeded.

The NuScale Power Plant design certification process utilized 21 main reports, Supplements 1-34, and NRC letter SECY-07-0110 to identify applicable generic issues, with Table 1.9-5 summarizing relevant TMI requirements and NRC positions. Conformance with operational experience, including Generic Letters, Bulletins, and 10 CFR 21 notifications, is evaluated in Table 1.9-6, and NuScale’s QA supplier evaluation program reviews 10 CFR 21 notifications for all Nuclear Safety Related suppliers before approval. The NuScale design partially conforms to RG 1.189 (Fire Protection for Nuclear Power Plants), excluding requirements specific to non-PWR designs or site-specific elements, which are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. Guidance for near-surface disposal of radioactive waste (Revision 5, 4.18) is not applicable to the NuScale design. Entry/exit control for protected, vital, and material access areas (Revision 5, 5.7) partially conforms, with site-specific and programmatic aspects assigned to the COL applicant or licensee. The standard plant design includes provisions for an onsite technical support center and operational support center per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xxv), partially conforming to DSRS 9.5.2, Rev 0. For TMI requirements under 10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(i), NuScale partially conforms by addressing reliability of core and containment heat removal systems in the design certification, with site-specific updates required from the COL applicant.

The NuScale design and Quality Assurance Program Description (QAPD) are based on NQA-1-2008 and the NQA-1a-2009 addenda, as endorsed in Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.28, Revision 4, rather than NQA-1-1994. NQA-1-2008 and NQA-1a-2009 (Subpart 2.4) reference IEEE Std.336-1985 instead of IEEE Std.336-1971. Additionally, the neutron flux and fluence calculation methods follow the guidance of RG 1.190, with exceptions detailed in NuScale Technical Report TR-0116-20781, "Fluence Calculation Methodology and Results" (Reference 5.3-7).

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents several departures and conformances with regulatory criteria. Positions C1.1.3 and C1.1.4 are not applicable because the NuScale design does not rely on operator action or active devices to mitigate or prevent debris accumulation. The design does not comply with regulatory position C1.3.12 (prototypical head loss testing), nor with position C.2 except for meeting the intent of chemical reaction effects (position 2.2), and does not comply with position C.3. For DSRS 5.4.7 (Decay Heat Removal System), the design partially conforms to GDC 54, featuring a closed-loop DHRS outside containment directly connected to the closed-loop SG system within the RPV, providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and the reactor pool outside the NPM. Regarding DSRS 6.2.2 (Containment Heat Removal Systems), the design supports an exemption from the power provisions of GDC 38. For DSRS 6.2.5 (Combustible Gas Control in Containment), GDC 42 and 43 are not applicable as the design lacks a containment atmospheric cleanup system. In DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, the design conforms to fuel cladding integrity requirements and does not include a steam pressure regulator. The exclusion zone and buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability review (DSRS 15.9.A) is not used in the NuScale design. Finally, for DC/COL-ISG-5, the NuScale design is similar to large PWRs in the existing fleet regarding effluent release calculations, rendering the specific interim staff guidance not applicable.

NuScale tailors the requirements of several IEEE standards—specifically IEEE 1012-2004 (Verification and Validation), IEEE 1028-2008 (Software Reviews and Audits), IEEE 828-2005 (Configuration Management), IEEE 829-2008 (Test Documentation), IEEE 1008-1987 (Software Unit Testing), IEEE 830-1998 (Software Requirements Specifications), and IEEE 1074-2006 (Software Life Cycle Processes)—to its I&C development lifecycle, which differs from the conceptual waterfall lifecycle referenced in the standards and related Regulatory Guides (e.g., RG 1.152). For each standard, applicable tasks are mapped to the NuScale I&C development process. However, NuScale partially conforms to these standards, taking exception to certain administrative mandatory requirements that conflict with established Engineering or Quality Assurance documentation practices. This approach is consistently applied across configuration management, test documentation, unit testing, and requirements specification activities for digital computer software used in safety systems of nuclear power plants.

NuScale employs a risk-informed, performance-based approach to safety classification that integrates deterministic engineering judgment with probabilistic methods, consistent with the requirements for licensees choosing this alternative framework. The NuScale methodology combines the traditional safety classification approach—using definitions from 10 CFR 50.2, guidance from RG 1.26, and SRP Section 3.2.2—with an alternative regulatory framework similar to that in 10 CFR 50.69 and RG 1.201, as well as NEI 00-04 endorsed by RG 1.201. This approach aligns with SECY-03-0047 and SECY-10-0034, which advocate for probabilistic, risk-informed SSC safety classification, and applies RG 1.201 and NEI 00-04 guidance as appropriate for the NuScale advanced reactor's baseline risk metrics. The potable and sanitary water systems are designed to prevent flooding or adverse impacts on essential SSCs in the event of failure. Instrument air system (IAS) moisture separators and dryer packages ensure air quality meets ANS/ISA S7.3-R1981 standards. Additionally, the design of NuScale's electrical systems partially conforms to NRC Generic Letter 88-15, with relevant portions for passive plant design considered.

The provided guidance is not applicable to the DCA for certain systems, but is relevant for reviewing specific NuScale containment parameters and design features, such as design leakage rate and systems leakage prior to containment isolation. The applicability of GDC 2 to the Seismic Category I Water System (SCWS) is limited to ensuring that failure of the nonsafety-related SCWS does not adversely affect a Seismic Category I SSC. Similarly, the applicability of GDC 4 to both the NuScale cooling water system and the NuScale Main Steam Supply System (MSS) is restricted to ensuring that failure of a nonsafety-related SSC does not adversely impact a safety-related SSC. Additionally, certain aspects of the B&W ICS design identified as deficiencies after the TMI incident are not relevant to the NuScale design.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) documents conformance with various regulatory criteria, noting that guidance for containment purge systems venting directly to the environment is not applicable, as the NuScale containment does not require or incorporate such systems due to its small size and lack of sub-compartments. The spent fuel pool (SFP) mitigating measure is also not required, as the NuScale SFP is below grade and cannot be drained. Radiation and shielding design reviews are performed for spaces that may contain accident source term radioactive materials, with adequate access provided, and the only vital areas per NUREG-0737 are those for combustible gas monitoring, the main control room, and the technical support center. The containment isolation system conforms to 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiv)(A)-(D), ensuring automatic isolation of non-essential systems, two isolation barriers for non-essential penetrations, no reopening of isolation valves on signal reset, and a containment set point pressure compatible with normal operation; however, NuScale requests an exemption from (E), which requires automatic closure on high radiation signals for systems venting to the environment. The requirement for redundant dedicated containment penetrations for external hydrogen recombiners is not applicable, as the NuScale design does not use external hydrogen recombiners.

The NuScale design ensures that the nonsafety-related Secondary Component Water System (SCWS) is constructed to meet the provisions of Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.29, Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i. For the Reactor Component Cooling Water (RCCW) system, which is not safety-related, NuScale applies RG 1.29 position C.2, ensuring that structures, systems, and components (SSCs) whose structural failure could impact the operability of safety-related SSCs are designed as Seismic Category II. No portion of the NuScale condensate storage system requires design and construction to withstand the safe-shutdown earthquake to prevent adverse effects on Seismic Category I SSCs, consistent with RG 1.29 C.1.i. The plant’s passive design and reduced reliance on AC power align with regulatory guidance for coping with design basis events. The NuScale main steam supply system conforms to RG 1.117, Appendix Positions 2 and 4, for tornado missile protection of important-to-safety SSCs. Unlike B&W reactor designs, which were sensitive to secondary system transients per NUREG-0933, the NuScale design does not exhibit such sensitivity.

The NuScale plant design differs significantly from large LWRs and is not addressed by guidance or codes developed for traditional PWR reactors, such as the PWRGALE code from the 1980s. The NuScale design meets the intent of relevant regulatory guidance through its passive safety features and reduced reliance on AC power to manage design-basis events, including station blackout scenarios, where compressed air is not required for core cooling. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale employs an alternate methodology, incorporating some aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1, since existing codes and rules do not apply to its unique design.

The provided passages summarize the conformance of various radiation protection and shielding guidance documents with respect to nuclear power plant design. The guidance for concrete radiation shields and generic shield testing (Section 1.69) is partially conformed to, with site-specific test program development and implementation being the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. Several sections of DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0, addressing radiation protection design features, reference partial conformance to regulatory guides: RG 1.97 (Sections II.4 and II.24), RG 1.183 (Section II.5), RG 1.89 (Section II.18), RG 4.21 (Section II.19), and RG 1.143 (Section II.22), with further details available in Table 1.9-2. For Chapter 11, partial conformance is noted for BTP 11-3 and SECY-94-198, but these are not applicable to the DCA regarding supplemental extended low-level waste (LLW) storage and the development of a Process Control Program (PCP), which are designated as COL applicant responsibilities. All referenced guidance documents are only partially conformed to, with specific implementation details or responsibilities deferred to the COL applicant or licensee.

The DSRS 11.2, Rev 0: Liquid Waste Management System partially conforms to the acceptance criterion for capability to meet dose design objectives, with the exception of site-specific cost-benefit analysis, which is the responsibility of the COL applicant. It fully conforms to provisions for controlling leakage, facilitating operation and maintenance (II.4), and automatic control features (II.5). Criteria for early site permit applications (II.7) are not applicable, as they pertain only to early site permit applicants. For the DSRS 11.3, Rev 0: Gaseous Waste Management System, the capability to meet dose design objectives also partially conforms, excluding site-specific cost-benefit analysis, and criteria for early site permit applications (II.9) are not applicable. In the DSRS 11.4, Rev 0: Solid Waste Management System, provisions to control leakage and facilitate operation and maintenance (II.9) partially conform, with site-specific activities being the COL applicant’s responsibility. The storage facility design for long-term onsite storage (II.11) is not applicable, as the NuScale design does not include a long-term storage facility for solid radioactive waste; this is also a COL applicant responsibility.

The passages confirm that the design of both the Liquid Waste Management System (DSRS 11.2, Rev 0) and the Solid Waste Management System (DSRS 11.4, Rev 0) conforms to regulatory requirements in several areas. Specifically, the liquid waste system meets anticipated processing requirements (II.2) and seismic design standards for structures housing its components (II.3). For the solid waste system, the sizing of processing equipment (II.2), seismic design and quality group classification of components and structures (II.8), and seismic design of structures housing the system (II.19) all conform to the applicable standards, as referenced in Table 11.4-1 and Tables 11.4-5 through 11.4-9. No exceptions or deviations are noted in any of these areas.

The passages summarize the conformance of the Gaseous Waste Management System (DSRS 11.3, Rev 0) to various design and operational criteria. The system conforms to requirements for anticipated processing (II.2), seismic design and quality group classification of components and structures (II.3), design, testing, and maintenance of HEPA filters and charcoal adsorbers (II.5), automatic control features (II.6), design to withstand hydrogen explosion effects (II.7), and postulated leakage or failure of waste gas storage tanks or offgas charcoal delay beds (II.8), with no exceptions noted. The system partially conforms to features minimizing contamination, facilitating decommissioning, and minimizing radwaste generation (II.4), as some aspects are site-specific and the responsibility of the COL applicant. Partial conformance is also noted for relevant regulatory guides, interim staff guidance, and branch technical positions (II.10), as described in acceptance criteria II.1, II.3, II.4, II.5, and II.8. For the Solid Waste Management System (DSRS 11.4, Rev 0), automatic control features (II.17) are not applicable, while the design of exhaust ventilation systems (II.18) conforms with no exceptions.

The DSRS 11.4, Rev 0: Solid Waste Management System outlines several acceptance criteria for solid waste management in nuclear facilities, with varying degrees of applicability to design certification and COL (Combined License) applicants. Criteria related to liquid and wet waste stabilization (II.3) and stabilization of other forms of wet waste (II.4) partially conform or are not applicable, as the development and implementation of a Process Control Program (PCP) are the responsibility of the COL applicant. Design objectives, criteria, treatment methods, expected effluent releases, and monitoring/control instrumentation setpoints (II.5) are also not applicable for design certification, being under the COL applicant’s purview along with the Offsite Dose Calculation Manual (ODCM). Guidance for waste containers, shipping casks, and waste packaging (II.6), as well as onsite waste storage facilities (II.7), partially conforms, applying to design certification except for site-specific, programmatic, and operational aspects assigned to the COL applicant. Processing and disposal of Class A, B, and C wastes (II.12), greater than Class C wastes (II.13), and mixed wastes (II.14) all partially conform, with site-specific, programmatic aspects of PCP development and implementation being the COL applicant’s responsibility. Finally, all effluent releases associated with SWMS operation (II.15) partially conform, with site-specific, programmatic aspects again delegated to the COL applicant.

The DSRS 11.5, Rev 0: Process and Effluent Radiological Monitoring Instrumentation and Sampling Systems criteria are partially met across several areas. For installation of instrumentation, monitoring equipment, and sampling and analyses of normal and potential effluent pathways (II.1), the acceptance criterion is applicable except for certain aspects of its subtier guidance, specifically referencing RG 1.21, RG 1.33, RG 1.97, RG 4.1, RG 4.15, and BTP 7-10. For instrumentation and monitoring of radioactive waste process systems (II.2), partial conformance is noted with exceptions related to RG 1.21, RG 1.33, RG 1.97, RG 4.15, RG 4.21, and BTP 7-10. Provisions for administrative and procedural controls (II.3) also partially conform, with exceptions for RG 1.21, RG 1.33, RG 1.97, and RG 4.15. Monitoring, sampling, and analyses of all identified gaseous effluent release paths (II.4) partially conform, except for aspects related to RG 1.97 and BTP 7-10. For monitoring, sampling, and analysis of all identified liquid effluent release paths (II.5), partial conformance is noted, with administrative and procedural controls being the responsibility of the COL applicant. Full conformance is achieved for the provision of continuous sampling of radioactive iodines and particulates in gaseous effluents from all potential accident release points, including onsite capability for analysis and measurement (II.F.1), with no exceptions noted.

The provided passages summarize compliance with various regulatory requirements for process and effluent radiological monitoring instrumentation and sampling systems. For DSRS 11.5, Rev 0, the design features and instrumentation for primary and secondary coolant system leakage detection are described and found to conform with requirements, with no exceptions noted. DSRS 11.6, Rev 0 addresses instrumentation and control design features for process and effluent radiological monitoring, as well as area radiation and airborne radioactivity monitoring. Compliance with GDC 63 and 64 is achieved via post-TMI action plan items, but only partially conforms regarding 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xxvi), as aspects related to testing and operational programs are the responsibility of the COL applicant. Compliance with 10 CFR 20.1406 is achieved via RG 4.21, NEI 97-06, 08-08A, and 07-07. Instrumentation for primary and secondary coolant system leakage detection is described and conforms to requirements. Additionally, instrumentation is provided to measure, record, and read out in the control room: (A) containment pressure, (B) containment water level, (C) containment hydrogen concentration, (D) containment radiation intensity (high level), and (E) noble gas effluents at all potential accident release points, as required by 50.34(f)(2)(xvii). For 50.34(f)(2)(xxvi), leakage control and detection for systems outside containment that may contain accident source term radioactive materials is partially conforming, applicable to the DCA as relevant to the standard plant design.

The passages summarize conformance with DSRS 11.6, Rev 0 guidance on instrumentation and control design features for process and effluent radiological monitoring, as well as area radiation and airborne radioactivity monitoring. Specifically, they confirm the installation of instrumentation or sampling equipment (II.1), monitoring of gaseous and liquid release points (II.2), and appropriate locations for radiation exposure rate and airborne concentration monitoring and sampling points (II.3). The process for developing, reviewing, verifying, validating, and auditing digital computer software is described (II.6). RETS/SREC and ODCM setpoints are noted as not applicable, as they are the responsibility of the COL applicant (II.7). Radiation monitoring and sampling are confirmed to conform to Technical Specifications, the Initial Test Program, and ITAAC (II.11). The types and ranges of radiation monitoring equipment are described (II.12), and reactor fuel storage area monitors are confirmed (II.13). No exceptions or additional comments are noted except where responsibilities are deferred to the COL applicant.

The reviewed sections address compliance with various radiation protection standards and guidance documents. Section 12.1.1 confirms full conformance with SRP 12.1, Rev 4, ensuring occupational radiation exposures are as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA). Section 12.3.1 states conformance with ANSI/ANS/HPSSC-6.8.1-1981 for radiation protection design features. Section 12.3.4 confirms conformance with ANSI/ANS-6.4-2006, while Section 12.3.2 notes partial conformance with a memo from Larry W. Camper to David B. Matthews and Elmo E. Collins dated 10-10-2006, specifying that only the design phase guidance applies to the DCA. Section 12.3.4 also indicates partial conformance with RG 1.140, referencing Table 1.9-2 for details. Section 12.3.6 reports partial conformance with RG 1.45, with further information in Table 1.9-2. Section 5.2.5 confirms conformance with NEI 97-06. No exceptions or additional comments are noted unless specified.

The review of DSRS 12.2 and 12.3-12.4, Rev 0, addresses conformance with various Regulatory Guides (RGs) related to radiation sources and radiation protection design features. For DSRS 12.2, Rev 0, partial conformance is noted with RG 1.183 and RG 1.112, while RG 1.7 is deemed not applicable; there is also confirmation that no radiation source is created from the determination of gaseous concentrations in containment following an accident, such as from sample lines outside containment. For DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0, partial conformance is indicated with RG 1.69, RG 8.8, and RG 8.38, while RG 8.15 is not applicable and RG 8.19 is fully conformed to. References to specific details and justifications for these determinations are provided in Table 1.9-2.

The passages summarize the conformance of DSRS 12.2, Rev 0: Radiation Sources with various regulatory guides and standards. For NUREG-0737, Task Action Plan Item II.B.2 and ANSI/ANS Standard 18.1, DSRS 12.2 fully conforms, with no exceptions noted. Regarding radiation sources for 10 CFR 50.49 (Environmental Qualification), DSRS 12.2 also conforms without exceptions. For RG 1.143, DSRS 12.2 only partially conforms, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2. Finally, DSRS 12.2 conforms to RG 1.26, RG 1.29, and RG 1.117, with no exceptions. All technical details, component names, and referenced standards are explicitly listed.

The referenced sections from DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0, address Radiation Protection Design Features and indicate that Regulatory Guides (RG) 1.7, 8.2, 8.10, and 8.25 are not applicable, with further details provided in Table 1.9-2. Specifically, RG 1.7 is not applicable because there is no radiation field generated from the determination of gaseous concentrations in containment following an accident, such as from sample lines outside containment. Similarly, RGs 8.2, 8.10, and 8.25 are also deemed not applicable, with references to Table 1.9-2 for additional information. Additionally, DSRS 12.5, Rev 0, concerning the Operational Radiation Protection Program, states that guidance on operational programs, procedures, facilities, and organizational responsibilities is site-specific and falls under the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the certified design.

Section 1.9 details the evaluation of conformance with regulatory criteria, specifically addressing compliance with various groups of Regulatory Guides (RGs): Division 1 (Power Reactors), Division 4 (Environmental and Siting), Division 5 (Materials and Plant Protection), and Division 8 (Occupational Health). The conformance status for each applicable RG is documented in Table 1.9-2, which includes the RG number, division, title, revision, conformance status, comments, and relevant section references. Notably, RG 1.37 has been withdrawn by the NRC. For example, RG 1.200, "An Approach for Determining the Technical Adequacy of Probabilistic Risk Assessment Results for Risk-Informed Activities" (Rev. 2), is confirmed as conforming, as referenced in SRP 19.0 regarding PRA quality and technical adequacy (Section 19.1). Similarly, RG 1.204, "Guidelines for Lightning Protection of Nuclear Power Plants," is also listed as conforming with no additional comments (Section 3.8). The table continues with additional RGs, each evaluated for conformance and cross-referenced to the appropriate sections of the documentation.

The provided passages detail the assessment of conformance with regulatory requirements and operating experience for a nuclear plant, as documented in tables such as 1.9-4 (Interim Staff Guidance), 1.9-5 (TMI Requirements per 10 CFR 50.34(f) and Generic Issues from NUREG-0933), and 1.9-6 (Evaluation of Operating Experience via Generic Letters and Bulletins). In accordance with 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8), the plant's compliance is evaluated against technically relevant Three Mile Island (TMI) requirements specified in 10 CFR 50.34(f), with exceptions for paragraphs (f)(1)(xii), (f)(2)(ix), and (f)(3)(v). Plant characteristics and programs addressing these requirements are referenced in the appropriate FSAR sections (e.g., 5.2, 6.3.1, 7.1, 7.2.13, 9.3.6, 19.2, 4.3.2, 6.3, 7.0.4, 11.5, 11.6, 12.3.4, 8.2.3). The RCS venting capability mandated by 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(vi) is noted as substantively similar to the requirements of 10 CFR 50.46a. Each table includes columns for item, regulation description/title, conformance status, comments, and section references, ensuring traceability and documentation of compliance with both regulatory and operating experience requirements.

Tables and figures in large "X.Y.Z" sections are numbered sequentially within each section (e.g., Table 3.9.3-1, Figure 3.9.3-1) and are placed at the end of the relevant section following the text. The EDSS conforms to RG 1.32 as described in Section 8.3.8.2. The 1997 version, with additional design enhancements, aligns with RG 1.63 and is referenced in Sections 3.8.2, 3.11, 8.1, 8.3, and 14.3. Regulatory Guide (RG) conformance, including preoperational test implementation (primarily the responsibility of the COL applicant), is detailed in Table 1.9-2, which is referenced across multiple sections (6.3, 7.2, 8.3, 11.2, 11.3, 12.1, 12.3, 12.5, 14.2, 14.3). QR-B5300 and QR-B5500 address qualification methods and shelf life preservation for nonmetallics, with exceptions described in Appendix 3C. Additionally, standard 485-1997 is applied with consideration of RG 1.212 for VRLA battery sizing, as noted in Section 8.3.

In accordance with 10 CFR 52.47(a)(21), proposed resolutions must be identified for any technically relevant unresolved safety issues and medium- to high-priority generic safety issues (GSI) listed in the version of NUREG-0933 current six months prior to the design certification application. The resolution and closure of these generic issues are managed through the NRC Generic Issues Program. NRC SECY-07-0110, dated July 6, 2007, provides the most recent supplemental status report of the Generic Issues Program prior to the FSAR submittal, and Appendix B of NUREG-0933, Rev. is referenced. Table 1.9-5 includes a brief conformance assessment notation, annotations of any exceptions, and references to relevant FSAR sections. Generic issues from NUREG-0933 determined to be non-applicable were excluded from Table 1.9-5 if they were resolved and removed from the latest Generic Issues Program list of Active and Regulatory Office Implementation Generic Issues.

The provided passages describe the classification of design conformance with regulatory requirements or guidance. "Conforms" indicates full compliance with selected regulatory options, while "Partially Conforms" applies when only relevant portions of the requirement or guidance are met, or when the intent is applicable but the specific language refers to a different LWR design or an SSC not present in the design, though an equivalent function is provided by another SSC. "Not Applicable" is used when the regulation or guidance does not apply, and thus conformance is not required. For Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs), after the first NPM is qualified as a valid prototype, subsequent NPMs are classified as non-prototype category I, and only the non-prototype portions of Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.20 apply. The responsibility for identifying departures from RG 1.20 lies with the COL applicant or licensee. Table 1.9-2 documents conformance status for various RGs, with several entries marked "Not Applicable," such as RG 5.49 on Internal Transfers of Special Nuclear Material, which is noted as issued for comment and not relevant to the design.

The introduction to 10 CFR 50, Appendix A allows for departures from the General Design Criteria as detailed in Section 3.1, and Three Mile Island (TMI) action items may be identified and justified as “not technically relevant” to a design in accordance with 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f). Some TMI action items are categorized as “Partially Conforms” or “Not Applicable” rather than “Departure,” typically because the requirements pertain to boiling water reactors (BWRs) or to structures, systems, and components (SSCs) not present in the NuScale design. An exemption is unnecessary when 10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(ii) applies only to technically relevant portions of the TMI requirements. For example, Table 1.9-5 documents conformance with TMI Requirements (10 CFR 50.34(f)) and Generic Issues (NUREG-0933), noting items such as Issue 204, “Flooding of Nuclear Power Plant Sites Following Upstream Dam Failures,” as “Not Applicable” since the guidance is site-specific.

Table 1.9-2 outlines conformance with various Regulatory Guides, detailing specific positions and standards applied. For Position C.1.1.2, requirements include minimization of debris source term, implementation of cleanness programs, monitoring and sampling for latent debris, insulation selection, and restrictions on coatings and cladding of carbon steel, as referenced in Section 6.3. Additionally, the provisions of ANSI/ANS 56.2-1984, Section 3.6.5, are applied to penetrations with both containment isolation valves (CIVs) located outside containment that serve non-Engineered Safety Feature (non-ESF) process systems, as noted in Section 6.2. Security-related conformance is addressed in Section 13.6 via the Security Technical Report.

The requirements and guidance for design certification under 10 CFR Part 52, including the level of detail required (SECY-90-241, SECY-90-377), have been incorporated into 10 CFR 52 and associated NRC guidance documents. Similarly, guidance on Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC) for design certifications and combined licenses (SECY-91-178, SECY-91-210) is also incorporated into 10 CFR 52 and NRC guidance. The use of Design Acceptance Criteria during Part 52 design certification reviews (SECY-92-053) conforms to NRC Orders, regulatory guidance, and pending rulemaking. Positions on providing ITAAC to demonstrate that a nuclear power plant referencing a certified design is built and operates consistent with the design certification (II.L), and on providing a description of the purpose, scope, objectives, and implementation of a design reliability assurance program (II.M), are both in conformance, with no additional requirements specified.

Maintenance activity controls are implemented to ensure that emergency core cooling system (ECCS) function is not compromised by changes to analytical inputs, assumptions, or activities that could introduce debris or potential debris sources into containment. Additional controls limit the introduction of coating materials into containment, and an inspection program verifies containment vessel cleanliness prior to closure for normal power operation. For any in-containment event resulting in core damage or degradation, containment isolation is automatically actuated on low-low pressurizer level and high containment pressure, providing a reliable alternative means to prevent radiological release from the containment evacuation system (CES) to the environment, consistent with acceptance criteria. The containment high pressure analytical limit is subatmospheric, ensuring that any setpoint up to this limit will prevent environmental release. Design basis events meet all thermal and hydraulic acceptance criteria without relying on CES isolation via high radiation signals, and no design basis event results in core damage or degradation. Section 19.2 analyses confirm that severe accident conditions resulting in core damage also generate reliable containment isolation signals, independent of high containment radiation isolation. Calculated peak containment pressures for design basis events remain below the containment vessel (CNV) internal design pressure, and even under severe accident progression, peak pressures do not challenge containment integrity, as referenced in TR-0716-50424, Section 2.8.

Both leakage detection methods comply with Regulatory Positions C.2.1 and C.2.2 in RG 1.45 by enabling detection, monitoring, and quantification of unidentified leakage into the primary reactor containment at rates ≥ 0.05 gpm, with a response time (excluding transport delay) of less than one hour for leakage rates above one gpm. Radiation detectors in the CES condenser vent line provide early indication of RCS leakage, consistent with Regulatory Position C.2.3, but all leakage is classified as unidentified due to limited capability to identify or quantify RCS leakage. Containment leakage remains unidentified until the source is located and quantified by other means; the RCS leakage rate into containment is determined by performing an RCS inventory balance and comparing it to the total flow rate into the CES, with operational unidentified leakage limits specified in plant technical specifications. Two primary methods for detecting leakage into containment are monitoring changes in containment pressure and measuring condensate collected from the CES, with RCS leakage detection instrumentation requirements detailed in plant technical specifications. The CES removes water vapor and noncondensable gases, condensing the water vapor for monitoring. Regular RCS inventory mass balances are performed as part of technical specification surveillance requirement 3.4.5.1, augmenting radiation instrumentation, chemistry analysis, and CES leak detection methods to help determine the source of a leak inside containment.

Regulatory Position C.2.4 is satisfied because the containment pressure method remains functional after a seismic event that does not require plant shutdown, with the vacuum pump operational. C.2.5 is met as both methods allow calibration and testing during plant operation. The Containment Evacuation System (CES), a nonsafety-related, NuScale Power Module-specific system, reduces and maintains internal containment pressure below the saturation pressure of the lowest surface temperature inside the Containment Vessel (CNV) by using vacuum pumps to remove water vapor and noncondensable gases. The CES inlet pressure instrumentation, designed to Seismic Category I, provides indication in the Main Control Room (MCR) and enables accurate trending of leakage data, detecting Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) leak rates below 1 gpm in one hour, with a minimum detectable leak rate of less than 0.05 gpm. Piping from the containment to the first seismic restraint outside the CNV and the CES inlet pressure instrumentation are Seismic Category I, ensuring safety function capability during and after a safe shutdown earthquake, while the remaining CES equipment is Seismic Category II or III. Loss of CES equipment or containment isolation during a seismic event results in loss of containment vacuum and a subsequent NuScale Power Module (NPM) trip. If the CES remains available during a seismic event, the sampling system continues to collect and measure condensate, including RCPB leakage, until failure of the non-seismic CES portion, after which containment pressure increases and isolation occurs. The Seismic Category I CES inlet pressure instrumentation ensures continued leak monitoring and detection of changes in containment atmospheric conditions, including RCPB leakage, during seismic events not resulting in NPM shutdown.

In the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, reactor coolant system (RCS) leakage into the containment vessel (CNV) atmosphere is conservatively classified as unidentified leakage, including leakage from reactor safety valves (RSVs) and reactor vent valves (RVVs), which are designed, tested, and inspected to ASME BPVC Sections III and XI criteria. Leakage from the reactor vessel (RPV) to the CNV is continuously monitored, constituting a VT-2 examination per Section XI IWA-5241(c). During normal operation, the CNV is maintained under a partial vacuum, causing any RCS leakage to vaporize and disperse rapidly within the containment, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage with the installed instrumentation. Leakage into containment may also originate from sources other than the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), such as reactor component cooling water. The RCPB leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive, supporting leak-before-break analyses as addressed in Section 3.6.3.

The containment evacuation system (CES) maintains containment pressure below saturation, preventing water vapor from condensing and making water level monitoring for reactor coolant system (RCS) leakage impractical. Leakage from reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, and actuators is exhausted directly into the containment atmosphere and included in total unidentified leakage, with no specific monitoring for these components. Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) leakage outside the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) is classified as identified leakage and is collected via closed piping, with effluents from pumps, valves, or flanges drained to the reactor building equipment drain sump and pumped to low conductivity waste collection tanks. The liquid radioactive waste system monitors tank levels and alarms at preset high leakage levels. Intersystem CVCS leakage is identified by increases in level, temperature, flow, pressure, relief valve actuation, or radioactivity. Reactor Component Cooling Water System (RCCWS) leakage is detected by monitoring expansion tank level, with alarms in the control room; if radioactivity enters RCCWS piping, radiation detectors downstream of key heat exchangers and coolers provide additional alarms. Primary to secondary leakage is monitored by radiation detectors on the condenser air removal system, main steam lines, and turbine sealing steam system, with the capability for grab sampling of steam and feedwater for further analysis. Additional details are referenced in Sections 9.2.2, 9.3.3, 9.3.4, and 11.5.

The CES (Condensate Evacuation System) sample vessel collects condensed water, which is monitored for pressure, temperature, level, and radioactivity using dedicated instrumentation. Radioactivity monitoring helps identify the source of containment leakage, with higher readings potentially indicating Reactor Coolant System (RCS) leakage. Level instrumentation in the sample vessel quantifies and trends leak rates into containment, while CES gaseous discharge radioactivity monitoring can detect fuel leaks or excessive RCS leakage. An alarm is provided to the main control room (MCR) when 75% of the maximum allowable pressure-temperature for a given reactor pool temperature is reached, alerting operators to the approaching operability limit. The CES enables monitoring of both gaseous and liquid effluents for radioactivity via remote instrumentation, with gaseous discharges directed to the process sampling system sample panel for continuous or grab sample analysis. A grab sample location on the CES sample vessel allows laboratory analysis, and the use of different radioisotope characteristics, including tracer gases, assists in determining the source of leaks, as further discussed in FSAR Section 11.5. The condensate measurement method is used to obtain an accurate leakage rate.

Vapor drawn from containment and condensed after the vacuum pump is measured as collected condensate, with a correction factor applied—based on the calculated carryover rate past the CES condenser—to ensure a conservative indicated leak rate. To ensure accurate leakage accounting, condensation within containment must be avoided. The method requires stabilizing containment pressure below saturation pressure so that the water vapor removal rate matches the water mass leak rate into containment. The CES inlet pressure instrumentation monitors containment pressure and serves as a leakage indicator. Leak rate is determined using the time for pressure change, containment gas load, and net mass flow rate; the leak must be large enough to cause a detectable pressure change, with higher temperature and pressure systems being easier to monitor. The instrumentation can detect a minimum RCS leak rate of 0.007 gpm, and a 1 gpm RCS leak will increase containment pressure by 0.1 psi in less than one minute. Upon observing a pressure change, operators initiate procedures to identify and quantify the leak. Combining pressure changes with other plant indications helps determine the leak source. Maintaining effective leakage detection with pressure and CES condensate monitoring depends on keeping containment pressure below the vapor pressure corresponding to the lowest internal wall temperature, conservatively assumed equal to the reactor pool bulk temperature. If containment pressure enters the Not Acceptable region (per Figure 5.2-3), internal condensation may occur, compromising monitoring accuracy. Containment leakage monitoring instrumentation only detects pressure boundary failures within the containment vessel.

Leak-before-break evaluations for feedwater and steam piping within containment determined that leakage is not plausible due to the absence of mechanical fittings or valves associated with these lines. Nevertheless, calculations were performed to assess the adequacy of leakage monitoring instrumentation, considering a through-wall flaw leak rate ten times greater than the minimum reactor coolant system (RCS) leak detection capability, and applying an additional safety factor of two between the critical break flaw size and the leakage flaw size. For further details, reference is made to Section 3.6.3. Regarding reactor pressure vessel (RPV) flange leak-off monitoring, bolted flanges and covers in the RCS are sealed with double concentric O-rings, and a leak-off port is located between the O-rings to allow pressurization and confirmation of seal integrity prior to operation. The maximum diameter of the leak-off connection is limited by an orifice to ensure that a break would be less than the normal makeup capacity of the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS). No additional RPV flange leak-off monitoring is provided.

The provided passages summarize the applicability of various regulatory guidance and requirements to the NuScale nuclear power plant design and the responsibilities of the Combined License (COL) applicant or licensee. Qualification and training of personnel (Section 1.8), application and testing of safety-related diesel generators (Section 1.9), and nuclear power plant simulation facilities for operator training (Section 1.149) are not applicable to the NuScale design, as these are site-specific, programmatic, or operational activities under the COL applicant’s purview. The NuScale design does not require safety-related emergency diesel generators. Guidance implementing operator staffing requirements of 10 CFR 50.54(m)(2)(i) and (iii) is also not applicable to COL applicants. The design conforms to protection against turbine missiles (Section 1.115). For general use of locks (Section 5.12), perimeter intrusion alarm systems (Section 5.44), vital area access controls and key/lock controls (Section 5.65), and protection against malevolent use of vehicles (Section 5.68), the NuScale design partially conforms, with site-specific and programmatic aspects being the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. Design considerations for systems measuring the mass of liquids (Section 5.48) are also applicable to the COL applicant or licensee. The design must allow compliance with vehicle protection requirements, such as those referenced in the F090 Site Layout Plan and 10 CFR 73.55.

The provided passages clarify that site-specific and operational aspects of various regulatory guidance documents—such as the amount and location of toxic chemicals, atmospheric dispersion factors, emergency planning activities, construction, installation, and testing criteria—are the responsibility of the Combined License (COL) applicant or licensee, not the design certification applicant. Design-related aspects of guidance, including those for air filtration and adsorption units (RG 1.140), minimization of contamination and radioactive waste generation (RG 4.21), occupational radiation dose assessment (RG 8.19), and control of access to high radiation areas (RG 8.38), are applicable to the NuScale standard plant design to the extent they pertain to design features, facilities, functions, and equipment. However, portions of these guidances that require site-specific information or address operational and programmatic activities are not applicable to the design certification and must be addressed by the COL applicant or licensee. Additionally, certain guidance, such as RG 1.145 on atmospheric dispersion models, is not applicable where it does not address relevant phenomena (e.g., building wake effects).

The table summarizes the conformance status of various Regulatory Guides (RGs) with respect to their applicability for a Combined Operating License (COL) applicant or licensee. RG 1.109 (Calculation of Annual Doses to Man from Routine Releases of Reactor Effluents for the Purpose of Evaluating Compliance with 10 CFR Part 50, Appendix I) partially conforms, as it is applicable except for site-specific information such as meteorological data, which is the responsibility of the COL applicant. RGs 4.13, 8.2, 8.7, 8.10, 8.13, 8.25, and 8.34 are not applicable, as they govern site-specific, programmatic, procedural, or equipment-related activities that fall under the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. RG 8.8 (Information Relevant to Ensuring that Occupational Radiation Exposures at Nuclear Power Stations Will Be as Low as Is Reasonably Achievable) partially conforms, with implementation being largely site-specific and the responsibility of the COL applicant. RG 8.23 is not applicable, as it pertains only to medical institutions.

The provided passages summarize the applicability of various Regulatory Guides (RGs) related to nuclear power plant site investigations, environmental monitoring, and reporting. RG 1.132 (Rev. 2) covers site investigations for nuclear power plant foundations as part of site selection, with site-specific guidance being the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. RGs 3.3, 3.5, 3.8, and 4.1 (Rev. 2) address radiological environmental monitoring, which is also the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. RG 4.2 (Rev. 2) and RG 4.11 (Rev. 2) pertain to the preparation of environmental reports and terrestrial environmental studies, respectively, both requiring site-specific evaluations by license or construction permit applicants. RG 4.4 governs procedures for mathematical models predicting heated effluent dispersion in natural water bodies, with activities assigned to the COL applicant or licensee. RG 4.7 (Rev. 2) provides general site suitability criteria, also under the COL applicant’s purview. RG 4.9 (Rev. 1), RG 4.14 (Rev. 1), RG 4.16 (Rev. 2), RG 4.17 (Rev. 1), RG 4.19, and RG 4.20 (Rev. 1) are not applicable to nuclear power plants, as they pertain to uranium enrichment facilities, uranium mills, fuel cycle facilities, high-level-waste geologic repositories, near-surface disposal of low-level radioactive waste, and non-reactor facilities, respectively. RG 4.15 (Rev. 2) on quality assurance for radiological monitoring programs is applicable to COL applicants.

The provided passages summarize NuScale's conformance with various NRC Regulatory Guides (RGs) as documented in Table 1.9-2 of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5. For RG 1.134 (Medical Evaluation of Licensed Personnel at Nuclear Power Plants), RG 8.15 (Acceptable Programs for Respiratory Protection), and RG 8.27 (Radiation Protection Training for Personnel at Light-Water-Cooled Nuclear Power Plants), the guidance governs site-specific operational or programmatic activities and is the responsibility of the COL (Combined License) applicant or licensee. RG 1.175 (Risk-Informed Inservice Testing) is applicable only to licensees seeking risk-informed changes to their licensing basis; NuScale is not using a risk-informed approach for IST. RG 1.177 (Risk-Informed Technical Specifications) applies to existing licensees seeking NRC approval for technical specification changes, but NuScale considered this guidance as appropriate in developing risk-informed technical specifications. RG 1.186 (Identifying 10 CFR 50.2 Design Bases) and RG 1.187 (Implementation of 10 CFR 50.59, Changes, Tests, and Experiments) are also the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. RG 5.27 (Special Nuclear Material Doorway Monitors) is applicable to the COL applicant, while RG 5.61 (Physical Protection Upgrade Rule Requirements for Fixed Sites) applies to fuel cycle facilities and is not applicable to NuScale. In all cases, compliance with these RGs is either not applicable to NuScale at this stage or is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee.

The provided passages summarize conformance with various NRC Regulatory Guides (RGs) related to nuclear power plant operations, decommissioning, and safety. For RGs 1.159, 1.160, 1.166, 1.167, 1.179, 1.184, 1.185, 1.188, 1.202, 1.219, and 4.22, the activities governed—such as decommissioning funding, maintenance monitoring, earthquake planning and response, post-earthquake restart, license termination planning, decommissioning planning and reporting, license renewal, decommissioning cost estimates, emergency plan changes, and decommissioning planning during operations—are all noted as the responsibility of the COL (Combined Operating License) applicant or licensee, and thus are marked "Not Applicable" to the current context. The only exception is RG 1.203, "Transient and Accident Analysis Methods," for which conformance is indicated with no additional comments. All technical details, including RG numbers, titles, revision numbers, and the specific responsibilities, are preserved as stated.

The provided passages summarize the applicability of various Regulatory Guides (RGs) related to special nuclear material (SNM) control, accountability, and physical protection. Most RGs, such as those on statistical terminology (5.3), nondestructive assay (5.11), physical inventories (5.13), error concepts (5.18), shipper-receiver differences (5.28), material control systems (5.29), specially designed vehicles for SNM shipment (5.31), statistical evaluation of material unaccounted for (5.33), physical protection plans (5.52), qualification and calibration for nondestructive assay (5.53), shipping and receiving controls (5.57), traceability of SNM measurements (5.58), physical protection of mixed oxide fuels (5.78), and tamper-indicating seals (5.80), are deemed not applicable to the NuScale design, as it does not process SNM, use mixed oxide fuels, or fall under the scope of fuel processing, fabrication, or Part 70 facilities. However, RG 5.79 on the protection of Safeguards Information is applicable, with NuScale conforming to its requirements to prevent unauthorized disclosure. Some RGs, such as those on material control systems (5.29), specially designed vehicles (5.31), shipping and receiving controls (5.57), traceability (5.58), and target set identification (5.81), may be relevant to a Combined License (COL) applicant or licensee, but not directly to the NuScale design itself.

The provided passages summarize the applicability of various Regulatory Guides (RGs) related to nuclear material measurement and analysis. RG 5.4 covers standard analytical methods for measuring uranium tetrafluoride (UF4) and uranium hexafluoride (UF6), directed at enrichment facility licensees. RG 5.5 addresses standard chemical, mass spectrometric, and spectrochemical analysis methods for nuclear-grade uranium dioxide powders and pellets, intended for fuel fabrication facility licensees. RG 5.9 provides guidelines for germanium spectroscopy systems for special nuclear material measurement but is not applicable to the NuScale design. RG 5.20 pertains to the training, equipping, and qualifying of guards and watchmen, applicable to COL applicants or licensees. RG 5.21 and RG 5.23 address nondestructive uranium-235 enrichment assay by gamma ray spectrometry and in situ assay of plutonium residual holdup, respectively, both applicable to Part 70 facilities. RG 5.22, concerning the assessment of normality using individual observed values, and RG 5.39, covering general methods for uranyl nitrate solution analysis, are not applicable to the NuScale DCA. RG 5.34 details nondestructive assay for plutonium in scrap material by spontaneous fission detection, applicable to Part 70 processing, while RG 5.36 recommends practices for dealing with outlying observations, applicable to special nuclear material licensees.

The passages summarize various regulatory guidance and requirements applicable to facilities handling special nuclear material, primarily under 10 CFR Part 70. Key topics include design considerations for minimizing residual holdup of special nuclear material in drying, fluidized bed, wet, and dry process operations (sections 5.8, 5.25, 5.42), and the selection of material balance areas and item control areas (5.26), all applicable to Part 70 facilities. Additional sections address plant security force duties (5.43), standard formats and content for safeguards contingency plans for fuel cycle facilities (5.55) and transportation (5.56), and physical security plans for the protection of special nuclear material of moderate or low strategic significance (5.59) and for strategic special nuclear material in transit (5.60), with applicability to COL applicants, licensees, or transportation activities as specified. Security officer attentiveness (Bulletin 2007-01) is applicable to holders of operating licenses for nuclear power reactors, while Bulletin 2011-01 on mitigating strategies was addressed to existing licensees. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report references conformance with these regulatory criteria.

The provided passages summarize regulatory guidance and requirements for Combined Operating License (COL) applicants or licensees regarding physical protection, security, and safeguards at nuclear power plants. Key areas covered include the development and implementation of site-specific safeguards contingency plans (5.54), reporting of safeguards events related to special nuclear material (SNM) (5.62), physical protection for transient shipments (5.63), and access authorization programs (5.66). Additional guidance addresses the application of radiological sabotage and theft/diversion design-basis threats in compliance with 10 CFR 73.55, 73.45, and 73.46 (5.69, 5.70), cyber security programs and event notifications (5.71, 5.83), management of the safety/security interface (5.74), training and qualification of security personnel (5.75), physical protection programs (5.76), insider mitigation programs (5.77), and fitness-for-duty requirements for construction sites (5.84). All these responsibilities are assigned to the COL applicant or licensee, with some requirements referenced via Security Technical Reports (e.g., 13.6) and site-specific requirements noted in SECY-14-088 regarding the Force-On-Force Inspection Program. Portions of RG 5.71 related to operational cyber security plans partially conform and are also the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee.

The provided passages outline the applicability of various NRC guidance documents related to bioassay programs, health physics surveys, and occupational radiation exposure. Guidance for acceptable concepts, models, equations, and assumptions for a bioassay program, as well as applications of bioassay for uranium and radioiodine, are designated as not applicable except where specifically required for COL (Combined Operating License) applicants or licensees. Health physics surveys for byproduct material are only applicable to processing and manufacturing plants, while bioassay at uranium mills and health physics surveys in uranium recovery facilities are limited to those specific facility types. Guidance for enriched uranium-235 processing and fuel fabrication applies solely to facilities handling U-235. Instruction concerning occupational radiation risks and criteria for establishing a tritium bioassay program are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee authorized to possess nuclear material. Overall, these documents govern site-specific, programmatic activities, procedures, equipment, and methods, with applicability restricted to the relevant facility or licensee type as specified.

The provided passages summarize the applicability of various Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) documents related to nuclear power plant licensing under 10 CFR Part 52. DC/COL-ISG-4 applies to all Early Site Permit (ESP) and Combined License (COL) applicants seeking limited work authorizations or considering preconstruction activities. DC/COL-ISG-13 guidance on mitigating design features and radioactive source term partially conforms, with site-specific aspects being the responsibility of the COL applicant; exposure scenarios and acceptance criteria are also site-specific and thus the COL applicant’s responsibility. DC/COL-ISG-16 notes that requirements from 10 CFR 50.54(hh)(2) have moved to 10 CFR 50.155(b)(2), and while not applicable to design certification applicants, COL applicants must describe equipment supporting mitigating strategies per 10 CFR 52.80(d). DC/COL-ISG-17 is relevant for reviewing seismic design information in COL applications. DC/COL-ISG-22 addresses the impact of new unit construction on operating units at multi-unit sites and is applicable to COL applicants. DC/COL-ISG-25 pertains to COL licensees with a Changes during Construction license condition under 10 CFR Part 52. DC/COL-ISG-26 covers environmental issues for ESP and COL applications, including those requesting limited work authorization. JLD-ISG-13-01, Draft, on estimating flooding hazards due to dam failure, is site-specific and the responsibility of the COL applicant.

The provided passages summarize several Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) documents relevant to nuclear power plant licensing and decommissioning. NSIR/DPR-ISG-02 and NSIR/DPR-ISG-03 address emergency planning and security exemption requests, respectively, and are applicable to license holders during decommissioning activities. JLD-ISG-12-01, Rev 1, and JLD-ISG-12-03, Rev 1, pertain to compliance with Orders EA-12-049 (mitigation strategies) and EA-12-051 (spent fuel pool instrumentation), and apply to holders and applicants of operating licenses, construction permits, and combined licenses.
