JLD-ISG-12-05 (Draft) and JLD-ISG-12-06 (Draft) provide guidance for integrated flooding assessments and tsunami, surge, or seiche hazard assessments, respectively, in response to the March 2012 50.54(f) request for information letter. JLD-ISG-2015-01, Revision 0, addresses compliance with Phase 2 of Order EA-13-109 regarding reliable hardened containment vents under severe accident conditions and is applicable to BWR licensees with Mark I and Mark II containments. DC/COL-ISG-020 remains the NRC’s current guidance for application of a Seismic Margin Assessment (SMA) to new reactor licensing.

The Containment Vessel (CNV) is an ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Class MC (steel) containment, designed, analyzed, fabricated, inspected, tested, and stamped as an ASME BPVC Class 1 pressure vessel. The CNV is maintained at a vacuum (subatmospheric pressure) during normal operations, eliminating the need for insulation between the reactor vessel (RPV) and the CNV, and minimizing moisture content, corrosion risk, and convective heat transfer. This vacuum also facilitates leakage detection, limits initial oxygen content for combustible gas control, and increases steam condensation rates during accidents. The CNV’s safety functions include containing radioactive material after postulated accidents, providing heat rejection to the reactor pool following Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) actuation, and supporting the RPV. Manways allow access to internal components, and penetrations in the upper head accommodate process piping, electrical power, and instrumentation. The CNV is partially (approximately 90%) immersed in the reactor pool, which acts as a passive heat sink for containment heat removal. In the event of ECCS actuation, steam vented from the RPV causes a temporary spike in containment pressure and temperature, but passive cooling and condensation by the reactor pool rapidly reduce and maintain these parameters below design limits indefinitely. The design ensures that any water lost from the RPV during an accident remains within containment and is passively returned, with no postulated small-break LOCA capable of uncovering nuclear fuel. The low-power core (160 MWt) reduces decay heat removal requirements, enhances in-vessel retention, and simplifies emergency planning. The absence of RPV insulation eliminates potential sump screen blockage and allows vessel exterior cooling during accidents. Passive safety systems ensure cooling and depressurization of the RPV/CNV even with loss of external power, making active safety systems unnecessary. When decay heat and RCS temperature are sufficiently low, the CNV is filled to the pressurizer (PZR) baffle plate by the containment flooding system, and passive cooldown continues as heat is transferred through the RPV and CNV to the reactor pool.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) design features an integral power module comprising a reactor core, two helical-coil steam generators (SGs), and a pressurizer, all contained within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV). This RPV is immediately surrounded by a steel containment vessel (CNV) that is approximately cylindrical and sits in the reactor pool. The reactor core is positioned below the helical-coil SGs inside the RPV. The design eliminates the need for external piping between the steam generators, pressurizer, and RPV, and utilizes natural circulation for reactor coolant system flow, removing the requirement for reactor coolant pumps. For valve actuation testing, a pilot-operated main valve with trip line tubing and a trip valve representative of the NPM design was used to simulate performance and assess potential boric acid buildup. Pressurizer level instrumentation is provided to the control room and the Module Control System (MCS), supporting an exemption from certain power supply requirements per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx). Pressurizer pressure is measured and provides input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS).

During Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) operation, reactor vent valves (RVVs) vent steam and noncondensible gases from the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and pressurizer (PZR) directly into the containment vessel (CNV), where the steam condenses and collects at the bottom. Reactor recirculation valves (RRVs) allow water to reenter the RPV and circulate through the core. As reactor coolant temperature drops below boiling, core cooling continues via conduction into the reactor pool. The ECCS cooling function is entirely passive, relying on heat conduction through the CNV wall to the reactor pool, with further details provided in Section 6.3. Upon stable shutdown, long-term decay heat removal is achieved by conduction and convection through the flooded containment and CNV shell to the reactor pool. When Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure falls below the ECCS valve inadvertent actuation block threshold, the RVVs and RRVs may be opened to promote circulation between the RCS and flooded containment. The pressure-retaining portions of the RCS maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), serving as a barrier to radionuclide release. There are no mechanisms for accumulation of noncondensible gases in the RPV during ECCS operation, as the open RVVs provide a direct vent path to containment, eliminating the need for additional high-point venting and ensuring long-term cooling is not adversely affected. The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) protects operators from accidental releases of toxic or radioactive gases.

The reactor component cooling water system (RCCWS) is a nonsafety-related, closed-loop system that removes heat from components such as the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs), non-regenerative heat exchangers for each chemical and volume control system (CVCS), and primary sampling system coolers, transferring it to the site cooling water system. The reactor pool cooling system and spent fuel pool cooling system are also nonsafety-related, closed-loop systems that transfer heat from their respective pools to the site cooling water system. The circulating water system is an open-loop system supplying continuous cooling water to the plant turbine condensers. The site cooling water system, also open-loop, provides continuous cooling water to the chilled water system, balance of plant component cooling water system, spent fuel pool cooling system, reactor pool cooling system, RCCWS, and condenser air removal system, with pumps drawing water from a common basin. The reactor coolant system (RCS) interfaces with systems including the chemical and volume control system (via the containment system), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves (reactor vent valves [RVVs] and reactor recirculation valves [RRVs]), containment vessel (CNV), control rod drive system, steam generator system (SGS), and the primary sampling system (through the chemical and volume control system).

The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) is a simple system whose operation is not credited during or after an accident. During normal operation, the CVCS recirculates a portion of reactor coolant through demineralizers and filters to maintain coolant cleanliness and chemistry, and supplies pressurizer spray for reactor pressure control. Injection pumps deliver borated or demineralized water into the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), with excess coolant let down to the radioactive waste system. Safety-related protection is provided against unintended RCS dilution due to CVCS equipment failure or operator error. The CVCS is normally connected to the RCS but is isolated following containment isolation actuation. During RCS pressure transients not resulting in isolation, overpressure protection is provided by the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) integral pressurizer (PZR), CVCS design, and relief valves. The CVCS enables chemical addition and removal of chemicals, suspended solids, and impurities via purification systems, and reduces concentrations through dilution with injection flow. It connects to the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) via normally open containment isolation valves (CIVs), with intersystem leakage considered for the boron addition, demineralized water, reactor component cooling water (RCCWS), and process sampling systems. The interface with the RCS is through the steam generators (SGs), which are subject to design, inspection, and testing controls, and primary-to-secondary leakage is monitored per technical specifications. Noncondensible gases collected in degasifiers are processed in the gaseous radioactive waste system. Further design details are provided in Sections 9.3.4, 11.2, and 11.3.

Reactor coolant inventory is managed by injecting additional water when coolant levels are low and letting down excess coolant to the liquid radioactive waste system when inventory is high. The reactor coolant system (RCS) water chemistry is controlled to minimize solid deposits on the reactor core and steam generators, maintain uniform soluble boron concentration for reactivity control during all operating conditions, and ensure adequate chemical and thermal mixing. Steady-state temperatures are maintained to keep the reactor vessel internals (RVI) and reactor pressure vessel (RPV) below design limits. Boric acid is added as a soluble neutron poison and adjusted as needed to compensate for changes in fuel reactivity over the fuel's lifetime. Lithium hydroxide enriched with lithium-7 is introduced via the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) charging flow, and excess lithium is removed using the CVCS delithiating ion exchanger to maintain required pH levels. Lithium hydroxide is selected for its compatibility with boric acid, stainless steel, zirconium alloys, and nickel-base alloys. Hydrogen is injected directly into the CVCS charging flow to suppress oxygen generated by water radiolysis and introduced with makeup water, while hydrazine is added during startup to scavenge dissolved oxygen at low temperatures. Chemical and makeup water quality are controlled to limit contamination, and oxygen, chlorides, and fluorides are controlled to minimize stress corrosion cracking of unstabilized austenitic stainless steels. Hydrogen use inhibits oxygen presence during operation, and gaseous argon may be added at the hydrogen injection tee to support primary-to-secondary leakage controls. The effectiveness of these controls is supported by test and operational experience, and precautions are taken to prevent contaminant intrusion during fabrication, shipping, and storage.

The NPM design incorporates Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves, specifically reactor vent valves (RVVs) on the reactor vessel pressurizer and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs) on the reactor vessel downcomer. A test program was established to demonstrate the functional performance of the ECCS valve system, including unique design features such as the configuration of ECCS valve components and the Integrated Actuation Block (IAB). The program aimed to verify ECCS valve operation at reactor operating pressures and temperatures, as well as in borated reactor coolant. Testing was conducted at the Curtiss-Wright Valve Group Target Rock facility in Farmingdale, New York, using high-pressure and temperature test cells. The test article represented an RRV and included the main valve, trip valve, reset valve, and IAB valve components. The reset valve was used to pressurize and close the main valve before each test, and a representative IAB valve was configured as in the NPM design. Tests included main valve actuation, IAB functionality, and boric acid effects, with the latter using a vessel simulating the main valve control chamber. Additional activities encompassed final piping stress analyses, design and qualification of piping supports, evaluation of subcompartment pressurization effects, completion of the Balance of Plant Pipe Rupture Hazards Analysis, and assessment of pipe whip/jet impingement mitigation devices, including multi-module impacts in common pipe galleries. The high-point degasification vent isolation valves are remotely operated from the control room. ECCS valves, forming part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) during normal operations, are tested and inspected both as part of the RCPB and to ensure their safety-related ECCS functionality, with further details provided in Sections 3.9.6, 6.3.4, and 5.2.4.

The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) utilizes three reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), with the RVVs being larger than the RRVs. Both valve types are pilot operated by remote-mounted trip and reset solenoid valves at the reactor containment boundary, and each main valve includes an inadvertent actuation block (IAB) feature located inside containment. The three RVVs are mounted on the top of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), connecting to the upper head and discharging the pressurizer (PZR) steam space directly to the containment vessel (CNV), thereby venting noncondensible gases during ECCS actuation. The two RRVs connect to the RPV shell just above the main closure flange. The ECCS design, including protection from inadvertent actuation and single failure capability, is detailed in Section 6.3, while the design basis for the RSVs and ECCS valves is provided in Sections 5.2.2 and 6.3. The NuScale design does not require separate safety-related high-point venting in the reactor coolant system (RCS), as the RVVs fulfill this function during and after a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA), ensuring noncondensible gases do not accumulate in the pressurizer space and core cooling is maintained. Mechanical failure of the reactor safety valve (RSV) is bounded by the analysis of inadvertent ECCS valve actuation in Section 15.6.6.

The provided passages summarize the applicability and conformance of various Regulatory Guides (RGs) and standards related to the installation, inspection, testing, and environmental qualification (EQ) of mechanical and electrical equipment in nuclear facilities. Safety Guide 30 (RG 1.30) endorses IEEE Std. 336-1971 for the installation, inspection, and testing of instrumentation and electric equipment. For environmental qualification, RG 1.63 (endorsing IEEE 317-1983 for electrical penetration assemblies) is applicable and conforms, while RG 1.89 (EQ of electrical equipment important to safety), RG 1.97 (EQ of post-accident monitoring equipment), and RG 1.180 (electromagnetic and radio-frequency interference in safety-related I&C equipment) only partially conform, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2. RG 1.153 (EQ of power, instrumentation, and control portions of safety systems) fully conforms. RG 1.158 (EQ of Class 1E lead storage batteries) and RG 1.204 (EQ of lightning protection systems) are not applicable, with the latter excluded because lightning protection is associated with external/natural events and not considered part of EQ.

Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.100 addresses the seismic qualification of electrical and active mechanical equipment, as well as the functional qualification of active mechanical equipment for nuclear power plants. The guidance is partially conformed to, with exceptions for cases where site-specific spectra exceed certified design spectra (e.g., Position C1.2.1.g) and for qualification of new and replacement equipment in older unresolved safety issue A46 plants (e.g., Position C.1.2.2.j). The guidance is not applicable to electrical equipment, and site-specific guidance is the responsibility of the COL applicant. RG 1.100 endorses ASME QME-1 2007, and NuScale complies with the non-mandatory Appendix QR-B, except for QR-B5200(g) regarding material activation energy. Environmental qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment partially conforms to RG 1.183 and RG 1.100, as referenced in DSRS 3.11. Relevant standards cited include IEEE Standard 344-2004 for seismic qualification of Class 1E equipment and IEEE Standard 497-2002 for accident monitoring instrumentation.

The passages summarize compliance with DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0, "Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical Equipment," across several criteria. The application conforms to requirements for clarification related to IEEE Std. 323, suitability of materials, parts, and equipment essential to safety-related functions, qualification of nonmetallic parts, design/purchase specifications for equipment performance under applicable environmental conditions, applicable documentation for environmental design and qualification of safety-related mechanical, electrical, and I&C equipment, and exposure of organic components on engineered safety features systems. No exceptions or additional clarifications are noted for these items. However, maintenance/surveillance programs to assure environmental design and qualification status of equipment in mild and harsh environments (II.22) and operational program implementation (II.23) are marked as not applicable, as these are the responsibility of the COL applicant.

The passages summarize compliance with DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0, "Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical Equipment," referencing specific regulatory guides (RGs) for various equipment types. The application of RG 1.73 addresses the environmental design and qualification of Class 1E electric valve operators; RG 1.152 covers computer-specific environmental design and qualification requirements; RG 1.211 pertains to the environmental qualification of Class 1E electric cables and field splices; and RG 1.156 applies to the environmental qualification of Class 1E connection assemblies. In all cases, the application conforms to the referenced RGs, with no exceptions noted.

The provided passages discuss the applicability of various Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and regulatory guidance to reactor designs that do not rely on forced reactor coolant flow, such as those using natural circulation. For these designs, the potential for loose parts entering or being generated in the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) is minimized due to low fluid velocities and limited small lines entering the RPV, as further justified in FSAR Section 4.4. SRP sections 15.3.1-15.3.4, which address loss of forced reactor coolant flow, pump rotor seizure, and shaft break, are only applicable to light-water reactor (LWR) designs with forced coolant flow and are not relevant to natural circulation systems. Similarly, SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5 guidance on startup of inactive or recirculation loops at incorrect temperatures and flow controller malfunctions is not applicable, as it specifically pertains to pressurized water reactor (PWR) designs with forced flow, coolant loops, and pumps. Additionally, mid-loop operation requirements for reliable residual heat removal (RHR) systems and considerations for station blackout (SBO) mitigation are not applicable, as the design in question does not use external loops or experience drain down conditions during refueling.

The referenced regulatory and technical guidance documents are largely not applicable to the NuScale plant design due to significant differences from conventional PWR systems. NuScale does not use the Constant Axial Offset Control (CAOC) operating scheme (BTP 4-1, Rev 3), nor does it employ a steam generator (SG) blowdown system (SRP 10.4.8, Rev 3), as its design places primary water outside and secondary water inside the SG tubes, resulting in different secondary chemistry requirements from those in the referenced EPRI report (SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3). The NuScale design also does not utilize top-feed, preheat, or once-through steam generator configurations, making the associated water hammer avoidance guidelines (BTP 10-2, Rev 4) inapplicable. Flow Accelerated Corrosion considerations are addressed and conform to requirements (DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0). For once-through steam generator designs, only the test and test procedure acceptance criterion (OTSGD B.2) is noted as conforming, while other criteria are not applicable to NuScale’s design.

The provided passages indicate that several Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and their acceptance criteria—specifically SRP 5.4.6 (Reactor Core Isolation Cooling System), SRP 5.4.8 (Reactor Water Cleanup System), SRP 5.4.13 (Isolation Condenser System), SRP 6.2.1.1.C (Pressure Suppression Type BWR Containments), SRP 6.7 (Main Steam Isolation Valve Leakage Control System), and SRP 9.3.5 (Standby Liquid Control System)—are not applicable as they pertain exclusively to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs). For SRP 5.2.2, Rev 3 (Overpressure Protection), the design for Pressurized Water Reactors (PWRs) operating at power only partially conforms because the overpressure analysis does not assume a secondary safety-grade signal from the Reactor Protection System (RPS) to initiate reactor trip, and the NuScale design lacks a secondary safety-grade reactor trip system. Additionally, Leak-Before-Break (LBB) is applied to the Main Steam (MS) and Feedwater (FW) lines inside containment, and the seismic classification approach for main steam lines is noted as not applicable since it pertains only to BWRs.

The NuScale Site Cooling Water System (SCWS), as referenced in SRP 9.2.1, Rev 5, is not safety-related, does not provide essential cooling to safety-related structures, systems, and components (SSC), and is not considered risk-significant. It partially conforms to GDC 4 (Environmental and Dynamic Effects) and fully conforms to GDC 5 (Sharing of Structures, Systems, and Components), as it does not serve a safety-related function. The SCWS is not applicable to GDC 44 (Cooling Water System) or GDC 46 (Cooling Water System Testing), since it does not perform safety-related cooling or accident mitigation functions. Similarly, the Condensate and Feedwater System (CFWS), as described in DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0, is not used to transfer heat to an ultimate heat sink and is therefore not applicable to GDC 44 (Heat Removal Capability), GDC 45 (Inspection), or GDC 46 (Testing).

The NuScale reactor component cooling water (RCCW) system partially conforms to SRP 9.2.2, Rev 4, Acceptance Criteria II.1 (Protection Against Natural Phenomena) and II.2 (Environmental and Dynamic Effects), with additional details on environmental and dynamic effects referenced in Sections 3.5 and 3.6. Criteria II.3 (Sharing of Structures, Systems, and Components), II.4 (Cooling Water System), II.5 (Cooling Water System Inspection), and II.6 (Cooling Water System Testing) are not applicable, as noted in the comments for II.1. For SRP 9.2.5, Rev 3 (Ultimate Heat Sink), Acceptance Criterion II.3 (Cooling Water System) partially conforms, except for aspects related to fiberglass piping per RG 1.72, which is not used in the NuScale design. Criteria II.4 (Cooling Water System Inspection) and II.5 (Cooling Water System Testing) fully conform. Under SRP 10.3.6, Rev 3 (Steam and Feedwater System Materials), Criterion II.1 (Materials Selection and Fabrication of Class 2 and 3 Components) is not applicable, as the NuScale design does not include Class 2 or 3 components.

The NuScale Chilled Water System (CHWS), as described in SRP 9.2.7, Rev 0, does not perform safety or containment isolation functions and is classified as a nonsafety system; therefore, quality standards and records, as well as requirements for cooling water system inspection and testing, are not applicable. The CHWS is not classified as Seismic Category I but conforms to protection against natural phenomena per RG 1.29, with structures, systems, and components (SSC) whose failure could adversely affect Seismic Category I SSC designed as Seismic Category II. The system conforms to environmental and dynamic effects criteria, with no additional requirements specified. For minimization of contamination, the CHWS operates at a higher pressure than the Liquid Radwaste System (LRWS) and Gaseous Radwaste System (GRWS) at their interfaces, preventing the introduction of radioactive contaminants into the CHWS.

The reviewed sections address compliance with Standard Review Plan (SRP) requirements for various turbine and water systems. For SRP 10.4.3, Rev 3 (Turbine Gland Seal), the system conforms to GDC 60 by preventing excessive releases of radioactivity to the environment. SRP 10.4.4, Rev 3 (Turbine Bypass System) meets GDC 4 requirements for piping failures, with no exceptions noted. The MSIV Alternate Leakage Path requirement under SRP 10.4.4, II.3 is not applicable as it pertains only to BWRs. SRP 10.4.5, Rev 3 (Circulating Water System) conforms to GDC 4 by addressing flooding of structures, systems, and components (SSC) important to safety. Finally, SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3 (Condensate Cleanup System) requirements for maintaining BWR plant water quality to prevent corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14) are not applicable, as this applies only to BWRs. No exceptions or additional comments are noted for the applicable sections.

SRP Section 15.1.5.A, Rev 2, which addresses the radiological consequences of main steam line failures outside containment in PWRs, is partially superseded for design certification applications by SRP Section 15.0.3, which takes precedence regarding radiological analyses, assumptions, acceptance criteria, and methodologies. Specifically, the acceptance criteria and methodologies in SRP 15.1.5.A and its Appendix A are overridden by those in SRP 15.0.3. For SRP 15.6.1, Rev 2, concerning inadvertent opening of a PWR pressurizer relief valve or a BWR pressure relief valve, the guidance applies only to LWRs with power-operated pressurizer relief valves. SRP 15.6.4, Rev 2, and SRP 15.6.5.D, Rev 1, which address radiological consequences of main steam line failure outside containment and main steam isolation valve leakage control system leakage, respectively, are applicable only to BWRs and not to PWRs. SRP 19.4, Rev 0, regarding strategies for loss-of-large areas of the plant due to explosions and fires, is partially applicable except for acceptance criterion II.17 (BWR-specific containment venting and vessel flooding) and II.20 (spent fuel pool mitigative measures), which are not applicable.

The guidance for these Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs) is provided in DSRS 15.4.6, which addresses the Inadvertent Decrease in Boron Concentration in the Reactor Coolant System for Pressurized Water Reactors (PWR).

The review of SRP 15.4.6, Rev 2, addressing an inadvertent decrease in boron concentration in the Reactor Coolant System (PWR), indicates conformance for the following criteria: Reactor Coolant and Main Steam System Pressures (II.1), Fuel Cladding Integrity (II.2)—noting that Critical Heat Flux (CHF) is the more appropriate terminology for the NuScale phenomenon—Incidents of Moderate Frequency (II.3), and Analysis Model, Methods, and Assumptions (II.5), with no additional comments provided for these sections. For Minimum Time Intervals for Required Operator Actions (II.4), the criterion is not applicable, as operator action is not required to mitigate an inadvertent boron dilution event.

The safety analysis for SRP 15.4.7, Rev 2 (Inadvertent Loading and Operation of a Fuel Assembly in an Improper Position) demonstrates no fuel failures and conforms regarding offsite radiological consequences. For SRP 15.4.8.A, Rev 1 (Radiological Consequences of a Control Rod Ejection Accident in PWRs), the guidance on calculating radiological consequences is applicable to the DCA and partially conforms, but the dose model acceptance criteria and assumptions are not applicable, as they are superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3. Similarly, for SRP 15.6.3, Rev 2 (Radiological Consequences of Steam Generator Tube Failure), the methodology and assumptions for radiological analysis are not applicable due to supersession by SRP Section 15.0.3. The technical content of SRP 15.7.3, Rev 2 (Radioactive Release from a Subsystem or Component) has been relocated to Branch Technical Position 11-6, referenced in Section 11.2, and only partially conforms. For SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1 (Radiological Consequences of Fuel Handling Accidents), both the acceptability of site and dose mitigating ESF systems (II.1) and the dose model and modeling assumptions (II.3) are not applicable, as their acceptance criteria and methodology are superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3.

The review of SRP 15.4.8, Rev 3, addressing the spectrum of rod ejection accidents in pressurized water reactors (PWRs), confirms conformance with requirements for monitoring instrumentation (II.1), effects of postulated reactivity accidents (II.2), and radiation dose limits (II.3), with no exceptions noted. For SRP 15.4.8.A, Rev 1, concerning the radiological consequences of a control rod ejection accident in PWRs, the review partially conforms; per SRP Section 15.0.3, Section I, Areas of Review, Item 10 under Review Interfaces, SRP Section 15.0.3 supersedes the radiological analyses, assumptions, acceptance criteria, and methodologies in SRP Section 15.4.8, Appendix A for design certification applications. SRP 15.4.9, Rev 3, and SRP 15.4.9.A, Draft Rev 3, which address rod drop accidents and their radiological consequences in boiling water reactors (BWRs), are not applicable, as their acceptance criteria pertain only to BWRs.

The provided passages summarize the applicability of various Standard Review Plan (SRP) and Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) sections regarding radiological consequence analyses for different postulated accidents in nuclear power plants. For SRP 15.6.2 (failure of small lines carrying primary coolant outside containment), the guidance on technical specifications for primary coolant system iodine activity is applicable to the Design Certification Application (DCA). For SRP 15.6.3 (steam generator tube failure in PWRs), the guidance on calculating radiological consequences of a postulated steam generator tube failure is applicable to the DCA. DSRS 15.6.5 (loss-of-coolant accidents from piping breaks within the reactor coolant pressure boundary) conforms to SRP 15.0.3, which supersedes previous radiological analyses, assumptions, acceptance criteria, and methodologies in SRP 15.6.5 for design certification reviews. SRP 15.6.5.A (radiological consequences of a design basis LOCA including containment leakage) is partially applicable to the DCA for both the calculation of radiological consequences of a hypothetical LOCA and the model for post-LOCA containment leakage contribution. SRP 15.6.5.B (radiological consequences of a design basis LOCA due to leakage from engineered safety feature components outside containment) is fully applicable regarding ESF system leakage assumptions, and partially applicable for combining radiological consequences from ESF component leakage with other fission product release paths. All technical details and guidance applicability are preserved as specified in the original passages.

The parameters to be verified include the limiting turbine missile spectrum (rotor and blade material properties), turbine rotor design, geometry and number of blades, final design of the reactor and control building exterior walls and grade-level slab, and the location of turbines relative to the reactor and control buildings. However, per SRP 10.2, Rev 3, there are no safety-related SSCs (Structures, Systems, and Components) in the Turbine Building. According to DSRS 10.2.3, Rev 0, Sections II.1 through II.5, and DSRS 3.5.1.3, Section I.1, for plants that use barriers to protect essential SSCs as specified in RG 1.115, it is not necessary to rely on turbine missile generation probabilities or turbine rotor integrity (including materials selection, fracture toughness, pre-service inspection, turbine rotor design, and inservice inspection).

The NuScale nuclear power plant design conforms to regulatory guidance for design-basis tornado and hurricane wind speeds and missile spectra, using bounding Region 1 characteristics per RG 1.76 for tornadoes and the highest wind speed postulated in RG 1.221 for hurricanes, with confirmation of site-specific characteristics delegated to the COL applicant or licensee. For tornado loads, bounding parameters are established, and acceptance criteria for tornado parameters and missile spectra conform to SRP 3.3.2, while demonstration that failure of non-tornado-designed structures will not affect safety-related SSCs also conforms. The design basis tornado-generated missile spectrum conforms to RG 1.221, which is also applied for hurricane-generated missiles. Implementation of RG 1.221 requirements for hurricane wind speed and missile spectra is consistent with DC/COL-ISG-24 guidance, with specific design requirements detailed in SRP 3.3.2 and 3.5.1.4. The FSAR uses a maximum tornado wind speed of 230 mph per RG 1.76 Revision 1, partially conforming to guidance by not using the outdated 300 mph value from SECY-93-087. Internal flood protection and seismic design/classification requirements also conform to SRP 3.4.1.

The passages summarize compliance with Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections related to missile protection in nuclear facilities. For internally-generated missiles outside containment (SRP 3.5.1.1, Rev. 3), both the statistical significance of identified missiles by probability analysis and acceptable methods of missile protection are addressed and found to conform, with no exceptions noted. Similarly, for internally-generated missiles inside containment (SRP 3.5.1.2, Rev. 3), the statistical significance analysis and protection methods also conform. For missiles generated by extreme winds, the statistical significance analysis conforms. Table 2.0-1 (SRP 3.5.2, Rev. 3) confirms that structures, systems, and components (SSC) are capable of withstanding the effects of externally-generated missiles, with no exceptions identified.

For turbine missile considerations, the probability of unacceptable damage, turbine missile generation, acceptably low missile generation probability, missile generation probability tables from turbine manufacturers (including Table 3.5.1.3-1), and inservice inspection and test programs for applicants obtaining turbines from manufacturers without NRC-approved procedures for calculating missile generation probabilities are all deemed not applicable. According to DSRS 10.2.3, Section I and DSRS 3.5.1.3, Section I.1, plants that utilize barriers to protect essential SSCs as specified in RG 1.115 are not required to rely on turbine missile generation probabilities, including turbine rotor integrity.

The NuScale plant addresses turbine missile hazards by employing protective barriers to safeguard safety-related structures, systems, and components (SSCs). For concrete barriers, NuScale departs from the Standard Review Plan (SRP) Section II.1.A by using finite element analysis to predict penetration distance of turbine missiles, instead of the modified National Defense Research Council (NDRC) formula; in some cases, this analysis predicts perforation and scabbing. For steel barriers, NuScale conforms to SRP 3.5.3, Rev.3, Section II.1.B procedures. Despite these considerations, the physical separation of redundant safety-related equipment ensures that no turbine missile can prevent essential systems from performing their safety functions. The design does not rely on turbine overspeed protection systems (SRP 10.2, Rev.3), but rather on physical barriers for missile protection, in accordance with General Design Criterion (GDC) 4.

The NuScale design assumes no proximity missiles (except aircraft) and no aircraft hazard missiles, rendering compliance with 10 CFR 100 and GDC 4 not applicable for SRP 3.5.1.5, Rev 4, and SRP 3.5.1.6, Rev 4, Sections II.1 and II.2. For SRP 3.5.1.6, Rev 4, Sections III.8.B.1 through III.8.B.4, the design conforms to requirements, with site parameters and their basis included as Tier 1 information and summarized in Table 2.0-1, all under the assumption of no aircraft hazard missiles. Regarding emergency planning (SRP 13.3, Rev 3, II.26), the NuScale design provides safety parameter displays in the technical support center, while the emergency operations facility is the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the NuScale design certification.

The inservice inspection program for Class 2 and 3 components encompasses pre-service examinations and periodic inservice inspections and tests to ensure that safety-related and risk-significant systems, structures, and components can perform their intended safety functions. Detailed information on this program is provided in Section 6.6, which references DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, and notes that the operational program and implementation milestones are the responsibility of the COL applicant. Section 3.9.6 outlines the Inservice Test Program and its compliance with 10 CFR 50.55a(f)(3)(iii)(B) and 10 CFR 50.55a(f)(3)(iv)(B). Additionally, operational inservice testing and inspection programs, as described in Sections 5.2.4 and 5.4.1.6, establish the testing and inspection requirements following initial plant startup, including the Steam Generator (SG) program.

The review of DSRS 5.2.4, Rev 0, "Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing," indicates that the system boundary subject to inspection (II.1) and accessibility (II.2) both conform to requirements, with no exceptions noted. Augmented inservice inspection (ISI) to protect against postulated piping failures (II.10) also conforms. For other inspection programs (II.11), partial conformance is noted, as a boric acid control program has not been established, though a brief description is provided in the Design Certification Application (DCA). Operational programs (II.12) and the risk-informed ISI program (II.14) are not applicable, as these acceptance criteria pertain to plant-specific programmatic information that is the responsibility of the Combined License (COL) applicant. Inspection, Test, Analysis, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC) (II.13) partially conforms, with a portion of the acceptance criterion applicable only to COL applicants.

The passages indicate that the applicant’s program for Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing (DSRS 5.2.4, Rev 0) conforms to regulatory requirements for examination categories and methods (II.3), inspection intervals (II.4), evaluation of examination results (II.5), code exemptions (II.7), relief requests (II.8), and code cases (II.9), with no exceptions noted. For Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection (DSRS 5.2.5, Rev 0), the criteria to meet GDC 14 (II.2) and GDC 30 (II.3) are also met without exceptions. Regarding Reactor Vessel Integrity (DSRS 5.3.3, Rev 0), the operating conditions (II.6) and inservice surveillance (II.7) conform, with inservice surveillance described in the DCD. Additionally, direct indication of relief and safety valve position in the control room, as required by 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi) (II.D.3), is provided, and consideration of ATWS conditions in the relief and safety valve test program is deemed unnecessary for ensuring acceptable performance.

The passages indicate that the steam generator materials and design conform to DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements, specifically regarding steam generator design (II.2), fabrication and processing of ferritic materials (II.3), compatibility of materials with both primary (reactor) and secondary coolant systems and cleanliness control (II.5), and provisions for accessing the secondary side of the steam generator (II.6). Additionally, the steam generator program conforms to DSRS 5.4.2.2, Rev 0, with respect to steam generator tube susceptibility to degradation (II.1). No exceptions or deviations are noted in any of these areas.

The review of DSRS 5.4.2.2, Rev 0: Steam Generator Program, indicates varying levels of conformance across its elements. The Steam Generator Monitoring Program Elements (II.2) and ITAAC (II.9) only partially conform, as portions of these acceptance criteria are applicable specifically to COL applicants referencing a certified design. The Steam Generator Program Elements in Technical Specifications (II.3) also partially conform, since certain subtier guidance documents referenced are only partially applicable. The Steam Generator Tube Repair Criteria (II.4) and Steam Generator Tube Preservice Inspection (II.6) fully conform with no exceptions noted. Periodic Tube Inspection and Testing in Certified Design Technical Specifications (II.7) and Operational Programs (II.8) partially conform, with the latter criterion governing plant-specific programmatic activities that are the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing a certified design.

The design requirements of the Residual Heat Removal System, as specified in DSRS BTP 5-4, Rev 0, are addressed as follows: Pressure Relief Requirements (B.2) and Implementation (B.5) both conform with no exceptions noted. Operational Procedures (B.4) only partially conform, as these procedures are site-specific and fall under the responsibility of the COL applicant. Additionally, for SRP 6.1.1, Rev 2, Engineered Safety Features Materials, the Materials and Fabrication criterion (II.1) conforms with no exceptions.

The review of DSRS 6.2.1.4, Rev 0, concerning the Mass and Energy Release Analysis for Postulated Secondary System Pipe Ruptures, confirms conformance in the areas of sources of energy (II.1), mass and energy release rate (II.2), and single-failure analyses (II.3). No exceptions or additional comments were noted for any of these sections.

The passages summarize compliance with DSRS 6.6, Rev 0 requirements for Inservice Inspection and Testing of Class 2 and 3 Components. The following sections are addressed: II.2 Accessibility, II.3 Examination Categories and Methods, II.4 Inspection Intervals, II.5 Evaluation of Examination Results, II.6 System Pressure Tests, II.7 Structural Supports, II.8 Augmented ISI to Protect Against Postulated Piping Failures, II.10 Relief Requests, and II.11 Code Cases. For each section, the documentation states "Conforms" with no exceptions or additional comments. Section II.13, Risk Informed ISI Program, is marked as "Not Applicable." No deviations or non-conformances are noted throughout the reviewed sections.

Section 1.9 of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) Revision 5 provides a tabular guide to conformance with regulatory criteria, assessing compliance with regulations in effect six months prior to the anticipated docket date. Table 1.9-1 defines the conformance status legend, where "Conforms" indicates full compliance with applicable regulations or guidance. Table 1.9-3 details conformance with NUREG-0800 (Standard Review Plan, SRP) and the Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS). For SRP 1.0, Rev 2 (Introduction and Interfaces), there are no specific acceptance criteria. SRP 3.5.3, Rev 3 (Barrier Design Procedures) is marked "Not Applicable" since NuScale does not use composite or multi-element barriers. DSRS 6.6, Rev 0 (Inservice Inspection and Testing of Class 2 and 3 Components) and DSRS 7.1.1, Rev 0 (Fundamental Design Principles) both "Conform" with no exceptions. DSRS 7.2.1, Rev 0 (Quality) "Partially Conforms" to RG 1.172, with further details in Table 1.9-2.7.2.1. DSRS 7.2.14, Rev 0 (Human Factors Considerations) "Conforms," using provided guidance for review, despite no specific acceptance criteria. DSRS 9.5.2, Rev 0 (Communication Systems) "Conforms" with General Design Criterion (GDC) 2, with no exceptions noted.

NuScale’s Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance with the Standard Review Plan (SRP), including review of Branch Technical Positions and referenced guidance. Operational experience insights, including those from NRC Bulletins and Generic Letters not incorporated into the most recent SRP within six months prior to the application docket date, are integrated into the design unless otherwise specified. For SRP Section 1.33 (Quality Assurance Program Requirements for Operation), the guidance is not applicable and is the responsibility of the Combined License (COL) applicant or licensee. NuScale conforms to SRP Section 1.34 (Control of Electroslag Weld Properties). Emergency planning requirements under SRP 13.3, including alternatives to NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1, ITAAC associated with the COL application, and generic emergency planning ITAAC, are not applicable and are the responsibility of the COL applicant. Similarly, SRP 13.5.2.1 (Operating and Emergency Operating Procedures) is not applicable to NuScale and is assigned to the COL applicant. SRP 17.3 (Quality Assurance Program Description) guidance applies only to existing NRC-approved QA programs and is not applicable to NuScale. NuScale conforms to SRP 17.5 (Quality Assurance Program Description for Design Certification, Early Site Permit, and New COL applicants) requirements for Procurement Document Control (II.D), Test Control (II.K), and Audits (II.R), with no exceptions noted.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, addresses conformance with various seismic-related regulatory criteria. For nuclear power plant instrumentation for earthquakes (RG 1.12), the report partially conforms, noting that specific equipment selection is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee, and seismic detectors cannot be installed inside containment, so they are placed in the Reactor Building (RXB) as indicated in Section 3.7.3. The analysis procedure for damping (DSRS 3.7.2, Rev 0) fully conforms with no exceptions. Regarding seismic instrumentation (SRP 3.7.4, Rev 2), the report partially conforms, with a COL item required for full compliance; while locations are identified per RG 1.12, seismic instrumentation cannot be placed inside containment. For other Seismic Category I steel structures (DSRS 3.8.4, Rev 0), the report fully conforms. The report references Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) on seismic issues of high frequency ground motion, directing to guidance in Sections 2, 3, 4, and 5. The draft JLD-ISG-12-04 on performing a seismic margin assessment in response to the March 2012 50.54(f) request for information is deemed not applicable.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) Tier 2, Revision 5, provides conformance status with various regulatory criteria. For Section 1.21 on measuring, evaluating, and reporting radioactive material in liquid and gaseous effluents and solid waste, NuScale partially conforms, with site-specific, programmatic, and operational aspects delegated to the COL applicant or licensee. Guidance related to environmental reports for near-surface disposal of radioactive waste (Section 4.18), direct-reading pocket dosimeters (Section 8.4), audible-alarm dosimeters (Section 8.28), operational programs for solid waste management systems (DSRS 11.4, II.16), process and effluent radiological monitoring instrumentation and sampling systems (DSRS 11.5, II.6), assurance of complete stabilization or dewatering for solid radioactive waste management systems (BTP 11-3, B.2), postulated radioactive releases due to liquid-containing tank failures (BTP 11-6, B.1), and operational considerations for assuring occupational radiation exposures are ALARA (SRP 12.1, II.3) are all marked as not applicable, as these are site-specific or programmatic responsibilities of the COL applicant. For DSRS 11.4, II.10, regarding features to minimize contamination, facilitate decommissioning, and minimize radwaste generation, NuScale partially conforms except for site-specific aspects, which are also the responsibility of the COL applicant. The guidance for near-surface disposal of radioactive waste is not applicable to the NuScale certified design.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents partial or non-conformance with several regulatory criteria. For metal primary reactor containment system components, NuScale partially conforms, except for 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(v), which is not required for design certification applicants per 10 CFR 50.34(f) and 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8). Welder qualification guidance is partially met, with site-specific weld fabrication and repair standards delegated to the COL applicant or licensee. Guidance for construction of Class 1 components in elevated-temperature reactors is not applicable, as it pertains to reactor types not relevant to NuScale. Safety-related concrete structures guidance is partially conformed to, with the intent applicable but the language endorsing ACI 349-1997 with exceptions. Guidelines for categorizing structures, systems, and components (SSC) by safety significance are partially met, as 10 CFR 50.69 allows a risk-informed process to remove low safety significance SSC from special treatment requirements. Reactor vessel integrity requirements under 10 CFR 52.47(b)(1) are not applicable, being the responsibility of the COL applicant. For fission product control systems and structures, partial conformance is noted, as some criteria apply only to LWR designs with containment fission product clean-up systems. Ice condenser fission product cleanup system requirements are not applicable, as they pertain only to designs with ice condenser containments. Regarding TMI requirements (10 CFR 50.34(f)), NuScale partially conforms to the requirement for a plant/site-specific probabilistic risk assessment, with design certification addressing core and containment heat removal system reliability and updates required by the COL applicant for site-specific conditions.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, Tier 2, provides conformance status with various regulatory guides (RGs) and standards. For RG 1.101 (Emergency Response Planning), the guidance is not applicable as it pertains to COL applicants for co-located sites. RG 1.110 (Cost-Benefit Analysis for Radwaste Systems) and RG 1.118 (Periodic Testing of Electric Power and Protection Systems) are partially conformed to, with site-specific analyses and guidance being the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. RG 1.189 (Fire Protection for Nuclear Power Plants) is partially conformed to, except for requirements specific to other reactor designs or site-specific fire protection, which are also the COL applicant’s responsibility. RG 1.205 (Risk-Informed, Performance-Based Fire Protection) is not applicable, as it applies to licensees developing such programs under 10 CFR 50.48(c). Supplement 1 to RG 4.2 (Environmental Reports for License Renewal) and RG 5.51 (Management Review of Nuclear Material Control and Accounting Systems) are not applicable, as they pertain to license renewal and fuel cycle facilities, respectively. SRP 9.5.1.1 (Fire Protection Program for New Reactor COL Applications) is partially conformed to, with acceptance criteria applying to COL applicants under 10 CFR 52. Guidance from DC/COL-ISG-20 (PRA-Based Seismic Margin Analysis) is not applicable, as it has been incorporated into SRP 19.0, Rev 3. DC/COL-ISG-27 (Environmental Guidance for Light Water SMRs) is also not applicable, as it is relevant to ESP, LWA, OL, CP, and COL applications for light water SMR technologies.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, Tier 2, demonstrates conformance with various regulatory criteria across multiple sections. Section 1.130 confirms compliance with service limits and loading combinations for Class 1 plate-and-shell-type supports. Section 1.9-56 aligns with SRP 3.3.2, Rev. 3, regarding procedures for transforming tornado parameters into equivalent loads on structures. Section 1.9-58 meets SRP 3.5.1.4, Rev. 4, for identifying appropriate design basis missiles generated by extreme winds. Section 1.9-65 conforms to SRP 3.9.1, Rev. 3, for specifying Service Level D limits for Code Class 1 and core support components. Section 1.9-66 complies with SRP 3.9.3, Rev. 3, covering loading combinations, system operating transients, and stress limits for ASME Code Class 1, 2, and 3 components and supports. Section 1.9-68 partially conforms to SRP 3.9.6, Rev. 3, meeting requirements for the inservice testing program for pumps except for aspects related to safety-related pumps. Section 1.9-90 conforms to BTP 5-3, Rev. 2, fracture toughness reporting requirements. No exceptions or comments were noted except for the partial conformance in Section 1.9-68.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance with various regulatory criteria for safety-related systems. For NRC RG 1.180, addressing electromagnetic and radio-frequency interference (EMI/RFI) in safety-related instrumentation and control (I&C) systems, NuScale partially conforms, applying relevant design guidance for SSCs. Regarding DSRS 3.11 (Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical Equipment), NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.89 for the environmental qualification program per 10 CFR 50.49, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2. Full conformance is achieved for RG 1.209, covering environmental design and qualification of safety-related computer-based I&C systems in mild environments, and for requirements on the effects of environmental conditions for all important-to-safety equipment. The requirement for design and procurement specifications (DSRS 3.11, II.25) is not applicable, as it is designated for the COL applicant. For setpoints (DSRS 7.2.7), NuScale’s methodology conforms to ISA-67.04.01-2006, addressing NRC RIS 2006-17. Additionally, the Instrument Air Supply (IAS) system conforms to Generic Letter 88-14, providing both instrument and service air to safety-related equipment.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance with various regulatory criteria across multiple sections. Guidance related to radiation exposures at medical institutions (Section 8.18) and the release of patients administered radioactive materials (Section 8.39) is marked as not applicable, as these pertain only to medical or radio-pharmaceutical facilities. The report confirms conformance with design parameters for expected radionuclide distributions and concentrations in the Solid Waste Management System (DSRS 11.4, II.1), and with requirements to ensure representative samples and provide additional operating experience information for process, effluent, area radiation, and airborne radioactivity monitoring (DSRS 11.6, II.5 and II.10). For radiation protection design features (DSRS 12.3-12.4), conformance is noted with ANSI/HPS N13.1-2011 (II.14), while RG 1.52 (II.2) is not applicable and RG 1.12 (II.25) is only partially conformed to, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2. The report also confirms conformance with requirements for offsite radiological consequences of postulated design basis accidents (DSRS 15.0.3, II.1) and for evaluating potential pathways for radioactivity and radiation that could affect control room habitability under accident conditions (50.34(f)(2)(xxviii)). No exceptions or additional comments are noted unless specified.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance with various regulatory criteria as outlined in the Standard Review Plan (SRP). For SRP 2.1.3 (Population Distribution), population data, exclusion area, low-population zone, nearest population center boundary, and population density are noted as not applicable and site-specific. For SRP 2.3.4 (Short-Term Atmospheric Dispersion Estimates for Accident Releases), SRP 2.3.5 (Long-Term Atmospheric Dispersion Estimates for Routine Releases), SRP 2.4.3 (Probable Maximum Flood on Streams and Rivers), SRP 2.4.6 (Probable Maximum Tsunami Hazards), SRP 2.4.10 (Flooding Protection Requirements), SRP 2.4.13 (Accidental Releases of Radioactive Liquid Effluents in Ground and Surface Waters), SRP 2.5.2 (Vibratory Ground Motion), and SRP 2.5.5 (Stability of Slopes), the report states that the basis for site parameters is included, conformance is achieved, and no exceptions or additional notes are provided. SRP 2.4.7 (Ice Effects) is marked as not applicable and site-specific.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance with various regulatory criteria. For SRP 3.6.2 (Rev 2), regarding determination of rupture locations and dynamic effects of postulated piping ruptures inside containment, NuScale fully conforms. Similarly, SRP 3.6.3 (Rev 1) on leak-before-break evaluation procedures and DSRS 9.3.4 (Rev 0) concerning Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) components, quality, and seismic classification also show full conformance, with the CVCS located outside the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). DSRS 11.1 (Rev 0) on coolant source terms and DSRS 15.6.5 (Rev 0) on loss-of-coolant accidents from postulated piping breaks within the RCPB are also fully met. However, partial conformance is noted for BTP 6-1 (March 2007) regarding spray water pH and water chemistry for fission product removal, as some acceptance criteria reference structures, systems, and components (SSC) not present in the NuScale design. DSRS 15.0 (Rev 0) on transient and accident analyses is partially met, with exceptions for BWR-specific aspects. For 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(x), which requires a test program for qualifying reactor coolant system relief and safety valves and, for PWRs, PORV block valves, NuScale partially conforms, excluding PORV block valve testing and ATWS condition considerations.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, demonstrates conformance with several regulatory criteria: it fully conforms to SRP 3.9.5, Rev 3 regarding the design criteria, loading conditions, and analyses for reactor pressure vessel internals (excluding core support structures); SRP 4.5.2, Rev 3 for nondestructive examination of reactor internal and core support structure materials; SRP 5.2.2, Rev 3 for overpressure protection, including TMI Action Plan requirements; and DSRS 5.3.2, Rev 0 for upper-shelf energy, pressure-temperature limits, and pressurized thermal shock, specifically the applicable regulations, codes, and basis documents for upper-shelf energy. For BTP 5-2, Rev 3, concerning overpressurization protection of pressurized-water reactors at low temperatures, the report partially conforms, specifically meeting the ASME Section XI Appendix G criteria for low-temperature overpressure protection operability. No exceptions or additional comments are noted except for the partial conformance in BTP 5-2.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, demonstrates conformance with multiple regulatory criteria across various system and design requirements. Specifically, it meets DSRS 4.4, Rev 0 for thermal and hydraulic design, including RPV fluid flow calculations and technical specifications; DSRS BTP 5-4, Rev 0 for residual heat removal system test requirements; DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0 for reducing containment pressure following a postulated design basis accident; DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0 for containment isolation valve requirements for engineered safety feature systems; DSRS 9.3.6, Rev 0 for containment evacuation and flooding systems, including TMI 10 CFR 50.34(f); DSRS 11.2, Rev 0 for the liquid waste management system’s exhaust ventilation system; and 50.34(f)(2)(xix) for providing instrumentation adequate for monitoring plant conditions following an accident involving core damage (II.F.3). Additionally, the report confirms conformance regarding equipment survivability and the applicability of environmental qualification and quality assurance requirements for plant features provided solely for severe-accident protection. No exceptions or deviations are noted in any of these areas.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance with various regulatory criteria across multiple systems and scenarios. For DSRS 5.4.2.2 (Steam Generator Program), Section II.5 confirms conformance regarding steam generator tube repair methods. DSRS 6.3 (Emergency Core Cooling System), Section II.5, also conforms for water hammer considerations. Under DSRS 10.4.7 (Condensate and Feedwater System), Section II.2, partial conformance is noted for fluid instabilities (GDC 4), with the intent to satisfy GDC 4 for protecting SSC from fluid flow instability effects such as water hammer. For DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4 (transients involving feedwater and steam flow changes and inadvertent system operations), Sections II.1, II.5, and II.4 confirm conformance for system pressure, identification of limiting single failure, and setpoint inaccuracies (using RG 1.105 guidance), respectively. DSRS 15.1.5 (Steam System Piping Failures), Section II.5, confirms conformance for the assumption of maximum-worth rod fully withdrawn. DSRS 15.1.6 (Loss of Containment Vacuum), Section II.1, confirms conformance for an initial power level of 102%. DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5 (various turbine and steam system transients), Section II.3, and DSRS 15.2.6 (Loss of Nonemergency AC Power), Section II.1, both confirm conformance for analytical models and reactor coolant/main steam system pressures, respectively. No exceptions or additional comments are noted except for the partial conformance regarding fluid instabilities in the condensate and feedwater system.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, demonstrates conformance with various regulatory criteria. For DSRS 6.2.4 (Containment Isolation System), the design conforms with station blackout requirements. DSRS 8.2 (Offsite Power System) confirms compliance with 10 CFR 50.63 for passive design, with details provided in Section 8.4 on station blackout. DSRS 8.3.1 (AC Power Systems, Onsite) states that compliance with 10 CFR 50.55a(h) is not applicable, as no onsite electrical AC power system equipment is required to conform to this regulation or IEEE standards. DSRS 8.3.2 (DC Power Systems, Onsite) partially conforms with RG 1.153, specifically for certain aspects of the EDSS design. SRP BTP 8-2 (Use of Diesel-Generator Sets for Peaking) is referenced without further detail. SRP BTP 8-8 (Onsite and Offsite Power Sources Allowed Outage Time Extensions) is not applicable, as the NuScale design does not rely on AC power for safety-related functions, making the associated Technical Specifications Allowed Outage Times inapplicable. For SRP 9.4.1 (Control Room Area Ventilation System), the design conforms with the requirement to maintain the ability to withstand and recover from a station blackout of specified minimum duration.

Per SRP Section 15.0.3, Section I, Areas of Review, Item 10 under the Review Interfaces subheading, for design certification applications, SRP Section 15.0.3 supersedes the radiological analyses, assumptions, acceptance criteria, and methodologies identified in SRP Section 15.4.8, which references guidance in RG 1.77. Specifically, Item 10.H clarifies that the acceptance criterion related to radiological analyses, assumptions, acceptance criteria, and methodologies is replaced by the guidance in SRP Section 15.0.3.

According to SRP Section 15.0.3, Section I, Areas of Review, under subheading Review Interfaces, Items 10.A, 10.C, and 10.E, the portions of the acceptance criteria that specify radiological acceptance criteria are superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3.

According to SRP Section 15.0.3, Section I, Areas of Review, under Item 10.C and Item 10.F in the Review Interfaces subheading, any portion of the acceptance criteria specifying radiological acceptance criteria is superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3.

According to SRP Section 15.0.3, Section I, Areas of Review, Item 10.H under the subheading Review Interfaces, the portion of the acceptance criterion specifying radiological acceptance criteria—and, in some cases, the analysis model—is superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3. Additionally, a portion of this guidance applies only to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs).

Post-seismic event procedures require measuring gaps between fuel racks and pool walls, as well as between individual fuel racks, with corrective actions such as rack repositioning or verifying acceptability per design basis analysis if needed. The NRC has deemed the referenced Regulatory Guide (RG) outdated; fuel and cladding failure criteria are now governed by SRP (NUREG-0800) 4.2, Appendix B, and radiological criteria by RG 1.183. For SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1, only the acceptance criteria related to fuel storage and handling systems inside the NuScale Reactor Building are applicable, while those for systems inside containment are not, as the NuScale design lacks a containment building for fuel handling. The acceptance criterion for radiation detection in containment partially applies in intent but not in specific language due to design differences from traditional LWRs. The RBC system adheres to the single-failure-proof guidelines of NUREG-0612, ensuring that any credible single component failure does not compromise the ability to stop and hold a critical load. For SRP 15.7.5, Rev 2 (Spent Fuel Cask Drop Accidents), criteria regarding site acceptability, dose mitigating ESF systems, radioactivity control features, and ESF grade atmosphere clean-up systems are not applicable, but the RBC system’s single-failure-proof design partially conforms to the criterion for plant design features eliminating the need for calculation.

The provided passages summarize the NuScale design’s conformance with various regulatory guidance and standards. Guidance specific to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs), such as assumptions for evaluating radiological consequences of a loss of coolant accident (Section 1.3) and ECCS suction concerns (Issue 193), is not applicable to NuScale. Instrument lines do not penetrate the NuScale Power Module (NPM) containment (Section 1.11). For water sources for long-term recirculation cooling following a loss-of-coolant accident (RG 1.82), the NuScale design partially conforms, meeting the intent of regulatory positions on design criteria, performance standards, and analysis methods, though its system design differs from those addressed in the guidance. The NuScale design supports exemptions from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding reactor coolant system and containment sampling (SRP 13.3, Rev 3, II.27) and from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K for ECCS performance evaluation (DSRS 15.6.5, II.1). For radiological consequences of main steam line failures outside containment (SRP 15.1.5.A, Rev 2), NuScale partially conforms to guidance on technical specifications for primary and secondary coolant iodine activity and leak rates. Issue 199, concerning updated probabilistic seismic hazard estimates, is not applicable as it pertains to currently-operating plants.

The provided passages detail the inapplicability of several regulatory guidance documents and technical requirements to the NuScale reactor design due to its reliance on passive safety features and natural circulation. Specifically, NuScale does not use reactor coolant pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, or safety-related motor-operated valves, making guidance on flywheel integrity, motor qualification, and thermal overload protection irrelevant. The design also does not employ safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, or safety injection tanks, so requirements for ECCS accumulator lines and related motor-operated valves do not apply. Additionally, NuScale does not use removal of electric power from safety-related valves to meet the single failure criterion, and guidance on gas accumulation in systems dependent on forced circulation pumps is not relevant. However, guidance remains applicable for evaluating loading transients and structural components for postulated breaks in CVCS piping and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed separately under SRP Section 6.2.4.

The NuScale Power Plant design does not require certain systems to function following an accident, and these systems are not credited in the determination of radiological consequences. The application of the single-failure criterion to safety systems is confirmed, with no exceptions noted. For radiological consequence analyses, NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, as its safety analysis demonstrates that core damage does not occur during any design basis event, making some RG 1.183 guidance only partially applicable. Departures from RG 1.183 for limiting dose consequence analysis are justified in a topical report, and NuScale applies the alternative source term (AST) methodology, specifically the non-LOCA or transient-specific guidance of RG 1.183, for Chapter 15 events. The design uses a modified AST methodology to evaluate radiological consequences of accidents, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis methods (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models for LOCAs addressing only the technically relevant features required by Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30.

The NuScale plant design does not include emergency diesel generators, ESF rooms, or pressurizer power operated relief valves. Several Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and Branch Technical Positions (BTPs) are not applicable to NuScale, including BTP 8-5 and BTP 8-7, as NuScale electric power systems are not engineered safety features and do not require safety-related emergency diesel generators. SRP sections 9.5.5 through 9.5.8, which address emergency diesel engine cooling water, starting, lubrication, and combustion air intake and exhaust systems, are also not applicable since these systems are not present in the NuScale design. SRP 9.4.4, concerning the turbine area ventilation system, does not apply because NuScale does not rely on this system for safety-related or risk-significant functions. For SRP 10.4.4, the NuScale design departs from the power provisions of GDC 34, instead complying with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion for residual heat removal. Additionally, DC/COL-ISG-21 guidance for gas turbine-driven standby emergency AC power systems is not applicable, as NuScale does not use such systems. For wind loadings (SRP 3.3.1), NuScale partially conforms by establishing bounding parameters.

The NuScale reactor design does not require or include reactor coolant pumps, making SRP 5.4.1.1, Rev 3: Pump Flywheel Integrity (PWR) and its acceptance criteria (II.1 through II.7) inapplicable. Similarly, SRP 5.4.11, Rev 4: Pressurizer Relief Tank and its criteria (II.1 and II.2) do not apply, as the NuScale design does not use a pressurizer relief tank. While much of the subtier guidance in DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: Condensate and Feedwater System references large LWR designs and is not relevant to NuScale, the design conforms to II.3 regarding sharing of structures, systems, and components (GDC 5), with no exceptions noted. Additionally, SRP 10.4.9, Rev 3: Auxiliary Feedwater System (PWR) is not applicable, as NuScale does not require or use an auxiliary feedwater system; instead, its decay heat removal system (DHRS) performs some similar functions.

The summarized passages clarify the applicability of various Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and Branch Technical Positions (BTP) to the NuScale reactor design. SRP 6.2.1.2 (Subcompartment Analysis) and SRP 6.2.3 (Secondary Containment Functional Design) apply only to LWRs with subcompartments or both primary and secondary containment, which the NuScale design lacks. SRP 6.2.1.5 and BTP 6-2 (Minimum Containment Pressure Analysis/Model for ECCS Performance) are relevant only to PWRs where a postulated LOCA results in core uncovery; in NuScale, a LOCA does not cause core uncovery. SRP 6.5.2 (Containment Spray as a Fission Product Cleanup System) and SRP 6.5.5 (Pressure Suppression Pool as a Fission Product Cleanup System) pertain to large LWRs with containment spray or pressure suppression pool systems, which NuScale does not employ. SRP 6.5.3 (Fission Product Control Systems and Structures) criteria for secondary containment are not applicable, as NuScale lacks secondary containment; the only credited engineered safety feature (ESF) fission product control system is the containment vessel with isolation valves and passive barriers. BTP 6-3 (Bypass Leakage Paths in Dual Containment Plants) is also inapplicable due to the absence of secondary containment. The NuScale design does not use an ice condenser containment. SECY-92-092 regarding containment performance goals and failure definitions for advanced LWRs is incorporated into NRC regulatory guidance and rulemaking.

The provided passages address NuScale’s conformance with SRP 15.8, Rev 2 requirements for Anticipated Transients Without Scram (ATWS). Acceptance criteria for BWRs (II.1) and PWRs (II.2) are not applicable to NuScale, as it is classified as an evolutionary plant, with relevant criteria found in II.3. For II.3.A.i, NuScale partially conforms by relying on diversity within the Module Protection System (MPS) to mitigate ATWS risk, rather than providing a separate diverse scram system. The alternative criterion (II.3.A.ii), demonstrating acceptable ATWS consequences, is not applicable, as NuScale’s approach is based on RPS diversity. For II.3.B, NuScale conforms, as the equipment requirements of 10 CFR 50.62(C)(1) do not apply or are unnecessary for ATWS risk reduction in this design. Regarding II.3.C, NuScale partially conforms by reducing the probability of scram failure through a diverse RPS, rather than a diverse scram system. Overall, NuScale’s ATWS protection strategy is based on system diversity within the MPS and RPS, partially conforming to current regulatory practices and design expectations.

The referenced regulatory requirements, primarily from 10 CFR 50.34(f), are deemed not applicable to the NuScale plant design for several reasons. The NuScale design does not include a typical auxiliary feedwater (AFW) system as found in large light water reactors (LWRs), nor does it utilize reactor coolant pumps or power-operated pressurizer relief valves, making evaluations related to these components unnecessary. Requirements pertaining to the separation of high pressure coolant injection and reactor core isolation cooling system initiation levels, system modifications to reduce relief valve failures, automatic depressurization system design, and additional space cooling for high pressure systems are specific to boiling water reactors (BWRs) and do not apply to NuScale’s pressurized water reactor (PWR) design. Additionally, the criterion for allowable actuation cycles of the emergency core cooling system (ECCS) and reactor protection system is only relevant to Babcock and Wilcox (B&W) designs, not to NuScale. As such, the NuScale design is exempt from performing the evaluations, analyses, and studies specified in these regulatory provisions.

The NPM control rod assemblies (CRAs) are organized into a regulating bank for normal operation and a shutdown bank for plant shutdown, with all 16 CRAs inserted during scram events. The fuel assembly design is similar to a standard 17x17 PWR, featuring 24 guide tube locations for control rods and a central instrument tube. The control rod drive mechanism (CRDM) includes unique features such as a remote disconnect mechanism and an extended drive shaft. Proof-of-concept testing at Curtiss Wright in Cheswick, PA, validated the remote connect/disconnect operations using a functional drive rod assembly, prototypic gripper coils and latches, a lift coil, and simulated CRA weights. The remote disconnect mechanism demonstrated reliable and repeatable engagement/disengagement of the CRA within the reactor pressure vessel, supporting hardware performance and informing drive rod position detection circuitry design, though not establishing final design basis. The NPM’s longer control rod drive shafts are aligned using multiple supports: CRDM nozzles in the vessel head, an integrated steam plenum, five upper supports in the riser section, and an alignment cone atop the CRA guide tube. The shorter fuel assembly and CRA, combined with the longer drive shaft, create a novel configuration without prior operational experience. To confirm operability, CRA-drop and drive shaft alignment tests were conducted at the AREVA Technical Center in Erlangen, Germany, under ambient pressure and temperature. The test setup included a full-length drive shaft, NPM CRA and fuel assembly, support structures, and a CRA guide tube assembly, all immersed in water without coolant flow. The assembly was dropped in various configurations, altering support and guide tube alignment and introducing mid-span fuel assembly deflection.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) reactor core design utilizes 37 nuclear fuel assemblies, each composed of a 17x17 square lattice of fuel rods with a rod-to-rod pitch, and each fuel rod is approximately 2 meters in length. The fuel assemblies are nominally half the height of standard pressurized water reactor (PWR) fuel assemblies and are supported by five spacer grids. The fuel consists of uranium dioxide (UO2) with less than 4.95% U-235 enrichment, and gadolinium oxide (Gd2O3) is homogeneously mixed as a burnable absorber in select rod locations. Unlike standard PWRs, the NPM employs low-flow natural circulation of primary coolant rather than pump-driven flow, with coolant entering from the bottom through the core inlet plenum and flowing upward for heat transfer. Critical Heat Flux (CHF) testing for the preliminary fuel design included the use of 5x5 fuel assembly simulation bundles—an industry-accepted practice for PWR CHF testing—mounted in a square flow channel and tested in various configurations, including different axial power shapes and subchannel arrangements, as detailed in the NuScale Power Critical Heat Flux Correlations topical report. The test program supported code development, safety analysis, and the development of the NSP2 CHF correlation for the final fuel design.

Tests specific to the NuScale fuel design are detailed in Sections 1.5.1.1 and 1.5.1.2, steam generator (SG) tests in Sections 1.5.1.3 and 1.5.1.4, control rod assembly tests in Sections 1.5.1.6 and 1.5.1.7, and integrated system phenomena tests in Section 1.5.1.5. Sensors, including thermocouples, pressure transducers, mass flow rate instruments, and strain gauges, were used to measure heater rod temperatures, fluid flow conditions, temperature, pressure, flow rate, and vibration at various points in the fluid loop and on both primary and secondary sides of the SG. These tests enabled NuScale to obtain fuel bundle subchannel exit data and benchmark thermal-hydraulic design and systems computer codes. Due to the unique helical SG design, which differs from those in the existing nuclear fleet, large-scale experimental data were required to validate NuScale’s thermal-hydraulic systems and computer codes, and to determine SG performance characteristics, particularly the secondary side (inside tube) thermal-hydraulic performance of individual helical tubes. The data also informed the sizing of SG inlet flow restrictors for stable secondary-side operation and defined steam outlet conditions as a function of primary heat generation and secondary side conditions. The SG test program was inspected by the NRC under Inspection Procedures IP 35034, 35017, and 36100. Analysis documented in Appendix A of TR-0516-49417 demonstrates that any SG secondary side flow oscillations are decoupled from primary side flow oscillations, ensuring that secondary side flow instabilities do not induce reactor power oscillations that could exceed fuel design limits.
