To meet fuel licensing requirements, two critical heat flux (CHF) test programs were conducted: one for the preliminary fuel design and another for the final fuel design. CHF testing is essential to determine how close the heat transfer mode is to transitioning to a state where a continuous steam layer covers the fuel rods, known as the CHF point, especially under off-normal conditions such as anticipated operational occurrences and postulated accidents. For the reduced-length fuel, CHF tests were performed over a wide range of operating conditions using instrumentation such as resistance temperature detectors (RTDs), thermocouples, pressure transducers, mass flow rate instruments, and electrical voltage and current meters to measure key parameters. Water was flowed over the test sections at discrete test points covering various hydraulic conditions to develop a CHF correlation spanning the NPM operational envelope. At each test point, the loop was set for specific flow, inlet temperature, and exit pressure, and bundle power was increased until CHF was detected, indicated by an excursion in the fuel simulator thermocouples.

Preliminary fuel design CHF (Critical Heat Flux) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, utilizing their high-pressure flow loop, electrical power supplies, heat rejection devices, water conditioning system, and data acquisition system. The test section and fuel simulators were specifically designed to represent the preliminary NuScale fuel design, employing 5x5 fuel assembly simulations with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations both with and without a simulated guide tube. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation of the primary coolant, generating data used to develop and validate the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Further testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided additional data to validate the NSP2 CHF correlation and to develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design. The occurrence of CHF was identified by excursions in fuel simulator temperatures. The collected data also included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for determining mixing coefficients, as well as single-phase and two-phase pressure drop characteristics across a range of bundle powers and hydraulic conditions.

Tests were conducted on vertical 5x5 fuel assembly configurations to cover a range of bounding conditions and axial power shapes, following detailed test matrices for steady-state and transient critical heat flux (CHF) testing, pressure drop, and thermal mixing as specified in the program documentation. The CHF tests used an electrically-heated 5x5 simulated fuel bundle constructed to prototypic geometry and incorporating AREVA HTP™/HMP™ grid technology. Assemblies with various power shapes were tested, including configurations with or without the center fuel rod replaced by a guide tube. Water was flowed through the test section at specified flow rates, inlet temperatures, and exit pressures, and bundle power was incrementally increased until CHF was detected, encompassing a range of hydraulic conditions representative of the NPM and various axial power shapes. All testing employed industry-accepted methodologies for test execution and acceptance.

During testing, loop flow conditions—including temperature, pressure, and flow—along with bundle power, rod power, and fuel simulator temperatures were recorded for each run. As-built data such as flow channel width, fuel simulator diameters, and spacer grid dimensions were also documented. The test facility featured a large pressure vessel capable of accommodating the tube bundle test section, enabling experiments at elevated pressures and temperatures. Heaters and pumps in the facility provided a range of flow rates and thermal-hydraulic conditions. Testing types included adiabatic, diabatic, transient, density wave oscillation, and fluid-elastic instability tests, each conducted at multiple test points to characterize relevant phenomena across various combinations of primary-side and secondary-side pressures, temperatures, and flow rates. All finished heater bundles underwent functionality verification per vendor acceptance criteria, with power ratings confirmed to be within 2 percent of the nominal rated power.

Testing was conducted over a range of conditions representative of the operational envelope to evaluate temperature and pressure distributions inside the tubes, as required by the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (Tier 2, Revision 5). The initial electrically-heated tests focused on secondary-side performance using three isolated, instrumented tubes with well-controlled boundary conditions and constant heat flux within each zone, simplifying the heat flux profile compared to fluid heating. This allowed precise control of tube wall heat flux and improved access to external instrumentation. These tests were performed at the Societa Informazioni Esperienze Termoidrauliche (SIET) facility in Piacenza, Italy, and included adiabatic, diabatic, transient, and density wave oscillation testing. Dynamic pressure measurements were recorded to develop power spectral density spectra, supporting evaluation of internal two-phase (boiling) pressure fluctuations and their potential contribution to flow-induced vibration of steam generator (SG) tubes. Subsequently, a second set of SG tests used a 252-tube helical bundle, fluid-heated on the exterior to more accurately represent primary-side SG conditions, modeling five of the 21 helical coil columns and operated at near-prototypic primary and secondary flow conditions, also at SIET using their fluid-heated hydraulic loop. Additionally, equipment requiring inspection or repair is positioned for accessibility to minimize time and radiation exposure during refueling and maintenance, allowing plant technicians to access components without excessive dose or the need to move numerous plates, shields, covers, or piping.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is a subsystem of the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) located inside the containment vessel (CNV). It comprises the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) with an integral pressurizer (PZR), reactor vessel internals (RVI), reactor safety valves (RSVs), RCS piping (including injection, discharge, PZR spray supply, and high-point degasification lines), the PZR control cabinet, and RCS instruments and cables. The RCS transports heat from the reactor core to two once-through, helical-coil steam generators (SGs) by natural circulation during normal operation. The SGs are situated in the annular space between the hot leg riser and the RPV inner wall, with tubes connected to feed and steam plenums via tube sheets. Pressure in the RCS is reduced using spray from the chemical and volume control system (CVCS). The RCS piping extends beyond the CNV, and the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) includes the RPV shell, integral PZR volume, RPV penetrations, several CNV nozzles and safe ends, SG tubes, integral steam and feed plenums, pressure-retaining portions of the RSVs, and the pressure housing of the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs). RCS injection, discharge, PZR spray, and high-point degasification piping up to the outermost containment isolation valves (CIVs) connecting to the CVCS are also part of the RCPB.

The Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) is an approximately cylindrical steel vessel with an inside diameter of about 9 ft and an overall height of 58 ft, designed for an operating pressure of 1,850 psia. It consists of three main sections: the head, upper, and lower sections. The torispherical RPV head is welded to the top of the upper section, while the upper and lower sections are flanged together with bolts. The upper section is cylindrical, with thicker areas at the feedwater inlet and steam outlet, and contains penetrations ranging from 2.25” to 25” in diameter for main steam piping nozzles and access ports, pressurizer heaters, feedwater piping and access ports, reactor recirculation valves, Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS), and pressure instrumentation. The lower section is also cylindrical with a welded torispherical lower head and contains no penetrations. An integral steel pressurizer baffle plate separates the saturated water in the pressurizer from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS). The RPV provides steam and feedwater plenums for the steam generators and RSV penetrations, and supports or attaches to the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs), CRDM seismic support structure, pressurizer heater bundles, in-core instrumentation, steam generator system piping, RCS piping, reactor safety valves (RSVs), reactor vent valves, and reactor recirculation valves. The RPV, including the steam generators, is certified and stamped per Article NCA-8000 of ASME BPVC, Section III. Pressure-temperature curves for the RPV comply with 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, ensuring operation below the pressure-temperature limits to prevent non-ductile failure. Two pressurizer heater bundles, each with a nominal capacity of 400 kW, are mounted through the RPV side, 180 degrees apart around the integral steam plenum assembly. The pressurizer baffle plate and its support structure are subject to BPVC and ANSI/ASME NQA-1 requirements, with inspections and testing conducted per BPVC standards. Material specifications for the RPV upper shell and head, as well as details on pressurizer spray nozzles and safe ends, are provided in referenced sections.

The Containment Vessel (CNV) is approximately 76 ft in height and 15 ft in outside diameter, comprising an upper section with a welded torispherical top head and a lower section with a welded head. These sections are joined by a bolted flange connection, allowing separation for refueling and maintenance access to the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV). The CNV is laterally supported by support lugs located just below the steam plenum elevation and vertically by a support skirt attached to its lower head. Internally, the RPV is supported both laterally and vertically by four support plates slightly below the steam plenum elevation and is further laterally supported at the center of its lower head. The RPV, a metal vessel forming part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), acts as a barrier to fission product release and contains most of the reactor coolant. It houses and supports the reactor core, reactor vessel internals (RVI), steam generators (SGs), and pressurizer (PZR). The RVI direct coolant flow from the core to the SGs and back within the RPV, and include sub-assemblies such as the core support assembly, surveillance capsule assemblies, lower and upper riser assemblies, flow diverter, and PZR spray nozzles. Each Nuclear Power Module (NPM) includes piping between the RPV and CNV, with the RPV being a pressure-retaining component of the reactor coolant system (RCS). The SGs are integral to the RPV, which is supported laterally and vertically by the CNV.

Reactor vent valves (RVVs) are opened during emergency core cooling system (ECCS) operation to discharge the pressurizer (PZR) steam space directly to the containment vessel (CNV), enabling removal of noncondensible gases from the PZR and providing overpressure protection at low temperatures. The reactor pressure vessel (RPV) features penetrations and attachment points for the control rod drive system, RCS instruments, ECCS valves (RVVs and reactor recirculation valves, RRVs), and reactor coolant system (RCS) piping, including injection, discharge, PZR spray supply, and high point degasification lines. The RCS injection line runs from the containment upper head to a welded penetration on the RPV side, continues through the lower upper riser assembly, and terminates near the riser center, allowing coolant injection above the reactor core. This line includes two branch connections to ECCS lines linked to the reset valves of the five ECCS valves (three RVVs and two RRVs). The RCS discharge line is routed from the containment upper head to a penetration below the steam generators (SGs), drawing suction from the annular region between the RPV wall and the riser. The pressure-retaining portions of the RCS help maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), serving as a barrier to radionuclide release. The ECCS valves, as part of the RCPB, also permit recirculation of water from the CNV back into the RPV and through the core during emergency cooling.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) interfaces with several systems, including the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) via the containment system, the emergency core cooling system (ECCS) through reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), the containment vessel (CNV), control rod drive system, steam generator system (SGS), and the primary sampling system (via the CVCS). The RCS piping external to the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) comprises two pressurizer (PZR) spray line branches from a common spray header, an RPV high point degasification line, an RCS injection line, and an RCS discharge line. The RCS injection line includes branch lines connecting to each ECCS valve reset valve. The reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) encompasses the RCS injection and discharge piping interfacing with the CVCS up to the outermost containment isolation valve (CIV) on the top of each nuclear power module (NPM). This piping, including associated isolation valves, is designed as ASME BPVC Section III, Class 1, while the piping from the outermost CIVs to the NPM flange connections is not part of the RCPB and is designed as Class 3. Pressure-retaining piping that penetrates the RCS and forms part of the RCPB includes the PZR spray supply, RCS injection, RCS discharge, and RPV high-point degasification piping, as defined in 10 CFR 50.2. Figures 5.1-2 and 6.6-1 illustrate the RCS piping configuration, including the location and number of RSVs and RVVs relative to the RPV and the routing from the CNV upper head to RPV penetrations. Design, fabrication, and operational provisions to mitigate stress-corrosion cracking are detailed in Sections 3.9, 3.12, and 5.2. The RCS piping supports CVCS functions (see Section 9.3.4), and preservice and inservice inspection (ISI) requirements for ASME Class 1 components, including RCS piping, are summarized in Section 5.2.

Two pilot-operated, self-actuating Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are connected to the top of the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head and discharge directly to the Containment Vessel (CNV), forming part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) and providing overpressure protection in accordance with the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code. The RSVs utilize RCS pressure routed to a chamber above the pilot disc, expanding the pilot valve bellows and seating the pilot disc, with relief pressure determined by the spring pre-load of the pilot valve and the main valve closing spring pressure. The metal bellows, spring-operated pilot valve arrangement is designed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subparagraph NB-7511.1, ensuring reliable operation to relieve Pressurizer (PZR) pressure under large differential pressure conditions. Redundant RSVs enhance system reliability for overpressure events. Additionally, the trip and reset valves for the Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) are solenoid pilot valves built to ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, with pilot actuators mounted outside the CNV and an inadvertent actuation block feature to prevent unintended valve opening at normal operating RCS pressure.

The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) for each Nuclear Power Module (NPM) aligns with the definition in 10 CFR 50.2 and includes all pressure-retaining components of the reactor coolant system (RCS) up to and including the outermost containment isolation valve (CIV) in system piping that penetrates the containment vessel (CNV), reactor safety valves (RSVs), and emergency core cooling system (ECCS) reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs). The RCS piping forming the RCPB penetrates both the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and the CNV up to the outermost CIV. Key components include the pressurizer (PZR) heater bundles, RVVs, RRVs, and their actuator assemblies, with the RPV serving as the primary component of the RCS and RCPB. The RCPB components are designed to allow periodic inspection, testing, and maintenance, in accordance with GDC 32, to assess structural and leak-tight integrity. This includes access to the RSV, PZR heaters, steam generator (SG) primary and secondary sides, instruments, electrical connections, and other components within the RCPB of the NPMs.

Section 3.9 details the design transients, loading combinations, stress limits, and evaluation methods used in the design and fatigue analyses of Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components, demonstrating that RCPB integrity is maintained. The RCPB components are designed, constructed, and maintained to quality standards that ensure overpressure protection of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS). Overpressure protection is also provided during low temperature conditions, maintaining sufficient margin to ensure non-brittle behavior and minimizing the probability of rapidly propagating fracture, in accordance with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, General Design Criteria (GDC) 31. The Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and pressure-retaining components associated with the RCPB are designed and fabricated to be compatible with the environmental conditions of the reactor coolant and containment atmosphere (GDC 4), and with sufficient margin to ensure non-brittle behavior and minimize the probability of rapidly propagating fracture and gross rupture (GDC 14 and 31).

Each Nuclear Power Module (NPM) is equipped with overpressure protection features to safeguard the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), including the primary side of auxiliary systems connected to the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) and the secondary side of the Steam Generators (SGs), from overpressurization. Overpressure protection for the RCPB is achieved through two ASME BPVC Section III safety valves during normal operations and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs), ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15. The system is designed to prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding 110% of design pressure during normal operations and AOOs, and is capable of functioning with a single active failure and concurrent loss of normal AC power. For the secondary system, overpressure protection ensures ASME BPVC service limits are not exceeded during specified service conditions, with RSVs provided in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Articles NB-7000 (RCPB) and NC-7120(b) (secondary system piping associated with SGs and the Decay Heat Removal System, DHRS). Secondary systems with design pressure equal to the RCPB, such as the SG system, DHRS, steam and feedwater portions of the containment system, the condensate and feedwater system downstream of the Feedwater Regulating Valves (FWRVs), and the main steam system upstream of the secondary Main Steam Isolation Valves (MSIVs), are designed to not exceed ASME BPVC service limits during normal operation or transients, eliminating the need for additional pressure relieving devices. The most severe AOO, a turbine trip at full power without bypass capability, is the bounding event for determining RSV capacity and RPV overpressure analyses. Overpressure protection during low temperature conditions is provided by the RVVs, which are part of the RCPB and designed in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB.

Overpressure protection for the NuScale Power Module Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) is provided by two Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) during power operations and by three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) during startup, shutdown, and low-temperature conditions when the RPV is not vented. The RSVs, which are redundant, safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A components, are designed to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the design pressure (2310 psia) and each is sized for 100% of the required relief capacity, in compliance with ASME BPVC Section III, Article NB-7000, and 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15. The RSVs are bolted to flanges on the RPV head for periodic inspection and testing, while the RVVs are connected above the pressurizer (PZR) volume and discharge steam and water directly into containment. Additionally, RSVs provide external overpressure protection for steam generator (SG) tubing and plena. Thermal relief valves, which are spring-operated, balanced-bellows, Quality Group B, Seismic Category I, and Class 2 per BPVC Section III, vent directly into containment and protect secondary system components and control rod drive system cooling piping during containment isolation events, but are not credited for safety-related overpressure protection during operation. Overpressure protection during operation is instead provided by system design pressure and the RSVs.

The Reactor System Valves (RSVs) provide overpressure protection for piping external to the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) that is part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), including RCS injection, discharge, degasification, pressurizer spray piping up to the outermost Containment Isolation Valves (CIVs), ECCS valve pilot actuator piping, and several containment vessel (CNV) nozzles and their safe ends. All RPV surfaces, both primary and secondary sides, are maintained in a dry environment using a non-chloride, non-corrosive desiccant, with humidity indicators and both sides shipped under positive pressure. The internal atmosphere on both sides of the Steam Generator (SG) tubes is evacuated and filled with nitrogen with a dew point below -20°F, and foreign material exclusion measures are implemented before shipping. Inside containment, a single line branches into two pressurizer spray supply lines, each welded to a penetration on the RPV upper head with corresponding spray nozzles near the top of the pressurizer space. A non-safety-related high-point degasification line, connected to a nozzle on the RPV upper head in the integral pressurizer region, allows venting to the Liquid Radioactive Waste System (LRWS) via the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS), where degasifiers remove noncondensible gases. During normal operation, accumulation of noncondensible gases in the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) and pressurizer steam space is minimized by removal through the LRWS degasifiers using the high-point degasification line as needed. The pressurizer, located at the top of the integral RCS inside the RPV, enables a single venting location for noncondensible gases, with a high-point degasification vent line provided for routing gases during startup and shutdown. The degasification vent line offers a removal path for noncondensible gases from the pressurizer, and during low temperature conditions, overpressure protection is provided by the Reactor Vessel Vent Valves (RVVs), which relieve to the containment volume. Two sets of electrical heaters are installed horizontally and directly immersed in the lower portion of the pressurizer liquid. Design stress limits, loading conditions, and design transients are detailed in Section 3.9, while operating conditions related to RPV integrity are covered in Sections 5.2.2, 5.3.2, and the plant technical specifications.

The Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are integral components of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) head. Each RSV is designed to actuate on high Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure, allowing direct flow to containment, and is classified as Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB. The RSVs prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding 110% of design pressure under all conditions, with design information in Table 5.2-2 and materials in Table 6.1-3. Each pilot-operated RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-7520. Environmental qualification details are in Section 3.11. The Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) provide sufficient pressure relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure and are also environmentally qualified per Section 3.11. Both RSVs and RVVs are designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-3500, meeting overpressure protection criteria in Article NB-7000, with applicable code editions and classifications detailed in Sections 5.2.1 and 3.2, and material specifications in Section 6.1. Functional qualification is performed per ASME QME-1 as endorsed by RG 1.100, with inservice testing and inspection ensuring continued reliability and conformance. RSVs are considered passive devices, and the reliability of both RSVs and RVVs is further discussed in Section 6.3.2.

The provided passages reference a series of figures (Figures 1.2-10 through 1.2-20) that depict various elevation and section views of the Reactor Building at specific heights: 24'-0", 35'-8", 50'-0", 62'-0", 75'-0", 86'-0", 100'-0", 126'-0", and 145'-6". Additionally, the figures include east, west, and south section views of the Reactor Building, illustrating its structural layout at these key elevations. Each figure corresponds to a specific page in the document, providing detailed visual documentation of the building's design at multiple vertical levels.

The provided passages reference a series of figures depicting various elevations and sectional views of the Control Building. Specifically, the figures illustrate the Control Building at elevations of 50'-0" (Figure 1.2-21), 63'-3" (Figure 1.2-22), 76'-6" (Figure 1.2-23), 100'-0" (Figure 1.2-24), and 120'-0" (Figure 1.2-25). These figures collectively detail the vertical and sectional architectural features of the Control Building at specified heights.

The provided passages reference a series of figures (Figures 1.2-28 through 1.2-31) depicting the elevations of the Radioactive Waste Building at specific heights: 71'-0", 82'-0", 100'-0", and 120'-0". Each figure corresponds to a different elevation view, illustrating the structural details and configuration of the building at these respective heights.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, provides detailed figures and diagrams related to the plant's systems and structures. The Radioactive Waste Building is depicted in multiple section and elevation views (Figures 1.2-28 through 1.2-33), though the actual images are withheld. The radioactive waste management system, including liquid, gaseous, and solid waste, is discussed in Chapter 11, with specific details in Sections 11.2, 11.3, and 11.4. Instrumentation and controls are illustrated in Table 1.7-1, covering system architecture, module protection, communication, reactor trip systems, power distribution, neutron monitoring, plant protection, safety display, and control system interfaces. Table 1.7-2 lists system drawings for major plant systems, including the reactor coolant, steam generator, decay heat removal, containment, emergency core cooling, control room habitability, spent fuel pool cooling, reactor pool cooling, pool cleanup, pool surge control, component cooling water, demineralized water, ultimate heat sink, condensate storage, site cooling water, chilled water, utility water, instrument and service air, nitrogen distribution, containment sampling, radioactive waste drain, balance-of-plant drain, chemical and volume control, boron addition, containment evacuation and flooding, HVAC for various buildings, fire protection, power conversion, turbine generator, main steam, condenser, auxiliary boiler, and detailed diagrams for liquid, gaseous, and solid radioactive waste systems (Figures 11.2-1a through 11.4-2b).

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, provides a general description of the plant design, emphasizing the implementation of digital control systems that ensure independence between safety-related protection systems and nonsafety-related control systems. Each reactor control system display is dedicated to monitoring a specific reactor, while additional display stations, including one for shared plant systems, enable control room operators to access comprehensive plant information for trending and diagnostics. Security measures are in place to verify identity, monitor access status, search for contraband, and oversee tamper alarm devices, ensuring controlled access to plant areas. Several figures depicting the control building at various elevations (50'-0", 76'-6", 100'-0") and sectional views (north and west) are referenced but withheld in the report.

The NuScale site features a conceptual layout where most buildings are situated within the protected area (PA), which is secured by a double fence and intrusion-detection equipment. The PA itself is enclosed within the security owner controlled area (SOCA), which is surrounded by an additional single fence. Outside the SOCA fence are the administration and training building and a warehouse. The site includes an upper tunnel for personnel access to the Reactor Building (RXB) and a lower utilities tunnel connecting the Control Room Building (CRB) and the RXB. The Main Control Room (MCR) and associated spaces are located below grade in the CRB and are serviced by the Control Room HVAC System (CRHS). Figures referenced in the report illustrate the conceptual site layout and specific elevations of the Control Building, though some details are withheld.

The referenced sections of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, Tier 2, provide a series of figures (Figures 1.2-9 through 1.2-19) detailing the Emergency Core Cooling System and various elevation views of the Reactor Building at specific heights: 24’-0”, 35’-8”, 50’-0”, 62’-0”, 75’-0”, 86’-0”, 100’-0”, 126’-0”, and 145’-6”. Additionally, an east and west section view of the Reactor Building is included. The technical content of these figures is withheld and not available in the provided text.

The reactor core heats the reactor coolant, which then flows upward through the lower and upper riser assemblies. At the top of the upper riser, the coolant is redirected downward by the PZR baffle plate into the annular space between the upper riser assembly and the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) wall, where the steam generator (SG) helical tube bundles are located. The primary coolant flows over the outside of the SG tubes, transferring heat to the secondary side fluid inside the tubes, which produces superheated steam with a moisture content no greater than 0.10 percent by weight during full-power operation. As the coolant cools and becomes denser, it sinks into the annular downcomer between the lower riser assembly and the core barrel, then returns to the lower plenum and reenters the reactor core, completing the natural circulation loop. The motive force for this flow is natural convection, driven by density differences between the hot coolant leaving the core and the colder coolant leaving the SGs, as well as the elevation difference between the core (heat source) and SGs (heat sink). The RCS flow path is designed for very low pressure losses, so pressure at any location is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging uncertainties do not affect the natural circulation flow rate. Small flow paths in the upper riser allow limited coolant bypass to maintain sufficient boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery conditions after non-LOCA transients. The entire RCS natural circulation loop is contained within the RPV, eliminating external piping and the risk of large break LOCA events, and the intertwined SG tube bundle configuration prevents asymmetric core cooling. As the RPV water level drops near the top of the riser, reduced flow area increases pressure losses and impedes natural circulation, resulting in a greater temperature difference between hot and cold legs.

The reactor coolant returns to the bottom of the vessel via the downcomer and re-enters the reactor core, completing the primary loop. On the secondary side, preheated feedwater is pumped into the tube side of the two independent steam generators (SGs), entering through nozzles into the lower feed plenums. The feedwater flows upward through the helical SG tubes, where heat is transferred from the reactor coolant across the tube walls, causing the feedwater to boil and become superheated steam. The steam is collected in the steam plena at the top of the SGs and exits through steam nozzles, passing through SG piping and containment penetrations to the main steam system and power conversion systems outside the Reactor Building. The superheated steam is directed to a dedicated steam turbine, which drives a generator to produce electricity delivered to the grid via a step-up transformer. A turbine bypass line can route up to 100% of the rated main steam flow directly from the SGs to the main condenser to remove reactor heat following load reductions or loss of electrical load. Steam exiting or bypassing the turbine is condensed in up to six condensers using a shared circulating water loop. The condensate is polished and pumped by variable speed feedwater pumps, with some steam extracted from turbine stages to preheat the feedwater for increased efficiency. Feedwater regulating valves control flow into the SGs. The total SG heat transfer area includes the full tube length from feed to steam plenums, with a 10% tube plugging margin and a fouling factor based on typical nuclear plant operating experience and secondary water chemistry control. The SG configuration maintains separation between the superheated steam at the tube tops and subcooled liquid at the bottoms, minimizing hydraulic instabilities and potential water hammer.

In this reactor design, two independent but intertwined once-through, helical-coil steam generators (SGs) are located inside the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), with primary-side reactor coolant flowing downward around the outside of the SG tubes and secondary-side feedwater and main steam flowing inside the tubes. This configuration differs from current PWR designs and facilitates efficient heat transfer from the primary coolant to the secondary coolant. The SGs serve as part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), providing a fission product barrier in the event of fuel cladding failure, and play a critical role in decay heat removal by transferring heat to the decay heat removal system (DHRS), which operates through naturally circulating closed loops to the reactor pool. In the event of an SG tube failure, the affected SG is isolated, feedwater and main steam isolation valves are closed, DHRS valves open, and cooling continues through the intact SG, maintaining symmetrical heat removal and preventing asymmetrical cooling in the reactor vessel. Overpressure protection is provided by a single thermal relief valve on each feedwater line upstream of the feed plenums and by relief and safety valves (RSVs) for DHRS internal pressure, especially during shutdown or if the secondary system is water solid and containment is isolated. The SGs are designed for ease of cleaning via direct connection to main steam and feedwater disconnect flanges during outages. The primary coolant system operates at a higher pressure than the secondary system, keeping the SG tubes in compression. The inservice inspection (ISI) program for the SGs must comply with Section XI of the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) as required by 10 CFR 50.55a(g).

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

The steam generator system (SGS) is an integral part of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), though not a component of the reactor coolant system (RCS). It comprises steam generator (SG) tubes, SG tube supports, steam and feedwater (FW) piping inside containment, SG tube inlet flow restrictors, feed and steam plenums, and associated access ports and covers. The SGS includes FW piping from the chemical and volume control system (CNTS) to the feed plenum access port, a thermal relief valve, steam plenum cap, and main steam (MS) piping from the steam plenum access port to the CNTS. The FW plenum is located within the feed plenum access port, with the tubesheet forming the boundary between the primary and secondary sides, while the MS plenum is within the RPV integral steam plenum shell, bounded by the steam plenum cap and RPV integral baffle plate. The RPV forms the SG shell and provides the outer pressure boundary, with the SG tubes, tube-to-tubesheet welds, and tubesheets forming part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB). Each SG, located inside the RPV, consists of interlacing helical tube columns connecting to two feed and two steam plena, with each SG’s feed and steam plena configured 180 degrees apart. The two SGs’ helical tube columns form an intertwined bundle around the upper riser assembly, resulting in four feed and four steam plena located 90 degrees apart around the RPV. Main steam and feedwater supply nozzles are integral to their respective plenum access ports, and the primary reactor coolant circulates outside the SG tubes, with steam generation occurring inside the tubes. Integrity of the SGs and associated components forming portions of the RCPB is addressed in referenced sections.

The steam generators (SGs) are equipped with inlet flow restrictors at the secondary side of each SG tube to ensure stable flow and prevent reactor power oscillations that could exceed fuel design limits. These flow restriction devices are specifically designed to reduce the potential for secondary side density wave oscillations, which can cause transient loads on the reactor coolant system (RCS), reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and SG. The restrictors are mounted on plates in each feed plenum access port, attached to the secondary-side face of the tubesheets using stud bolts welded to the tubesheet, with spacers maintaining plate separation. The individual flow restrictors extend into the tubes and are removable to facilitate SG inspection, cleaning, tube plugging, and maintenance. Locking features on the bolts and nuts minimize the risk of loose parts. The restrictors provide the necessary pressure drop at the feedwater inlet plenum interface to preclude unacceptable secondary flow instabilities, particularly under boiling conditions. Flow-induced vibration analyses identify SG tubes, tube supports, lower SG supports, and inlet flow restrictors as susceptible to mechanisms such as fluid elastic instability, vortex shedding, turbulent buffeting, leakage flow, and, for other components, acoustic resonance, galloping, and flutter. The restrictors are rigid with high fundamental frequency, resulting in low vibration amplitudes and negligible turbulence-induced stresses, thus avoiding impact with the SG tube inner diameter during steady-state operation. SG inlet flow restrictors are non-structural RPV attachments and are designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NC.

Each steam generator (SG) tube consists of a seamless helical tube with bends at each end, transitioning to a straight section at the tube sheet entry. The tubes terminate at the feed and steam plenum tubesheets, where they are secured by expansion fit and welded on the secondary side. Instead of typical SG tube support plates, the design uses austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies that provide non-continuous, full-circumferential support, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion product buildup. Tube support assemblies are positioned between each column of helical tubes, with stamped tabs enveloping part of the tube circumference above and below to provide vertical support and minimize flow stagnation at the tube-to-support interface. Outer and middle spacers, welded into the tube supports, allow tabs from adjacent columns to nest, creating a continuous support path and optimizing circumferential spacing to minimize tube free span lengths while accommodating tube transitions to the plena. The tube support assemblies are attached to upper and lower SG supports welded to the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) inner surface, providing contact with each tube at eight circumferential locations. The use of eight sets of tube support assemblies limits unsupported tube lengths, ensuring high modal frequencies to prevent unacceptable flow-induced vibration. Lower SG supports permit thermal growth and provide lateral support. Tubes at the bundle inlet (cold leg) are evaluated to preclude vortex shedding, as they lack downstream obstructions, but the tortuous flow path within the bundle and the axial alignment of supports (angle of attack near 0.0, aspect ratio > 2.0) prevent stable vortex formation, galloping, and flutter. The upper SG supports are welded to the RPV and steam plenum and are excluded from analysis due to their rigidity, while the lower SG supports are welded to the RPV shell below the tube bundle.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements and locations for reactor vessel internals, focusing on core support components and internal structures. Key components include core support blocks in the downcomer, lower and upper core plates, control rod assembly guide tubes and support plates, riser transitions, pressurizer baffle, containment vessel, and reactor vessel. Inspection elements are categorized primarily under B-N-3 and B-N-2, with visual examination methods VT-1 and VT-3 specified. VT-3 is often augmented to VT-1 for accessible surfaces or critical components such as reflector blocks, core barrel, lower and upper core plates, fuel pins, and alignment pins. Internal structures like ICI guide tubes, CVCS makeup piping, upper CRDS support, riser sections, trunnions, spacers, bellows, flexible and rigid pipes, and surveillance capsules are inspected mainly using VT-3. Some inspections, such as for reflector block alignment pins, are only required when components are removed for other reasons. The inspection scope includes interfaces between major assemblies, fasteners, dowels, guide cards, and connections to the pressurizer and steam plenum, ensuring comprehensive integrity assessment of the reactor coolant boundary.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is designed to maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s design life. Each NPM complies with 10 CFR 50.55a, ensuring that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and all pressure-retaining components associated with the RCPB are designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 1 in accordance with Section III of the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), and meet the applicable conditions specified in 10 CFR 50.55a(b). The materials used for the RCPB, including weld materials, conform to the fabrication, construction, and testing requirements of ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, and comply with ASME BPVC Section II material requirements. Inspection requirements and conditions for Class 1 pressure-containing components and their supports are met as detailed in Section XI of the ASME BPVC. Additionally, the design utilizes specific ASME Code Cases, including N-759-2 (allowable external pressure and compressive stresses for Class 1, 2, and 3 components), N-62-7 (internal and external valve items for Classes 1, 2, and 3), and N-60-5 (material for core support structures).

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, for the Integrity of the Reactor Coolant Boundary, specifies compliance with 10 CFR 50.55a(b) and allows the use of ASME OM Code editions or addenda not endorsed by the NRC if requirements of 10 CFR 50.55a(z) are met. The report references the Electric Power Research Institute's "Pressurized Water Reactor Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines" (EPRI #3002000505, 2014) for water chemistry management. Table 5.2-5 details reactor coolant water chemistry controls, specifying normal operating ranges and regulatory limits: chloride ≤ 0.05 ppm (RG 1.44 limit 0.15 ppm), fluoride ≤ 0.05 ppm (RG 1.44 limit 0.15 ppm), dissolved oxygen ≤ 0.005 ppm (RG 1.44 limit 0.10 ppm), sulfate ≤ 0.05 ppm, hydrogen 25–50 cc/kg, and boron 0–2000 ppm. The integrity process for components continues until they are sealed for shipment. Tables 5.2-3 and 5.2-4 are not used.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes integrated overpressure protection for secondary side components with the same design pressure as the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), ensuring ASME BPVC service limits are not exceeded during normal operations and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system is designed with sufficient capacity to prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits specified in 10 CFR 50 Appendix G when below the LTOP enable temperature, thereby maintaining reactor pressure vessel (RPV) pressure below brittle fracture limits during all operational, maintenance, testing, or postulated accident conditions. During normal operational transients at full power, the system and core parameters are managed to prevent safety valve lift; if a safety valve does lift, the pressurizer (PZR) steam space is sized to avoid liquid discharge. LTOP analysis assumes a maximum PZR heater output of 880 kW and an additional 2 MW from core decay heat, demonstrating that reactor vent valves (RVVs) open before reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure exceeds the low temperature pressure limit. Table 5.2-10 provides LTOP pressure setpoints as a function of cold temperature, with PZR pressure limits ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, and LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Figure 5.2-4 illustrates the variable LTOP setpoint (PZR pressure) as a function of temperature, in relation to the saturation curve.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details overpressure protection for the steam generator (SG) internal pressure, which is provided by the primary system Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs). During shutdown, thermal relief valves protect the secondary side of the SGs, feedwater and steam piping within containment, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) when the secondary system is water solid and containment is isolated. Three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs), classified as safety-related, Seismic Category I, and Quality Group A, are constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, and are designed to prevent brittle fracture stress limits from being exceeded on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and associated Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components at low temperatures. Direct position indication for each RSV and RVV is provided in the control room in accordance with 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi) and the Three Mile Island action plan Item II.D.3. Each reactor module has two RSVs, each with a 3-inch inlet and 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, and design temperature of 650°F. The minimum design capacity at 3% overpressure is 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam. The nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% for the first valve and 2100 psia ±1% for the second, with a blowdown from set pressure of 10% ±2%. The RSV design includes a main piston, main spring, main disk, main valve seat, and a position sensor for flow monitoring.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report specifies that Type 3XX series austenitic stainless steel used in the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) and subjected to sensitizing temperatures after solution heat treatment must have a carbon content limited to no more than 0.03 wt%. Carbide precipitation on grain boundaries occurs when the material is heated between 800°F and 1500°F. Welding of ferritic materials for RCPB components is performed using procedures qualified per ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-4300, and Section IX. Control of welding variables, as well as examination and testing during both procedure qualification and production welding, is conducted in accordance with ASME Code requirements. Welders and welding operators are qualified according to ASME Section IX and Regulatory Guide 1.71, Revision 1. Inspections comply with selected Class 1 requirements of ASME BPVC Section XI, Subsection IWB (as specified in Table 6.2-8), and all Section XI, Subsection IWE required inspections are met.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, details the integrity and leakage detection methods for the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). Inspections for evidence of reactor coolant system (RCS) leakage include visual and nondestructive techniques, with specified inspection frequencies. The leak detection system is capable of identifying leakage from both the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and Containment Vessel (CNV) during normal operation. The body-to-bonnet seals on the ECCS trip/reset actuator valve, which form part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, are tested at RCS operating pressure before operation. Unlike traditional light water reactors, each NuScale Power Module (NPM) RCS does not use components with designed leakage rates, such as pump seals or valve stem shafts. The CES condensate monitoring system provides real-time indication and alarms in the main control room, enabling monitoring and trending of liquid leakage into the containment atmosphere. This system can detect RCS leak rates of less than 1 gpm within one hour and has a minimum detectable leak rate of less than 0.05 gpm. Diverse detection methods are used to help identify the location of reactor coolant leakage, ensuring operators have sufficient time to act before technical specification limits are reached. Figure 5.2-3 illustrates containment leakage detection acceptability.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report specifies that threaded fasteners used in the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) main closure flange, pressurizer (PZR) heater bundle closures, reactor coolant system (RCS) piping flanges, and reactor safety valve (RSV) flanges are made of nickel-based Alloy 718 (UNS N07718). Table 5.2-4 details the materials for reactor coolant pressure boundary components: the lower RPV section flange shell and bottom head use SA-508 Grade 3, Class 1, while the RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum, upper RPV flanged transition shell, upper and lower RPV steam generator (SG) shells, and feed plenum access ports use SA-508 Grade 3, Class 2. RPV support gussets and plates are SA-533 Type B, Class 2. Core barrel guides are SA-479 or SA-240 Type 304 (max 0.03% carbon), and core support blocks are SA-240 Type 304L. Various instrument seal assemblies and connectors use SA-240 or SA-479 Type 304/304L or 316/316L, with set screws of SA-193 B8, Class 1. RPV flange leak detection tubes are SA-312 Type 316L seamless. Studs, nuts, and washers for main RPV flange, RSV flanges, RPV instrument seal assemblies, and PZR heater access ports are SB-637 Alloy 718. PZR pressure taps and thermowell nozzles use SB-166 Alloy 690 (UNS N06690), and PZR heater closure flanges use SB-168 Alloy 690. Weld filler materials are specified for low alloy steel (SFA 5.5, 5.23, 5.28, 5.29), stainless steel (SFA 5.4, 5.9, 5.22; E308, E308L, E309, E309L, E316, E316L, ER308, ER308L, ER309, ER309L, ER316, ER316L, EQ308L, EQ309L), and nickel-based alloys (SFA-5.11, 5.14; ENiCrFe-7, ERNiCrFe-7, ERNiCrFe-7A, EQNiCrFe-7, EQNiCrFe-7A). SG tube supports and upper/lower SG supports are SA-240 Type 304/304L, and the integral steam plenum cap is SB-564 Alloy 690. RCS piping uses SA-312 Grade TP304/304L, with reducers and elbows of SA-479 Type 304/304L and tee connections of SA-182 Grade F304/F304L. RCS piping supports and pressurizer support plates are SA-

NuScale reactor vessel internals inspection requirements are developed in accordance with the Materials Reliability Program (MRP) 227 to supplement the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), Section XI, with no exceptions identified in applying the MRP approach. Table 5.2-7 details the inservice inspection (ISI) examination categories and methods for the reactor vessel internals. The reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated to ensure a minimum end-of-life Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy of 50 ft-lb, with an initial minimum value of 75 ft-lb. Figure 5.3-2 illustrates the core barrel horizontal cross-section, showing the locations of the four capsule holders and their elevation on the core barrel. Plant operating procedures are developed to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, as addressed in Section 13.5, maintaining compliance with technical specifications during normal operation and anticipated transients. Nondestructive examination requirements are described in Section 5.3.1. The inspection program references the ASME BPVC, 2013 Edition, Section XI, "Rules for Inservice Inspection of Nuclear Power Plant Components."

Figure 5.2-3 presents a containment pressure saturation curve as a function of reactor pool bulk temperature, incorporating adjustments for containment pressure instrumentation uncertainty. The pressure-temperature curves referenced do not include location correction or instrument uncertainty; for location correction, allowable pressure is considered at the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) bottom. Reactor criticality is only permitted when pressure-temperature conditions are to the right of the criticality curve in Figure 5.3-3. Table 5.3-6 details pressure-temperature limits for normal heatup (both core not critical and core critical) and normal cooldown, with minimum core critical temperatures determined from transient and steady-state ISLH (Inservice Leak and Hydrostatic) curves. Table 5.3-7 provides pressure-temperature limits for inservice leak and hydrostatic tests, including transient and steady-state ISLH values. Figure 5.3-2 shows the location of surveillance capsules, while Figures 5.3-3, 5.3-4, and 5.3-5 illustrate pressure-temperature limits for normal heatup and criticality, normal cooldown with decay heat removal and containment vessel flooding, and inservice leak and hydrostatic tests, respectively.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, details the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, specifying inspection and examination requirements for various components. Reactor vent valve flanges, reactor safety valves, RPV high point degasification, and CRDM nozzles are addressed, with inside corner region examinations not required for pressurizer nozzles per ASME BPVC Section XI. For RPV main flange bolts (B-G-1), surface examination is permitted when bolts are removed, as noted in B-G-1. Table 5.2-8 outlines that dual valve, single body isolation valves to safe-end (B-J) require both surface and volumetric examinations, with further discussion on welds in Section 5.2.4.1. Table 5.2-9 lists Class 1 support inspection elements: CVCS to PZR, RPV ledge gussets, CRDM legs, and containment bolting (F-A) require VT-3 examination; RCS discharge/injection piping, PZR spray supply, and various support connections (B-N-1) also require VT-3; and RCCW to containment head, CRDM lower legs, DHRS piping, NPM platform, and DHRS heat exchanger supports (B-K) require surface examination. Figure 5.2-2 provides a simplified diagram of the reactor safety valve pilot valve assembly, showing the main valve piston, pilot spring, bellows, pilot disc, pilot seat, and safety valve main body, with flow from the main valve inlet to the main valve piston.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, is prepared in accordance with 10 CFR 52.47(a) as part of the standard design certification application under 10 CFR 52, Subpart B. The report includes detailed descriptions of the plant's general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, with process effluent radiation monitoring and sampling systems addressed in Section 11.5.1.2.2. The NuScale Power Plant features a nominal net output of 570 MWe, comprising 12 power modules, each with a single reactor rated at 160 MWth thermal power and a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at a normal pressure of 1,850 psia and utilizes two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, operating on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine with extraction. The reactor core uses UO2 fuel with enrichment below 4.95% and has a 24-month refueling interval. Key safety enhancements include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps, thereby eliminating pump-related accident scenarios. The high containment design pressure ensures that even in the worst-case design-basis accident, containment integrity is maintained, minimizing the potential for radioactive releases. Figures in the report illustrate the functional boundaries, schematic of a single power module, and the layout of a multi-module plant, with certain structures such as the administration and training building and warehouse excluded from the FSAR scope.

Sections 1.4.1 and Table 5.4-4 are marked as "Not Used." Section 1.5.3 provides references on page 1.5-8, and section 1.6 lists material referenced. Section 5.1.1 covers the design basis, followed by section 5.1.2, which contains the system description on page 5.1-2, and section 5.1.3, which details system components. Section 5.1.4 presents the system evaluation. Section 5.3.4 and section 5.4.6 both provide references, located on pages 5.3-7 and 5.4-40, respectively.

The provided passages reference several tables and figures detailing the design and characteristics of the NuScale Power Plant and its modules. Table 1.2-1 presents the overall characteristics of a NuScale Power Plant, while Table 1.2-2 outlines specific design features of a NuScale Power Module. Figure 1.2-2 defines the functional boundaries of the NuScale system, and Figure 1.2-3 provides a schematic of a single NuScale Power Module with its associated secondary equipment. Figures 1.2-4 and 1.2-5 illustrate the layout of a multi-module NuScale plant and a cutaway of the 12-module configuration, respectively. Figure 1.2-6 offers a cutaway view of a NuScale Power Module, and Figure 1.2-7 depicts the steam generator and reactor flow. Table 1.6-2 lists referenced technical reports for NuScale, and Table 1.7-1 provides instrumentation and controls functional and electrical one-line diagrams. Additionally, NuScale Power, LLC’s report TR-0516-49417-P-A Revision 1 describes the evaluation methodology for stability analysis of the NuScale Power Module.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides a detailed comparison between the NuScale Power Plant and a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR) based on nominal values. The NuScale module (NPM) has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a core thermal output of 160 MWt, compared to 1,186 MWe and 3,411 MWt for a typical PWR. NuScale uses 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, 6.56 ft effective length, 264 rods per assembly) versus 193 assemblies (17x17 lattice, 12 ft length) in a PWR. The average linear heat rate is 2.5 kW/ft for NuScale and 5.4 kW/ft for PWR. NuScale has 16 control rod assemblies and a 60-year design life, compared to 53 assemblies and 40 years for PWR. The NuScale reactor coolant system has no external loops or coolant pipes, operates at 1,850 psia, and uses a stacked stainless steel reflector block for thermal shielding, with top-mounted in-core instrumentation. Its steam generator is a helical coil type (2 units, ~18,000 ft² area, 1,380 tubes), while PWR uses four vertical U-tube generators (55,000 ft², 5,626 tubes). NuScale has no reactor coolant pumps, pressurizer surge nozzle, or residual heat removal pumps, and its containment is a steel pressure vessel (14 ft 2 in. diameter, 75 ft 8.5 in. height), compared to a PCCV (140 ft diameter, 205 ft height) for PWR. Many traditional PWR safety systems—such as containment spray, high/low pressure safety injection, accumulators, emergency diesel generators, and emergency feedwater pumps—are not present in NuScale. The NuScale I&C system is digital, and it uses 6 reactor component cooling water pumps for 12 NPMs. Table 1.3-2 shows that both designs include core protection systems such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment vessel, reactor coolant system, decay heat removal, emergency core cooling, control rod drive, containment isolation, and ultimate heat sink. However, several PWR safety systems (e.g., residual heat removal, safety injection, auxiliary feedwater, emergency service water, hydrogen recombiner, containment spray, reactor coolant pumps, safety-related electrical distribution, off-site power, emergency diesel generators, Class 1E battery, and ATWS system) are not required in the NuScale design.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is a modular, movable nuclear steam supply system (NSSS) comprising a reactor core, pressurizer, and two steam generators integrated within a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and housed in a compact steel containment vessel (CNV). The NuScale plant design is scalable, supporting from one to twelve NPMs within a single Reactor Building, with each module rated at 160 MWt (totaling 1,920 MWt for 12 modules) and approximately 50 MWe electrical output per module (600 MWe total), with a net output of about 570 MWe for a 12-module facility after accounting for house loads. The plant includes all necessary primary and secondary systems, such as main steam systems, turbine generator sets, condensate and feedwater systems, shared external cooling water systems, module assembly equipment, fuel handling equipment, and turbine maintenance equipment. The PIM code, developed by NuScale, is used to assess NPM stability during operation, while the NIST-1 is a scaled stainless steel representation of the NPM reactor, containment, and reactor pool systems, with a maximum operating pressure of 1650 psia (11.4 MPa) and temperature of 630°F (605 K). The design and safety bases of the reactor coolant system (RCS) and its major components are closely interrelated, and the facility’s documentation generally describes individual modules, with multi-module information provided where relevant.

The NuScale Power Plant features a compact nuclear island and is capable of operating in "island mode." It is designed for sites with characteristics (seismology, hydrology, meteorology, geology, etc.) within the bounds specified in Chapter 2, Site Characteristics. The plant includes two sets of cooling towers for 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs). Principal design criteria emphasize a simple, safe reactor with reliable, passive safety systems that do not rely on electrical power, and safety features that ensure a core damage frequency significantly lower than current light water reactor fleets. Key NPM features include a small, modular design; an integral pressurized water reactor (PWR) nuclear steam supply system (NSSS) that combines the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating external piping; natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, removing the need for reactor coolant pumps; an RPV housed in a steel containment partially immersed in water for passive heat removal; and a steel containment operated at a vacuum, eliminating the need for RPV insulation. The integral design eliminates external coolant loop piping, removing large-break loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) scenarios. Passive safety systems provide decay heat removal, emergency core cooling, and control room habitability without external power, ensuring that small-break LOCAs do not challenge plant safety. The design results in a lower core damage frequency and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional 1,000 MWe reactors, reducing the amount of radioactive material available for release during postulated accidents.

The NuScale Power Plant, comprising 12 operating NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), delivers a net total output of approximately 570 MWe. The plant design features no reactor pressure vessel (RPV) or containment penetrations below the top of the reactor core and utilizes modular construction for in-shop fabrication of reactor and containment components. Each NPM operates at full power using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps. The reactor core consists of 37 fuel assemblies and 16 control rod assemblies (CRAs). The torispherical RPV head supports the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs) and includes 2” to 8” diameter penetrations for pressurizer spray, reactor vent valves, reactor safety valves, degasification instrumentation, I&C instrument channels, and CRDM nozzles. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) provides passive decay heat removal during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) and consists of three independent reactor vent valves and two independent reactor recirculation valves, all closed during normal operation. The module protection system (MPS) monitors safety-related parameters such as temperature, flow, neutron flux, and pressure, while the neutron monitoring system provides core power indication and safety inputs to the MPS. The power conversion system includes a main steam system, turbine generator set, standard condenser and cooling tower, and condensate and feedwater system. Hoisting and handling equipment, as well as pipe fittings and electrical connections, are located above the reactor pool water level for manual connection/disconnection during NPM installation, refueling, or removal. Refueling operations for each NPM are independent of the status of other modules, and the plant allows for shipment of used NPMs off-site. The cooling system is a closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation system with two trains of decay heat removal equipment—each train, attached to a steam generator (SG) loop and equipped with a passive condenser immersed in the reactor pool, is capable of removing 100% of the decay heat load and cooling the reactor coolant system (RCS).

The NuScale Power Plant includes several key structures classified as CDI, such as the Turbine Generator Buildings (TGBs) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs). There are two nonsafety-related TGBs, each housing six turbine generator sets, their auxiliaries, condensers, condensate systems, and feedwater systems. The plant also features two DGBs, each containing a single backup diesel generator, associated support equipment, generators, a DGB HVAC system, and a maintenance area; these buildings house no safety-related systems and do not support engineered safety features (ESFs). The Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) provides cooling water to plant auxiliary systems. Additionally, the Central Utility Building (CUB), also nonsafety-related, contains common utility services such as chiller equipment, instrument and service air systems, chemical treatment and demineralized water equipment, maintenance and life safety areas, and security functions. The plant design emphasizes minimizing human error through fail-safe design, centralized multi-modular control, advanced automation, digital displays, soft control technology, and optimized workload management.

NuScale has conducted and is continuing several testing programs focused on unique design features of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) lacking prior data or operational experience. Central to these efforts is the NuScale Integral System Test Facility (NIST-1) at Oregon State University, which replicates integrated thermal-hydraulic phenomena in the reactor coolant system, containment, safety systems, and reactor pool. NIST-1 is scaled from NPM design volumes, lengths, heat transfer, and flow areas using detailed scaling analysis to ensure accurate simulation of plant processes. Data from NIST-1 support validation of safety-related software, specifically the NRELAP5 and PIM codes. NRELAP5, developed by NuScale and based on RELAP5-3D, is tailored for NPM-specific thermal-hydraulic design, safety analysis, and licensing, while PIM is used for reactor stability analyses. Completed NIST-1 tests include facility characterization for NRELAP5 model development, loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) tests for LOCA and containment analysis validation, flow-stability tests for PIM validation, non-LOCA (anticipated operational occurrence) tests for NRELAP5 validation, and long-term cooling tests for NRELAP5. The resulting data have successfully validated NRELAP5 and PIM for LOCA, containment, non-LOCA, flow stability, and long-term cooling applications. The NRC inspected this test program under Inspection Procedures IP 35034, 35017, and 36100.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details several test programs and methodologies supporting the NuScale reactor design. Temperature measurements were used to determine mixing coefficients and to obtain single-phase and two-phase pressure drop characteristics across a range of bundle powers and hydraulic conditions, providing all necessary data for critical heat flux (CHF) correlation development and evaluation. The primary objective of the CHF testing program was to obtain CHF data for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology, thereby augmenting the existing database from the preliminary fuel design. Additionally, the NuScale integral system test program generated thermal-hydraulic data for system characterization and safety code validation using a scaled representation of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, also informing safety methodology development. NuScale specifically evaluates CHF, which is considered more applicable to its design than the departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR). Reference is made to NuScale Power, LLC’s “NuScale Power Critical Heat Flux Correlations,” TR-0116-21012-P-A, Revision 1.

Section 1.9.5 addresses advanced and evolutionary light-water reactor (ALWR) design issues in accordance with guidance from SRP Section 1.0, referencing NRC policy and licensing issues documented in SECY-93-087 and its associated staff requirements memorandum (SRM) dated July 21, 1993. Table 1.9-7 provides a conformance assessment for each design issue identified in relevant SECYs and SRMs, including any exceptions. For example, SECY-90-016, which focused on evolutionary LWR certification issues, is noted as "Partially Conforms" due to its original applicability to evolutionary ALWRs, with later applicability to passive plants established in SECY-93-087 and SECY-94-084. SECY-94-302, addressing source-term-related technical and licensing issues for evolutionary and passive LWR designs, is marked as "Conforms" and has been incorporated into 10 CFR 52 and NRC guidance documents. Additional details and conformance status for specific issues are provided in Table 1.9-8, which continues the assessment for SECY-93-087 issues. Relevant sections referenced include 5.4, 8.1–8.4, 9.2.5, Appendix 9A, 15.0.4, 18.7, 18.10, and 19.3.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) includes detailed conformance assessments with regulatory criteria for advanced and evolutionary light water reactor (ALWR) designs, specifically referencing SECY-93-087, "Policy, Technical, and Licensing Issues Pertaining to Evolutionary and Advanced Light-Water Reactor Designs." Table 1.9-7 provides a cross-reference between SECY issues and the corresponding FSAR sections, confirming conformance with documents such as SECY-89-013 and SECY-93-087, with further details addressed in Table 1.9-8. Table 1.9-8 presents a separate assessment of SECY-93-087 line items, including the use of a physically-based source term that incorporates engineering judgment and a more realistic source term in design, deviating from the siting requirements in 10 CFR 100. The FSAR also references the NuScale Power, LLC “Gap Analysis Summary Report,” NP-RT-0612-023, Revision 1, and confirms adherence to guidelines for evaluating fatigue analyses related to the life reduction of metal components due to light-water reactor environments for new reactors. Figure 1.2-1 provides the conceptual site layout.

The FSAR generally follows the format and content guidelines of Regulatory Guide 1.206, with some sections skipped or additional sections inserted as applicable. Notably, it includes Chapter 20, Mitigation of Beyond-Design-Basis Events, and Chapter 21, Multi-Module Design Considerations, which are not part of RG 1.206. The technical guidance from the Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) and Standard Review Plan (SRP) forms the basis for the NuScale design, with a detailed evaluation of conformance provided in Section 1.9.1. Tables and figures are identified by their section number and numbered sequentially. The FSAR does not contain proprietary or safeguards information, but some sensitive portions are withheld from public disclosure in accordance with 10 CFR 2.390 and RIS 2005-26; these are clearly marked, and a separate public version is provided. Acronyms and abbreviations used in the FSAR are listed in Table 1.1-1. Table 1.9-2 presents an evaluation of conformance with NRC regulatory guides effective six months prior to the FSAR submittal, including descriptions of deviations and justifications for alternative approaches.

NuScale identifies certain regulations within the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) as literally applicable but seeks departures based on the unique design and safety basis of the NuScale reactor, arguing that strict conformance may have minimal or negative safety impacts given the regulations were developed for traditional light water reactors (LWRs). Departures may be pursued via exemption requests under 10 CFR 52.7 or other specific regulatory processes. For example, NuScale considers departure from Three Mile Island (TMI) requirements appropriate when such requirements are not suitable for its design. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) documents conformance with regulatory guides, noting full conformance with RG 1.28 (Quality Assurance Program Criteria), but identifies several guides as not applicable, such as RG 5.73 (Fatigue Management for Nuclear Power Plant Personnel) and multiple sections of Digital I&C-ISG-04, which are instead addressed in topical report TR-1015-18653-A. For Digital I&C-ISG-05, NuScale partially conforms, with site-specific operational elements deferred to the COL applicant. Additionally, NuScale supports an exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii), as documented in Table 1.9-8 regarding conformance with SECY-93-087.

The Regulatory Guide (RG) endorses ISA-67.04.01-1994, but the NuScale Instrument Setpoint Methodology Technical Report (TR-0616-49121) applies guidance from ISA-67.04.01-2006; a key distinction is that the 1994 version uses an allowable value to determine instrument channel operability during surveillance testing and calibration. NuScale’s approach to safety classification of structures, systems, and components (SSC) combines the traditional definitions from 10 CFR 50.2, RG 1.26, and SRP Section 3.2.2 with an alternative regulatory framework similar to 10 CFR 50.69, RG 1.201, and NEI 00-04 (endorsed by RG 1.201).
