Regarding SECY-94-084 and the Regulatory Treatment of Non-Safety Systems in Passive Plant Design (RTNSS), NuScale’s Fire Protection System contains no RTNSS equipment, and the guidance is partially incorporated into 10 CFR 52 and NRC guidance documents. NuScale conforms to the latest endorsed codes and standards, or others on a case-by-case basis. For electrical distribution, NuScale addresses positions from SECY-91-078 by providing an alternate power source to nonsafety-related loads and ensuring at least one offsite circuit is connected directly to each redundant safety division without intervening nonsafety-related buses.

The NuScale passive Advanced Light Water Reactor (ALWR) design adheres to the passive plant guidance outlined in SECY-93-087 and SECY-94-084, rather than SECY-90-016, as detailed in Table 1.9-8. SECY-91-078, which addresses evolutionary ALWR designs and Chapter 11 of the EPRI Requirements Document, is not directly applicable to passive plant designs like NuScale; however, Section C of SECY-91-078, concerning safe shutdown requirements for passive ALWRs, is relevant to the NuScale Power Plant. The design partially conforms to Section IV of SECY-96-128, which discusses policy and key technical issues for the Westinghouse AP600 standardized passive reactor. SECY-14-038, regarding a performance-based framework for nuclear power plant emergency preparedness oversight, is not applicable. The applicability of the SECY-90-016 Station Blackout (SBO) issue to passive ALWRs was deferred and addressed in SECY-94-084 and SECY-95-132, with NuScale conforming to the guidance in these documents. Additionally, fire protection positions focus on design configuration and features of the fire protection system and other management schemes to ensure safe reactor shutdown.

The referenced SECY documents address key regulatory and technical issues for advanced and evolutionary nuclear reactor designs. SECY-91-074 on prototype decisions and SECY-95-132 on regulatory treatment of non-safety systems in passive plant designs have both been incorporated into 10 CFR 52 and related NRC guidance documents. SECY-91-229, covering severe accident mitigation design alternatives for certified standard designs, and SECY-91-262, addressing the resolution of selected technical and severe accident issues for evolutionary LWR designs, have been incorporated into NRC Orders, regulatory guidance, and are subject to pending rulemaking. All documents are noted as conforming to current regulatory frameworks.

The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

The NuScale Nuclear Steam Supply System (NSSS) is a passive, NuScale-designed small modular pressurized water reactor (PWR), with each NuScale Power Module (NPM) featuring its own compact steel containment vessel mounted to the Reactor Building module compartment walls and at the bottom within the Reactor Building pool. Unlike traditional large light water reactor (LWR) designs, the NuScale containment vessel does not have subcompartments, internal compartments as referenced in GDC 50, or reactor coolant loops, and does not house high-energy piping or fuel storage and handling systems inside containment. Instead, fuel handling operations are performed in the Reactor Building pool where the NPMs reside, making containment design provisions for such operations inapplicable to NuScale’s containment vessel. The NuScale containment vessel’s high-pressure capability eliminates the need for features such as a 3-foot opening for venting, which were requirements for traditional large LWR containment structures.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) relies on inherent passive design features for fission product removal and control, with the only credited system being the containment vessel (CNV) in conjunction with the containment isolation system. Fission product control is achieved through passive aerosol deposition within the CNV atmosphere, as detailed in Section 6.5 of the ESF documentation. The NuScale containment vessel does not include dedicated fission product cleanup systems, pressure suppression systems (such as suppression pools), or active containment heat removal systems like containment spray for dose mitigation. Instead, the compact containment vessel is submerged in the reactor pool, enhancing passive fission product control. Although the design includes an evacuation system, it is not a purge system and is intended to prevent radiological release to the environment, as referenced in DSRS 6.2.4, AC II.13.

Boric acid concentrations for the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design were selected to bound both refueling and operating boron concentrations, with tests conducted across the full range of reactor operating pressures and temperatures; this test program was inspected by the NRC per Inspection Procedures IP35034.1.5.2, and further details on startup testing are provided in Section 14.2. The NuScale design does not utilize combustible gas control systems and supports an exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), as detailed in DCA Part 7, section 2. Additionally, the design supports an exemption from 50.34(f)(2)(xiv) and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, regarding containment isolation valve closure times, with no deviations noted. Hydrogen control systems within containment are not required, as combustion does not impact containment vessel (CNV) integrity, further supporting the exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2). The design also supports an exemption to GDC 27, complying instead with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion as described in Section 3.1.4. For post-accident assessment, the NuScale design relies on radiation monitors under the bioshield and core exit temperature indications, supporting an exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding the requirement to obtain and analyze post-accident samples of the reactor coolant system and containment without exceeding prescribed radiation dose limits.

The NuScale Power Plant design does not utilize or credit engineered safety feature (ESF) filter or ventilation systems, including ESF atmosphere cleanup systems, for mitigating the consequences of a design basis accident. Instead, nonsafety-related normal ventilation systems, such as the Reactor Building HVAC system, provide atmosphere cleanup capability as necessary, in accordance with the design, testing, and maintenance guidelines of RG 1.140, but these systems are not credited in dose analyses or for meeting offsite dose limits. The reactor building ventilation system is not credited for venting steam or moisture to protect safety-related components, as such protection is not required by the design. The Turbine Building HVAC system (TBVS) is also not relied upon to control airborne radioactivity or gaseous effluents during normal or accident conditions, nor is its performance required to support safety-related functions. For control room habitability, the NuScale design uses compressed air tanks to supply clean air during postulated accident events, eliminating the potential for radioactive material or toxic gases to enter the control room via ventilation inlets, and does not rely on emergency filtration systems. References to ESF ventilation systems are therefore not applicable to the NuScale design, and operational or programmatic activities related to these systems are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee.

The NuScale ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System) design differs from traditional systems by not utilizing high-pressure or low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, or a sump. Instead, core decay heat removal is achieved through steam condensation and natural reactor coolant recirculation. During ECCS operation, water accumulates inside the NuScale containment vessel and is recirculated back to the reactor core, fulfilling the intent of relevant acceptance criteria. The design does not require personnel access during reactor operation, does not include pumps, and does not rely on containment pressure for ECCS recirculation as in typical PWRs. Consequently, purge or vent capability as specified by 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xv) is not required or included. Pre-operational testing of the ECCS will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.

The NuScale design does not require operator action to mitigate debris accumulation and partially complies with certain regulatory positions. Specifically, it does not comply with regulatory position C1.1, except for the intent of positions C1.1.1.9 (assessment of downstream clogging) and C1.1.1.10 (buildup of debris and chemical reaction products downstream). The design does not comply with regulatory position C.1.2 regarding alternative water sources for inoperable strainers. While NuScale complies with the intent of regulatory position C.1.3 (evaluation of long-term recirculation capability), it does not comply with position C.1.3.1 (net positive suction head), portions of C.1.3.2 inconsistent with the design, C1.3.7 (upstream effects), or C.1.3.9 (strainer structural integrity). Additionally, the design does not comply with C1.3.12 (prototypical head loss testing), C.2 (except for the intent of chemical reaction effects in position 2.2), or C.3. For conservatism, the NuScale design uses 0.10% Cu instead of the maximum 0.06 wt% Cu.

The NuScale reactor design features an integral pressurizer located within the reactor pressure vessel, eliminating the need for a pressurizer surge line and thus avoiding issues of thermal stratification. Unlike conventional designs, NuScale does not use power-operated relief valves (PORVs), which are susceptible to inadvertent opening; instead, it employs spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves that do not share this vulnerability. The design does not rely on pressurizer heaters to establish or maintain natural circulation during hot shutdown conditions, making the requirements for pressurizer heater power supply and associated control interfaces in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiii) inapplicable—NuScale considers its hot shutdown condition equivalent to the regulatory hot standby condition. Furthermore, NuScale does not include pressurizer relief or block valves, rendering the power supply requirements for these components in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) not technically relevant. For safe shutdown (Mode 3) with reactor coolant temperatures below 200°F, NuScale equates this state to cold shutdown as defined in OM Code ISTA-2000, and its DHRS actuation valves meet ASME OM Code exercising and observation requirements. Overall, these design features support exemptions from certain TMI-related regulatory requirements.

The NuScale reactor design does not require or include reactor coolant pumps, and therefore, safety-related pumps are not used in the design. The only pumps within the scope of safety criteria are the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) pumps, which are classified as ASME Class III because they contain reactor coolant during normal operation, but they do not serve any safety function. Additionally, the potential for a postulated startup reactivity accident, such as one initiated by an abnormal startup sequence, has been identified as an event requiring consideration in the NuScale reactor design.

The NuScale design employs passive natural circulation of the primary coolant, which eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps. This approach conforms to DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials, specifically regarding the selection, processing, testing, and inspection of materials, with no exceptions noted.

The NuScale design supports exemptions from several NRC regulatory requirements, including 10 CFR 50.46a and 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(vi) related to reactor coolant system high point venting, and portions of 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) concerning pressurizer level indicators. The NuScale Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) does not include holdup tanks subject to vacuum conditions as described in Bulletin 80-05, but will provide appropriate venting and draining capability as required by the last sentence of the relevant acceptance criterion. The design's features eliminate the need for high-point vents, and the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) high-point degasification vent does not perform a specific safety function.

The NuScale reactor design features an LTOP (Low Temperature Overpressure Protection) system that is not connected to a low-pressure system, but the guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation from the primary system remains applicable. NuScale supports an exemption from selected portions of 10 CFR 50, Appendix K, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, regarding Mass and Energy Release Analysis for Postulated Loss-of-Coolant Accidents (LOCAs), with no additional inspections, tests, analyses, or acceptance criteria (ITAAC) required. In the event of a LOCA, the NuScale design ensures that core uncovery does not occur, and it conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 regarding the sharing of structures, systems, and components, with no exceptions. The design does not credit or utilize a suppression pool and conforms to DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, for inservice inspection and testing of Class 2 and 3 components, with all relevant components subject to inspection and no additional requirements.

The NuScale reactor design omits a containment spray system and sump, but during Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) operation, water accumulates inside the containment vessel for recirculation to the reactor core, making the pH guideline in the acceptance criterion applicable. The ECCS design does not utilize pumps or refueling water storage tanks, and operates via natural circulation without the need for pumps. The condensate storage systems and components are located outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, and failure of these facilities poses no reasonable risk to safety-related systems or plant operation. The Instrumentation and Controls (I&C) review process described in DSRS 7.0 applies to the Design Certification Application (DCA), but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Regarding gas accumulation, NuScale has determined that gas buildup will not affect ECCS performance under accident conditions; the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) does not interface with the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) and is instead connected to the secondary system.

The NuScale plant design does not include an Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system typical of large Light Water Reactors (LWRs), but instead utilizes a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) that serves a similar function. The NuScale steam generator design eliminates potential water hammer issues without requiring DHRS water to be supplied through an externally mounted top discharge header, as specified by certain acceptance criteria. For spent fuel pool (SFP) mitigation, NuScale is exempt from NEI 06-12 requirements because its SFP is located below grade and cannot be drained. The design does not use power-operated relief valves, and the Anticipated Transient Without Scram (ATWS) provisions are not technically relevant, as NuScale relies on protection system diversity to prevent ATWS rather than on relief and safety valves for mitigation. Consequently, the module response to ATWS is not analyzed in FSAR Section 15.8, and the performance of relief and safety valves is not relied upon to meet ATWS safety criteria. Section 19.2 of the FSAR provides further information on the NuScale Power Plant's response to anticipated transients without scram.

The provided passages summarize conformance with various Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and ASME Code requirements for nuclear plant materials and fabrication processes. For threaded fasteners in ASME Code Class 1, 2, and 3 applications, the design aspects, including Table 3.13-1, conform to SRP 3.13, Rev. For reactor internal and core support structure materials, both austenitic stainless steels and other materials conform to SRP 4.5.2, Rev 3. Regarding reactor coolant pressure boundary materials, the fabrication and processing of ferritic materials, control of ferritic steel welding, and non-destructive examination (NDE) of both ferritic and austenitic stainless steel tubular products all conform to SRP 5.2.3, Rev 3. For engineered safety features materials, both austenitic stainless steels and ferritic steel welding conform to SRP 6.1.1, Rev 2. No exceptions or deviations are noted in any section.

BTP 3-1, Rev 2 guidance on classification of main steam components other than the reactor coolant pressure boundary applies only to BWR plants and is not applicable in this context. SRP 5.2.3, Rev 3, Section II.1 (Material Specifications) and Section II.2 (Compatibility of Materials with the Reactor Coolant) are partially conforming, as their acceptance criteria are applicable except for references to subtier guidance relevant only to BWRs. Section II.4.G (Operational Programs) of SRP 5.2.3, Rev 3 is not applicable, as it pertains to plant-specific programmatic information that is the responsibility of the COL applicant.

The reviewed sections confirm full conformance with BTP 3-3, Rev 3 requirements for protection against postulated piping failures in fluid systems outside containment, specifically regarding plant arrangement (B.1), design features (B.2), analyses and effects of postulated piping failures (B.3), and implementation (B.4), with no exceptions noted. Additionally, the documentation demonstrates conformance with BTP 3-4, Rev 2 for postulated rupture locations in fluid system piping both inside and outside containment, covering high-energy fluid system piping (B.A), moderate-energy fluid system piping (B.B), and types of breaks and leakage cracks in fluid system piping (B.C), again with no exceptions identified. Relevant sections referenced include 3.6, 15.1, 15.2, 15.5, and 15.6.

The review of SRP 5.2.2, Rev 3: Overpressure Protection indicates that material specifications (II.1) conform to requirements, while design requirements for BWRs operating at power (II.2) are not applicable, as the guidance pertains only to BWR plants. For PWRs operating at low temperature during startup and shutdown (II.4), as well as testing and inspections (II.5), the criteria are met and conform to the guidance. However, for technical specifications (II.6), only partial conformance is achieved because certain subtier guidance documents referenced in the acceptance criterion are either not applicable or only partially applicable.

Section 5.2.3 of SRP 5.2.3, Rev 3, addresses Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Materials. For II.3.A (Fracture Toughness - 10 CFR 50, Appendix G), the criteria are fully met with no exceptions. For II.4 (Fabrication and Processing of Austenitic Stainless Steel), II.4.A (GDC 4 Compatibility of Components - Measures to Avoid Sensitization), II.4.B (GDC 4 Compatibility of Components - Controls to Avoid Stress Corrosion Cracking), and II.4.D (Control of Welding of Austenitic Stainless Steels), the criteria are only partially met; the exceptions pertain to references and subtier guidance applicable solely to BWRs or large LWRs using nonmetallic thermal insulation, or to Regulatory Guide 1.37 (endorsing NQA-1-1994). NuScale does not use nonmetallic thermal insulation on reactor coolant pressure boundary components, making related guidance not applicable.

The reviewed sections from DSRS 5.3.1, Rev 0 (Reactor Vessel Materials) and DSRS 5.3.3, Rev 0 (Reactor Vessel Integrity) confirm conformance with requirements for reactor vessel materials, including the selection of materials, special manufacturing and fabrication processes, nondestructive examination methods, special controls for ferritic and austenitic stainless steels, fracture toughness, material surveillance, and reactor vessel fasteners. Additionally, conformance is noted for reactor vessel integrity regarding materials of construction, fabrication methods, inspection requirements, and shipment and installation. For steam generator materials (DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0), the fabrication and processing of austenitic stainless steel also conform to requirements. No exceptions or deviations are identified in any section.

The passages review compliance with DSRS 5.3.2, Rev 0, covering Pressure-Temperature Limits, Upper-Shelf Energy, and Pressurized Thermal Shock. The sections confirm conformance with applicable regulations, codes, and basis documents for pressure-temperature requirements (II.1.B), upper-shelf energy requirements (II.2.B), and pressurized thermal shock requirements (II.3.B), as well as the relevant regulatory basis (II.1.A, II.3.A). Additionally, DSRS 5.3.3, Rev 0, Reactor Vessel Integrity, Section II.1 (Design), is confirmed as conforming. No exceptions or additional comments are noted in any section.

The review of BTP 5-1, Rev 3, regarding monitoring of secondary side water chemistry in PWR steam generators, indicates that the Secondary Water Chemistry Program meets industry guidelines and fully conforms to acceptance criterion B.1. However, for B.2 (Sampling Schedule for Critical Parameters) and B.3 (Records), the criteria are only partially met, as some requirements are site-specific and are the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the certified design. Criterion B.4 (Program Change Evaluation and Reporting) is not applicable and is also the responsibility of the COL applicant. For BTP 6-1 (March 2007), concerning pH for emergency coolant water in PWRs, the minimum pH requirement (B.1) and hydrogen generation from aluminum corrosion (B.3) both conform to the acceptance criteria, though certain language in BTP 6-1 refers to SSCs not present in the NuScale design.

The overpressurization protection system for pressurized-water reactors operating at low temperatures, as described in BTP 5-2, Rev 3, conforms to NRC regulatory requirements and technical specifications in the following areas: system design, installation, and capabilities (B.1); ability to withstand a single active component failure (B.3); instrumentation and controls design (B.4); operability testing (B.5); applicable guidance (B.6); and design to withstand an operating-basis earthquake (B.7). Analyses considering inadvertent system actuation (B.9) also conform. However, for interlocks to ensure overpressure protection (B.10), the system only partially conforms, as the acceptance criterion is intended for large LWR designs with pressure relief from a low-pressure system not normally connected to the primary system. No additional comments or exceptions were noted for the other criteria.

Section 5.2.2 of BTP 5-3, Rev 2 addresses fracture toughness requirements, specifying that preservice fracture toughness test requirements partially conform, with exceptions noted for Acceptance Criteria 1.1 and 1.2. Section 5.3 covers operating limitations for fracture toughness, confirming full conformance for general operating limitations (2), pressure-temperature operating limitations (2.1), recommended bases for operating limitations (2.2), and reporting requirements (2.3), with no additional comments. Inservice surveillance of fracture toughness (3) partially conforms, with exceptions for Acceptance Criteria 3.4 and 3.5, while surveillance program requirements (3.1), SAR criteria (3.2), and surveillance test procedures (3.3) all fully conform without further comment.

BTP 5-3, Rev 2: Fracture Toughness Requirements, Section 1.1 on determination of RTNDT for vessel materials partially conforms, as some acceptance criteria apply only to older plants lacking comprehensive fracture toughness testing, while the remaining guidance is applicable to the NuScale design. Section 1.2 on estimation of Charpy V-Notch upper shelf energies also partially conforms, with the exception of references to NUREG-0744, which is relevant only for operating reactors not meeting the minimum fracture toughness criteria in BTP 5-3. Sections 3.4 (Reporting Criteria) and 3.5 (Technical Specification Changes) are not applicable, as they pertain to plant-specific responsibilities of the COL applicant referencing the NuScale certified design. Section 4.1 (Pressurized Thermal Shock Requirements) fully conforms with no exceptions. Additionally, DSRS BTP 5-4, Rev 0, Section B.1 (Design Requirements of the Residual Heat Removal System: Functional Requirements) also fully conforms with no exceptions.

The in-core instrumentation system in the NuScale Power Plant monitors various reactor core and reactor coolant system (RCS) parameters, transmitting data to the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) for display and evaluation. Under normal conditions, the AC electrical power distribution system supplies continuous power for startup, operation, and shutdown. However, the NuScale design does not require onsite or offsite AC electrical power to cope with design-basis events, making the Alternate AC Power Source not applicable. No AC or DC power is relied upon for the performance of plant safety functions. The design employs onsite backup diesel generators instead of gas turbine generators, but these standby AC generation sources and their associated AC distribution systems are not safety-related and are not required to fulfill safety functions during the first 72 hours following a design basis accident.

The plant’s safety systems do not rely on AC or DC electrical power for actuation. The 13.8 kV and switchyard system supplies power from the turbine generators and auxiliary AC power source to the 13.8 kV AC buses and connects the onsite AC system to the switchyard. The medium voltage AC electrical distribution system delivers 4,160V AC to buses for medium voltage loads, while the low voltage AC system provides 120V AC and 480V AC to buses for low voltage loads. A highly reliable DC power system supplies a failure-tolerant 125V DC source to critical plant loads such as emergency lighting, MPS, PPS, and post-accident monitoring, while the normal DC power system serves nonsafety control and instrumentation loads. Backup power is provided by diesel generators at 480V AC and by the auxiliary AC power source at 13.8kV AC. Diesel generators are strictly used for supplying standby power to designated loads and are not interconnected with other AC sources except briefly for load testing.

The onsite AC power systems do not include Class 1E distribution systems, but the EDSS design meets guidance for independence between redundant standby power sources and their distribution systems (Safety Guide 6). Preoperational testing guidance for redundant onsite electric power systems is partially applicable to divisional EDSS load groups, but not to NuScale AC power systems or the EDNS. The onsite AC power systems conform to GDC 2 (Section 8.3.1.2.1), GDC 4 (Section 8.3.1.2.2), and GDC 50 (electrical penetration assemblies, Section 8.3), and partially conform to GDC 5 as described in Section 8.3.1. Compliance with 10 CFR 52.47(b)(1) is confirmed. For DC power systems, full conformance is noted with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, and GDC 50, with electrical design requirements for EPAs included in Section 8.3.

DSRS Table 8-1 summarizes the NRC requirements, guidance, Commission policy documents, and industry codes and standards used as acceptance criteria for reviewing electrical systems in Sections 8.2, 8.3.1, 8.3.2, and 8.4, with partial conformance noted. For DSRS 8.3.2 (DC Power Systems, Onsite), the design partially conforms to RG 1.32, RG 1.81, RG 1.118, and RG 1.153 as they apply to certain aspects of the EDSS (Electrical Distribution System and Subsystems) design, and fully conforms to RG 1.75. Conformance with RG 1.63 is also partial, with reference to Table 1.9-2. Regarding Generic Letter 91-06 and the resolution of Generic Issue A30 on the adequacy of safety-related DC power supplies (per 10 CFR 50.54(f)), there are no safety-related DC systems, but relevant portions are considered in the design of the non-Class 1E EDSS.

Sections 8.1.4, 8.2.2, 8.3.1, 8.3.2, and 8.4, as well as DSRS 8.2, Rev 0 (Offsite Power System) and DSRS 8.3.1/8.3.2, Rev 0 (AC and DC Power Systems Onsite), specify that compliance with General Design Criterion (GDC) 5 and 10 CFR 50.65(a)(4) is not applicable at this stage, as the responsibility for conformance and development of the maintenance rule program—including identification of structures, systems, and components (SSC) requiring assessment—rests with the Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the certified design. Similarly, conformance with Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.160 is also not applicable at this stage and is the responsibility of the COL applicant.

The NuScale design supports exemptions from GDC 17 and GDC 18, including their associated offsite power system requirements. Analyses of grid stability and capacity, as referenced in SRP BTP 8-3, Rev 3, are the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the NuScale design certification. The criteria in SRP BTP 8-6, Rev 3, regarding voltage protection for the offsite power system and proper operation and sequencing of Class 1E loads, are not applicable because the NuScale offsite power system does not supply power to Class 1E loads or support safety-related functions. Additionally, per Generic Letter 2006-02, the NuScale Power Plant design does not rely on offsite power for safety-related or risk-significant functions, and grid stability studies remain the responsibility of the COL applicant.

The NuScale design supports exemptions from several General Design Criteria (GDC) requirements as outlined in the DSRS sections. Specifically, NuScale seeks exemptions from GDC 33 for both offsite and onsite AC and DC power systems (DSRS 8.2.3, 8.3.1, 8.3.2), and from GDC 17 and 18 for onsite DC power systems (DSRS 8.3.2). For GDCs 34, 35, 38, 41, and 44, NuScale complies with a set of principal design criteria instead of these GDCs (DSRS 8.2.3). Additionally, for communication systems, NuScale supports an exemption from GDC 19, complying instead with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion as described in Section 3.1.2 (DSRS 9.5.2).

The NuScale design departs from several General Design Criteria (GDC) requirements for onsite power systems. Specifically, NuScale supports exemptions from GDC 17 and GDC 18, including their associated requirements for the onsite AC power system, as described in DSRS 8.3.1, Rev 0. Additionally, for both AC (DSRS 8.3.1) and DC (DSRS 8.3.2) onsite power systems, NuScale complies with a set of principal design criteria in lieu of GDCs 34, 35, 38, 41, and 44. These departures reflect NuScale’s alternative approach to meeting regulatory requirements for onsite power system design.

The review of DSRS 8.4, Rev 0: Station Blackout indicates partial conformance with regulatory requirements. Compliance with 10 CFR 50.63 and the guidelines of RG 1.155 is only partially achieved. For passive plant designs, all safety-related functions can be performed without reliance on AC power for 72 hours following a station blackout (SBO) event, as detailed in Section 8.4, and an RTNSS (Regulatory Treatment of Non-Safety Systems) process has been implemented per Section 19.3. While DC power supplies are not required to meet the SBO mitigation requirements of 10 CFR 50.63, the independence of SBO-related power supplies, specifically EDSS (Emergency Direct Current Supply System), is addressed in Section 8.3.

The provided passages reference multiple figures and tables detailing the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and related reactor safety systems. Figure 1.2-8 and Figure 5.4-10 present diagrams of the DHRS, while Table 5.4-5 provides its design data and Table 5.4-8 offers a failure modes and effects analysis. The system's performance is illustrated in several figures: Figure 5.4-11 shows primary coolant temperature during two-train DHRS operation over 4 hours; Figures 5.4-12 and 5.4-13 depict temperature cooldown with one-train operation at low system inventory for 4 and 36 hours, respectively; Figures 5.4-14 and 5.4-15 show cooldown with one-train operation at high system inventory for 4 and 36 hours. Additional referenced systems include the Emergency Core Cooling System (Figure 1.2-9), Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents (Section 5.4.4), and Pressurizer Design Data (Table 5.4-6). These materials collectively provide comprehensive technical details on the design, operation, and safety analysis of the DHRS and associated reactor coolant systems.

The NuScale reactor facility features site-specific cooling tower location, orientation, equipment design, and operation, with the reactor core housed in a core support assembly seated in the lower reactor pressure vessel (RPV) assembly and connected to a central hot leg riser. Each Reactor Coolant System (RCS) line enters containment through welded penetrations on the containment upper head, with two containment isolation valves mounted externally, and a single pressurizer spray supply line also entering through the containment head. The pressurizer is integral to the reactor vessel, comprising the upper region above the naturally circulating coolant, separated by a baffle plate. The pressurizer includes the RPV upper shell and head, baffle plate, heater bundles, and two spray nozzles (each 1 inch in diameter). Instrumentation measures the steam-water interface level, providing input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS), including low water level protection for the heaters. The pressurizer heaters maintain RCS pressure during normal operation, supplying at least 785 kW (with a 15% margin), divided into three control groups per bundle, with two heater bundles per pressurizer, each bundle rated at 400 kW. Heater parameters include 480 Vac voltage and 60 Hz frequency, with heater sheath temperature at 800°F. The pressurizer operates at an internal pressure of 2100 psia, external pressure of 1000 psia, and temperature of 650°F. Pressurizer level is maintained within its operating band by operator action to add or reduce coolant inventory via the LRWS.

The provided passages are excerpts from the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, Tier 2. Chapter 5 covers the Reactor Coolant System and Connecting Systems, beginning with section 5.1, Summary Description. The List of Tables includes Table 5.1-1, which details Reactor Coolant System Volumes, and Table 5.4-7, which presents Pressurizer Heater Parameters. These sections and tables provide technical data and descriptions relevant to the reactor coolant system's design and operation.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report includes simplified and schematic flow diagrams of the Reactor Coolant System (Figures 5.1-2 and 5.1-3), with Figure 5.1-3 identifying system components by numbered labels. The condenser design features upper and lower headers connected to a series of tubes, which serve as the heat transfer surface and form the heat exchanger's pressure boundary. The headers and tubes contain secondary system fluid internally, while their exteriors are exposed to reactor pool fluid. Table 5.4-4 is noted as not used.

The provided passages from the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, detail the thermal performance of the reactor coolant system under various decay heat removal scenarios. Figures 5.4-11 through 5.4-16 present primary coolant temperature cooldown profiles for both one-train and two-train operations of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), considering different system inventories (high and low) and initial pool temperatures. For two-train DHRS operation (Figure 5.4-11), primary coolant temperatures (hot leg, cold leg, and average) are tracked over 14,000 seconds (approximately 4 hours), with temperatures ranging from 250°F to 600°F. One-train DHRS operation is analyzed for both low and high system inventories over 4-hour (Figures 5.4-12, 5.4-14) and 36-hour (Figures 5.4-13, 5.4-15) periods, with temperature ranges from 300°F to 600°F and time scales up to 120,000 seconds. Figure 5.4-16 specifically examines one-train DHRS operation with high system inventory and an initial pool temperature of 140°F over 36 hours, showing primary coolant temperatures between 380°F and 600°F. These figures collectively illustrate the reactor coolant system’s ability to manage decay heat removal under various post-shutdown conditions, with detailed temperature and time data for critical system components.

The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) is designed for natural circulation of the primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps or external piping. The RCS serves as the core neutron moderator and includes reflector blocks to improve neutron economy. It features a degasification line to remove noncondensible gases from the pressurizer (PZR) volume during normal operation. The reactor vessel internals (RVI) support and align the core, control rod assemblies, drive shafts, surveillance capsules, and instrument guide tubes. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core region—89 ft³, steam generator (SG) region—621 ft³, and PZR region—578 ft³ (with the cylindrical portion at 487 ft³). Each integrated NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) housing the reactor, RVI, PZR, two SGs, reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, RCS injection and discharge lines, pressurizer spray and high-point degasification vent lines, and a decay heat removal system (DHRS). The system is depicted in Figure 5.1-2, showing key components and flow paths.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides detailed operational and design data for the primary system and decay heat removal system. Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates at various reactor power levels, with reactor power ranging from 0% (0 MWt) to 100% (160 MWt). At 100% power and best-estimate flow, the primary flow is 587.0 kg/s, with core ΔT of 99.8°F, TCold at 496.6°F, Tavg at 543.3°F, and THot at 590.1°F. Minimum and maximum design flows at 100% power are 538.5 kg/s and 660.5 kg/s, respectively, with corresponding temperature variations. When core decay heat is unavailable, the module heatup system drives natural circulation. Transient load contributions, including fatigue, are considered in component calculations as per Table 3.9-3. For conservative safety analysis, an initial core power of 102% is assumed to account for measurement uncertainty, and a 1.2 multiplier is applied to core decay heat. The decay heat removal system is designed with an internal pressure of 2100 psia, actuation valve external pressure of 60 psia, passive condenser external pressure of 27 psia, and operates at 650°F. Each of the two condensers contains 80 tubes (1.315-inch outer diameter, 0.109-inch wall thickness), with 258.2 ft² external surface area per condenser and a fouling factor of 0.0005 hr-ft²-F/BTU. The system schematic (Figure 5.4-10) shows two passive condensers located in the reactor pool within the containment vessel, with associated valves, pressure transmitters, level transmitters, and connections to the steam generators.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report for Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design (Tier 2, Revision 5) specifies that system monitoring is performed with a resolution of at least 500 Hz, ensuring the sampling rate covers the dominant frequencies of steam generator system (SGS) components. Preservice examinations, in accordance with BPVC Section V as modified by Section III, Paragraph NB-5111, require 100 percent inspection of pressure boundary welds and a volumetric, full-length preservice inspection of 100 percent of the tubing in each steam generator. Materials used for fabrication conform to BPVC Section II specifications and meet Section III, Article NC-2000 requirements, with further details on material compatibility, fabrication, process controls, and welding controls for ASME Class 1 components provided in Section 5.2.3, and cleaning and cleanliness controls for steam generators detailed in Section 5.2.3.4.2. The report also notes that a steam generator (SG) tube failure is indicated by pressure changes, with lower pressure suggesting a secondary side break or leak, and that actuation valves are equipped with position indication to verify that the valve position matches the demanded position.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the steam generator (SG) design and operating conditions for the Reactor Coolant System. At full load, the SGs collectively transfer 159.13 MWt, with each SG capable of handling half this load. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with outlet and inlet temperatures of 584.4°F and 299.7°F, respectively, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. The SG is a helical, once-through type, featuring 1,380 helical tubes arranged in 21 columns per NuScale Power Module (NPM). Both the internal and external pressures for the secondary and primary sides are 2,100 psia, while the SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia. The internal and external temperatures are 650°F, with SG piping in containment at 550°F. Tubes have an outer diameter of 0.625 inches and a wall thickness of 0.050 inches. The steam tubesheet and feed tubesheet thicknesses (without clad) are 4.0 inches and 6.0 inches, respectively, with secondary and primary clad thicknesses of 0.250 inches and 0.375 inches. With clad, the steam and feed tubesheet thicknesses are 4.625 inches and 6.625 inches. The total heat transfer area is 17,928 ft², and the fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. The minimum SG tube transition bend radius is at least 6.250 inches. Figures in the report illustrate the helical tube bundle geometry, transition regions, tube support arrangements, and the use of support tabs for tube stability within the SG columns.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the configuration and design of the reactor coolant system components and subsystems, focusing on the steam generators and associated plena within the upper reactor pressure vessel (RPV) section. Figures 5.4-2 through 5.4-9 illustrate the arrangement of four steam plena (STEAM 1-4) and four feedwater plena (FEED 1-4) positioned at 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270° around the RPV. The integral steam plenum assembly includes a heater bundle, removable access cover, steam access port, main steam supply nozzle, and cap. The feedwater plenum features a removable access cover, tubesheet, flow restrictor mounting plate, feedwater supply nozzle, flow restrictors, stud bolts, and mounting plate spacers. The steam generator flow restrictor assembly consists of a flow restrictor, flow restrictor bolt, cladding, feed plenum tubesheet, and mounting plate. The simplified steam generator diagram shows two steam generators (01-SG-SG-0101 and 01-SG-SG-0201) connected to the main steam (MS), feedwater (FW), containment (CNT), and decay heat removal system (DHRS), with flow paths indicated for steam, feedwater, and connections to the RPV and containment vessel (CNV).

The passages reference SRP 2.0 (March 2007) concerning Site Characteristics and Site Parameters, specifically addressing various scenarios for Combined License (COL) applications. Section II.2 applies only to COL applicants not referencing a Design Certification Application (DCA) and not referencing an Early Site Permit. Section II.3 and II.4 pertain to COL applications referencing a Certified Design, or both an Early Site Permit and a Certified Design, respectively, requiring applicants to meet the design parameters established in the DCA. Section II.5 is applicable only to COL applicants that do not reference either an Early Site Permit or a Certified Design, and also do not reference the DCA. All sections are marked "Not Applicable" in the provided context.

Administrative and procedural controls are the responsibility of the COL (Combined License) applicant. For SRP 13.7.2, Rev 0: Fitness for Duty (Construction), and DSRS 14.2, Rev 0: Initial Plant Test Program - Design Certification and New COL applicants, various sections are addressed as follows: The summary of the test program and objectives (II.1) and test programs' conformance with regulatory guides (II.2) both conform with no exceptions. For initial test program administrative procedures (II.3), there is partial conformance: Subheading DC Applicant, Items A through D, apply to the DCA, while Subheading COL/OL applicants, Items A through H, apply only to the COL applicant. For individual test descriptions/abstracts (II.5), the section conforms with no exceptions. For initial test program acceptance criteria (II.6), there is partial conformance: Subheading DC Applicant, Items A through C, apply to the DCA, and Subheading COL/OL applicants, Items A through C, apply to the COL applicant.

Several acceptance criteria from SRP 17.4 and SRP 17.5, Rev 1, as well as requirements under 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(i), are identified as either "Partially Conforms" or "Not Applicable" for the design certification applicant, with full conformance or applicability deferred to the Combined License (COL) applicant. Specifically, site-specific and programmatic aspects regarding software reviews, onsite/offsite/operational/maintenance organizational elements (SRP 17.5 II.A.3), inservice and modification inspection provisions (SRP 17.5 II.J), control of special processes (SRP 17.5 II.I), and inspection, test, and operating status (SRP 17.5 II.N) are all the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the certified design. The Reliability Assurance Program (SRP 17.4 II.B) and administrative procedures for evaluating operating, design, and construction experience (10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(i)) are also only applicable to COL applicants. Where noted, the design certification applicant fully conforms to requirements, such as SRP 17.4 II.A and SRP 17.5 II.O (Nonconforming Materials, Parts, or Components), with no exceptions.

The guidance in SRP 17.1, Rev 2, and SRP 17.2, Rev 2, applies exclusively to existing NRC-approved Quality Assurance (QA) Programs that are based on ANSI N45.2 and its associated daughter standards, covering both the design and construction phases (SRP 17.1) and the operations phase (SRP 17.2). Additionally, requirement 50.34(f)(3)(iii) under section 17.4 mandates the establishment of a QA Program based on specified considerations (I.F.2), with partial conformance noted for the Design Certification Application (DCA) to the extent that it pertains to design activities supporting the DCA.

The NuScale Quality Assurance (QA) program for the design certification phase partially conforms to SRP 17.5, Rev 1, Section II.B, as provisions for site-specific and operational QA are not applicable at this stage and will be addressed by the Combined License (COL) applicant in their operational QA program. The NuScale QA program fully conforms to SRP 17.5, Rev 1 requirements for Corrective Action (II.P), Quality Assurance Records (II.Q), Training and Qualification Criteria (II.S), Training and Qualification for Inspection and Test (II.T), and Quality Assurance Program Commitments (II.V), with no exceptions noted. SRP 17.6, Rev 2 (Maintenance Rule) is not applicable, as it pertains to a site-specific operational program under the COL applicant’s responsibility. Additionally, SRP 18.0, Rev 2, Section II.A (Human Factors Engineering aspects of a new plant) is fully met with no exceptions.

The passages reference SRP 17.5, Rev 1: Quality Assurance Program Description for Design Certification, Early Site Permit, and New COL applicants, confirming conformance in the following areas: II.C Design Control and Verification, II.E Instructions, Procedures, and Drawings (Controlled Documents), II.G Control of Purchased Material, Equipment, and Services, II.L Control of Measuring and Test Equipment, and II.U Nonsafety-Related SSC Quality Controls. No exceptions or additional comments are noted for any section.

SRP 17.5, Rev 1 outlines Quality Assurance Program requirements for Design Certification, Early Site Permit, and new COL applicants. For section II.F, Document Control, the certified design only partially conforms, as site-specific and operational document control provisions are the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the certified design. Sections II.H, Identification and Control of Materials, Parts, and Components, and II.M, Handling, Storage, and Shipping, are marked Not Applicable, since these acceptance criteria pertain to activities that are also the responsibility of the COL applicant referencing the certified design.

The provided passages summarize the applicability and conformance of various Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) documents to Combined License (COL) applicants. DC/COL-ISG-2 addresses financial qualifications and is applicable to COL applicants. DC/COL-ISG-8 specifies the necessary content of plant-specific technical specifications, including identification and timing of resolution for generic technical specification COL action items, and compliance options, all of which are relevant only to COL applicants. DC/COL-ISG-13 outlines evaluation findings for combined license reviews and is explicitly directed toward COL application reviews. ESP/DC/COL-ISG-15 provides guidance on post-combined license commitments, replacing Section C.III.4.3 of RG 1.206, and is also intended for COL applicants. Additionally, ESP/DC/COL-ISG-15 discusses anticipated NRC revisions to NUREG-0800, SRP Chapter 1.0, with partial conformance noted; relevant portions for the Design Certification Application (DCA) include discussions on COL action items and information items, with COL action items identified throughout the FSAR, and avoidance of the term “COL holder item.”

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, closed-loop system designed to remove decay heat from the reactor coolant system (RCS) by utilizing the steam generators (SGs). Water from the DHRS condenser circulates through the SG tubes, where it absorbs decay heat and is converted to steam. This steam flows back to the DHRS condenser, where it releases heat to the reactor pool water and condenses, promoting natural circulation in both the RCS and DHRS. The DHRS is connected to the steam and feedwater piping between the main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and feedwater isolation valves (FWIVs), forming a closed-loop outside the containment and providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and the reactor pool. Each DHRS train is associated with one of the two NPM SGs, with piping connecting to the main steam and feedwater lines upstream of the MSIV and downstream of the main feedwater isolation valve (MFIV). Two DHRS actuation valves, arranged in parallel per train, isolate the system during normal operation. An orifice moderates flow between the actuation valves and the passive condensers, which are located at a higher elevation than the SGs to promote natural circulation. The DHRS remains filled and pressurized via the feedwater system and is in standby during normal operation, with actuation dependent on closure of the MSIVs and FWIVs. The system differs from an auxiliary feedwater system in design, operation, and response to small break LOCA events. The SGs are designed for reliability, minimizing tube corrosion, vibration, and wear. System performance is optimized across power levels by establishing steady-state parameters as a function of reactor power. RCS temperature and pressure sensors confirm DHRS operation, indicated by decreasing values after reactor trip and DHRS actuation.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), as described in DSRS 5.4.7 Rev 0, is connected to the secondary system and does not directly interface with the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), making GDC 14 not applicable. Leakage detection and isolation from containment are not considered important to safety due to this lack of direct interface. The DHRS has no other interfaces or shared components with the RCPB and conforms to requirements regarding system interfaces. DHRS operation relies on passive natural circulation flow, making direct flow measurement less useful for the NuScale design; instead, control room indication focuses on other system parameters to ensure adequate monitoring of DHRS operation and reactor core cooling. The DHRS is designed to remove post-reactor trip residual and core decay heat, transitioning the NuScale Power Module (NPM) to safe shutdown without external power, and to cool the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) at a rate that maintains fuel design limits and RCPB design conditions during return to power events. Following DHRS actuation, any subsequent ECCS actuation allows both systems to continue residual heat removal. The DHRS can be initiated from the control room or from outside the Main Control Room (MCR) in the MPS equipment rooms. It is a passive system with two independent trains, each capable of performing the safety function in the event of a single failure, and utilizes natural circulation from the steam generators (SGs) to dissipate heat to the reactor pool. The DHRS is designed with leakage detection and control to minimize worker and public exposure and ensure system availability, and is not directly connected to the RCS. In compliance with 10 CFR 50.63, the DHRS can maintain adequate core cooling for 72 hours during a station blackout. Radioactive contamination in the DHRS originates indirectly from the feedwater and main steam systems, and is minimized by an all-welded design, leakage detection provisions, and facility programs that limit contamination and radioactive waste generation.

Main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and feedwater isolation valves (FWIVs) are installed outside the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) on the main steam and feedwater headers, respectively, atop the containment vessel (CNV) at the top support structure platform. Their isolation functions are detailed in Section 6.2.4. Figure 5.4-10 shows a simplified diagram of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), including operational flowpaths and major components, while Table 5.4-5 summarizes DHRS design data. Before power operations, feedwater pumps fill the DHRS up to the actuation valves. Upon actuation, the MSIVs and FWIVs close, and the DHRS actuation valves open—these are designed to open upon loss of control power or system actuation. This allows the DHRS piping water column to drain into the feedwater system and plenum, enabling steam from the steam generator (SG) to flow into the DHRS piping and passive condenser. If an MSIV fails to close, a backup MSIV provides isolation for the DHRS loop; if the FWIV fails, the feedwater regulating valve isolates the loop. These actions ensure the SGs and DHRS loops are isolated from the steam and feedwater systems, maintaining adequate water inventory in the passive closed loop. The MSIV and main feedwater isolation valve (MFIV) functions and actuation are described in Section 6.2 and Chapter 7, SGs in Section 5.4.1, and steam/feedwater piping in Chapter 10. DHRS flow is driven by natural circulation due to density differences between steam and condensate in the DHRS and SG. Stress analyses for related piping are in Section 3.9.5.4.3.2.1. The DHRS actuation valves, located atop the NPM between the steam line connection and DHRS condenser upper header, are arranged in two parallel valves per train. Upon DHRS actuation, the actuation valves open and the FWIVs and MSIVs close; the actuation valves are designed to fully open or close within 30 seconds of receiving the respective signal, provided the differential pressure between the feedwater and main steam systems is 50 psid or less.

Manual vent and drain valves are provided for the main steam and feedwater plena to allow draining prior to cover removal for outage maintenance and testing; these valves are used only for maintenance, remaining normally closed and capped. During operation, turbine isolation is required to prevent damage, with normal cooling maintained via feedwater and the unaffected Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) train remaining available. Valve position indication, steam pressure, steam temperature, and passive condenser temperature are monitored. Spurious DHRS actuation, due to electrical/I&C faults or operator error, causes both DHRS trains to operate and closure of Main Steam Isolation Valves (MSIVs) and Feedwater Isolation Valves (FWIVs). DHRS inventory is maintained by continuous feedwater makeup; major valve seat leakage actuates both DHRS trains, while minor seat leakage has no effect. Valve bonnet leakage is managed by periodic inspection (minor) or monitoring of passive condenser temperature and steam pressure (major). Failure modes include partial or slow valve opening/closure (mechanical or electrical/I&C), spurious closure/opening (electrical/I&C or operator error), and leakage (mechanical), with system redundancy ensuring continued DHRS operation. Major system leakage is addressed under DHRS Loop Pressure Boundary failures. Flow blockage in a valve reduces flow in the affected train, but redundant actuation valves maintain adequate DHRS flow. Secondary MSIV closure mitigates inventory loss, and the system can function with a range of initial inventories, making loss of cooling in one train not credible. MSIV bypass valves (MS-HOV-0103, MS-HOV-0203) are normally closed, fail closed, and maintain the DHRS pressure boundary; spurious opening is mitigated by the downstream secondary MSIV. Failure detection methods include valve position indication, steam pressure, passive condenser temperature, RCS temperatures, and periodic testing and inspection.

The safety-related Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature of the NPM design, with its reliability evaluated through the reliability assurance program (Section 17.4) and risk significance determined per Chapter 19 guidance; its classification and risk categories are detailed in Table 3.2-1. The DHRS meets 10 CFR 50 Appendix A General Design Criteria (GDC 1, 2, and 4), is classified as Quality Group B, and is designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 2 in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, adhering to the highest quality standards per the Quality Assurance Program (Chapter 17). The DHRS is engineered to withstand natural phenomena and remain functional under normal, maintenance, testing, and postulated accident conditions. DHRS actuation valves, piping, and passive condensers are Quality Group B, Class 2 per BPVC Section III, and remain operable after a design basis seismic event; supports are Class 2 per BPVC Section III, Subsection NF. Classification details and applicability are in Chapter 3, while Section 6.1 covers material selection, fabrication methods, and fluid compatibility, with Table 6.1-1 listing material specifications for DHRS components and supports. Welding follows ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NC-4300, and Section IX. The DHRS condenser, actuation valves, and piping are Seismic Category I, and the system is protected from natural phenomena by the reactor building. DHRS instrumentation and control components are seismically qualified per IEEE 344-2004. Table 5.4-8 provides the DHRS failure modes and effects analysis. Each NPM and its DHRS are housed within the Reactor Building for protection from external events (Section 3.8), with DHRS piping branching from the main steam lines outside and above the CNV, upstream of the MSIVs. Section 3.9 describes the vibration assessment program, identifying that DHRS components exposed to secondary side flow are susceptible to acoustic resonance, with no other credible flow-induced vibration mechanisms.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is designed to ensure the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) average temperature remains below 420°F within 36 hours following an initiating event, without challenging the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) or uncovering the core. The 420°F threshold is based on the safe shutdown temperature recommended by EPRI for passive plant designs and accepted by the NRC. The DHRS does not require actuation of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) and is shown to be insensitive to a wide range of reactor pool temperatures and to the failure of a Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV) to close. Performance analyses demonstrate that the DHRS can remove significant heat across a broad range of loop inventories and accommodate fouling and steam generator (SG) tube plugging. With two DHRS trains operating under nominal conditions, the RCS temperature drops below 420°F in less than 2 hours and stabilizes below 300°F within 36 hours. In low and high DHRS inventory cases, the RCS temperature stabilizes below 350°F and 400°F, respectively, within 36 hours, even with an initial reactor pool temperature of 140°F, consistent with Technical Specifications. The DHRS is also capable of cooling the RCS below 420°F in less than 36 hours with one train operating under limiting off-normal conditions and a single active MSIV failure. The system can remove heat in excess of decay heat after 36 hours, even with the RCS at 420°F and the pool at boiling. Initial testing will use the MHS to bring the RCS to near-normal conditions, then actuate the DHRS and monitor RCS temperature to verify performance against design basis requirements.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) actuation valves are designed to fail open using nitrogen accumulation pressure upon loss of electrical power, ensuring system function without electrical power. These valves prevent flow within the closed DHRS loop when not in operation and are classified as Category B valves per OM Code Subparagraph ISTC-1300(b), as seat leakage in the closed position does not affect required functions. The valves are torque-opened using pneumatic pressure to overcome seat and actuator friction, preventing reclosing for the required operational period. Full-stroke exercising of the valves is performed during cold shutdown conditions, as in-power exercising is impractical. DHRS actuation is automatic via the engineered safety features actuation system (ESFAS) portion of the Module Protection System (MPS), with actuation signals and requirements detailed in Section 7.1 and plant technical specifications. Manual actuation is possible locally at the MPS cabinets or via the nonsafety module control system (MCS) if enabled, but is not credited for design basis events. In the event of a single valve failure, normal DHRS flow is maintained through the redundant valve. The system allows for safe shutdown without ECCS actuation using the remaining DHRS train, and overpressure protection is provided when the steam generator system (SGS) is water solid. A nonsafety-related containment flood and drain system enables cooldown by convection heat transfer from the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) shell to the containment vessel (CNV) shell, facilitating NPM transfer for refueling. Minor leakage or passive mechanical failures, such as pipe rupture within the DHRS loop, result in the affected train becoming inoperable, but do not impact overall DHRS operation if redundancy is maintained. Key monitored parameters include steam pressure, pressurizer pressure and level, valve position, passive condenser temperature and level, and containment leakage.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is designed to provide overpressure protection by ensuring system pressures do not exceed BPVC service limits during normal operation, design basis accidents, or transients, eliminating the need for low-pressure interlocks or pressure relief devices. Each DHRS train is equipped with four level transmitters (two per steam pipe) near the actuation valves to detect noncondensible gas accumulation and ensure the gas limit is not exceeded, maintaining safety function. A low-level alarm in the control room alerts operators if the DHRS is not filled with liquid water, confirming successful actuation when the system drains. Steam pressure is monitored by eight sensors per NPM (four per train, two per steam supply line), providing safety-related signals for reactor trip, DHRS actuation, and post-accident monitoring. Both steam pressure and valve position indications are classified as Type D accident monitoring variables per IEEE 497-2002 and RG 1.97, Revision 4. Each DHRS train has two condensate temperature sensors and three condensate pressure sensors in the condenser lower header, also classified as Type D variables. Condensate temperature increases above reactor pool temperature during operation, indicating active condensation and flow, while deviations may signal reduced water level or lack of circulation. Condensate pressure is expected to match saturation pressure at RCS temperature; deviations may indicate SG tube failure, DHRS break, or leak. A nitrogen accumulator pressure element ensures adequate pressure for valve operation, with alarms for low pressure. DHRS control is limited to on/off actuation via the MPS, with no modulation capability.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) thermal-hydraulic performance analysis assessed the orifice loss coefficient under various initial conditions to confirm that the restriction orifice size ensures consistent heat transfer. Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS steam lines was identified as a factor that degrades system performance. As a two-phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance depends on factors such as Reactor Coolant System (RCS) temperature, with higher RCS temperatures increasing the driving temperature difference and thus DHRS heat transfer. The analysis, performed using NRELAP5, included three model categories: a Steady State Model (with constant primary side temperature, flow rate, reactor pool temperature, and DHRS inventory), Nominal Transient Models (to demonstrate DHRS cooling for typical initiating events), and Off-Normal Transient Models (to show adequate cooling under the most limiting conditions). The steady state model calculated the DHRS heat transfer rate and evaluated the loss coefficient in the DHRS steam line to ensure acceptable performance across a range of DHRS inventories, including scenarios with additional steam volume due to MSIV failure, DHRS and SG fouling, SG tube plugging, and the presence of noncondensible gas. The limiting mass of dissolved gas that could be released during DHRS operation was also considered. Nominal transient models analyzed DHRS cooldown capability for cases with and without containment isolation, affecting the timing of pressurizer heater isolation. Off-normal transient models assessed DHRS performance under limiting conditions, including heat transfer surface fouling, plugged SG tubes, reactor pool water temperature, and noncondensible gas presence, with conservative biasing of valve timings to simulate worst-case DHRS inventory conditions. The overall analysis confirmed the DHRS capability to remove heat over a range of loop inventories.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is filled by condensation, with liquid fill pressure not exceeding saturation pressure. The DHRS, steam generators, and secondary system piping lack safety-related high-point venting, but noncondensible gases are continuously vented via the main steam system during normal operation. When the DHRS is not in service, noncondensible gas can accumulate in the DHRS steam piping between the closed actuation valves and the level switches; a conservative mass of this gas is calculated for performance analysis. The minimum noncondensible gas temperature maximizes oxygen and nitrogen solubility, and the selected pressure of 552.3 psia is based on feedwater plenum pressure at 102% power, with actual pressure near the DHRS actuation valves being lower due to hydrostatic head. The secondary water chemistry program limits dissolved gas concentration in the feedwater to 50 ppb, while the saturation concentration of air at 552.3 psia is 1.22E+7 ppb. Diffusion equalizes noncondensible gas concentration between DHRS and feedwater piping, with no mechanism for higher concentration in DHRS piping. The DHRS is filled with degassed feedwater at startup, and any leaks would be from the high-pressure secondary to the environment. For analysis, 10% of the saturation concentration (1.22E+6 ppb) is assumed as the maximum noncondensible gas concentration in the DHRS, providing margin for degassing equipment failure. The bounding mass of noncondensible gas is 0.422 kg, determined by the volume between the DHRS level sensor and actuation valves; level sensor elevations are chosen to limit this volume. The largest possible noncondensible gas volume is below the actuation valves and above the level sensor, and the sensors ensure the system stays within evaluated limits for heat transfer. Sufficient water inventory is required to wet heat transfer surfaces while maintaining adequate two-phase area for boiling and condensation. Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS condenser can impede condensation heat transfer, especially as RCS temperature and DHRS pressure decrease, allowing the gas to expand and further reduce performance. Assessment shows it is unlikely for noncondensible gas to accumulate to the level sensor during operation, but if the limit is reached, the affected DHRS train may not perform its safety function.

The control of stainless steel weld cladding on low-alloy steel components conforms to regulatory guidance, while the control of processing and use of stainless steel partially conforms, except for the specification of applying RG 1.37 for cleaning and flushing finished surfaces. Nondestructive examination (NDE) requirements for austenitic stainless steel tubular products are detailed in Section 5.2.3.4.5, requiring compliance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticles NB-2550 through NB-2570, and Section XI. Austenitic stainless steel materials used in nuclear steam supply components are handled, protected, stored, and cleaned using recognized methods to minimize contamination and prevent stress corrosion cracking. Suppliers implement a written cleanliness control plan covering maintenance of cleanliness, prevention of foreign material introduction, water purity, prevention of detrimental material contact, support system cleanliness and inspection, use of temporary plugs or seals with identifying stickers, detection and removal of foreign objects, cleanliness during welding, brazing, and heat treating, tools and loose parts accountability, and removal of temporary markings before heating, welding, assembly, or shipment. Controls are established to minimize introduction of harmful contaminants such as chlorides, fluorides, and low melting point alloys. In accordance with RG 1.44, all cleaning solutions, processing equipment, degreasing agents, and foreign materials are removed before elevated temperature treatments; acid pickling is avoided, and abrasive work is minimized to prevent surface coldwork and contamination. Tools for abrasive work and the use of cold worked austenitic stainless steel are addressed in Sections 4.5.2.4 and 4.5.1.1, respectively.

Threaded fasteners for ASME Code Class 1, 2, and 3 components conform to the preservice and inservice inspection requirements, as detailed in Table 3.13-2, with no exceptions noted. The fastener materials meet ASME BPVC Sections II and III requirements and are selected for compatibility with borated water in the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) and reactor pool. Section 3.13 further describes the design of threaded fasteners for the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and pressure-retaining components, including the use of Alloy 718 to mitigate stress corrosion cracking (SCC). RPV closure studs, nuts, and washers utilize SB-637 Alloy 718 instead of traditional low alloy steels such as SA-540 Grade B23 or B24, to prevent general corrosion during plant startup and shutdown when submerged. Alloy 718, an austenitic, precipitation-hardened, nickel-based alloy, is permitted for bolting by ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB-2128, and is not subject to the concerns of RG 1.65, Revision 1, position 2(b) due to its corrosion resistance. As Alloy 718 is not ferritic, the fracture toughness requirements of NB-2333 do not apply. Threaded inserts, which are both externally and internally threaded, are used for RPV fasteners except for the main RPV flange studs and steam generator inlet flow restrictor hardware. These inserts are designed so that both the external threads of the insert and the internal threads of the flange bolt holes carry mechanical loads during normal and off-normal operations, including ECCS actuation. Table 5.2-4 lists the materials and specifications for the threaded inserts.

The materials specified for the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) include ferritic low alloy steels, austenitic stainless steels, and nickel-based alloys, with grades and types detailed in Table 5.2-4 and final metallurgical conditions defined by the relevant ASME BPVC material specifications. Austenitic stainless steel weld materials are analyzed for delta ferrite content and restricted to 5 FN to 20 FN per RG 1.31, Revision 4, and ASME BPVC Section III, Paragraph NB-2433. The use of cold worked austenitic stainless steel is minimized, and material with yield strength exceeding 90,000 psi (0.2% offset) is not used in RCPB fabrication. External RCPB surfaces do not expose ferritic materials, ensuring compatibility with borated water and resistance to general corrosion. Welding procedures for austenitic stainless steel follow ASME BPVC Sections III, Subarticle NB-4300, and IX. Nickel-based alloy base metals in contact with reactor coolant system (RCS) primary fluid are limited to a maximum sulfur content of 0.02 wt%, and all nickel-based alloy materials, including welds, comply with ASME BPVC Section III and Section II Parts B and C. Low-alloy steel weld metals for the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) meet the minimum mechanical property requirements of the base metal, and safe ends larger than 5 inches using SB-167 UNS N06690 material comply with ASME BPVC 2015 edition, Section II. Further details on RPV materials are provided in Section 5.3.

The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) surface materials in contact with reactor coolant or pool water during refueling, including welds, are composed of austenitic stainless steel or nickel-based alloys. Alloy 600 base metal and Alloy 82/182 cladding and weld metal are not used in the RCPB design. Reactor Coolant System (RCS) water chemistry is controlled to minimize corrosion and corrosion product transport. Ferritic low alloy steels used in pressure-retaining applications are clad with austenitic stainless steel or Ni-Cr-Fe cladding on surfaces exposed to reactor coolant, and low alloy steel forgings have an average grain size of five or finer per ASTM standards. Cladding of ferritic base materials undergoes post-weld heat treatment as required by ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-4622. Both inside and outside surfaces of the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) low alloy steels, including attachments and appurtenances in contact with reactor coolant, secondary water, or pool water, are clad with austenitic stainless steel or Ni-Cr-Fe. Larger through-holes in the baffle plate (e.g., for CRDM shafts and pressurizer flow, approximately two inches or greater) are also clad with austenitic stainless steel. The use of cobalt-based alloys is minimized, with established limits to reduce cobalt intrusion into the reactor coolant; cobalt-based alloys are used for hard surfacing and wear-resistant parts in the Control Rod Drive Mechanisms (CRDMs), with low cobalt or cobalt-free alloys permitted if qualified by testing. Tools for abrasive work must not be contaminated by prior use on carbon or low alloy steels or other non-corrosion-resistant materials to prevent intergranular or stress-corrosion cracking. The external surfaces of the RPV are clad with austenitic stainless steel. Surfaces of pressure-retaining parts of the Steam Generators (SGs), including weld filler and bolting materials, are made of corrosion-resistant materials such as stainless steel or nickel-based alloys, with all SG materials having a proven history in light water reactor environments and detailed in Table 5.2-4.

The fabrication and welding of unstabilized American Iron and Steel Institute (AISI) Type 3XX series austenitic stainless steels for pressure-retaining components are conducted in compliance with Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.44, Revision 1, to prevent sensitization and intergranular corrosion caused by chromium depletion at grain boundaries during welding and heat treatment. Sensitization, which occurs when materials are exposed to temperatures between 800 and 1500 °F, is managed by restricting the carbon content of both base and weld filler materials to a maximum of 0.03 wt%. Solution treatment prior to precipitation hardening is limited to 1800–1850 °F. Rapid cooling, typically by water quenching or equivalent methods, is employed to avoid carbide formation; if alternative cooling methods are used, nonsensitization is verified by corrosion testing per ASTM A262 Practices A or E. For components subjected to sensitizing temperatures for more than 60 minutes during post-weld heat treatment, additional verification of nonsensitization is required by ASTM A262 testing, as specified by RG 1.44. Low-alloy steel weld metals for reactor pressure vessels (RPVs) are matched to the base metal in chemical composition.

Nickel-based Alloy 690 is utilized as a base metal in Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components and structures, along with Alloy 52/152 cladding and weld metals, and similar alloys developed for improved weldability. Alloy 690 and 52/152 exhibit high resistance to general corrosion, fast fracture, and possess superior tensile properties at elevated temperatures. Steam generator tubes specifically use thermally treated Alloy 690. To prevent Primary Water Stress-Corrosion Cracking (PWSCC) in nickel-based alloy components within the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), Alloy 690/152/52 are employed, with controlled chemistry, mechanical properties, and thermo-mechanical processing—such as solution annealing and thermal treatment—ensuring an optimum microstructure for resistance to intergranular corrosion.
