EPRI Materials Reliability Program Reports MRP-111 and MRP-258 confirm that Alloy 690, 52/52M, and 152 are highly resistant to PWSCC and are acceptable for Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) applications. No signs of PWSCC have been observed in Alloy 690 materials in operating PWRs, and laboratory tests in simulated primary water environments further demonstrate Alloy 690’s and its weld metals’ high resistance to PWSCC.

The austenitic stainless steel cladding on the inside surfaces of components such as the integral steam plenum, steam plenum cap, and feed plenum access port is applied in at least two layers, with the first layer being Type 309L and subsequent layers Type 308L; the outside surfaces are clad with at least one layer of Type 309L. Ni-Cr-Fe cladding, specifically Alloy 52/152, is used for certain regions, including the upper surface of the baffle plate in the steam generator (SG) integral steam plenum areas for compatibility. Weld overlay cladding procedures are qualified per ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-4300, and Section IX. The baffle plate, made of low-alloy steel, has through-holes for twelve incore instrumentation guide tubes, which are sleeved with Alloy 690 inserts. Both upper and lower surfaces of the baffle plate are weld clad with two layers of stainless steel (first layer Alloy Type ER309L, second layer Alloy Type ER308L) in accordance with SFA-5.9 of BPVC Section II. Heater bundle assemblies use austenitic stainless steel or Ni-Cr-Fe Alloy, while cover plates are made from Type 304, Grade F304 stainless steel or Alloy 690 (UNS N06690), complying with BPVC Section II, Specifications SA-182 and SB-168. Heater sheaths are made from Type 304L and Type 316 stainless steel per Specification SA-213, and bolting material is nickel-based Alloy 750 (UNS N07718) per Specification SB-637. All materials are selected for corrosion resistance and compatibility with their operating environments, following relevant BPVC specifications.

The fracture toughness properties of ferritic materials used in Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components, including bolting materials, pressure-retaining base materials, and weld filler materials, comply with the requirements of 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, "Fracture toughness requirements," and ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), Section III, Subarticle NB-2300. These materials are selected from ASME BPVC, Section II, and meet the specifications of Article NB-2000 of ASME BPVC, Section III. Further discussion of fracture toughness requirements for Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) materials is provided in Section 5.3.

Stainless steel corrosion-resistant weld overlay cladding of low alloy steel components meets the requirements of RG 1.43, Revision 1, including controls to limit underclad cracking of susceptible materials. Prior to cladding, surfaces are examined using magnetic particle or liquid penetrant tests per ASME BPVC Section III, Paragraphs NB-2545 or NB-2546. Electroslag welding is not used except for austenitic stainless steel cladding of low alloy steel, where it is permitted and complies with RG 1.34. Forgings are employed to achieve required geometries with minimal welding, and reactor pressure vessel cladding is addressed in Section 5.2.3. Measures are implemented to prevent sensitization of austenitic stainless steel during fabrication, with heat treatment parameters conforming to ASME BPVC Section II. Welding of ferritic steels for reactor vessel components follows procedures qualified to ASME Code Section III, NB-4300, and Section XI, with further details in Section 5.2.3.3. Welding of austenitic stainless steel components also uses procedures qualified to ASME Code Sections III and XI, with additional information in Section 5.2.3.4. Electroslag welding is not used for joining materials other than for cladding low alloy steel.

Controls for preheating and interpass temperatures during welding of carbon and low alloy steel in the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), including weld deposited cladding, comply with ASME BPVC Section III, Division 1, Non-mandatory Appendix D, and are specified in the welding procedure specification as required by ASME BPVC Section IX, Article V. Preheat temperature control for low alloy steel forgings follows RG 1.50, Revision 1. Procedure qualification records and welding procedure specifications for low alloy steel welds in the RCPB are qualified per ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-4600 and Section IX. Welders and welding operators are qualified according to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-4300 and Section IX. Welding controls for ferritic steels under limited accessibility conditions adhere to RG 1.71, Revision 1. The post weld heat treatment temperature for RPV low alloy steel material is 1125°F ± 25°F, and alternative post weld heat treatment times and temperatures from Subparagraph NB-4622.4(c) of ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB are not used. RCPB components do not contain ferritic steel tubular products. Welding of nickel-base alloys in the RCPB is performed per procedures qualified to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-4300 and Section IX, with control of welding variables, examination, and testing in accordance with ASME Code requirements. Welders and welding operators for nickel-base alloys are qualified per ASME Section IX and RG 1.71, Revision 1.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system safeguards the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions by utilizing reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, which relieve RCS pressure directly into containment. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined by linear interpolation of values in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4, based on the downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation. The setpoint incorporates margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs with their actuators and controls ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). LTOP analysis assumes a maximum pressurizer heater output of 880 kW and 2 MW core decay heat, demonstrating RVVs open before RCS pressure exceeds the low temperature limit. LTOP setpoints range from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, with LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in Tables 5.3-1, 5.3-5, 5.3-6, 5.3-7, and Figures 5.3-2 through 5.3-5, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, and 10 CFR 50.61 for a 60-year plant life. The inadvertent actuation block arming setpoint is set above the limiting RPV pressure for LTOP conditions to avoid interference with LTOP actuation. Pressure-temperature curves and containment pressure saturation curves are provided, with corrections for location and instrument uncertainty as described. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are in Section 6.3, and additional testing and inspection information is found in Sections 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report and supporting documentation confirm that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values, after irradiation and temperature corrections, of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F of this due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, and diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, with clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The surveillance and testing programs ensure ongoing compliance with fracture toughness, pressure-temperature, and inspection criteria throughout the vessel’s service life.

Section 5.2 of the nuclear engineering document addresses the integrity of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), detailing compliance with applicable codes and code cases (Section 5.2.1), material specifications (5.2.3), inservice inspection and testing requirements (5.2.4), and leakage detection methods (5.2.5), with supporting references in 5.2.6. The RCPB encompasses all pressure-retaining components of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) up to and including the outermost containment isolation valve (CIV), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) reactor vent valves (RVVs), and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), as defined in 10 CFR 50.2. Key components include the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), pressurizer (PZR) heater bundles, RVVs, RRVs, actuator assemblies, and steam generator (SG) primary and secondary sides. RCPB components are designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and maintained to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section III, 2013 Edition (no Addenda), with Class 1 components classified as Quality Group A and subject to the highest quality standards per the Quality Assurance Program (Chapter 17), in compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 1, 4, 14, 30, 31, and 32. Table 5.2-2 lists reactor safety valve design parameters; Table 5.2-4 details RCPB component specifications, alloy designations, grades, and classes; Table 5.2-5 covers reactor coolant water chemistry controls; and Table 5.2-6 outlines RPV inspection elements. Section 5.3 provides chemical composition data for RPV beltline materials (Table 5.3-2) and 1/4-T adjusted reference temperature at 57 effective full-power years fluence (Table 5.3-3). Figures include containment leakage detection acceptability (Figure 5.2-3) and variable LTOP setpoints (Figure 5.2-4). RCPB components are designed for periodic inspection, testing, and maintenance, with cleaning, handling, storage, and shipping meeting ASME NQA-1 requirements (internal surfaces "Class B," external "Class C"). Systems connecting to the RCS, except the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS), require isolation but are not part of the RCPB. The RPV bolting material is not subject to RG 1.65 Positions 1(a)(i) and 2(b). Overpressure protection is ensured during all operating conditions, including low temperature, to maintain non-brittle behavior and minimize the probability of rapidly propagating fracture or gross rupture. The SGS and connected components up to the CNTS are Seismic Category I

The provided passages comprehensively detail the inspection, testing, and fabrication requirements for the NuScale reactor vessel and associated systems, referencing specific sections, tables, and figures from the Final Safety Analysis Report and ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC). Section 5.3 and related subsections outline reactor vessel materials, fabrication methods, and inspection elements, with Table 5.2-7 listing internals inspection elements and Figures 5.2-1, 5.2-2, 5.3-1, and 5.4-17 illustrating key components. Piping systems subject to testing include ASME BPVC Section III, Class 1, 2, and 3 piping, high-energy piping in Seismic Category I structures, and moderate-energy piping outside containment, with stringent requirements for welds between containment and Containment Isolation Valves (CIVs), including volumetric and surface examinations per Section XI, IWB-2500. Valve position verification is performed every 24 months and set pressure testing every 5 years per ASME OM-2012, with ISI inspection elements detailed in Tables 5.2-6 through 5.2-9. System leakage and VT-2 examinations are conducted before startup, and special seal verification tests are used for valves in the reactor pool. Class 1 piping welds require ultrasonic preservice examination, with surface finish and flatness per NB-4424.2, and examination requirements vary by pipe size and location. O-ring leakage testing and RPV/containment flange bolt inspections are performed per Section XI, with continuous VT-2 monitoring of RPV leakage in the containment vessel. Examination requirements for nozzles and welds are specified in Table IWB-2500-1, with volumetric and surface exams required for various nozzle types and safe ends, and exemptions for certain small or open-ended components. Reactor vessel internals inspections, developed per MRP-227 and ASME BPVC Section XI, focus on core support blocks, plates, guide tubes, riser transitions, and fasteners, using VT-1 and VT-3 methods, with augmented inspections for critical components. The reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated for Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (minimum 75 ft-lb initial, 50 ft-lb end-of-life), and plant procedures ensure compliance with pressure-temperature limits. Class 1 support inspections and valve assembly details are provided in Tables 5.2-8 and 5.2-9 and Figure 5.2-2, respectively, specifying required examination methods for supports, bolting, and valve components. Overall, the passages describe a rigorous inspection and testing regime to ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary and associated systems, in full compliance with ASME BPVC and NRC requirements.

The inspection, testing, and maintenance strategy for the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) is based on designing components to facilitate required inspections and implementing an Inservice Inspection (ISI) program that defines inspection intervals, frequencies, component and weld selection, and expansion criteria. The program adheres to the latest ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section III (2013 Edition) for construction, Section XI for ISI, and ASME OM-2012 for operation and maintenance, as incorporated by reference in 10 CFR 50.55a and relevant Regulatory Guides (RGs 1.84, 1.147, 1.192). Inspection elements for ASME Class 1 piping and supports are detailed in Tables 5.2-8 and 5.2-9. The NPM’s reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), including vessels, pumps, valves, bolting, and supports, is designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 1 per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, with materials meeting Section II requirements. Preservice and inservice inspections, including volumetric and surface exams, follow Section III (Paragraph NB-5280) and Section XI (Subarticle IWB-2200), with system pressure and functional tests per Articles IWA-5000 and IWB-5000. Overpressure protection equipment is tested per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, ASME OM Code Mandatory Appendix I, and 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(x). The containment vessel, classified as Class MC per Section III, Subsection NE, is designed and stamped as a Class 1 vessel and receives enhanced inspection requirements exceeding Section XI, Subsection IWE standards. The ISI program specifies the applicable Code edition and addenda for each 120-month inspection interval, and the use of ASME Code Cases (e.g., N-759-2, N-62-7, N-60-5) is governed by those listed in the BPVC edition specified in 10 CFR 50.55a or the relevant RGs, subject to NRC approval. Steam generator system (SGS) components, including integral steam and feed plenums and access ports, are designed and inspected as Class 1, with materials and specifications detailed in Table 5.4-3. The inspection and testing program addresses unique NPM requirements, ensuring plant safety throughout its operating life, and incorporates industry guidelines and research, such as EPRI documents and studies on tube vibration in helical tube steam generators.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details stringent material and fabrication controls for Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components, emphasizing the use of Type 3XX series austenitic stainless steel with a maximum carbon content of 0.03 wt% to prevent sensitization and intergranular corrosion, particularly when exposed to 800–1500°F. Welding of ferritic and austenitic materials, as well as nickel-based alloys, is performed using procedures qualified per ASME BPVC Section III (notably Subarticles NB-4300, NB-4600, and NB-4622), Section IX, and in accordance with Regulatory Guides (RG) 1.44, 1.43, 1.50, and 1.71. Welders and operators are qualified per ASME Section IX and RG 1.71. Austenitic stainless steel and Ni-Cr-Fe cladding (e.g., Alloy 52/152) are applied to low alloy steel surfaces in contact with reactor coolant, with specific layering (first layer Type 309L, subsequent layers Type 308L) and post-weld heat treatment at 1125°F ± 25°F. Alloy 600 and Alloy 82/182 are excluded from RCPB design. All cladding and weld overlay procedures are qualified per ASME requirements, and electroslag welding is only permitted for austenitic stainless steel cladding of low alloy steel. Nondestructive examination (NDE) of austenitic stainless steel tubular products follows ASME BPVC Section III, NB-2550–NB-2570, and Section XI. Material handling, storage, and cleaning are controlled to prevent contamination and stress corrosion cracking, with written cleanliness plans addressing water purity, foreign material exclusion, and removal of chlorides, fluorides, and low melting point alloys. Acid pickling and abrasive work are minimized, and tools must not be contaminated by prior use on non-corrosion-resistant materials. Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and Steam Generator (SG) pressure-retaining surfaces, including welds and bolting, are made from corrosion-resistant materials with proven performance in light water reactor environments, as detailed in Table 5.2-4. Cobalt-based alloys are minimized, with alternatives qualified by testing. Preheat and interpass temperature controls for welding comply with ASME BPVC and RG 1.50, and all inspections meet ASME BPVC Section XI and IWE requirements. Sensitization control includes rapid cooling after solution treatment (1800–1850°F) and verification by ASTM A262 corrosion testing when necessary. All fabrication, welding, and inspection processes are designed to ensure long-term corrosion resistance, structural integrity, and regulatory compliance for nuclear safety.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report specifies detailed material selections for Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components, as outlined in Table 5.2-4. Threaded fasteners for the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) main closure flange, pressurizer (PZR) heater bundle closures, reactor coolant system (RCS) piping flanges, and reactor safety valve (RSV) flanges are made of nickel-based Alloy 718 (UNS N07718), with studs, nuts, and washers for these applications also using SB-637 Alloy 718 to mitigate stress corrosion cracking (SCC) and general corrosion during plant startup and shutdown. Other key materials include SA-508 Grade 3, Class 1 for the lower RPV section flange shell and bottom head, SA-508 Grade 3, Class 2 for the RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum, and steam generator (SG) shells, and SA-533 Type B, Class 2 for RPV support gussets and plates. Austenitic stainless steels such as SA-479 or SA-240 Type 304/304L are used for core barrel guides and support blocks, with instrument seal assemblies and connectors using SA-240/SA-479 Type 304/304L or 316/316L. RPV flange leak detection tubes are SA-312 Type 316L seamless, and PZR pressure taps and thermowell nozzles use SB-166 Alloy 690, while PZR heater closure flanges use SB-168 Alloy 690. Weld filler materials are specified for low alloy steels, stainless steels, and nickel-based alloys, with precise AWS/ASME designations. SG tube supports and supports are SA-240 Type 304/304L, the steam plenum cap is SB-564 Alloy 690, and RCS piping uses SA-312 Grade TP304/304L, with reducers and elbows of SA-479 Type 304/304L and tees of SA-182 Grade F304/F304L. All materials conform to ASME BPVC Sections II and III, with austenitic stainless steel welds restricted to 5–20 FN delta ferrite per RG 1.31 and ASME BPVC NB-2433, and cold worked austenitic stainless steel minimized. Nickel-based Alloy 690 and weld metals 52/152 are used for their high resistance to general corrosion and primary water stress corrosion cracking (PWSCC), as confirmed by EPRI MRP-111 and MRP-258, with no PWSCC observed in operating PWRs. Fracture toughness requirements for ferritic materials, including bolting and welds, meet 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, and ASME BPVC NB-2300. Threaded fasteners for ASME Code Class 1, 2, and 3 components meet all preservice and inservice inspection requirements

The reviewed sections demonstrate full conformance with Standard Review Plan (SRP) requirements. For SRP 1.0, Rev 2 (Introduction and Interfaces), all referenced acceptance criteria (II.2) and new safety feature and design qualification testing requirements (II.3) are met without exceptions. SRP 9.3.2, Rev 3 (Process and Post-Accident Sampling Systems) requirements for process sampling system functional design (II.3) and seismic design/quality group classification (II.4) are satisfied, with technical specifications (II.2) deemed not applicable due to prior NRC-approved TSTF 366-A. SRP Chapter 7 acceptance criteria are detailed in individual sections, summarized in Section 7.1, Table 7-1, and Appendix 7.1-A, with DSRS Appendix 7.0-A, Rev 0 (I&C Hazard Analysis) fully conforming. SRP 14.3.8, Rev 0 (Radiation Protection ITAAC) is not applicable, with ITAAC methodology addressed in SRP 14.3. Nonradiological analyses in SRP Section 15.1.5, Appendix A, and Section 15.4.8, Appendix A, remain applicable to the DCA. SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0 ("Crediting Manual Operator Actions in D3 Analyses") supersedes DI&C ISG-05, Section 3, for all review phases (Analysis, Preliminary Validation, Integrated System Validation, and Maintaining Long-Term Integrity), and is referenced in Sections 7.1.5, 18.4, 18.6, and 18.7, with full conformance confirmed. Acceptance criteria in Digital I&C-ISG-05, Section 3 (pp. 13–21), are now superseded by Chapter 18, Appendix 18-A. For design certification applications, SRP Section 15.0.3, Section I, Areas of Review, supersedes radiological analyses, assumptions, acceptance criteria, and methodologies in SRP Section 15.4.8 (referencing RG 1.77), as clarified in Items 10, 10.A, 10.C, 10.E, 10.F, and 10.H under Review Interfaces; in some cases, this guidance applies only to BWRs.

The provided passages collectively summarize compliance and conformance with various sections of the Standard Review Plan (SRP) revisions relevant to nuclear facility licensing, focusing on site characteristics, hazards, meteorology, hydrology, flooding, groundwater, accidental releases, and geologic and seismic site characterization. For each SRP section—such as SRP 2.0, 2.1.1, 2.2.1–2.2.3, 2.3.1–2.3.5, 2.4.1–2.4.14, 2.5.1–2.5.5, and 3.3.1, 3.4.1—NuScale or the referenced applicant documentation is confirmed to conform to requirements for postulated site parameters, inclusion as Tier 1 information, summary tables, and basis for site parameters, with all relevant subsections (e.g., III.4.B.i, III.5.B.ii, III.6.B.iii, III.8.B.iv) addressed and no exceptions or deviations noted. Many requirements are identified as site-specific or not applicable for generic evaluations, particularly for meteorological measurements, flooding protection, and geologic or seismic details. Emergency planning sections (SRP 13.3, Rev 3, Sections II.15–II.21) are marked "Not Applicable" except for early site permit (ESP) applications, with no technical details provided for other application types. Across all reviewed passages, compliance with SRP criteria is either confirmed or noted as site-specific, with no additional technical details, numerical values, or exceptions provided.

The provided passages detail the conformance of various NuScale nuclear plant systems to regulatory requirements. The Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity Design (SRP 9.1.4, Rev 4) fully meet GDC 2, 5, 61, and 62, while the Overhead Heavy Load Handling Systems (SRP 9.1.5, Rev 1) fully meet GDC 1, 2, 4, and 5, all with no exceptions. For radioactive waste management (RG 1.143), the design criteria are generally met except for steam generator blowdown systems, which are not applicable. Solid radioactive waste management (BTP 11-3, Rev 4) conforms to processing and storage guidance, with exceptions for Process Control Program (PCP) development and implementation, and portable system controls, which are COL applicant responsibilities. The Liquid Waste Management System (DSRS 11.2, Rev 0) and Gaseous Waste Management System (DSRS 11.3, Rev 0) partially conform to dose design objectives, excluding site-specific cost-benefit analyses, and fully conform to leakage control, operation, maintenance, and automatic control features; early site permit criteria are not applicable. The Solid Waste Management System (DSRS 11.4, Rev 0) partially conforms to leakage control and operation/maintenance, with long-term storage facility design not applicable to NuScale. Both liquid and solid waste systems meet seismic design and equipment sizing standards, with no exceptions. The Gaseous Waste Management System also conforms to HEPA/charcoal filter design, hydrogen explosion resistance, and postulated leakage criteria, with partial conformance for contamination minimization and decommissioning features due to site-specific COL applicant responsibilities. For solid waste, criteria for stabilization, treatment, monitoring, waste packaging, storage, and disposal of Class A, B, C, and mixed wastes partially conform, with site-specific and programmatic aspects assigned to the COL applicant. The NuScale FSAR Tier 2, Rev 5, indicates partial conformance for effluent measurement and reporting, with many operational and site-specific requirements—such as environmental reporting, dosimetry, operational programs, radiological monitoring, and ALARA considerations—delegated to the COL applicant or deemed not applicable to the certified design.

The passages detail the conformance of nuclear plant Instrumentation and Control (I&C) systems and related equipment to various regulatory and industry standards. The 2006 ISA-67.04.01 provides updated guidance for evaluating instrument channel operability by comparing as-found and as-left calibration values, while compliance with IEEE 7-4.3.2-2003 Clause 5.5 is influenced by the use of field programmable gate arrays, making some tests inapplicable. Digital I&C safety systems adhere to multiple sections of IEEE Std 7-4.3.2-2003, as endorsed by RG 1.152, including requirements for equipment qualification (Section 5.4), reliability and integrity (Sections 5.5, 5.15), computer-based interlocks, identification (Section 5.11), and test/calibration capability (Sections 5.7, 5.5.2, 5.5.3), with partial conformance to RG 1.118 for test and calibration. These criteria apply to safety systems such as the Engineered Safety Feature Digital System (EDSS). The I&C system architecture, independence, redundancy, predictability, repeatability, and quality conform to DSRS Appendices 7.0-B, 7.0-C, and DSRS 7.1.2–7.2.1, Rev 0, with reference to RG 1.75, RG 1.152, RG 1.53, and RG 1.28, and displays/monitoring requirements conform to DSRS 7.2.13, Rev 0, with partial conformance to RG 1.97 and full conformance to RG 1.47. For Digital I&C-ISG-05, the Minimum Inventory criterion is partially met, while criteria for Phases 1–3 are superseded by Chapter 18 Appendix 18-A; Digital I&C-ISG-06 and -07 are not applicable. Environmental qualification per DSRS 3.11, Rev 0, is met for all relevant criteria, including IEEE Std. 323, material suitability, qualification of nonmetallic parts, and documentation, with maintenance/surveillance and operational program implementation marked as not applicable (COL applicant responsibility). Inservice Inspection and Testing of Class 2 and 3 Components per DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, is fully met for accessibility, examination methods, intervals, evaluation, pressure tests, supports, augmented ISI, relief requests, and code cases, with the Risk Informed ISI Program noted as not applicable and no exceptions or deviations identified.

The document outlines conformance to various Regulatory Guides (RGs) and Generic Letters (GLs) as referenced in DSRS sections, specifying full conformance to RG 1.152 (see Table 1.9-2.7.2.1), RG 1.209 (no exceptions), and GL 91-04 (applied to setpoint methodology per TR-616-49121). Partial conformance is reported for RGs 1.168, 1.169, 1.170, 1.171, 1.173, 1.151, 1.204, and 1.105, with details in Table 1.9-2 or Table 1.9-2.7.2.1. DSRS 7.2.6 (system inputs), 7.2.8 (auxiliary features), and 7.2.10 (interaction between sense/command features and other systems) state full conformance, noting the absence of specific DSRS acceptance criteria but use of relevant guidance. DSRS 7.2.2 (Equipment Qualification) partially conforms to RG 1.180 (see Table 1.9-2), while DSRS 7.2.4 (Operating and Maintenance Bypasses), 7.2.9 (Control of Access, Identification, and Repair), 7.2.11 (Multi-Unit Stations), and 7.2.12 (Automatic and Manual Controls) fully conform to RGs 1.47, 1.75, 1.53, and 1.62, respectively, with additional information in Table 1.9-2 as applicable. DSRS Table 8-1 summarizes NRC requirements, guidance, Commission policy documents, and industry codes and standards used as acceptance criteria for electrical systems in Sections 8.2, 8.3.1, 8.3.2, and 8.4, with partial conformance noted. For DSRS 8.3.2 (DC Power Systems, Onsite), the design partially conforms to RGs 1.32, 1.81, 1.118, and 1.153 for certain EDSS aspects, and fully conforms to RG 1.75; conformance with RG 1.63 is also partial (see Table 1.9-2). For GL 91-06 and Generic Issue A30 on safety-related DC power supplies (10 CFR 50.54(f)), there are no safety-related DC systems, but relevant portions are considered in the non-Class 1E EDSS design.

The reviewed sections of DSRS 6.2.1 and 6.2.4 (Rev 0) indicate that there are no specific requirements for containment functional design, with applicable acceptance criteria addressed in other SRP and DSRS sections. The containment isolation system conforms to requirements for instrument line isolation (no process lines penetrate containment), isolation and leak detection in engineered safety feature systems, and containment isolation valve requirements, but many criteria are not applicable due to the absence of relevant lines, valves, or systems; relief valves are not used as containment isolation valves. For DSRS 11.5, Rev 0, process and effluent radiological monitoring instrumentation and sampling systems criteria are partially met, with exceptions primarily related to guidance in RG 1.21, RG 1.33, RG 1.97, RG 4.1, RG 4.15, RG 4.21, and BTP 7-10, and some responsibilities deferred to the COL applicant. Full conformance is achieved for continuous sampling of radioactive iodines and particulates in gaseous effluents from all potential accident release points. DSRS 11.6, Rev 0, confirms compliance with GDC 63 and 64 via post-TMI action plan items, and with 10 CFR 20.1406 via RG 4.21 and NEI guidance, but only partial conformance with 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xxvi) due to COL applicant responsibilities. Instrumentation is provided for measuring and recording containment pressure, water level, hydrogen concentration, radiation intensity, and noble gas effluents at all potential accident release points, as required by 50.34(f)(2)(xvii). DSRS 11.6 also confirms installation of required instrumentation and sampling equipment, monitoring of gaseous and liquid release points, appropriate monitoring locations, and describes the software development process for digital systems. RETS/SREC and ODCM setpoints are not applicable, and radiation monitoring and sampling conform to Technical Specifications, the Initial Test Program, and ITAAC. Reactor fuel storage area monitors are confirmed, with no exceptions noted except where responsibilities are deferred to the COL applicant.

The passages collectively summarize the conformance of various sections of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, and associated DSRS chapters with regulatory guides, standards, and criteria related to radiation protection, shielding, and radiation sources in nuclear power plant design. Full conformance is confirmed for SRP 12.1, Rev 4 (ALARA), ANSI/ANS/HPSSC-6.8.1-1981, ANSI/ANS-6.4-2006, NEI 97-06, NUREG-0737 Task Action Plan Item II.B.2, ANSI/ANS 18.1, 10 CFR 50.49, RG 1.26, RG 1.29, RG 1.117, and DSRS 11.4, II.1, and 11.6, II.5/II.10. Partial conformance is noted for guidance on concrete radiation shields and shield testing (Section 1.69), RG 1.97, RG 1.183, RG 1.89, RG 4.21, RG 1.143, BTP 11-3, SECY-94-198, RG 1.112, RG 1.69, RG 8.8, RG 8.38, RG 1.140, RG 1.45, and RG 1.12, with implementation details or responsibilities often deferred to the COL applicant or licensee, as detailed in Table 1.9-2. Several regulatory guides (RG 1.7, 8.2, 8.10, 8.15, 8.19, 8.25, 1.52) and guidance related to medical facilities (Sections 8.18, 8.39) are deemed not applicable. Site-specific operational radiation protection programs, supplemental extended LLW storage, and Process Control Program (PCP) development are also designated as COL applicant responsibilities. Table 1.9-2 provides detailed justifications and references for each conformance determination, including requirements for debris minimization, cleanness programs, insulation selection, and security-related conformance. The report confirms compliance with requirements for offsite radiological consequences of design basis accidents (DSRS 15.0.3, II.1) and control room habitability (50.34(f)(2)(xxviii)), with no exceptions or additional comments unless specified.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, and associated documentation detail the inapplicability of numerous regulatory criteria and guidance documents—primarily those specific to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs) and large Pressurized Water Reactors (PWRs)—due to NuScale’s unique design features, such as the absence of forced reactor coolant flow, coolant loops, pumps, and certain auxiliary systems. Regulatory guides and standards such as RG 1.79.1, BTP 3-2, SRP 4.6 II.8, BTP 10-1, and sections of SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5 are not applicable, nor are requirements for core spray and low pressure coolant injection systems (10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(viii)). The Strategic Water Chemistry Plan aligns with EPRI PWR Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines (e.g., EPRI #3002000505) and fuel vendor recommendations, specifying sampling and analysis frequencies, corrective actions, and impurity limits (e.g., chloride, fluoride, sulfate ≤ 0.05 ppm, hydrogen 25–50 cc/kg, boron 0–2000 ppm), with chemistry parameters conforming to RG 1.44, Rev 1. The plan ensures reactor pressure boundary and fuel integrity, with alkaline-reducing chemistry, controlled pH and lithium, dissolved hydrogen, and zinc addition to mitigate PWSCC and radiation fields. Effluent release calculations and coolant source terms follow methodologies from NUREG-0017, PWR-GALE86, ANSI/ANS 18.1-1999, and are consistent with NuScale’s technical reports, with decontamination factors and failed fuel fractions significantly less than 0.25%. Partial conformance is noted with RG 1.110, RG 1.112, and 10 CFR 50.34(b)(3), 50.34a, and 52.79(a)(3), while DC/COL-ISG-5 is not applicable. NuScale’s design does not use CAOC, SG blowdown, or once-through/top-feed/preheat steam generators, rendering related guidance (BTP 4-1, SRP 10.4.8, SRP 10.4.6, BTP 10-2) inapplicable; only OTSGD B.2 test acceptance criteria for once-through SGs are relevant. Flow Accelerated Corrosion is addressed per DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0. For loss-of-coolant accident and recirculation cooling (RG 1.82), NuScale partially conforms, meeting intent but differing in system design. Exemptions are supported for 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) and portions of Appendix K, and partial conformance is noted for radiological consequences of main steam line failures (SR

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents the design’s conformance with a range of regulatory criteria, noting both full and partial compliance depending on the applicability of specific systems and components. For BTP 5-2, Rev 3, NuScale’s low-temperature overpressurization protection (LTOP) system aligns with intent by not depending on offsite power. Partial conformance is noted for BTP 6-1 (emergency coolant water pH) and SRP 9.2.4 (potable and sanitary water), as some referenced SSCs are not present or systems do not interface with radioactive systems. Full conformance is achieved for SRP 9.2.1 (station service water) and SRP 9.2.6 (condensate storage), as these systems are neither safety-related nor risk-significant. Several SRPs, including 9.2.7 (chilled water), 9.5.4 (emergency diesel engine fuel oil storage), 10.2 (turbine generator inservice inspection), DSRS 10.2.3 (turbine rotor integrity), and 10.3.6 (steam and feedwater system materials), are not applicable due to the absence of relevant systems or reliance on design barriers. The design does not use safety-related pumps; overpressure is relieved directly to containment. Table 1.9-3 shows conformance with NUREG-0800, SRP, and DSRS across multiple sections (14.2, 15.1.1–15.1.6, 15.2.8, 15.4.2, 15.4.6, 15.6.1, 15.6.6) with no additional comments. Section 6.1 details material selection and fabrication for ESF components, conforming to SRP 6.1.1 except for post-accident pH control, and only partially to cleaning guidance due to withdrawal of RG 1.37; thermal insulation guidance is fully met, while SRP 6.1.2 on protective coatings is not applicable. The design excludes fiberglass-reinforced thermosetting resin piping in spray pond and UHS applications, and fully conforms to SRP 3.6.1 for protection against postulated piping failures. The RCCW system partially conforms to SRP 9.2.2 (criteria II.1 and II.2), with other criteria not applicable; for SRP 9.2.5 (UHS), partial conformance is noted for II.3 due to non-use of fiberglass piping, while II.4 and II.5 fully conform. SRP 10.3.6, criterion II.1, is not applicable due to the absence of Class 2 or 3 components. Tier 2 sections confirm compliance with service limits, tornado and missile

The NuScale design incorporates several unique features and regulatory approaches. The use of a single-failure-proof crane eliminates the need for load drop accident analyses for spent fuel casks or Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs). Containment atmosphere management minimizes oxygen during normal operations and employs continuous hydrogen and oxygen monitoring post-accident, conforming to the intent of RG 1.7 but without active combustible gas control systems; the design partially meets DSRS 6.2.5 for gas concentration analysis but fully meets requirements for equipment survivability, containment integrity, and atmosphere mixing, supporting exemptions from containment leakage rate testing (GDC 52, 10 CFR 50 Appendix J Type A). The Decay Heat Removal (DHR) System generally conforms to GDC 5 and related requirements, with exemptions for GDC 19 and 34, and supports NuScale-specific principal design criteria; for containment heat removal (DSRS 6.2.2), the design meets GDC 39 but departs from GDC 40, and for combustible gas control (DSRS 6.2.5), it supports an exemption from GDC 41 power provisions. Equipment survivability ensures radiation protection, and the design does not rely on prompt sampling for accident source term assessment (10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii)). The Control Rod Drive System (CRDS) conforms to GDCs 4, 23, 25, 28, and 29, clarifies GDC 26 does not require two safety-related reactivity controls, and departs from GDC 27 in favor of a design-specific PDC. Testing confirmed CRA drop time and impact force, with partial conformance to RG 1.77, SRP 6.5.3, BTP 6-1, SRP 9.2.4, and SRP 9.3.2, and full conformance to SRP 4.2, 9.2.6, 15.4.2, and 15.4.7; the condensate storage system is non-safety-related, and potable/sanitary water systems do not interface with radioactive systems. The FSAR documents non-applicability or non-conformance with several regulatory positions (e.g., C1.1.3, C1.1.4, C1.3.12, C.2 except 2.2, C.3), partial conformance to GDC 54 for DHRS, and exemptions from GDC 38, 42, and 43 due to design features. The design does not use exclusion zone/buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability and is similar to large PWRs for effluent release calculations, making certain interim guidance inapplicable. Containment purge systems venting directly to the environment and SFP mitigating measures are not required due to design features (small containment, below-grade SFP

The NuScale Ultimate Heat Sink (UHS) pool structures, which provide credited makeup water for spent fuel cooling during accidents, meet Seismic Category I requirements but are not designed to Quality Group C standards. The Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup System partially meets GDC 4, as its normal makeup water supply and source are not designed for postulated accidents, but fully conforms to GDC 5, GDC 61, GDC 63, and 10 CFR 20.1101(b). The UHS system itself is a Seismic Category I supply system, with its makeup supply line built to Quality Group C and Seismic Category I standards, meeting GDC 4, and protected by the Seismic Category I RXB structure and emergency makeup line. The system does not require an ESF ventilation system (per RG 1.52) and uses passive cooling, making RG 1.27 not applicable; compliance with GDC 2 is demonstrated via RG 1.13. The Main Steam Supply System (MSS) is not safety-related or risk-significant, but meets GDC 2 and GDC 4 for protection against natural phenomena and turbine missile effects, with secondary MSIVs as backups. The MSS partially conforms to SBO requirements (10 CFR 50.63) and does not share safety-related SSCs (GDC 5). The Condensate and Feedwater System is confirmed for seismic protection (GDC 2), but is not used for heat transfer to the UHS and is not applicable to GDC 44, 45, or 46. The SCWS is nonsafety-related, not risk-significant, partially conforms to GDC 4, fully to GDC 5, and is not applicable to GDC 44 or 46. The Chilled Water System (CHWS) is nonsafety-related, not Seismic Category I, but SSCs whose failure could affect Seismic Category I SSCs are designed as Seismic Category II; it conforms to environmental and dynamic effects criteria and prevents contamination by operating at higher pressure than interfacing radwaste systems. Regulatory guidance such as RG 1.29 and RG 1.117 is applied as appropriate, and the NuScale design avoids deficiencies and sensitivities noted in legacy B&W designs. ITAAC acceptance criteria are not applicable, as they pertain only to COL applicants.

The NuScale compressed air systems are classified as non-safety and non-risk-significant, conforming to GDC 5 but not required to meet GDC 1 or 2, and only partially conforming to 10 CFR 50.63 for station blackout (SBO) coping. The design eliminates the need for an alternate AC power source during SBO, consistent with Commission policy, and meets requirements for fuel cladding integrity, moderate frequency incidents, GDC 10 and 15, single failure analysis, and loss of AC power analysis per DSRS 15.2.6, with no exceptions noted. Regulatory guide conformance varies: partial for RG 1.128 and RG 1.129 due to use of VRLA batteries and application of IEEE standards; not applicable for RG 1.157, RG 1.210, and RG 1.158 (noting DC system batteries are non-Class 1E); full for RG 1.211; and partial for RG 1.212. The Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) Revision 5 demonstrates conformance with DSRS 6.2.4 (containment isolation), DSRS 8.2 (offsite power, 10 CFR 50.63), DSRS 8.3.1 (onsite AC power, 10 CFR 50.55a(h) not applicable), DSRS 8.3.2 (onsite DC power, partial RG 1.153 conformance), and SRP 9.4.1 (control room ventilation SBO coping). Onsite AC power systems lack Class 1E distribution but meet Safety Guide 6 for independence, conform to GDC 2, 4, 5 (partial), and 50, and comply with 10 CFR 52.47(b)(1); DC systems fully conform to GDC 2, 4, 5, and 50. NuScale supports exemptions from GDC 17, 18, 19, and 33 for onsite and offsite power systems, and complies with principal design criteria instead of GDCs 34, 35, 38, 41, and 44. Offsite power system requirements for Class 1E loads and grid stability studies are not applicable, with responsibility for grid studies assigned to the COL applicant. For DSRS 8.4 (SBO), partial conformance with 10 CFR 50.63 and RG 1.155 is noted; all safety-related functions are maintained for 72 hours post-SBO without AC power, with RTNSS applied and EDSS independence addressed.

The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. The design does not use combustible gas control systems, supporting exemptions to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), 50.34(f)(2)(xiv), and GDC 27, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, for containment isolation valve closure times. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. The design ensures no core uncovery during LOCA, conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 for sharing of SSCs, does

The NuScale design features a steel containment vessel without concrete or prestressed tendons, making regulatory guides and requirements related to inservice inspection, prestressing forces, and tendon grouting for prestressed concrete containments inapplicable. Guidance specific to concrete containments, such as SRP 1.136, SRP 3.8.1, and SRP 3.8.3, as well as regulatory positions C.1 and C.14 concerning internal concrete structures, do not apply since the NuScale containment lacks internal concrete or steel structures. The design references the 2006 version of ACI 349 for anchoring components and structural supports, partially conforming to guidance that endorses Appendix B of ACI 349-2001, and uses the 2012 version of ANSI/AISC N690 for barrier design procedures, rather than the referenced 1994 version with Supplement 2 (2004). The steel containment design fully conforms to DSRS 3.8.2, Rev. 0 requirements, including applicable codes, standards, loads, analysis procedures, acceptance criteria, materials, quality control, construction techniques, and inservice surveillance. The description of other Seismic Category I structures conforms to DSRS 3.8.4, Rev. 0. Site-specific evaluations for explosions near transportation routes are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. The NuScale design does not require operator action to mitigate debris accumulation and only partially complies with certain regulatory positions, such as C1.1 (except for the intent of C1.1.1.9 and C1.1.1.10), C.1.3 (intent only), and C.2 (intent of chemical reaction effects in 2.2), but does not comply with positions C.1.2, C.1.3.1, portions of C.1.3.2, C.1.3.7, C.1.3.9, C.1.3.12, or C.3. For conservatism, the design uses 0.10% Cu instead of the maximum 0.06 wt% Cu.

The NuScale reactor design employs the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26, with its only safety-related function being the prevention of inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The CVCS, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0, meeting requirements for functional performance, pumping capacity, defense-in-depth RCS makeup, single failure criteria, GDC 5, minimization of contamination, system design, leakage detection, and ITAAC, but only partially meets criteria for holdup tank wall buckling/failure and venting/draining due to design differences. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design diverges from traditional PWRs by omitting high- and low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, and a sump; instead, it relies on steam condensation and natural recirculation of water that accumulates inside the containment vessel for core cooling, fulfilling ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46 and related guidance. The ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, and departures are noted for inservice inspection, operability testing, and combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions, though actuation signal guidance is applied. The design ensures RCS pressure remains below 110% of design value during inadvertent ECCS operation, maintains minimum DNBR, provides high point venting for noncondensible gases, and includes control room instrumentation for unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(vi) and (xviii). The ECCS does not require personnel access, pumps, or containment pressure for recirculation, and does not include refueling water storage tanks; condensate storage components are outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, with no safety risk from their failure. The Instrumentation and Controls review per DSRS 7.0 applies to the DCA but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Gas accumulation is not expected to affect ECCS performance, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) interfaces only with the secondary system, not the RCS. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) are met for the emergency zone, and pre-operational ECCS testing will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.

The NuScale plant design departs significantly from traditional large light water reactors (LWRs) and is not addressed by legacy regulatory guidance or codes such as PWRGALE. Its safety strategy relies on passive features and natural circulation, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, safety injection tanks, and safety-related motor-operated valves. Consequently, regulatory requirements and guidance related to these components—including flywheel integrity, motor qualification, thermal overload protection, ECCS accumulator lines, and gas accumulation in forced circulation systems—are not applicable. The design also omits an auxiliary feedwater (AFW) system, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead using a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves, which are not susceptible to inadvertent opening. The integral pressurizer is located within the reactor vessel, with no surge line or connecting piping, eliminating thermal stratification and ensuring core uncovery cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint. The containment vessel is a high-strength steel structure immersed in a water pool, operating at sub-atmospheric pressure, and the compact core results in a reduced source term. The plant is scalable, supports multi-unit configurations, and uses comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls. Debris management and chemical effects are minimized by the elimination of pumps, sumps, strainers, and trash racks. Fire protection measures ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational, compensating for the inability to passively separate redundant safety trains due to the modular design. Regulatory requirements from 10 CFR 50.34(f) and NEI 06-12, as well as ATWS provisions, are largely inapplicable, and NuScale is exempt from related evaluations and analyses. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale uses an alternate methodology incorporating aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1. The only pumps within safety scope are ASME Class III CVCS pumps, which do not serve a safety function. The design meets DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials. For spent fuel pool mitigation, NuScale is exempt from certain requirements due to its below-grade, undrainable SFP. The design considers postulated startup reactivity accidents and addresses loading transients and structural evaluations for postulated breaks in CVCS and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed under SRP Section 6.2.4. Overall, NuScale’s unique passive and modular features support exemptions from many TMI-related and traditional LWR regulatory requirements.

The NuScale Power Plant design omits emergency diesel generators, ESF rooms, and pressurizer power operated relief valves, rendering several Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and Branch Technical Positions (BTPs)—including BTP 8-5, BTP 8-7, and SRP 9.5.5 through 9.5.8—inapplicable, as NuScale’s electric power systems are not engineered safety features and do not require safety-related emergency diesel generators or their support systems. The plant includes nonsafety-related Turbine Generator Buildings (TGBs), each with six turbine generator sets and auxiliaries, and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), each with a single backup diesel generator and support equipment, none of which house safety-related systems or support ESFs. The Central Utility Building (CUB) provides common utility services, and the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) supports plant auxiliaries. The design does not utilize or credit ESF filter or ventilation systems for accident mitigation; instead, nonsafety-related HVAC systems, such as the Reactor Building HVAC, provide atmosphere cleanup per RG 1.140 but are not credited in dose analyses or for offsite dose limits. Control room habitability is ensured by compressed air tanks supplying clean air during accidents, eliminating reliance on emergency filtration systems. The in-core instrumentation system monitors reactor and RCS parameters, transmitting data to the Monitoring and Control System (MCS). The plant’s safety systems do not depend on AC or DC power for actuation, and no onsite or offsite AC power is required to cope with design-basis events, making alternate AC power sources not applicable. Onsite backup diesel generators, housed in DGBs, are nonsafety-related and provide standby power at 480V AC and 13.8kV AC but are not required for safety functions during the first 72 hours post-accident and are not interconnected with other AC sources except for brief load testing. The electrical distribution system includes 13.8kV and switchyard systems, 4,160V AC medium voltage, 120V/480V AC low voltage, and a highly reliable 125V DC system for critical loads such as emergency lighting, MPS, PPS, and post-accident monitoring, with a separate normal DC system for nonsafety loads. The plant emphasizes fail-safe design, centralized multi-modular control, advanced automation, digital displays, soft control technology, and optimized workload management to minimize human error.

The NuScale Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive system that supplies clean, compressed, breathable air to the Main Control Room (MCR) during a radioactive release or loss of AC power, maintaining positive pressure relative to adjacent areas, with sufficient air storage for at least 72 hours post-initiating event. The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, safety-related system that provides secondary side reactor cooling for non-LOCA events when normal feedwater or secondary-side cooling is unavailable, using redundant, single active failure-proof trains and natural circulation without pumps. The DHRS is analogous to the Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system in large PWRs but is specifically designed for NuScale reactor transients, with tailored actuation and indication features such as DHRS pressure, valve position, and reactor coolant system (RCS) parameters. The DHRS interfaces with the secondary system via the steam generators (SGs) and does not directly connect to the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), making GDC 14 and certain regulatory requirements inapplicable. The system is actuated automatically by the Module Protection System (MPS), with manual initiation possible from the control room or MPS equipment rooms, but remote access is restricted for security. The DHRS can be actuated without reducing RCS pressure or temperature and is designed to prevent flow disruption from noncondensible gas accumulation, with analyses accounting for heat transfer surface fouling, SG tube plugging, and maximum gas accumulation at 60°F. The system is capable of maintaining core cooling for 72 hours during a station blackout, in compliance with 10 CFR 50.63, and can transition the NuScale Power Module (NPM) to safe shutdown without external power, maintaining fuel and RCPB design limits. The DHRS is designed with leakage detection and control to minimize radioactive contamination, which originates indirectly from the feedwater and main steam systems, and utilizes an all-welded design and facility programs to limit contamination and waste. In the event of extended DHRS cooldown with low boron concentration and unavailable CVCS, there is a potential for reestablishing critical neutron power if the most reactive control rod remains stuck out. The DHRS has no direct interface or shared components with the RCPB, and its operation is monitored via system parameters rather than direct flow measurement due to its passive natural circulation design.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) is a safety-related, engineered feature designed to independently remove decay heat, meeting 10 CFR 50 Appendix A General Design Criteria (GDC 1, 2, 4, and 5) and classified as Quality Group B, ASME BPVC Section III Class 2, and Seismic Category I. The DHRS is protected from both external and internal missiles by the Reactor Building and NPM bay configuration, with no credible internal missile sources above the NPM due to the absence of rotating equipment. Each NPM’s DHRS is fully independent, sharing only the reactor pool as the ultimate heat sink for passive condensers; shared power supplies do not affect DHRS safety since active components fail-safe on power loss. The DHRS is unavailable during NPM movement and refueling, with decay heat then removed by conduction through the RPV and containment shell or direct pool contact. The DHRS is not affected by LOCA loads, as it is not connected to the RCS and its critical components are outside the CNV. Protection during NPM movement is provided by a single failure-proof Reactor Building crane. The DHRS is engineered to withstand natural phenomena, remains functional under all operational and accident conditions, and is protected from pipe whip and flow-induced vibration, with preservice and inservice inspections per BPVC Section XI for Class 2 components. Four condensation-induced water hammer mechanisms were evaluated and found not applicable or acceptable for DHRS design life. DHRS actuation valves, piping, and passive condensers are Class 2, Quality Group B, and seismically qualified, with supports per BPVC Section III, Subsection NF; welding follows BPVC Section III, NC-4300, and Section IX. Instrumentation includes four level transmitters per train for noncondensible gas detection, eight steam pressure sensors per NPM for safety-related signals, and condensate temperature and pressure sensors, all classified as Type D accident monitoring variables per IEEE 497-2002 and RG 1.97. The DHRS is filled by condensation, with liquid fill pressure not exceeding saturation pressure, and noncondensible gases are vented during normal operation. The maximum assumed noncondensible gas concentration is 1.22E+6 ppb (10% of saturation at 552.3 psia), with a bounding mass of 0.422 kg, limited by level sensor placement. Accumulation of noncondensible gas can impede heat transfer, but analysis shows it is unlikely to reach critical levels during operation; if it does, the affected train may not perform its safety function. The DHRS provides overpressure protection without the need for low-pressure interlocks or relief devices, and control is limited to on/off actuation via the MPS. All DHRS components are subject to rigorous quality assurance, inspection, and seismic qualification, ensuring reliable performance under all design conditions.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes the design, operation, and failure modes of key Reactor Coolant System components, with emphasis on the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and its actuation valves (DHR-HOV-0101A/B, DHR-HOV-0201A/B). These valves are normally closed and fail open upon actuation signal or loss of control power, using nitrogen accumulation pressure to ensure DHRS operation without electrical power or added inventory. They are torque-opened pneumatically and classified as Category B valves per OM Code ISTC-1300(b). Redundant parallel valves per train ensure continued DHRS function in case of single valve failure. Main steam isolation valves (MS-HOV-0101, MS-HOV-0201) and feedwater isolation valves (FW-HOV-0137, FW-HOV-0237) are normally open and fail closed to establish a closed DHRS cooling loop; their failure to close is mitigated by downstream valves or temporary inventory addition. These isolation valves are located outside the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) atop the containment vessel, and their actuation is automatic via the engineered safety features actuation system (ESFAS) or manually via the Module Protection System (MPS), though manual actuation is not credited for design basis events. DHRS actuation valves are designed to fully open or close within 30 seconds when the differential pressure is 50 psid or less. The DHRS operates by natural circulation, driven by density differences between steam and condensate in the DHRS and steam generator (SG). SG thermal relief valves (SG-RV-0102, SG-RV-0202) provide pressure boundary protection, but spurious opening with failure to close results in DHRS train inventory loss and inoperability. Failure modes analyzed include spurious opening/closure, failure to open/close, partial or slow operation, and leakage, with system redundancy and backup valves ensuring continued operation. Detection methods include valve position indication, steam and pressurizer pressures, passive condenser temperature and level, and periodic testing. Manual vent and drain valves are provided for maintenance, remaining closed and capped during operation. The system is designed to maintain safe shutdown without ECCS actuation using the remaining DHRS train, and overpressure protection is provided when the SGS is water solid. Minor leakage or mechanical failures in one train do not compromise overall DHRS function if redundancy is preserved.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, closed-loop safety system designed to remove decay heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) using the steam generators (SGs) and natural circulation. The DHRS operates by circulating water from its condenser through the SG tubes, where it absorbs decay heat, converts to steam, and returns to the condenser to release heat to the reactor pool water. The system is connected to the steam and feedwater piping between the main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and feedwater isolation valves (FWIVs), forming a closed loop outside containment and providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and reactor pool. Each DHRS train is associated with one of the two NPM SGs, with actuation valves and orifices arranged to promote natural circulation and moderate flow. The DHRS is designed to maintain the RCS average temperature below 420°F within 36 hours of an initiating event, as recommended by EPRI and accepted by the NRC, without challenging the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) or uncovering the core, and without requiring Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) actuation. Performance analyses, including steady-state and transient models using NRELAP5, confirm the DHRS can remove heat across a wide range of loop inventories, accommodate SG tube plugging, fouling, noncondensible gas accumulation, and MSIV failure, and is insensitive to reactor pool temperature variations. With two DHRS trains, RCS temperature drops below 420°F in less than 2 hours and stabilizes below 300°F within 36 hours; with one train and limiting conditions, RCS temperature remains below 420°F within 36 hours. Figures and tables referenced (e.g., Figures 1.2-8, 5.4-10 to 5.4-16; Tables 5.4-5, 5.4-6, 5.4-8) provide detailed design data, failure modes analysis, and temperature cooldown profiles for various DHRS operational scenarios, demonstrating robust decay heat removal capability under both nominal and off-normal conditions. System performance is monitored by RCS temperature and pressure sensors, and initial testing will verify DHRS operation against design basis requirements.

The passages collectively detail the applicability and responsibilities associated with various regulatory guides, Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections, and technical requirements for nuclear plant licensing, particularly for Combined License (COL) applicants and the NuScale certified design. Regulatory Guide 1.178 and SRP 3.9.8 assign the development and implementation of risk-informed inservice inspection (RI-ISI) programs for piping to the COL applicant or licensee, while SRP 16.1, Rev 1, and SRP 19.0/19.1, Rev 3, provide probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) guidelines for technical specification revisions, site-specific hazard evaluation, and PRA technical adequacy. DC/COL-ISG-3 guidance on PRA and severe accident assessments is now incorporated into SRP 19.0, Rev 3, and section 17.4 II.N requires external event analysis beyond the design basis in the plant PRA. Several SRP sections and appendices, such as SRP 2.1.2, 13.1.1–13.1.3, 13.2.1–13.2.2, 13.3, 13.4, 13.5.1.1, 13.5.1.2, 13.6.1, 13.6.3, 13.6.6, and 13.7.1, are identified as not applicable to COL applicants, with no technical requirements specified. SRP 13.6.2, Rev 2, applies to physical security within the NuScale design boundary, and SRP 13.6.4, Rev 1, addresses access authorization for COL applicants. SRP 14.3.12, Rev 1, is partially conforming, with COL applicants responsible for ITAAC on physical security hardware outside the nuclear island. Emergency planning requirements under SRP 13.3, including NRC notifications, communications, and offsite planning, are assigned to the COL applicant, with no changes proposed for existing emergency response facilities. SRP 3.7.4, Rev 2 (Seismic Instrumentation), and SRP 12.1, Rev 4 (ALARA), are not applicable, though ALARA expectations per 10 CFR 20.1101 are noted for COL applicants. NuScale’s Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance with relevant SRP sections, Branch Technical Positions, and operational experience, but assigns responsibility for SRP 1.33, 13.3, 13.5.2.1, and 17.3 to the COL applicant. NuScale conforms to SRP 1.34 and SRP 17.5 requirements for Procurement Document Control, Test Control, and Audits, with no exceptions.

The passages summarize regulatory guidance and evaluation criteria for Combined License (COL) applicants and licensees under 10 CFR Part 52, focusing on Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC) closure per 10 CFR 52.99, which is deemed acceptable for compliance. Containment structural integrity evaluations for internal pressure loadings above design-basis pressure fully conform with requirements. Standard Review Plan (SRP) 14.3 (March 2007) addresses ITAAC scope, with partial conformity for some acceptance criteria for COL applicants and full conformity for others, referencing methodologies for ITAAC development across structural, piping, reactor, electrical, plant systems, human factors engineering, and emergency planning (SRP 14.3.2, 14.3.3, 14.3.4, 14.3.6, 14.3.7, 14.3.9, 14.3.10). Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) documents and SRP sections, such as DC/COL-ISG-13, NUREG-0800 SRP Section 11.2, and Branch Technical Position 11-6, partially conform regarding accidental radioactive releases from liquid waste tanks, with site-specific assessments and implementation (e.g., Offsite Dose Calculation Manual) delegated to COL applicants. Calculations for groundwater/surface water transport and exposure scenarios are not applicable at the design certification stage. DC/COL-ISG-17 (seismic input for site response and soil-structure interaction) is not applicable at design certification but is relevant for COL reviews, while DC/COL-ISG-28 (Advanced Light-Water Reactor probabilistic risk assessments) fully conforms for both design certification and COL applicants. Requirements for design certification detail (SECY-90-241, SECY-90-377), ITAAC guidance (SECY-91-178, SECY-91-210), and use of Design Acceptance Criteria (SECY-92-053) are incorporated into 10 CFR 52 and NRC guidance, with positions on ITAAC demonstration (II.L) and design reliability assurance program description (II.M) in full conformance and no additional requirements specified.

SRP Section 14.2.1 (August 2006) provides guidelines for extended power uprate testing, applicable only to extended power uprate license amendment requests. For Quality Assurance Program Descriptions (QAPDs) in new reactor applications, SRP Section 17.5, based on NQA-1, supersedes SRP 17.1 and 17.3 and is the appropriate guidance for reviewing the NuScale QAPD and its incorporation into the Design Certification Application (DCA). SRP 2.0 (March 2007) addresses Site Characteristics and Site Parameters for Combined License (COL) applications, with all referenced sections (II.2–II.5) marked "Not Applicable" in this context. Administrative and procedural controls are the COL applicant’s responsibility. For SRP 13.7.2, Rev 0 and DSRS 14.2, Rev 0, the test program summary (II.1), regulatory guide conformance (II.2), and individual test descriptions (II.5) fully conform, while initial test program administrative procedures (II.3) and acceptance criteria (II.6) partially conform, with DCA and COL/OL applicant responsibilities delineated. Acceptance criteria from SRP 17.4, SRP 17.5, Rev 1, and 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(i) are either "Partially Conforms" or "Not Applicable" for the design certification applicant, with full conformance deferred to the COL applicant for site-specific and operational aspects, including software reviews, organizational elements (SRP 17.5 II.A.3), inservice/modification inspection (II.J), special process control (II.I), and inspection/test/operating status (II.N). The Reliability Assurance Program (SRP 17.4 II.B) and administrative procedures for experience evaluation (10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(i)) are also COL applicant responsibilities, while the design certification applicant fully conforms to SRP 17.4 II.A and SRP 17.5 II.O (Nonconforming Materials, Parts, or Components). SRP 17.1, Rev 2, and 17.2, Rev 2, apply only to existing NRC-approved QA Programs based on ANSI N45.2, covering design/construction (SRP 17.1) and operations (SRP 17.2), with 50.34(f)(3)(iii) requiring a QA Program for design activities supporting the DCA (partial conformance). The NuScale QA program for design certification partially conforms to SRP 17.5, Rev 1, Section II.B, as site-specific/operational QA is deferred to the COL applicant, but fully conforms to requirements for Corrective Action (II.P), QA Records (II.Q), Training and Qualification (II.S, II.T), and QA Program

Section 1.8 outlines interfaces with the certified design and details Combined License (COL) Information Items in Table 1.8-2, which lists item numbers, descriptions, and section references, including sections 3.6, 3.7, 3.13, 6.6, and 12.3. The COL applicant must identify the responsible operating organization group for maintaining procedures (section 13.5). COL Items 20.1-1 through 20.1-6 are marked "Not used" in section 20.1. Table 1.9-1 provides a conformance status legend. The COL applicant or licensee is responsible for design, installation, and testing practices addressing EMI/RFI and power surges on safety-related I&C systems, as well as emergency response communication systems, with acceptance criteria in DSRS 9.5.2, Rev 0, and site-specific compliance with GDC 1 and physical security plan requirements. Lighting systems (SRP 9.5.3, Rev 3) meet requirements with no additional notes. Inservice testing per the ASME OM Code is the COL applicant’s responsibility, with Code Case acceptability not addressed. Technical Specification acceptance criteria reference existing or plant-specific specifications to be developed by the COL applicant, indicating partial conformity. For DC/COL-ISG-6, 10 CFR 20.1406 compliance partially conforms due to incorrect guidance references. Compliance with GL 2007-01 is limited to power cables under 10 CFR 50.65, with conformance via RG 1.218 as detailed in Chapter 8. Continued compliance with 10 CFR 50.54(hh)(2) is required and addressed in Section 20.2. Various ISGs are summarized: DC/COL-ISG-4 applies to ESP and COL applicants for limited work authorizations; DC/COL-ISG-13 and DC/COL-ISG-16 assign site-specific and mitigating strategies responsibilities to COL applicants; DC/COL-ISG-17, -22, -25, and -26 address seismic, construction, and environmental issues for COL applicants; JLD-ISG-13-01 (Draft) on flooding hazards is site-specific. Sections 8.1.4, 8.2.2, 8.3.1, 8.3.2, 8.4, and DSRS 8.2/8.3.1/8.3.2, Rev 0, specify that GDC 5, 10 CFR 50.65(a)(4), and RG 1.160 compliance are not applicable at this stage and are the COL applicant’s responsibility. Additional ISGs—DC/COL-ISG-2 (financial qualifications), DC/COL-ISG-8 (technical specifications), DC/COL-ISG-13 (evaluation findings

The passages detail the applicability and conformance of various regulatory guides (RGs), safety guides, and requirements for nuclear power reactor safety evaluations, as documented in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, Tier 2. RG 1.4 and RG 1.183 address loss of coolant accident (LOCA) assumptions for PWRs, with RG 1.183 specified for new reactors per SRP Section 15.0.3. Safety Guide 5 applies only to BWR steam line break accidents, while RG 1.24 covers PWR radioactive gas storage tank failures, requiring site-specific guidance from the COL applicant or licensee. RG 1.25 governs fuel handling accidents in both BWRs and PWRs using TID14844 source terms unless the alternative source term under 10 CFR 50.67 is authorized, in which case RG 1.183 is used instead of RG 1.25 and RG 1.195. RG 1.98 applies to BWR offgas system failures, and RG 1.195 provides methods for evaluating design basis accident radiological consequences for reactors using TID14844 source terms. DC/COL-ISG-10 guidance on adverse flow effects is applicable except for BWR-specific aspects. Certain requirements, such as 10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(xii) on alternative hydrogen control systems and 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(vii) on management plans, are excluded or limited in applicability. Issue 191 on PWR sump debris accumulation is confirmed as conforming. RG 1.111 and RG 1.112 address atmospheric and effluent dispersion calculations, with site-specific data required from the COL applicant or licensee; RG 1.113 on aquatic dispersion is not applicable. RG 1.77’s approach is used in Section 15.4.8 analyses, and portions of RG 1.97 are addressed in Section 19.2.3.3.8. Compliance with SRP 5.2.1.1 and 5.2.1.2 is achieved using RGs 1.26, 1.84, 1.147, and 1.192, with partial conformity noted for some. Main Steam System design follows RG 1.29 and related guidance, and ISI procedures are summarized in Sections 5.2.4 and 6.6. RG 1.99 and RG 1.126 requirements are fully met, while RG 1.147 (ISI code cases) and RG 1.162 (thermal annealing) assign responsibilities to the COL applicant or licensee. RG 1.193 code cases are not used unless NRC-authorized. RG 1.194 is referenced for atmospheric dispersion factors in EAB and LPZ assessments, with conformity for control room habitability. Section 1.9 documents conformance with

The passages detail the conformance status of various regulatory guidance and requirements for nuclear power plant design and licensing, particularly as they pertain to the NuScale Power Module (NPM) and similar advanced LWR designs.
