Full conformance is noted for guidance on plugging degraded PWR steam generator tubes, beyond-design-basis aircraft impact assessment (1.217), multiple steam generator tube failure analysis (II.R), conversion of design certification PRA to plant-specific PRA (II.S), ALWR alarm system recommendations (II.T), regulatory treatment of active nonsafety systems (III.A), redefinition of passive failures as active (III.B), periodic testing of protection system actuation functions (11.5, 1.22), bypassed/inoperable status indication (3.6, 5.2, 6.2, 9.3, 11.5, 14.2, 14.3, 1.47), control of preheat temperature for welding (1.50), criteria for independence of electrical safety systems (1.75), environmental qualification of safety-related computer-based I&C systems (1.209), QA list requirements (50.34(f)(3)(ii)), and testing of safety-related logic circuits (GL 96-01). Partial conformance is indicated for seismic floor response spectra development (1.122), protective coatings (1.54), electric penetration assemblies (1.63), instrument sensing lines (1.151), station blackout (1.155), qualification tests for safety-related actuators (1.73), and initial startup/preoperational testing programs (14.2, 1.68.2, 1.68.3), with site-specific or operational aspects delegated to the COL applicant or licensee. Some requirements are not applicable, such as content requirements for the Updated FSAR (1.181), SBWR thermal-hydraulic stability (III.C), meteorological monitoring (1.23), license termination (1.86), main steam isolation valve leakage control (1.96), post-seismic event restart (1.167), risk-informed licensing basis changes (1.174), and RG 5.49 on internal transfers of special nuclear material. The first operational NPM is classified as a prototype per RG 1.20, with subsequent NPMs as non-prototype category I, and prototype provisions only applying to the first unit. The FSAR documents quality group classifications per RG 1.26, and Table 1.9-2 records the conformance status for each regulatory guide. "Conforms" indicates full compliance, "Partially Conforms" applies when only relevant portions are met or equivalent SSCs are used, and "Not Applicable" is used when the guidance does not pertain to the design. Responsibility for site-specific implementation and identifying departures from certain RGs lies with the COL applicant or licensee.

The passages detail the applicability and conformance of various regulatory guides (RGs), standards, and Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections to the seismic, environmental, and electrical qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment in nuclear power plants. For seismic instrumentation (SRP 3.7.4, Rev. 2), comparison with RG 1.166 is not applicable, while SRP 3.10, Rev. 3 shows full conformance for electrical equipment qualification and records, partial conformance for RG 1.97 instrumentation testing, and non-applicability for experience-based qualification. Regarding electrical power systems, RG 1.31 fully conforms, RG 1.32 is only partially applicable, and RG 1.81 is not relevant except for some EDSS portions. SRP BTP 8-9, Rev. 0 is partially conformed to for passive plant design, and Generic Letter 2007-01 and Bulletin 2012-01 are partially conformed to due to the absence of risk-significant underground cables and compliance with SRP BTP 8-9, respectively. For installation, inspection, and environmental qualification, RG 1.30 (endorsing IEEE 336-1971) and RG 1.153 fully conform, RGs 1.63, 1.89, 1.97, and 1.180 partially conform, and RGs 1.158 and 1.204 are not applicable. Seismic qualification guidance in RG 1.100 is partially conformed to, with exceptions for site-specific spectra and older plants, and is not applicable to electrical equipment; NuScale complies with ASME QME-1 2007 except for QR-B5200(g). Environmental qualification partially conforms to RGs 1.183 and 1.100, with IEEE 344-2004 and IEEE 497-2002 cited for seismic and accident monitoring instrumentation. Full conformance is noted for DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0, with RGs 1.73, 1.152, 1.211, and 1.156 applied to Class 1E valve operators, computer systems, cables, and connection assemblies, respectively, with no exceptions.

The passages collectively detail the applicability and conformance of various NRC Regulatory Guides (RGs) and Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) documents to nuclear power plant design certification and Combined Operating License (COL) applicants or licensees. Regulatory Guides 1.166 and 1.167 provide acceptable procedures for pre- and post-earthquake actions and plant restart, while RG 1.132, RG 1.138, and RG 1.198 address site investigations, laboratory soil/rock studies, and seismic soil liquefaction, all of which are site-specific and the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. RG 1.137 is not applicable due to the absence of safety-related emergency diesel generators in the design. NSIR/DPR-ISG-01 and SECY-14-088 provide emergency planning and security guidance, also assigned to the COL applicant. Design-related aspects of RGs such as 1.140 (air filtration), 4.21 (contamination minimization), 8.19 (occupational dose assessment), and 8.38 (high radiation area access) apply to the NuScale standard plant design, but operational and site-specific portions are the COL applicant’s responsibility. RG 1.145 on atmospheric dispersion is not applicable where it does not address relevant phenomena. The conformance table indicates that RG 1.109 and RG 8.8 partially conform, with site-specific implementation by the COL applicant, while RGs 4.13, 8.2, 8.7, 8.10, 8.13, 8.25, 8.34, and 8.23 are not applicable due to their programmatic or institutional focus. RGs 3.3, 3.5, 3.8, 4.1, 4.2, 4.4, 4.7, and 4.11 pertain to environmental monitoring, reporting, and site suitability, all requiring site-specific evaluation by the COL applicant. RGs 4.9, 4.14, 4.16, 4.17, 4.19, and 4.20 are not applicable as they address non-reactor facilities. RG 4.15 on quality assurance for radiological monitoring is applicable to COL applicants. For decommissioning, maintenance, and emergency planning (RGs 1.159, 1.160, 1.179, 1.184, 1.185, 1.188, 1.202, 1.219, 4.22), all responsibilities are assigned to the COL applicant or licensee, except RG 1.203 (Transient and Accident Analysis Methods), which is fully applicable. ISGs such as NSIR/DPR-ISG-02, NSIR/DPR-ISG-03, JLD-ISG-12-01, JLD-ISG

The passages collectively summarize NuScale’s conformance with NRC Regulatory Guides (RGs) as documented in Table 1.9-2 of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, detailing the applicability of various RGs related to operational, programmatic, and physical protection requirements. For RGs governing medical evaluation (1.134), respiratory protection (8.15), radiation protection training (8.27), design bases (1.186), and implementation of changes (1.187), as well as those addressing safeguards, security, and physical protection (e.g., 5.27, 5.54, 5.62, 5.63, 5.66, 5.69, 5.70, 5.71, 5.74, 5.75, 5.76, 5.77, 5.83, 5.84), responsibility lies with the Combined License (COL) applicant or licensee, not NuScale at the design stage. RGs specific to risk-informed inservice testing (1.175) and technical specifications (1.177) are either not used by NuScale or were considered as guidance. Most RGs related to special nuclear material (SNM) control, measurement, and accountability (e.g., 5.3, 5.4, 5.5, 5.8, 5.9, 5.11, 5.13, 5.18, 5.20, 5.21, 5.22, 5.23, 5.25, 5.26, 5.28, 5.29, 5.31, 5.33, 5.34, 5.36, 5.39, 5.42, 5.43, 5.52, 5.53, 5.55, 5.56, 5.57, 5.58, 5.59, 5.60, 5.78, 5.80, 5.81) are not applicable to NuScale, as it does not process SNM, use mixed oxide fuels, or operate as a Part 70 facility; some may become relevant for COL applicants or licensees. RG 5.79 (protection of Safeguards Information) is applicable, and NuScale conforms to its requirements. Guidance on bioassay programs, health physics surveys, and occupational radiation exposure is only applicable to specific facility types or is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. Portions of RG 5.71 related to operational cyber security plans are partially addressed, with full responsibility assigned to the COL applicant or licensee. In summary, most RGs either do not apply to the NuScale design or are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee, with NuScale referencing or conforming to applicable

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, demonstrates conformance with regulatory criteria for air filtration, atmosphere cleanup, ventilation, and accident mitigation systems, with several exceptions due to the unique NuScale design. Engineered Safety Feature (ESF) filter and atmosphere cleanup system guidance for post-design basis accident fission product removal (RG 1.52, SRP 9.4.5) is not applicable, as NuScale does not rely on ESF ventilation systems for accident mitigation, including in the spent fuel storage area (SRP 15.7.4). Control room habitability guidance (SRP 6.4) is also not applicable for emergency ventilation during design basis accidents, since the system does not use emergency standby filtration for operator protection. The control room habitability system conforms to SRP 6.4, Rev 3 and SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3 for ventilation, pressurization, and radioactive material release control, except for ESF atmosphere cleanup aspects. For toxic gas hazards (SRP 6.4.1 II.7), partial conformance is achieved, with programmatic requirements assigned to the COL applicant. During a station blackout, compressed air tanks pressurize the control room envelope, and the structure acts as a passive heat sink. The spent fuel pool area ventilation system (SRP 9.4.2) conforms to GDC 2, 5, and 61, except for ESF atmosphere cleanup, and partially conforms to cask drop accident criteria (SRP 15.7.5), with the reactor building crane meeting single-failure-proof requirements per NUREG-0612. The Equipment and Floor Drainage System (SRP 9.3.3, Rev 3), Control Room Area Ventilation System (SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3), and Lighting Systems (SRP 9.5.3, Rev 3) all conform to requirements for protection against natural phenomena, environmental and dynamic effects, and lighting levels, with no exceptions. Up to 12 modules share the same control room ventilation system during normal operations. The Auxiliary and Radwaste Area Ventilation System (SRP 9.4.3, Rev 3) conforms to GDC 2 and 5, but GDC 60 is not applicable to the Radwaste Building Ventilation (RWBV) system, as exhaust filtration is performed by the Reactor Building Ventilation (RBV) system.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, Tier 2, demonstrates partial conformance with several regulatory criteria related to radiological safety and control room habitability. For Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.196, only control room habitability design aspects within the standard plant design scope are applicable to the Design Certification Application (DCA). SRP 15.1.5.A (Rev 2) guidance on radiological consequences of a main steam line break outside containment, SRP 15.6.2 (Rev 2) for small reactor coolant line failure outside containment, and SRP 15.6.5.B (Rev 1) for leakage from engineered safety feature components outside containment are applicable, but only for the specified portions. SRP 15.4.8.A (Rev 1) and SRP 15.6.3 (Rev 2) are relevant only for technical specification requirements on primary-to-secondary leak rate and coolant system iodine activity, respectively. Control room habitability evaluation references RG 1.78 (partially applicable), excludes RG 1.197 inleakage testing (responsibility of COL applicant/licensee), and applies SRP 6.4, Rev 3, II.6.B dose guidelines for new reactors (conforming), while II.6.A for operating reactors is not applicable. RG 1.183 is partially applicable, DSRS 15.0.3, II.2 requirements for control room radiological habitability fully conform, and II.3 for Technical Support Center (TSC) habitability partially conform, with dose acceptance criteria met for the TSC when AC power is available. SRP 18.0, Rev 2, II.B on human factors engineering is not applicable, and analytical methods for control room habitability, including dose limits and accident duration, conform to standards. Post-seismic event procedures require measuring gaps between fuel racks and pool walls, with corrective actions as needed. The NRC considers the referenced RG outdated; fuel and cladding failure criteria are now governed by SRP (NUREG-0800) 4.2, Appendix B, and radiological criteria by RG 1.183. For SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1, only acceptance criteria for fuel storage and handling systems inside the Reactor Building apply, not those for containment, as NuScale lacks a containment building for fuel handling. The radiation detection criterion in containment partially applies in intent. The RBC system meets NUREG-0612 single-failure-proof guidelines, ensuring critical load safety. For SRP 15.7.5, Rev 2 (Spent Fuel Cask Drop Accidents), criteria for site acceptability, ESF systems, radioactivity control, and atmosphere clean-up are not applicable, but the RBC system’s single-failure-proof design partially conforms to plant design feature criteria, eliminating the need for calculation.

The passages review the applicability and compliance of various Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and regulatory guidance to the NuScale reactor design and similar advanced reactors. For SRP 9.2.6 (Condensate Storage Facilities), SRP 9.3.3 (Equipment and Floor Drainage System), SRP 10.4.1 (Main Condensers), and SRP 10.4.2 (Main Condenser Evacuation System), the NuScale design meets requirements for environmental and dynamic effects, sharing of SSCs, radioactive release control, and 10 CFR 20.1406, with no safety impairments or containment barrier penetrations. Emergency planning (SRP 13.3) partially conforms, as containment and effluent monitoring programmatic aspects are assigned to the COL applicant. For designs using natural circulation, SRP sections 15.3.1–15.3.4 and 15.4.4–15.4.5, as well as mid-loop operation and SBO mitigation requirements, are not applicable due to the absence of forced coolant flow, external loops, or drain down conditions. Several SRP sections—5.4.6, 5.4.8, 5.4.13, 6.2.1.1.C, 6.7, and 9.3.5—are not applicable as they pertain only to BWRs. SRP 5.2.2 (Overpressure Protection) only partially conforms for PWRs at power, as the NuScale design lacks a secondary safety-grade RPS trip signal. Leak-Before-Break is applied to MS and FW lines inside containment, while seismic classification for main steam lines is not applicable. SRP 6.2.1.2, 6.2.3, 6.2.1.5, BTP 6-2, 6.5.2, 6.5.3, 6.5.5, and BTP 6-3 are not applicable due to the absence of subcompartments, secondary containment, containment spray, pressure suppression pools, or ice condenser containment in NuScale. The only credited ESF fission product control system is the containment vessel with isolation valves and passive barriers. SECY-92-092 guidance on containment performance goals is incorporated into NRC regulatory guidance and rulemaking.

The reviewed passages summarize conformance with various Branch Technical Positions (BTPs) and Standard Review Plan (SRP) criteria for nuclear plant systems, with distinctions between certified design responsibilities and those assigned to the Combined License (COL) applicant. For BTP 11-5, Rev 4, Section B.1 (Waste Gas System Leak or Failure Analysis) and BTP 11-6, Rev 4, Section B.3 (Radioactive Source Term), partial conformance is noted, with exceptions for BWR-specific or site-specific aspects, which are the COL applicant’s responsibility. Sections B.2 of BTP 11-5 and BTP 6-1 (pH and hydrogen generation in PWRs) fully conform, while other sections such as BTP 11-6, Rev 4, Sections B.2, B.4, B.5, B.6, and B.7, and BTP 5-1, Rev 3, Sections B.2, B.3, and B.4, are not applicable or only partially met due to site-specific requirements assigned to the COL applicant. Full conformance is demonstrated for BTP 3-3, Rev 3 (protection against postulated piping failures outside containment) and BTP 3-4, Rev 2 (rupture locations in fluid system piping), with no exceptions. For BTP 5-3, Rev 2 (fracture toughness), preservice and inservice surveillance requirements partially conform due to exceptions for older plant criteria and references not applicable to the NuScale design, while operating limitations and surveillance program requirements fully conform. DSRS BTP 5-4, Rev 0, Section B.1 (Residual Heat Removal System functional requirements) also fully conforms. Relevant sections referenced include 3.6, 15.1, 15.2, 15.5, and 15.6.

The review of the NuScale design against relevant Standard Review Plan (SRP) criteria and regulatory requirements demonstrates broad conformance across multiple technical areas. For SRP 3.2.2, Rev 2 (System Quality Group Classification), the design meets GDC 1 and 10 CFR 50.55a with no exceptions, except for partial conformance regarding the use of RG 1.85 (withdrawn in 2004 and incorporated into RG 1.84) and some language in App.A and Table A-1 referencing SSCs not present in NuScale. Foundations testing, inservice surveillance (II.7), and specification of transients for mechanical components (SRP 3.9.1, Rev 3, II.1) fully conform. For SRP 3.9.4, Rev 3 (Control Rod Drive Systems), descriptive information is adequate, with a minor typographical error noted in seismic design references; construction codes, load combinations (ASME Code Section III, NB-3113), and material specifications (SRP 4.5.1, Rev 3) are met, with RG 1.85 withdrawal acknowledged. Requirements for austenitic stainless steel, cleaning, and the NuScale QAPD (ANSI/ASME NQA-1-2008, NQA-1a-2009, RG 1.28, Rev. 4) are satisfied. The Control Rod Drive Systems Operability Assurance Program (SRP 3.9.4, Rev 3, II.4), reactor pressure vessel internals (SRP 3.9.5, Rev 3, II.1, II.2, II.4), and reactor internal/core support structure materials and welding (SRP 4.5.2, Rev 3, II.1, II.2) all fully conform. Partial conformance is noted for asymmetric blowdown loads (SRP 3.9.5, Rev 3, II.5) and flow-induced vibration/acoustic resonance (App A, II.6) due to references to non-applicable LWR/SSC conditions and BWR-specific aspects. Fuel system and nuclear design aspects meet all requirements of SRP 4.2, Rev 3, SRP 4.3, Rev 0, and DSRS 4.4, Rev 0, including fuel rod failure, coolability, structural response, reactivity coefficients, control rod worth, analytical methods, and thermal margin, with no exceptions or deviations noted.

The review confirms full conformance with SRP 3.6.2, Rev 2, including all evaluated sections on postulated pipe rupture locations, analysis methods, break criteria, dynamic effects, jet impingement modeling, and effects on mechanical components, with no exceptions. For SRP 3.6.3, Rev 1, Leak-Before-Break Evaluation Procedures, compliance with GDC 4 is met. Under SRP 3.9.1, Rev 3, the use of computer programs for dynamic and static analyses conforms, while experimental stress analysis methods are not applicable. SRP 3.9.2, Rev 3, shows partial conformance for vibration, thermal expansion, and dynamic effects testing, with test performance and corrective actions assigned to the COL applicant; all other sections, including compliance with GDC 2, analytical solutions, preoperational test programs, structural design adequacy, test/analysis correlation, and test specifications, fully conform. SRP 3.9.3, Rev 3, requirements for pressure relief devices, component supports, and core support structures for ASME Code Class 1, 2, and 3 components are met, with the component supports section not applicable due to design differences from PWRs. SRP 3.12, Rev 1, requirements for piping systems, components, and supports, including analysis methods, modeling, stress criteria, and support design, are fully met. SRP 3.9.6, Rev 3, shows partial conformance for functional design and qualification of pumps, valves, and dynamic restraints (except safety-related pumps), and for inservice valve testing, with further details in referenced sections; inservice testing for dynamic restraints is not applicable, and relief requests/proposed alternatives fully conform. Operational programs are not applicable, as they are the COL applicant’s responsibility. SRP 3.10, Rev 3, requirements for valve qualification and inservice testing of safety-related pumps and valves, as well as the use of new industry codes and standards, are fully met. For turbine and water systems, SRP 10.4.3, Rev 3 (Turbine Gland Seal) conforms to GDC 60, SRP 10.4.4, Rev 3 (Turbine Bypass System) meets GDC 4, with the MSIV Alternate Leakage Path not applicable, SRP 10.4.5, Rev 3 (Circulating Water System) conforms to GDC 4, and SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3 (Condensate Cleanup System) is not applicable as it pertains only to BWRs. No exceptions or additional comments are noted for applicable sections.

The passages detail the applicability and supersession of various Standard Review Plan (SRP) and Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) sections for radiological consequence analyses of postulated accidents in nuclear power plants. For main steam line failures outside containment in PWRs, SRP 15.1.5.A, Rev 2, is partially superseded by SRP 15.0.3, which now governs radiological analyses, assumptions, acceptance criteria, and methodologies; similarly, SRP 15.1.5.A Appendix A is overridden. SRP 15.6.1, Rev 2, applies only to LWRs with power-operated pressurizer relief valves, while SRP 15.6.4, Rev 2, and SRP 15.6.5.D, Rev 1, are applicable only to BWRs. SRP 19.4, Rev 0, is partially applicable except for BWR-specific containment venting, vessel flooding, and spent fuel pool mitigative measures. For SRP 15.4.7, Rev 2, the safety analysis demonstrates no fuel failures and conforms for offsite radiological consequences. SRP 15.4.8.A, Rev 1, and SRP 15.6.3, Rev 2, have their radiological consequence methodologies and assumptions superseded by SRP 15.0.3. The technical content of SRP 15.7.3, Rev 2, is now in Branch Technical Position 11-6 (Section 11.2), with only partial conformance. For SRP 15.7.4, Rev 1, acceptance criteria and methodology for site and ESF system dose mitigation and dose modeling are superseded by SRP 15.0.3. SRP 15.4.8, Rev 3, for rod ejection accidents in PWRs, fully conforms for monitoring instrumentation, reactivity accident effects, and dose limits, while SRP 15.4.8.A, Rev 1, is partially superseded by SRP 15.0.3 for radiological analyses. SRP 15.4.9, Rev 3, and 15.4.9.A, Draft Rev 3, for BWR rod drop accidents, are not applicable to PWRs. For SRP 15.6.2, guidance on primary coolant system iodine activity technical specifications is applicable to the DCA. For SRP 15.6.3, guidance on steam generator tube failure radiological consequences is applicable. DSRS 15.6.5 conforms to SRP 15.0.3, which supersedes prior radiological analysis guidance in SRP 15.6.5. SRP 15.6.5.A is partially applicable for LOCA radiological consequence calculations and post-LOCA containment leakage modeling, while SR

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, demonstrates full conformance with SRP 3.9.5, Rev 3 for reactor pressure vessel internals (excluding core support structures), SRP 4.5.2, Rev 3 for nondestructive examination of reactor internal and core support structure materials, SRP 5.2.2, Rev 3 for overpressure protection (including TMI Action Plan requirements), and DSRS 5.3.2, Rev 0 for upper-shelf energy, pressure-temperature limits, and pressurized thermal shock, adhering to all applicable regulations, codes, and basis documents. For BTP 5-2, Rev 3, regarding overpressurization protection of PWRs at low temperatures, the report partially conforms, specifically meeting ASME Section XI Appendix G criteria for low-temperature overpressure protection operability, with no other exceptions noted. BTP 3-1, Rev 2, which addresses main steam component classification outside the reactor coolant pressure boundary, is not applicable as it pertains only to BWRs. SRP 5.2.3, Rev 3, Sections II.1 and II.2 are partially conforming due to BWR-specific subtier guidance, while Section II.4.G is not applicable as it is the responsibility of the COL applicant. For SRP 5.2.2, Rev 3, material specifications (II.1) conform, BWR-specific design requirements (II.2) are not applicable, and PWR low-temperature operation (II.4) and testing/inspections (II.5) conform, but technical specifications (II.6) only partially conform due to inapplicable or partially applicable subtier guidance. The overpressurization protection system for PWRs at low temperatures, per BTP 5-2, Rev 3, conforms to NRC requirements for system design, installation, single active failure, instrumentation and controls, operability testing, applicable guidance, seismic design, and inadvertent actuation analyses, but only partially conforms for interlocks (B.10), as the criterion targets large LWRs with pressure relief from low-pressure systems not normally connected to the primary system; no other exceptions or comments are noted.

The NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application (DCA) partially addresses emergency planning and regulatory requirements, conforming to SRP 13.3, Rev 3 by including an emergency response data system and a technical support center, while leaving site-specific elements—such as evacuation time estimates, operational support center, emergency operations facility, and emergency planning ITAAC—to the Combined License (COL) applicant. The DCA applies regulatory guidance only as necessary to ensure COL applicants can meet requirements, with full implementation—including site-specific design, construction, testing, inspection, and record maintenance—remaining the COL applicant’s responsibility. The NuScale DCA (Revision 5, July 2020) details principal design criteria, safety features, emergency systems, instrumentation, controls, electrical and cooling systems, fuel handling, waste management, and auxiliary systems. COL applicants must develop a Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) referencing the NuScale FSAR and provide site-specific operational program details and schedules for areas such as inservice inspection, environmental qualification, fire protection, radiation protection, emergency planning, security, quality assurance, and operator training, in accordance with Regulatory Guide 1.33, Rev 3. Additional COL requirements include procedures for ultimate heat sink instrumentation (NEI 12-02, Rev 1), firefighting enhancements (10 CFR 50.155(b)(2)), and hazard evaluations for multi-unit sites (10 CFR 52.79(a)(31)). NuScale Power, LLC retains overall design responsibility, while COL applicants must address specific items such as flood mitigation (COL Item 3.4-2), fuel performance for non-baseload operations (4.2-1), module stability (7.0-1), critical load handling (9.1-5), operating procedures (13.5-5), a Startup Administration Manual (14.2-2), and human performance monitoring (18.12-1). Certain items (1.4.1, 5.2-1) are not used. Personnel qualification and training, safety-related diesel generators, and operator simulation facilities are not applicable to the NuScale design, as these are site-specific or operational responsibilities of the COL applicant. The design does not require safety-related emergency diesel generators and is not subject to operator staffing requirements of 10 CFR 50.54(m)(2)(i) and (iii). The design conforms to turbine missile protection (Section 1.115) and partially to requirements for locks, intrusion alarms, access controls, and vehicle protection, with site-specific implementation by the COL applicant or licensee. Compliance with vehicle protection (F090 Site Layout Plan, 10 CFR 73.55) and liquid mass measurement systems (Section 5.48) is also required at the COL or licensee level. The NRC inspected the test program using IP 35034, 35017, and 36100, and all design information is subject to NuScale Power LLC’s copyright.

A Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must address a comprehensive set of site-specific and programmatic requirements. The applicant must identify the plant location and address reactor vessel internals reliability and aging degradation through programs such as the Comprehensive Vibration Assessment Program and steam generator programs. A Boric Acid Control Program must be developed, including inspection types and frequencies to ensure reactor coolant pressure boundary integrity, and a reactor vessel material surveillance program consistent with NUREG 0800, Section 5.3.1.5.3 must be described, with compliance to 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, and RG 1.161 if surveillance indicates non-compliance. The applicant must verify containment vessel flange contact pressure at accident conditions and demonstrate that the as-built containment internal free volume provides sufficient margin for design pressure and temperature, considering internal structures and manufacturing processes. A certified Overpressure Protection Report per ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticles NB-7200 and NC-7200, is required for both the reactor coolant pressure boundary and secondary system, including low temperature overpressure protection. The applicant is responsible for all plant-specific programmatic information, including the reactor vessel surveillance program.

For piping systems, the applicant must complete routing outside the containment and bioshield, identify high- and moderate-energy line locations, update Tables 3.6-1 and 3.6-2 as needed, and perform a pipe rupture hazards analysis—including dynamic and environmental effects—for lines outside the reactor pool bay in the Reactor Building (RXB), evaluating multi-module impacts and subcompartment pressurization. Site-specific seismic demands for critical structures, systems, and components must be shown to be bounded by the certified design, or the design must be modified. Structure-soil-structure interaction analyses for the Reactor Building, Control Building, Radioactive Waste Building, and Turbine Generator Buildings are required, as well as configuration analyses for the Reactor Building and NuScale Power Module layout. The applicant must demonstrate that seismic demands for empty dry dock conditions are bounded by FSAR capacity, determine interaction planes for soil-structure interaction, and establish bioshield lifting methods, ensuring component integrity under required loads. COL Item 3.6-4 is not used.

The applicant must develop a site-specific, strain-compatible soil profile, confirm seismic site characteristics are within certified design parameters, and ensure nearby structures will not collapse and affect safety-related buildings during a safe shutdown earthquake. Soil-structure interaction analyses using NuScale SASSI2010 models are required, along with a seismic monitoring system and program per RG 1.12 and RG 1.166, and post-earthquake procedures using seismic instrumentation data. Performance-based response spectra analyses at various depths must be provided, justifying V/H spectral ratios, and in-structure response spectra for the NuScale Power Module and building locations must be shown to be bounded by certified spectra (referencing specific FSAR figures and tables); otherwise, the design

The NuScale Power Plant design certification requires Combined License (COL) applicants to address specific COL information items, including providing site-specific construction and operation schedules for each power module (COL Item 1.1-2), identifying construction and operation contractors (COL Item 1.4-1), listing departures from the certified design (COL Item 1.8-1), reviewing conformance with regulatory criteria effective six months prior to the COL application docket date (COL Item 1.9-1), and specifying the seismic monitoring program implementation milestone (COL Item 3.7-8). The design power includes a 2% margin for measurement uncertainty. Applicants must submit site-specific diagrams and legends for electrical, I&C, and piping systems (COL Items 1.7-1, 1.7-2), demonstrate that site characteristics are bounded by Table 2.0-1 or justify deviations (COL Item 2.0-1), and provide detailed descriptions of the switchyard, offsite power connections, grid stability studies, and testing procedures (COL Items 8.2-1, 8.2-2, 8.2-3). Site-specific auxiliary AC power, electrical heat tracing, and grounding and lightning protection systems must also be described (COL Items 8.3-1, 8.3-2, 8.3-3). The applicant is responsible for implementing life cycle processes for I&C systems per IEEE Std 1074-2006 and IEEE Std 1012-2004, following the NuScale Digital I&C Software Configuration Management Plan, and ensuring compliance with ANSI/ANS-40.37, RG 1.143, 10 CFR 20.1406, NRC IE Bulletin 80-10, and 10 CFR 50.34a for mobile equipment. Site-specific process control and effluent monitoring programs must be developed per NEI 07-10A, ANSI N13.1-2011, ANSI N42.18-2004, and Regulatory Guides 1.21, 1.33, and 4.15, with additional programs to demonstrate compliance with 10 CFR 20.1406 and RG 4.21. Construction, testing, and repairs are the applicant’s responsibility. The NuScale design adheres to RG 1.141 via ANS N271-1976 for containment isolation, and the COL holder determines applicability of cable condition monitoring per RG 1.218 during maintenance rule program development. The FSAR distinguishes between Conceptual Design Information (CDI) and COL items, with Table 1.8-2 listing all COL items and their FSAR sections. Table 1.8-1 summarizes certified design interfaces, Section 1.2 lists included structures, systems, and components, and COL Item 1.1-1 requires specification of the plant location. The safety display and indication system is integrated into the control room

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must evaluate hazards from construction activities to safety-related and risk-significant SSCs of both existing and new units at co-located sites per 10 CFR 52.79(a)(31), identify management and administrative controls to mitigate these hazards, and ensure limiting conditions for operation are not exceeded (excluding individual NuScale Power Plants with operating modules). The applicant must describe the use of probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) and risk-informed applications throughout the COL application, construction, and operational phases, evaluate site-specific external event hazards (e.g., liquefaction, slope failure), screen them for risk significance, and assess risks from external hazards not bounded by the design certification. Key PRA assumptions and data must be confirmed or modified for as-built, as-operated conditions, and severe accident management guidelines for beyond-design-basis events must be developed. The applicant must provide detailed site-specific information, including geographic, demographic, meteorological, hydrologic, geologic, seismologic, and geotechnical characteristics, update Table 3.2-1 for SSC classification, confirm final locations and routing for flood protection, develop inspection and maintenance programs for water-tight features, ensure tanks or water sources cannot flood critical buildings, and specify waterproofing/dampproofing measures with design life considerations. The applicant must also confirm that nearby structures exposed to flooding or extreme wind loads (e.g., tornadoes, hurricanes) will not collapse and impact the Reactor or Seismic Category I Control Buildings, demonstrate that turbine and automobile missile hazards are bounded or mitigated, and evaluate site-specific hazards for more energetic missiles. Required programs and documentation include containment cleanliness, control room habitability per Regulatory Guide 1.78 Rev. 1, fuel handling and storage procedures (including criticality control, cask use, transfer procedures, periodic testing, heavy loads handling, and structural evaluation of storage racks), a neutron absorber material qualification report per TR-0816-49833, and a leakage control program for systems outside containment that may contain accident source term radioactive materials.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must develop and implement a Strategic Water Chemistry Plan, including site-specific chemistry control programs that comply with the latest EPRI Pressurized Water Reactor Secondary Water Chemistry Guidelines and NEI 97-06. The applicant must establish procedures to evaluate neutron attenuation uncertainty due to material lot variability, inspect as-manufactured materials for contamination and defects, and select chemicals for the reactor component cooling water system based on site-specific water quality and materials. Potable water sources, pre-treatment methods, sanitary waste storage and disposal, and measures to prevent long-term corrosion and organic fouling in the site cooling water system must be described, ensuring compatibility with plant chemistry requirements. A flow-accelerated corrosion monitoring program for steam and power conversion systems must be implemented per Generic Letter 89-08 and EPRI NSAC-202L, and the size and number of new and spent resin tanks in the condensate polishing system, auxiliary boiler fuel supply, and secondary water chemistry analysis must be specified. For liquid and gaseous effluents, the applicant must calculate public doses using site-specific parameters, compare results to 10 CFR 50 Appendix I, 10 CFR 20.1302, and 40 CFR 190, evaluate accidental releases per NRC Branch Technical Position 11-6, and perform dilution flow evaluations. An Offsite Dose Calculation Manual (ODCM) per NEI 07-09A and a Radiological Environmental Monitoring Program (REMP) per NUREG-1301 and NUREG-0133 must be developed, considering local land use and all radiation pathways. The applicant must also describe an ALARA program, identify additional site-specific contained radiation sources exceeding 100 millicuries, and establish administrative controls for high and very high radiation areas per Regulatory Guide 8.38. A cost-benefit analysis per 10 CFR 50.34a, 10 CFR 50 Appendix I, and Regulatory Guide 1.110, as well as a site-specific analysis, is required. Fitness-for-duty (FFD) programs compliant with 10 CFR 26 for operations and construction must be described, along with a schedule for the Initial Test Program, test abstracts for pre-operational testing of potable water and seismic monitoring systems, and selection of the plant configuration for the Island Mode Test. Site-specific selection methodologies, inspections, tests, analyses, and acceptance criteria for emergency planning and plant structures, systems, and components must be provided, along with final plant-specific information for Technical Specifications and an owner-controlled requirements manual. The Human Factors Engineering (HFE) program must integrate operational experience from nuclear and non-nuclear industries, as required by 10 CFR 52.47(a)(22) and 10 CFR 52.41(b)(1), and address NRC guidance in the FSAR. The applicant is responsible for developing a risk-informed, performance-based fire protection program per 10 CFR 50.48(c), and must use Regulatory

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must provide comprehensive, site-specific documentation and plans in several key areas. This includes detailed descriptions of the offsite communication system and its interface with onsite systems to ensure continuous command and control during normal and emergency conditions (COL 9.5-1), and the location of security power equipment within a vital area per 10 CFR 73.55(e)(9)(vi)(B) (COL 9.5-2). The applicant must describe the corporate and onsite management and technical support organizations, including qualification requirements for all management, operating, technical, and maintenance positions (COL 13.1-1, 13.1-2, 13.1-3), and provide initial and requalification training programs for reactor operators, senior reactor operators, and non-licensed staff (COL 13.2-1, 13.2-2). Detailed plans for the onsite Operational Support Center (OSC), its direct communication with the control room (COL 13.3-1), and an emergency operations facility for overall emergency response management (COL 13.3-2) are required, along with a comprehensive emergency plan compliant with 10 CFR 50.47, 10 CFR 50 Appendix E, 10 CFR 52.48, and 10 CFR 52.79(a)(21) (COL 13.3-3). Security plans must address physical security, training, safeguards contingency, site security during construction and operation, and compliance with Table 5-1 of TR-0416-48929 (COL 13.6-1, 13.6-2). The applicant must also submit detailed site-specific operating, maintenance, and emergency procedures, including their classification, format, and content, and plans for their development, implementation, and control, with schedules and responsible organizational groups identified. Emergency Operating Procedures (EOPs) require a Procedures Generation Package with technical guidelines, a writer’s guide, verification and validation programs, and operator training. The applicant is responsible for establishing a plant procedure improvement program per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(ii), covering emergency procedures, reliability analyses, human factors engineering, crisis management, operator training, and industry coordination. The facility design meets 10 CFR 50.47(b)(8) and Appendix E requirements for emergency facilities and equipment, with control room and remote shutdown station alarm system reliability per SECY-93-087, and communication equipment in the control room per PDC-19 to support all operations, even during subsystem failure or power loss. While the design conforms to GDC 3 and GDC 4, some acceptance criteria under 10 CFR 73.45 are not applicable, and site-specific security communication systems are the applicant’s responsibility. The design partially conforms to 10 CFR 73.55(j) for physical protection and communication. Control room design and layout support NRC guidance (RG

The pressurizer (PZR) is an integral component of the reactor coolant system (RCS), occupying the volume above the baffle plate within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and containing heater bundles, spray nozzles, and instrument lines. Its primary function is to maintain RCS pressure within the permitted operating range during normal operation and transients, using electrical heaters to increase pressure by generating steam and spray nozzles to decrease pressure by condensing steam. Two heater bundles above the baffle plate heat water to a temperature higher than the core outlet, ensuring a saturated water-steam interface, while two spray nozzles—supplied by the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS)—provide pressure reduction and maintain chemistry equilibrium. Minimal spray flow is maintained during normal operation to reduce thermal stresses and maintain equilibrium. The pressurizer is sized to accommodate expected RCS volume changes, with a total volume approximately 23% of the RCS, and is designed to prevent reactor trips or safety system actuation during transients when support systems are functional. Nominal water level is programmed as a function of reactor power, controlled between 50–60% at full power, with analytical limits of 80% (upper) and 35% (lower), and is adjusted by the CVCS to absorb volume changes, especially between hot zero power (HZP) and 15% power. Instrumentation monitors pressure and level, providing data to the Module Protection System (MPS), Module Control System (MCS), and control room. During shutdown and refueling, decay heat is removed via the steam generator system (SGS) and decay heat removal system (DHRS), with the PZR not relied upon for safe shutdown. Overpressure protection is provided by reactor safety valves (RSVs) and relief valves, with the PZR, CVCS, and automatic isolation features preventing RCS solid conditions and protecting the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). During low-temperature operations, nitrogen is used for initial pressurization and bubble replacement, and the PZR is vented to match containment pressure before opening vent and relief valves. The MPS disconnects heater power if water level drops below the heater elements, using Class 1E circuit breakers for safety isolation. Pressurizer heaters are grouped, with Group 1 providing steady-state control and Groups 2 and 3 serving as backup during pressure drops. The PZR design ensures sufficient surge and steam volume to prevent liquid or two-phase flow to the RSVs during transients, and effective fluid mixing is achieved through thermal effects and flow over SG tube bundles. All PZR regions are constructed for required inspection and tested as part of RPV testing, with compliance to BPVC and seismic qualification addressed in referenced sections.

The pressurizer baffle plate is a critical, safety-related internal structure within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), forming the lower boundary of the pressurizer region and integrating with the upper steam plenums. It functions as a thermal barrier, limits heat transfer between the pressurizer and reactor coolant system (RCS) coolant, and provides hydraulic communication via eight 4-inch diameter flow holes for rapid pressure equalization and mixing during various operational states. The baffle plate also serves as the tubesheet for the upper termination of steam generator tube bundles, provides penetrations for control rod drive shafts and incore instrument guide tubes, and supports the upper riser assembly. The pressurizer, defined by the RPV head and a portion of the cylindrical height below, maintains a saturated steam-water interface to regulate RCS pressure, with level controlled by the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS). The RPV is a cylindrical steel vessel, approximately 9 ft in diameter and 58 ft high, designed for 1,850 psia, comprising a torispherical head, cylindrical upper and lower sections, and flanged connections. It contains penetrations for main steam, feedwater, pressurizer heaters, CVCS, and instrumentation, and supports or attaches to the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs), seismic supports, heater bundles, in-core instrumentation, steam generator piping, RCS piping, reactor safety valves (RSVs), vent valves, and recirculation valves. The RPV and steam generators are certified per ASME BPVC Section III, with pressure-temperature curves compliant with 10 CFR 50, Appendix G. Two 400 kW pressurizer heater bundles are mounted 180 degrees apart through the RPV side. The containment vessel (CNV) is a 76 ft tall, 15 ft diameter cylindrical steel vessel with a bolted flange for maintenance access, supporting the RPV both laterally and vertically via support lugs, plates, and a skirt. The CNV houses the RPV, CRDMs, and associated piping, forming part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) and acting as a barrier to fission product release. The RPV contains the reactor core, reactor vessel internals (RVI), steam generators (SGs), and pressurizer, with the RVI directing coolant flow and including sub-assemblies such as core support, surveillance capsules, riser assemblies, flow diverter, and pressurizer spray nozzles. Reactor System Valves (RSVs) provide overpressure protection for RCPB piping external to the RPV, including RCS injection, discharge, degasification, pressurizer spray, ECCS pilot actuator piping, and CNV nozzles. All RPV surfaces are kept dry with non-chloride desiccant and shipped under positive pressure, while SG tubes are filled with nitrogen at a dew point below -20°F. Inside containment,

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during both power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps and external piping, with all major components—including the core, pressurizer (PZR), two steam generators (SGs), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, and decay heat removal system (DHRS)—integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). The core heats the coolant, which rises through the lower and upper riser assemblies, is redirected by the PZR baffle plate, and flows over the SG helical tube bundles, transferring heat to the secondary side and producing superheated steam with a moisture content ≤0.10% by weight at full power. The cooled, denser coolant descends through the annular downcomer and reenters the core, completing the natural circulation loop, with flow driven by density differences and elevation between the core and SGs. The RCS flow path is optimized for low pressure losses, so pressure is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging does not affect natural circulation flow rate. The system includes small bypass flow paths in the upper riser to maintain boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery after non-LOCA transients. The pressurizer, integral to the RPV above the coolant and separated by a baffle plate, operates at 2100 psia and 650°F, with two 1-inch spray nozzles and heater bundles (each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to maintain RCS pressure. Instrumentation provides control room indication of RCS pressure, pressurizer level, spray flow, and heater output, with the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) automating RCS level and pressure control. The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) maintains pressurizer level (normally 50–60%), adjusts RCS chemistry, and controls boron concentration, supplying continuous spray flow to minimize thermal stresses and maintain chemistry. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core—89 ft³, SG region—621 ft³, and PZR—578 ft³ (487 ft³ cylindrical). During off-normal transients, initial pool temperatures of 100°F or 140°F have minimal effect on RCS cooldown after 36 hours, even with reduced heat removal from fouling, tube plugging, or noncondensible gas accumulation. The system’s design, including intertwined SG tube bundles and elimination of external piping, prevents asymmetric core cooling and large break LOCA events. Key operational parameters—coolant temperatures, flow rates, press

The leakage control program incorporates an initial test program, a re-testing schedule, and measures to minimize system leakage, with containment penetrations designed for local leak rate tests (Type B and Type C) in accordance with ANSI/ANS 56.8, Regulatory Guide 1.163, and NEI 94-01, though the performance-based containment leak-test program is not applicable. Hydrogen control systems for a 100% fuel-clad metal-water reaction are not required per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f), and the design aligns with regulatory positions on minimizing ISLOCA probability, hydrogen measurement and mitigation, containment performance, and includes a dedicated containment vent penetration. Containment leak rate testing partially conforms to Type C test duration requirements, and post-accident sampling addresses analysis of dissolved hydrogen, oxygen, and chloride. Leakage detection methods comply with RG 1.45 Positions C.2.1 and C.2.2, detecting unidentified leakage into containment at rates ≥0.05 gpm with <1 hour response for rates >1 gpm. Radiation detectors in the CES condenser vent line provide early RCS leakage indication (C.2.3), but all leakage is classified as unidentified until located and quantified by other means, with RCS inventory balance and CES flow comparison used for determination. Primary detection methods include monitoring containment pressure changes and CES condensate, with requirements detailed in technical specifications. The CES maintains containment pressure below saturation, making water level monitoring impractical; leakage from RSVs, ECCS valves, and actuators is exhausted into containment and included as unidentified leakage, while CVCS leakage outside the RCPB is identified and collected via closed piping. RCCWS leakage is detected by expansion tank level monitoring and radiation detectors, and primary-to-secondary leakage is monitored by radiation detectors on various systems, with grab sampling capability. The CES sample vessel collects condensed water, monitored for pressure, temperature, level, and radioactivity, with alarms for approaching operability limits and remote monitoring of gaseous and liquid effluents. Accurate leakage rates are obtained by measuring condensate, applying a correction for carryover, and ensuring containment pressure remains below saturation. The system can detect minimum RCS leak rates of 0.007 gpm, with a 1 gpm leak causing a 0.1 psi pressure rise in under a minute. Effective detection relies on maintaining containment pressure below the vapor pressure of the lowest internal wall temperature, as internal condensation can compromise accuracy. Containment leakage monitoring instrumentation detects only pressure boundary failures within the containment vessel.

Maintenance activity controls are implemented to ensure Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) functionality is not compromised by changes to analytical inputs, assumptions, or activities that could introduce debris or potential debris sources into containment. Additional controls limit the introduction of coating materials, and an inspection program verifies containment vessel (CNV) cleanliness before closure for power operation. Automatic containment isolation is actuated on low-low pressurizer level and high containment pressure during in-containment events with core damage, reliably preventing radiological release from the containment evacuation system (CES). The containment high pressure analytical limit is subatmospheric, ensuring setpoints prevent environmental release, and all design basis events meet thermal and hydraulic criteria without relying on CES isolation via high radiation signals; no design basis event results in core damage. Section 19.2 analyses confirm severe accident conditions also generate reliable containment isolation signals, independent of high radiation. Calculated peak containment pressures for design basis events remain below the CNV internal design pressure, and even under severe accident progression, peak pressures do not challenge containment integrity (TR-0716-50424, Section 2.8). The CNV is an ASME BPVC Class MC (steel) containment, designed, fabricated, and tested as a Class 1 pressure vessel, maintained at a vacuum during normal operations to eliminate insulation, minimize moisture and corrosion, facilitate leakage detection, limit oxygen for combustible gas control, and increase steam condensation rates during accidents. The CNV contains radioactive material post-accident, provides heat rejection to the reactor pool after ECCS actuation, and supports the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Manways and penetrations allow access and accommodate process piping, electrical, and instrumentation. Approximately 90% of the CNV is immersed in the reactor pool, which acts as a passive heat sink. During ECCS actuation, steam vented from the RPV causes a temporary spike in containment pressure and temperature, but passive cooling and condensation by the reactor pool rapidly reduce and maintain these parameters below design limits. Water lost from the RPV during an accident remains within containment and is passively returned, with no postulated small-break LOCA capable of uncovering fuel. The low-power core (160 MWt) reduces decay heat removal requirements and simplifies emergency planning. The absence of RPV insulation eliminates potential sump screen blockage and allows vessel exterior cooling. Passive safety systems ensure cooling and depressurization of the RPV/CNV even with loss of external power. When decay heat and RCS temperature are sufficiently low, the CNV is filled to the pressurizer baffle plate by the containment flooding system, and passive cooldown continues via heat transfer to the reactor pool. During ECCS operation, reactor vent valves (RVVs) vent steam and noncondensible gases from the RPV and pressurizer directly into the CNV, where steam condenses and collects at the bottom, while reactor recirculation valves (RRVs) allow water to reenter the RPV and circulate through

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) employs a comprehensive containment leakage detection and monitoring strategy, primarily utilizing the Containment Evacuation System (CES), which is a nonsafety-related, module-specific system designed to reduce and maintain internal containment pressure below the saturation pressure of the lowest surface temperature inside the Containment Vessel (CNV) by removing water vapor and noncondensable gases via vacuum pumps. The CES inlet pressure instrumentation, classified as Seismic Category I, provides real-time indication and trending of leakage data in the Main Control Room (MCR), enabling detection of Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) leak rates below 1 gpm within one hour, with a minimum detectable leak rate of less than 0.05 gpm. Piping from the containment to the first seismic restraint and the CES inlet pressure instrumentation are Seismic Category I, ensuring functionality during and after a safe shutdown earthquake, while the rest of the CES is Seismic Category II or III. Loss of CES equipment or containment isolation during a seismic event results in loss of containment vacuum and an automatic NPM trip, but if CES remains available, condensate sampling and RCPB leakage measurement continue until non-seismic CES portions fail. RCS leakage into the CNV is conservatively classified as unidentified leakage, including from reactor safety valves (RSVs) and reactor vent valves (RVVs), all designed and inspected to ASME BPVC Sections III and XI. The CNV is maintained under partial vacuum during normal operation, causing RCS leakage to vaporize and disperse, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage with installed instrumentation. Leak-before-break evaluations for feedwater and steam piping determined leakage is implausible due to the absence of mechanical fittings or valves, but instrumentation adequacy was still assessed using conservative leak rate assumptions and safety factors. RPV flanges use double concentric O-rings with a leak-off port and orifice-limited connection, ensuring any break is less than the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) makeup capacity; no additional flange leak-off monitoring is provided. The leak detection system, including CES condensate monitoring, supports real-time alarms and trending, and diverse detection methods help identify leakage locations, ensuring operators can act before technical specification limits are reached. All systems and inspection frequencies are detailed in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, with Figure 5.2-3 illustrating containment leakage detection acceptability.

The reactor coolant system (RCS) is a subsystem of the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) located within the containment vessel (CNV), comprising the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) with integral pressurizer (PZR), reactor vessel internals (RVI), reactor safety valves (RSVs), RCS piping (injection, discharge, PZR spray supply, high-point degasification), PZR control cabinet, and associated instruments and cables. The RCS transports heat from the reactor core to two once-through, helical-coil steam generators (SGs) by natural circulation, with SGs positioned in the annular space between the hot leg riser and RPV inner wall. The RCS interfaces with the chemical and volume control system (CVCS), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) via reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), CNV, control rod drive system, steam generator system (SGS), and primary sampling system. The RCS piping external to the RPV includes two PZR spray line branches, an RPV high-point degasification line, an RCS injection line (with ECCS branch connections), and an RCS discharge line, all up to the outermost containment isolation valves (CIVs), forming part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) and designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Class 1. The RCS injection line allows coolant injection above the core and includes ECCS connections, while the discharge line draws suction from the annular region between the RPV wall and riser. RVVs are opened during ECCS operation to discharge PZR steam space to the CNV, removing noncondensible gases and providing overpressure protection at low temperatures; ECCS valves also enable recirculation of water from the CNV to the RPV during emergencies. The CVCS, a nonsafety-related system, maintains coolant chemistry and cleanliness, supplies pressurizer spray, injects borated or demineralized water, and removes excess coolant to the radioactive waste system, with safety-related protection against unintended RCS dilution. The CVCS is normally connected to the RCS but is isolated after containment isolation actuation, and interfaces with the RCPB via normally open CIVs. Reactor coolant inventory is managed by water injection or letdown, with chemistry controlled to minimize deposits, maintain boron concentration, and ensure proper pH using lithium hydroxide (enriched with lithium-7) and boric acid. Hydrogen is injected to suppress oxygen from radiolysis, and hydrazine is used during startup for oxygen scavenging. The RCCWS, reactor pool cooling, and spent fuel pool cooling systems are nonsafety-related, closed-loop systems transferring heat to the site cooling water system, while the circulating water and site cooling water systems are open-loop, supplying cooling to various plant systems. The RCS and its interfaces are subject to design, inspection,

Overpressure protection for each NuScale Power Module (NPM) is provided by two pilot-operated, self-actuating Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, discharging directly to the Containment Vessel (CNV) and forming part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). The RSVs, designed and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, are Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the design pressure (2310 psia), with each valve capable of 100% of the required relief capacity. The RSVs utilize RCS pressure routed to a chamber above the pilot disc, with relief pressure determined by the pilot valve spring pre-load and main valve closing spring, and feature a metal bellows, spring-operated pilot valve arrangement per NB-7511.1. Each RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, with periodic inspection and testing facilitated by bolted flange connections. The RSVs have a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, design temperature of 650°F, and a minimum design capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. Redundant RSVs ensure system reliability and provide overpressure protection for both the RCPB and steam generator (SG) tubing and plena. Overpressure protection during startup, shutdown, and low-temperature conditions is provided by three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs), also safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, with solenoid pilot actuators mounted outside the CNV and inadvertent actuation block features. RVVs prevent brittle fracture stress limits from being exceeded at low temperatures and provide sufficient relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure. Thermal relief valves, spring-operated and balanced-bellows, protect secondary system components and control rod drive system cooling piping during containment isolation but are not credited for safety-related overpressure protection during operation. All valves are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication provided in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). The system is designed to comply with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15, and ASME BPVC service limits, with the most severe anticipated operational occurrence (AOO)—a turbine trip at full power without bypass—used as the bounding event for RSV capacity and RPV overpressure analyses.

The described system employs bistable outputs that generate a logic “1” when the monitored variable crosses a setpoint, with configuration-dependent directionality. An AND gate logic requires at least 2 of 4 inputs to be true for actuation. The Main Control Room (MCR) provides indications for system trip/actuation status, operational bypass permissive status, and features a first out annunciator. Annunciators display trip/actuation status, and the system is divided into four redundant separation groups (A, B, C, D). Control interfaces include both momentary and maintained-position hand switches in the MCR and local switches for maintenance/testing. A momentary MCR hand switch can initiate functions with a preset time delay, but if the input resets before the delay ends, the output remains false. The MCS momentary manual control in the MCR allows nonsafety control of MPS-actuated equipment. Some outputs are nonlinear or unspecified functions of the input, while others are algebraic sums, as detailed in system notes.

The NuScale certified design features a centrally located Reactor Building (RXB), a rectangular, reinforced concrete, Seismic Category I structure approximately 350 ft long, 150 ft wide, and 81 ft above grade, with a foundation 86 ft below grade (92 ft under the elevator pit/refueling pool). The RXB houses the Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs) in individual three-walled bays within a common reactor pool, as well as the ultimate heat sink (a large, stainless steel-lined, reinforced concrete pool below grade comprising the reactor, refueling, and spent fuel pool areas), fuel handling and maintenance areas, a remote shutdown station, and primary systems. The RXB is designed to withstand aircraft impact, environmental conditions, design basis accidents, and threats, while providing radiation protection. The adjacent Radioactive Waste Building (RWB) processes, compacts, and stores radioactive waste and contains HVAC equipment with HEPA filtration for both RXB and RWB.

The Control Building (CRB), located about 30 ft east of the RXB, is a rectangular structure (120 ft x 80 ft at 100'-0" elevation), spanning above and below grade. The MCR is located below grade in the CRB, containing controls and indications for NPM operation, with the technical support center (TSC) above the MCR and outside the radiological controlled area. Portions of the CRB above 120'-0" elevation are nonsafety-related, Seismic Category II, constructed with steel and metal siding, while the remainder below 120'-0" is safety-related, Seismic Category I concrete. The lowest CRB elevation houses electrical equipment and CRHS air bottles. The RXB and CRB are connected by a two-level tunnel. The Annex Building (ANB), a nonsafety-related structure, controls access to the radiologically controlled area and provides health physics, tooling, security, and personnel services.

The NuScale Power Plant consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each partially immersed in a below-grade reactor pool with the water surface approximately 6 feet below grade. The reactor pool, spent fuel pool (SFP), most primary systems, and some radioactive waste equipment are all located below grade, providing additional barriers for fission product retention beyond the fuel, cladding, reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and containment. Each NPM features passive safety systems, including dedicated Emergency Core Cooling Systems (ECCS) and Decay Heat Removal Systems (DHRS), with three independent reactor vent valves and two independent reactor recirculation valves for passive decay heat removal during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA). The ECCS and DHRS are capable of removing 100% of the decay heat load for at least 72 hours without water addition. The NPM core contains 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, ~2 m rod length, <4.95% U-235 enrichment, Gd2O3 burnable absorber) and 16 control rod assemblies (CRAs) organized into regulating and shutdown banks. The fuel assemblies are supported by five spacer grids and utilize low-flow natural circulation for primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps. The torispherical RPV head supports the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs) with unique features such as a remote disconnect mechanism and extended drive shaft, validated through proof-of-concept and alignment testing. Refueling operations are independent for each NPM, with bioshields designed for removal and stacking, and the reactor core remaining in the lower RPV during refueling. The RBC (Reactor Building Crane) facilitates NPM movement, and specialized tools and dry dock areas support disassembly, inspection, and maintenance. The module control system (MCS) and plant control system enable monitoring and control from the main control room (MCR), which features dedicated panels for each NPM and integrates safety, alarm, and radiation monitoring systems. The power conversion system includes a main steam system, turbine generator set, condenser, cooling tower, and condensate/feedwater system, with all hoisting, handling, and electrical connections located above the pool water level. The plant delivers a net output of approximately 570 MWe and allows for modular construction, incremental module operation, and off-site shipment of used NPMs.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is a modular, integral pressurized water reactor (PWR) featuring a reactor core, two helical-coil steam generators, and a pressurizer all housed within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which is itself enclosed by a compact, high-pressure steel containment vessel (CNV) partially immersed in a reactor pool. The design eliminates external piping between major components and utilizes natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, removing the need for reactor coolant pumps and large-break loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) scenarios. Each NPM is rated at 160 MWt (about 50 MWe), with up to twelve modules deployable per plant for a total of 1,920 MWt (600 MWe gross, 570 MWe net). The containment system meets DSRS 6.2.1.1.A requirements, including pressure margin, heat removal, external pressure protection, instrumentation, and accident evaluation, and does not use ice condenser or suppression pool systems. Passive safety systems provide decay heat removal, emergency core cooling, and control room habitability without external power, resulting in a core damage frequency and radioactive source term significantly lower than conventional 1,000 MWe reactors. The CNV, operated at a vacuum and submerged in water, provides passive fission product control via aerosol deposition, with no active containment heat removal or fission product cleanup systems. The plant includes all necessary primary and secondary systems, cooling towers, and support equipment, and is designed for flexible siting and "island mode" operation. Extensive testing, including at the NIST-1 facility, has validated safety-related codes (NRELAP5 and PIM) for LOCA, non-LOCA, flow stability, and long-term cooling analyses. The containment vessel lacks subcompartments, high-energy piping, or internal fuel handling, and does not require features such as large vent openings. Instrumentation for pressurizer level and pressure supports safety and control systems, with certain exemptions from power supply requirements. The design and safety bases of the reactor coolant system and containment are closely integrated, with documentation addressing both single and multi-module configurations.

Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The FSAR references tables and figures detailing plant and module characteristics (Tables 1.2-1, 1.2-2), system boundaries (Figure 1.2-2), module schematics (Figure 1.2-3), multi-module layouts (Figures 1.2-4, 1.2-5), module cutaways (Figure 1.2-6), steam generator and reactor flow (Figure 1.2-7), technical reports (Table 1.6-2), and I&C diagrams (Table 1.7-1). The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, provides comprehensive technical details and figures regarding plant systems, structures, and operational parameters. The plant design emphasizes digital control systems with strict independence between safety-related protection and nonsafety-related control systems; each reactor control display is dedicated to a specific reactor, with additional stations for shared systems, and security measures control access and monitor for tampering.
