Section 5.4 of the document describes the design and configuration of Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components, with a focus on the steam generators (SGs) and their integration within the upper reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Key technical data are provided in Table 5.1-2 (primary system temperatures and flow rates), Table 5.2-1 (ASME Code Cases), Table 5.4-1 (full-load thermal-hydraulic operating conditions), Table 5.4-2 (SG design data), and Table 5.4-3 (system component materials). Figures 5.4-2 through 5.4-9 illustrate the arrangement and details of the four steam plena (STEAM 1-4) and four feedwater plena (FEED 1-4) at 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270° around the RPV, the main steam and feedwater plena, the integral steam plenum, feedwater plenum access port, SG tube supports and support tabs, SG flow restrictor assembly, and a simplified SG diagram. The SG system, integral to the RPV but not part of the RCS, includes SG tubes, tube supports, steam and feedwater piping, tube inlet flow restrictors, feed and steam plena, access ports, and covers. Each SG, located inside the RPV, consists of interlacing helical tube columns connecting to two feed and two steam plena, with the two SGs’ tube columns forming an intertwined bundle around the upper riser assembly, resulting in four feed and four steam plena spaced 90° apart. The RPV serves as the SG shell and outer pressure boundary, with the SG tubes, tube-to-tubesheet welds, and tubesheets forming part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB). On the secondary side, preheated feedwater is pumped into the SGs, flows upward through the helical tubes, and is converted to superheated steam, which is collected in the steam plena and routed via nozzles and piping to the main steam system and turbine generator. A turbine bypass line can direct up to 100% of rated main steam flow to the condenser for heat removal during load reductions or loss of electrical load. The SG design includes a 10% tube plugging margin and a fouling factor based on nuclear plant experience and water chemistry control, with configuration ensuring separation of superheated steam and subcooled liquid to minimize hydraulic instabilities. Heat from the circulating water loop, serving up to six condensers in one Turbine Generator Building, is rejected to the atmosphere via evaporative mechanical-draft cooling towers, with water circulated from a common basin through the condensers and towers. The SG system also includes main steam and feedwater supply nozzles, flow restrictors, access covers, and connections to the chemical and volume control system (CNTS) and decay heat removal system (DHRS

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) steam generator (SG) is an integral, once-through, helical tube design located within the reactor pressure vessel, featuring two collocated SGs per module. Each SG contains 1,380 seamless helical tubes arranged in 21 columns, with a total heat transfer area of 17,928 ft² and a 10% tube plugging margin. The SGs operate at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with inlet and outlet temperatures of 299.7°F and 584.4°F, respectively, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. Both primary and secondary sides are rated at 2,100 psia and 650°F, while SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia and 550°F. Tubes have an outer diameter of 0.625 inches, a wall thickness of 0.050 inches, and a minimum transition bend radius of 6.250 inches. The steam and feed tubesheets are 4.0 and 6.0 inches thick (without clad), increasing to 4.625 and 6.625 inches with clad, which is 0.250 inches (secondary) and 0.375 inches (primary) thick. The SGs utilize austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies—eight sets per column—providing full-circumferential, non-continuous support to minimize crevice formation and corrosion product buildup, and to ensure high modal frequencies for vibration resistance. The design eliminates traditional tube support plates and minimizes crevices at tube-to-tubesheet interfaces by full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore, with all tube-to-tubesheet contact in the primary coolant environment. The once-through design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet, preventing impurity accumulation and negating the need for blowdown systems; impurities are managed by chemistry control and periodic cleaning using chemical or mechanical methods. Secondary side surfaces are constructed of corrosion-resistant materials such as nickel alloy or stainless steel, allowing for safe cleaning and inspection access to both sides of the tubesheets. System monitoring is performed at a minimum 500 Hz resolution, and preservice inspections include 100% volumetric, full-length tube examination and weld inspections per BPVC requirements. The SG tube support structure is located within the primary coolant, preventing secondary system corrosion product deposition on the SG shell. Chemistry control for both primary and secondary water follows industry guidelines, with modifications for the NPM, and the secondary water quality program adheres to NEI 97-06. The fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. SG tube failure is indicated by pressure changes, and actuation valves are equipped with position indication.

The steam generator (SG) tube program encompasses comprehensive management of tube integrity, including assessment of degradation, inspection requirements, integrity evaluation, tube plugging, leakage monitoring, shell side integrity, water chemistry control, foreign material exclusion, loose parts management, contractor oversight, self-assessment, and reporting. SG tubes, made from SB-163 Alloy 690 (UNS N06690) with a nominal wall thickness of 0.050 inch (including a 0.010-inch lifetime degradation allowance), are fully accessible for nondestructive volumetric examination along their entire length due to the absence of U-bends, as specified in Item B16.10 of Table IWB-2500-1 (B-Q). Preservice examinations are performed per BPVC Section III, Paragraph NB-5280, and Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2200, after installation and hydrostatic testing but before initial power operation, establishing a baseline for future inservice inspections. Tubes with flaws exceeding 40% wall thickness, compromising integrity before the first inservice inspection, or affecting future inspectability must be plugged. The SG Program, based on NEI 97-06, Regulatory Guide 1.121, ASME Code Sections III and XI, EPRI guidance, and 10 CFR 50.55a(b)(2)(iii), ensures structural and leakage integrity, minimizes worker dose by allowing component access without excessive removal, and is integrated into the Inservice Inspection (ISI) program. Prototypic testing of tube supports validates performance, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism. The program supports tube plugging criteria and inspection frequencies consistent with the operating PWR fleet, and requires COL applicants referencing the NuScale design to implement periodic monitoring per Appendix B to 10 CFR 50. The fouling factor is applied to the tube inner surface, with the thin oxide layer on the outer surface excluded from calculations. Degradation evaluations confirm tube integrity with up to 50% wall loss under all loading conditions, with minimum wall thickness assessed for combined design basis pipe break and safe shutdown earthquake loads, ensuring compliance with ASME Code Level D Service condition stress limits. SGs and associated components are classified per Table 3.2-1, with pressure boundary materials and welds meeting BPVC Section II and III requirements. Feedwater and main steam piping are Quality Group B, Class 2, and all welding follows ASME BPVC requirements. SG supports are designed per BPVC Section III, Subsection NG, but are not Code stamped as “Core Supports,” though a 10 CFR 50 Appendix B quality program is required. The SG program monitors performance and condition to ensure continued intended function, with additional design, operational, and material details referenced in relevant sections and tables.

Feedwater is supplied to the steam generators (SGs) via external piping routed through the containment vessel (CNV) and into the lower SG plena, penetrating the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) wall. The reactor features two independent, once-through, helical-coil SGs located inside the RPV, with primary reactor coolant flowing downward around the outside of the SG tubes and secondary-side feedwater and main steam flowing inside the tubes. Feedwater enters the SG tubes at their lowest point. The SGs form part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), serving as a fission product barrier and facilitating decay heat removal via the decay heat removal system (DHRS), which operates through naturally circulating closed loops. In the event of an SG tube failure, the affected SG is isolated, and cooling continues through the intact SG to maintain symmetrical heat removal. Overpressure protection is provided by a thermal relief valve on each feedwater line and by relief and safety valves (RSVs) for DHRS internal pressure. The SG design incorporates features to minimize tube damage from loose parts wear, allows for periodic inspection and testing, and uses vertical bars and austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies for vibration and seismic support. Tube support assemblies provide non-continuous, full-circumferential support at eight locations per tube, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion, and are attached to upper and lower SG supports welded to the RPV. Inlet flow restrictors, mounted on plates in each feed plenum access port and attached to the tubesheets, ensure stable flow, prevent density wave oscillations, and are removable for maintenance. Flow-induced vibration analyses address mechanisms such as fluid elastic instability, vortex shedding, and turbulent buffeting, with the restrictors designed for high rigidity and low vibration amplitudes. Key SG component materials include SA-312 Type 304/304L, SA-182 Grade F304/F304L, SA-479 Type 304/304L, SB-166 Alloy 690, SB-637 Alloy 718, and SA-508 Grade 3 Class 2, with dual certification for stainless steel. The SGs are designed for ease of cleaning and maintenance, and the inservice inspection (ISI) program complies with ASME BPVC Section XI as required by 10 CFR 50.55a(g), while inlet flow restrictors are designed and inspected per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NC.

The evaluation of secondary side instabilities in the NuScale steam generator (SG) design demonstrates that in-phase oscillations of secondary flow do not occur, and out-of-phase oscillatory flows in individual tubes cancel out, resulting in no net oscillating secondary flow. Acceptable instabilities are limited to tube mass flow fluctuations that do not induce reactor power oscillations exceeding fuel design limits and comply with ASME Code criteria. The comprehensive vibration assessment program, in accordance with Regulatory Guide 1.20, Revision 3, covers the SGs, main steam piping up to and including the main steam isolation valve (MSIV), SG supports, and SG tube supports within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) fluid volume. Flow-induced vibration (FIV) screening criteria were developed, and the low primary coolant flow rates in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design result in low flow energy available to excite tube vibration. The analysis identified fluid elastic instability (affecting SG tubes), vortex shedding (affecting SG tubes and lower SG supports), and turbulent buffeting (affecting SG tubes, SG tube supports, lower SG supports, and SG inlet flow restrictors) as mechanisms requiring evaluation, considering both beam and lower frequency helical "breathing" vibration modes. Testing shows that the onset of fluid elastic instability in helical tube arrays is governed by the same parameters as straight tubes, with Connor's constants applied, and effective cross-flow velocities provide at least a 33% margin to instability onset. Vortex shedding is precluded for downstream tubes due to turbulence, and turbulent flow analysis yields a 20.8% fatigue usage factor and a maximum fretting wear thickness ratio of 34.2% over the 60-year design life, below the 40% allowable tube wall wear depth. The vibration analysis confirms that FIV is either not predicted or is acceptable for SG pressure retaining components, including main steam piping, MSIVs, and SG tube support assemblies. SG reliability and risk significance are evaluated per Sections 17.4 and Chapter 19, with low flow rates reducing FIV wear degradation risk and additional tube wall and FIV turbulent buffeting wear margins supporting the application of existing PWR SG Program requirements to the NuScale design. Alloy 690 tubing in the NuScale SG exhibits little-to-no corrosion from either primary or secondary-side fluids, with chemistries equivalent to existing PWR SGs, though secondary-side boiling conditions differ, leading to the assumption of uniform inner diameter thinning. The design incorporates thicker SG tube walls for greater degradation allowance and lower reactor coolant flowrates, addressing historical PWR issues such as wastage and stress corrosion cracking. The risk of foreign object introduction is comparable to existing designs, requiring no changes to SG program guidelines. Notable differences include the helical tube shape—within PWR operational experience—and a novel tube support structure retaining essential attributes of traditional plate supports. Preliminary assessments identified potential secondary-side corrosion mechanisms, such as under deposit p

NuScale conducted a comprehensive suite of tests to validate its unique fuel and steam generator (SG) designs, employing detailed programs as outlined in Sections 1.5.1.1–1.5.1.7. Fuel-specific tests included two critical heat flux (CHF) programs—one for preliminary and one for final fuel designs—using vertical 5x5 electrically-heated simulated fuel bundles with AREVA HTP™/HMP™ grid technology, covering a wide range of hydraulic conditions, axial power shapes, and configurations (including guide tube substitutions). Instrumentation such as thermocouples, resistance temperature detectors (RTDs), pressure transducers, mass flow rate instruments, and strain gauges measured heater rod temperatures, fluid flow, pressure, and vibration on both primary and secondary SG sides. CHF was detected by excursions in fuel simulator thermocouples as bundle power was incrementally increased at specified flow, temperature, and pressure conditions, with all test execution and acceptance following industry standards. Large-scale SG tests, required due to the helical design’s departure from conventional nuclear fleet SGs, were performed at the SIET facility in Italy, using both electrically-heated isolated tubes and a 252-tube helical bundle fluid-heated on the exterior to model primary-side conditions. These tests characterized secondary-side thermal-hydraulic performance, informed SG inlet flow restrictor sizing, and defined steam outlet conditions. Dynamic pressure measurements supported evaluation of two-phase boiling-induced pressure fluctuations and flow-induced vibration risks. The SG test program was inspected by the NRC under IP 35034, 35017, and 36100, and analysis in Appendix A of TR-0516-49417 confirmed that secondary-side flow oscillations are decoupled from the primary side, preventing reactor power oscillations that could exceed fuel design limits. All heater bundles were verified to meet vendor acceptance criteria, with power ratings within 2% of nominal. Facility design ensured accessibility for inspection and maintenance to minimize technician dose and refueling time.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, details the design’s conformance with a wide range of regulatory criteria. For RG 1.45, NuScale employs both containment pressure monitoring and leakage collection systems for reactor coolant system leakage detection. Reactor vessel closure stud inspection is assigned to the COL applicant, with bolting material referenced in Table 5.2-4. Austenitic stainless steel compatibility with thermal insulation is not applicable, and system pressure tests fully conform to DSRS 5.2.4. The design departs from SRP 5.4.12 for high point vents, citing the integral system’s prevention of non-condensable gas interference. Low-temperature overpressure protection partially conforms to BTP 5-2, meeting ASME Section XI Appendix G. For containment isolation system radiation monitors, the design departs from DSRS 6.2.4, as the containment evacuation system is isolated upon high pressure or low-low pressurizer level. DSRS 5.2.5 leakage detection fully conforms to GDC 2, but DSRS 6.2.1.3 departs from GDC 50 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix K by excluding metal-water reaction energy. Spent Fuel Pool Cooling partially conforms to GDC 2 due to non-seismic Category I makeup water supply, though ITAAC are met. SRP 9.2.1 is not applicable as the site cooling water system is not safety-related. DSRS 11.1 partially conforms to RG 1.140, but fully for neutron activation products. Cold shutdown using a nonsafety-related containment flood and drain system conforms with SECY guidance. The new and spent fuel storage facility partially conforms to Regulatory Position C.8 for makeup water, fully meets SRP 9.1.1 (GDC 62), DSRS 9.1.2 (GDCs 2, 4, 5, 61, 63), and 10 CFR 20.1101(b), with no ESF ventilation required and no exceptions except for makeup water. Full conformance is achieved for SRP 3.6.2, SRP 3.6.3, DSRS 9.3.4, DSRS 11.1, and DSRS 15.6.5, while BTP 6-1 and DSRS 15.0 are only partially met due to SSCs not present and BWR-specific exceptions. 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(x) is partially met, excluding PORV block valve testing and ATWS. The report also confirms full conformance with DSRS 4.4, BTP 5-4, DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, DSRS 6.2.4, DSRS 9.3.6, DSRS 11.2, and 50.34(f

The criticality analysis adheres to DSS-ISG-2010-01 by using a single fuel assembly design without credit for burnup, zoning, loading patterns, or lumped fission products; depletion analysis, axial burnup profile, and interface considerations all explicitly state that burnup credit is not applied. The rack model is appropriate for the analyzed conditions, accounting for an additional adjacent assembly under normal conditions. Code validation includes area of applicability, trend analysis, bias, and uncertainty, with code-to-code comparisons performed and no reliance on precedents; all references are publicly available and cited in SFP criticality analyses, and assumptions are conservative or justified. The reactor design conforms to DSRS 4.4, Rev 0 and DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0, meeting requirements for subchannel hydraulic analysis, core oscillations, thermal-hydraulic instabilities, CHF calculations, process monitoring, and core cooling instrumentation, with no exceptions except for the loose parts monitoring system, which is not required due to low primary system flow. Core stability during ATWS is not applicable due to diverse RTS signals that prevent ATWS and flow instabilities; DSRS 15.9.A II.1 has no requirements, II.2 is met via GDC 12, and II.3 and trip-before-CHF criteria are not applicable as reactor trip signals act before instabilities or CHF violations. For II.5, partial conformance is achieved as existing trip signals provide a more limiting exclusion zone than required for flow instability and SAFDL protection. The review of DSRS 15.1.6, Rev 0, "Loss of Containment Vacuum," confirms full conformance with all criteria and analytical parameters: reactor coolant pressure and cladding integrity are maintained, AOOs do not worsen conditions, instrument spans and setpoints follow RG 1.105, the limiting single failure is identified, a conservative scram is used, core burnup is considered, RCS-to-containment/pool heat transfer is maximized, and setpoint inaccuracies are addressed per RG 1.105, with no exceptions or comments. Containment heat removal capability and LOCA design margin (DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0, II.3) meet requirements with no exceptions; mass and energy release analysis for postulated LOCAs (DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, II.3) and its ITAAC are not applicable to this design. Containment heat removal systems (DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, II.5 and II.6) comply with 10 CFR 50.46(b)(5) for long-term cooling and water level margin in the presence of LOCA-generated and latent debris. The LOCA acceptance criteria use a temperature limit more restrictive than 2,200°F, conforming to DSRS 15.0, Rev 0, I.

The review of NuScale’s transient and accident analyses, as documented in DSRS 15.0, Rev 0, SRP 15.0.2 (March 2007), and related sections, indicates general conformance with regulatory guidance for categorization, classification, and analysis of anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs) and postulated accidents. Initiating events (IEs) are appropriately classified, with some deterministically treated as AOOs, and causes and frequency classifications are identified per guidance. Analysis acceptance criteria are largely met, though critical heat flux (CHF) is used instead of departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR) for fuel cladding thermal margin, and guidance for items 4.B.ii and 4.B.iv is only partially satisfied. Sequence of events and system operation criteria are partially met, with Item B.vi relevant only to COL applicants. Uncertainty analyses for non-LOCA events use sensitivity or bounding analyses, and quality assurance requirements are fulfilled. For DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0, covering events such as feedwater and steam flow transients and inadvertent system operations, NuScale’s analyses maintain the Minimum Critical Heat Flux Ratio (MCHFR) above the 95/95 limit, use conservative assumptions (e.g., initial power at 102%, scram characteristics, core burnup), and ensure instrument setpoints conform to RG 1.105. Event classification and analytical parameters align with SRP requirements, and AOOs are prevented from escalating without independent faults. For DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0, addressing turbine and steam system transients, all analytical model parameters—initial power, scram, burnup, and instrumentation—meet requirements with no exceptions. DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2, Rev 0, on Chemical and Volume Control System malfunctions, confirms proper AOO classification, adequate event sequence description, justified evaluation models, and appropriate input parameters for scram and burnup, with no deficiencies noted.

The reviewed DSRS sections confirm that the NuScale design meets regulatory criteria and assumptions for a range of accident scenarios, including steam system piping failures (DSRS 15.1.5), loss of normal feedwater flow (DSRS 15.2.7), feedwater system pipe breaks (DSRS 15.2.8), and loss-of-coolant accidents (DSRS 15.6.5), with no exceptions unless specifically noted. For steam system piping failures, NuScale satisfies requirements for reactor coolant and main steam system pressures, core damage potential (using critical heat flux as a descriptor), safety-related system classification, decay heat removal system auto-initiation, initial power level, plant operating mode, and core burnup. Radiological criteria for steam line breaks are met, with assumptions including loss of offsite power and worst-case single active component failure. Operator action is not required for steam line break mitigation. For loss of normal feedwater flow and feedwater system pipe breaks, NuScale conforms to criteria for system pressures, core damage evaluation (using heat generation rate limits), site boundary doses, and initial condition assumptions. The design meets TMI Action Plan requirements for loss-of-coolant accidents. Evaluation against DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5 shows full conformance for most criteria, except partial conformance for steam pressure regulator events, as NuScale lacks this component. Analyzed AOOs include loss of load, turbine trip, loss of condenser vacuum, MSIV closure, steam pressure regulator failure, loss of AC power, loss of feedwater, and inadvertent DHRS operation, with detailed analyses in DCD Chapter 15. The Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance for steam generator tube repair (DSRS 5.4.2.2), water hammer (DSRS 6.3), and system pressure and setpoint accuracy (DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4), with partial conformance for fluid instabilities in the condensate and feedwater system (DSRS 10.4.7, GDC 4). Conformance is also confirmed for maximum-worth rod assumptions (DSRS 15.1.5), initial power level (DSRS 15.1.6), analytical models (DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5), and system pressures (DSRS 15.2.6). No exceptions or additional comments are noted except for the partial conformance regarding fluid instabilities.

For NuScale reactor events involving uncontrolled control rod assembly withdrawal from subcritical or low power startup conditions (SRP 15.4.1), at power (SRP 15.4.2), and control rod misoperation (SRP 15.4.3), critical heat flux (CHF) is used instead of departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR) to assess thermal margin limits. NuScale applies a heat generation rate limit to ensure fuel centerline melting limits are not exceeded during slower reactivity insertions. The uniform cladding strain criterion, specified in the SRP for BWRs, does not apply to NuScale; instead, the 95/95 minimum critical heat flux ratio (MCHFR) approach is used to prevent cladding or fuel failures. All relevant criteria for thermal margin limits and fuel centerline temperatures are met, with no additional requirements for uniform cladding strain under control rod misoperation scenarios. For Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs), guidance is provided in DSRS 15.4.6, which addresses inadvertent decreases in boron concentration in the Reactor Coolant System for PWRs. Review of SRP 15.4.6, Rev 2, confirms conformance with criteria for Reactor Coolant and Main Steam System Pressures (II.1), Fuel Cladding Integrity (II.2)—with CHF as the appropriate term for NuScale—Incidents of Moderate Frequency (II.3), and Analysis Model, Methods, and Assumptions (II.5). The criterion for Minimum Time Intervals for Required Operator Actions (II.4) is not applicable, as operator action is not required to mitigate inadvertent boron dilution events.

The inservice inspection program for Class 2 and 3 components includes pre-service and periodic inservice inspections and tests to ensure safety-related and risk-significant systems, structures, and components can perform their intended functions, with detailed requirements outlined in Section 6.6 (referencing DSRS 6.6, Rev 0) and implementation milestones assigned to the COL applicant. The Inservice Test Program, described in Section 3.9.6, complies with 10 CFR 50.55a(f)(3)(iii)(B) and (iv)(B), while operational inservice testing and inspection programs (Sections 5.2.4 and 5.4.1.6) establish post-startup requirements, including the Steam Generator program. Review of DSRS 5.2.4, Rev 0, confirms conformance for system boundary, accessibility, and augmented ISI, with partial conformance for other inspection programs due to the absence of a boric acid control program, and notes that operational and risk-informed ISI programs are COL applicant responsibilities. ITAAC partially conforms, with some criteria applicable only to COL applicants. The applicant’s program for Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing (DSRS 5.2.4, Rev 0) meets requirements for examination categories, intervals, evaluation, code exemptions, relief requests, and code cases, with no exceptions; leakage detection (DSRS 5.2.5, Rev 0) meets GDC 14 and 30, and reactor vessel integrity (DSRS 5.3.3, Rev 0) conforms for operating conditions and inservice surveillance. Direct indication of relief and safety valve position in the control room is provided per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi), and ATWS conditions are not required for the valve test program. The Residual Heat Removal System design (DSRS BTP 5-4, Rev 0) conforms for pressure relief and implementation, with operational procedures partially conforming as they are site-specific and COL applicant responsibilities; materials and fabrication for Engineered Safety Features (SRP 6.1.1, Rev 2) also conform. DSRS 6.2.1.4, Rev 0, Mass and Energy Release Analysis for Postulated Secondary System Pipe Ruptures, conforms for energy sources, mass and energy release rate, and single-failure analyses, with no exceptions. Compliance with DSRS 5.3.2, Rev 0, is confirmed for pressure-temperature limits, upper-shelf energy, and pressurized thermal shock, as well as the regulatory basis and reactor vessel integrity design (DSRS 5.3.3, Rev 0), with no exceptions or additional comments.

The passages collectively confirm that the steam generator materials and design fully conform to DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements, including steam generator design (II.2), fabrication and processing of ferritic materials (II.3), material compatibility and cleanliness control (II.5), and secondary side access provisions (II.6), with no exceptions or deviations. The steam generator program also conforms to DSRS 5.4.2.2, Rev 0 regarding tube susceptibility to degradation (II.1), while other elements such as Monitoring Program Elements (II.2), ITAAC (II.9), Technical Specifications (II.3), and Periodic Tube Inspection and Testing (II.7, II.8) only partially conform due to applicability to COL applicants referencing a certified design. Full conformance is noted for Tube Repair Criteria (II.4) and Preservice Inspection (II.6). Additionally, the materials and fabrication processes for ASME Code Class 1, 2, and 3 threaded fasteners, reactor internals, core support structures, reactor coolant pressure boundary, and engineered safety features conform to SRP 3.13, 4.5.2, 5.2.3, and 6.1.1, with no exceptions. DSRS 5.3.1 and 5.3.3 reviews confirm conformance for reactor vessel materials and integrity, including material selection, fabrication, NDE, fracture toughness, surveillance, fasteners, and installation. Fabrication and processing of austenitic stainless steel for steam generators also meet DSRS 5.4.2.1 requirements, with no deviations identified.

The NRC developed a NuScale Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS) to supplement NUREG-0800, the Standard Review Plan (SRP), for reviewing the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR). Section 1.9 of FSAR Revision 5 provides a tabular assessment of NuScale’s compliance with regulatory criteria effective six months before the anticipated docket date. Table 1.9-3 documents NuScale’s conformance with applicable SRP and DSRS sections, listing for each section the title, acceptance criteria (AC), AC description, conformance status, and comments. Several sections are marked "Not Applicable," such as those related to spent fuel reprocessing, special nuclear material use or transport, and composite barrier design, reflecting features not present in the NuScale design (e.g., clean air is provided by compressed air tanks). For example, SRP 1.0 (Introduction and Interfaces) has no specific AC, SRP 3.5.3 (Barrier Design Procedures) is not applicable, DSRS 6.6 (Inservice Inspection and Testing of Class 2 and 3 Components) and DSRS 7.1.1 (Fundamental Design Principles) both "Conform" with no exceptions, DSRS 7.2.1 (Quality) "Partially Conforms" to RG 1.172 with details in Table 1.9-2.7.2.1, DSRS 7.2.14 (Human Factors Considerations) "Conforms" despite no specific AC, and DSRS 9.5.2 (Communication Systems) "Conforms" with GDC 2. Improved standard technical specification guidance from DSRS NUREGs-1430 through -1434 and NUREG-2194 was used as appropriate. In accordance with 10 CFR 52.47(a)(21), Table 1.9-5 addresses technically relevant unresolved safety issues and medium- to high-priority generic safety issues (GSI) from NUREG-0933, referencing NRC SECY-07-0110 and Appendix B of NUREG-0933. Only unresolved or active GSIs are included; resolved or non-applicable issues are excluded. Each table entry includes conformance status, exceptions, and references to relevant FSAR sections.

The NuScale Instrumentation and Control (I&C) design’s Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) assessment adheres to NUREG/CR-6303 guidelines as outlined in DSRS 7.1.5 and Table 1.9-3, with D3 analyses of reactor protection systems meeting regulatory expectations. Staff positions and guidance from Digital I&C-ISG-02 (including BTP 7-19 Position 4, common cause failure, echelons of defense, and single failure) and Digital I&C-ISG-03 (risk-informed digital I&C) are deemed not applicable to the NuScale Design Certification Application (DCA). Section 7.1.5 of DSRS Rev 0 confirms conformance with SECY-93-087, GL 85-06, 10 CFR 50.62, and the quality assurance program in Section 17.5, as well as RG 1.53 and RG 1.62 (referenced in Table 1.9-2). The Module Protection System (MPS) employs internal diversity, satisfying the diverse scram requirements of the ATWS Rule and acceptance criteria II.3.A.ii and II.3.C(2) for evolutionary plants. Digital I&C-ISG-02 and ISG-04 (Highly Integrated Control Rooms and Digital Communication Systems) are not applicable to the NuScale DCA; however, interdivisional communications are addressed in NuScale’s topical report TR-1015-18653-P-A, with conformance to Interim Staff Guidance documented in Table 1.9-4.

The provided passages detail NuScale’s conformance with various NRC regulatory requirements and guidance documents. Enhanced firefighting capabilities address Section 4.1.3 of TR-0816-50796, but several SRP 9.5.1.1 acceptance criteria—such as those for Fire Protection Probabilistic Risk Assessment, License Renewal, Decommissioning, and Operational Program Milestones—are not applicable, as they pertain to COL applicants or decommissioning reactors. NuScale partially conforms to NRC fire protection guidelines (II.3, II.6), favoring passive separation of redundant trains for safe-shutdown. For initial test programs and remote shutdown capability, NuScale partially conforms to guidance (sections 3.13, 5.3, 1.68, 14.2, 1.68.2), with site-specific implementation assigned to the COL applicant. Full conformance is achieved for Class 1 linear-type supports (sections 3.7, 3.12, 1.124), while guidance on hydraulic and water-control structures (sections 3.9, 1.125, 4.2, 1.127) is not applicable due to design features. Regulatory Guide (RG) conformance is summarized in Table 1.9-2: RG 1.68.1 and RG 1.79 are partially conformed to, RG 1.70 is not applicable, RG 1.190 is partially conformed to with exceptions in TR-0116-20781, and RG 1.191 is not applicable. The Quality Assurance Program Description (QAPD) is based on NQA-1-2008 and NQA-1a-2009, referencing IEEE Std.336-1985, and neutron flux/fluence calculations follow RG 1.190 with noted exceptions. Compliance with TMI requirements (10 CFR 50.34(f)) and operating experience is documented in tables (1.9-4, 1.9-5, 1.9-6), with exceptions for non-relevant paragraphs and site-specific issues. Departures from General Design Criteria and TMI action items are justified as “Not Applicable” or “Partially Conforms” when requirements do not pertain to NuScale’s design. The FSAR, Revision 5, documents partial or non-conformance for metal containment components, welder qualification, and safety-related concrete structures, with certain requirements delegated to the COL applicant or deemed not applicable (e.g., ice condenser systems, elevated-temperature reactors). Categorization of SSCs by safety significance partially conforms, leveraging 10 CFR 50.69 for risk-informed processes. Reactor vessel integrity and some fission product control requirements are COL applicant responsibilities or not applicable. Probabilistic risk assessment requirements are partially met, with design certification addressing core and containment heat removal reliability and site-specific updates required from the COL applicant.

The NuScale nuclear power plant (NPP) design addresses internal flooding mitigation by identifying and ensuring reliable protection for safety-related structures, systems, and components (SSCs), with the facility sited above the probable maximum flood height, including wind-induced wave run-up, thus rendering certain flood protection requirements not applicable. The design conforms or partially conforms to NRC guidance (RG 1.102, SRP 3.4.2, 1.105), consistently assuming the NPP is above the most severe flood and groundwater levels. Seismic design conforms to RG 1.61, with an alternative damping value for the NPM substructure, and includes a metal-lined concrete dike around the PSCS storage tank. Operator guidance partially conforms, as site-specific procedures are the responsibility of the COL applicant. The electrical system meets SECY-94-084, Section G, and the design conforms to positions on generic bounding seismic hazard curves, seismic PRA, leak-before-break, and includes Severe Accident Mitigation Design Alternatives (SAMDAs). For turbine missile hazards, the design verifies parameters such as turbine missile spectrum, rotor design, and building wall integrity, but since there are no safety-related SSCs in the Turbine Building (SRP 10.2, DSRS 10.2.3, DSRS 3.5.1.3), reliance on missile generation probabilities or rotor integrity is not required if barriers per RG 1.115 are used. The plant employs protective barriers, using finite element analysis for concrete barriers and conforming to SRP 3.5.3 for steel barriers, ensuring physical separation of redundant safety-related equipment and not relying on turbine overspeed protection systems, in accordance with GDC 4. For tornado and hurricane hazards, the design uses bounding Region 1 characteristics per RG 1.76 and RG 1.221, with a maximum tornado wind speed of 230 mph, and conforms to SRP 3.3.2, 3.5.1.4, and DC/COL-ISG-24 guidance, while site-specific confirmation is delegated to the COL applicant. Proximity and aircraft missiles are assumed not applicable except for aircraft, with compliance to 10 CFR 100 and GDC 4 not required for SRP 3.5.1.5 and 3.5.1.6, but site parameters are included as Tier 1 information. Emergency planning provides safety parameter displays in the technical support center, with the emergency operations facility assigned to the COL applicant. Overall, the NuScale design conforms or partially conforms to relevant regulatory criteria for external flooding, seismic, tornado, hurricane, and turbine missile protection, relying on physical barriers and site assumptions to ensure safety-related SSCs are protected.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) defines the Certified Design Interface (CDI) using double brackets ([[]]), with security-related information marked by double braces {{ }} and withheld per 10 CFR 2.390(d)(1). Figures 1.2-1 through 1.2-3 illustrate the facility layout, CDI boundaries, and power generation systems, specifying that only clouded portions—such as CDI cooling towers and certain circulating water systems—within CDI buildings are included in the CDI. Table 1.8-1 details interfaces between the NuScale certified design and site-specific (COL) plant elements, listing CDI systems, structures, and components like turbine generator buildings, annex building, cooling towers and associated systems (e.g., cooling tower basin, circulating water pumps, cooling tower fans, chemical treatment building), security buildings, central utility building, diesel generator buildings, offsite power transmission system, main switchyard, transformer area, auxiliary AC power system, site cooling water system, circulating water system, grounding and lightning protection system, and plant exhaust stack, while potable and sanitary water systems, resin tanks, site drainage, raw water, site parameters, communications, turbine generators, and the Operational Support Center are site-specific. The FSAR references relevant sections for each interface. Operational experience conformance is assessed in Table 1.9-6, with all 10 CFR 21 notifications reviewed for impact on the NuScale design before supplier approval and during annual reviews; to date, no notifications have affected safety-related work by NuScale-approved suppliers. The NRC may make unlimited copies of NuScale reports for regulatory purposes, subject to 10 CFR 2.390 for proprietary information, with all copies bearing copyright and proprietary notices as applicable; proprietary or safeguards information is provided separately, with referenced reports listed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2. The design documentation includes design bases, operational limits, safety analyses, and all information required by 10 CFR 52.47(a), considers NRC DSRS and Interim Staff Guidance, and addresses 10 CFR 50.54(o), Appendix J test frequencies, and Appendix K requirements for small break LOCAs; compliance with RGs and GDC 55 and 57 is addressed, with some responsibilities assigned to the COL applicant. The design certification process used 21 main reports, Supplements 1-34, and NRC letter SECY-07-0110 to identify applicable generic issues, with Table 1.9-5 summarizing TMI requirements and NRC positions, and Table 1.9-6 evaluating operational experience. The design partially conforms to RG 1.189 (Fire Protection), with non-PWR and site-specific elements assigned to the COL applicant, and partially conforms to entry/exit control and technical/operational support center requirements, with site-specific updates required. NuScale identifies certain CFR regulations as literally applicable but seeks departures via exemption requests (e.g., from

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) details the verification and confirmation testing program for unique design features supporting the plant’s safety analysis (Section 1.5), and incorporates numerous topical and technical reports by reference (Section 1.6), as listed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2. These reports cover areas such as quality assurance (NP-TR-1010-859-NP-A, Rev. 5), risk significance, safety classification of passive electrical systems, protection system platforms, accident source term methodology, fuel methodology, nuclear analysis codes, stability analysis, LOCA evaluation, subchannel analysis, non-LOCA analysis, critical heat flux, rod ejection, and fuel assembly structural response, as well as technical topics like fluence calculation, advanced sensors, physical security, containment response, instrument setpoints, combustible gas control, vibration assessment, fuel storage, loss of large areas, and mitigation strategies for loss of all AC power. Section 1.9.5 and related tables (1.9-7, 1.9-8) provide a comprehensive conformance assessment with NRC regulatory criteria for advanced and evolutionary light-water reactor (ALWR) designs, referencing SECY-93-087, SECY-94-084, and other SECY documents, and noting partial or full conformance, exceptions, and applicability to passive plant designs. The FSAR follows the format of Regulatory Guide 1.206, with additional chapters on beyond-design-basis events (Ch. 20) and multi-module design (Ch. 21), and bases its technical content on the Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) and Standard Review Plan (SRP), with conformance evaluations in Section 1.9.1 and Table 1.9-2. Sensitive information is withheld per 10 CFR 2.390 and RIS 2005-26, with public and non-public versions clearly marked. The NuScale design adheres to passive plant guidance from SECY-93-087 and SECY-94-084, with specific SECY documents (e.g., SECY-90-016, SECY-91-078, SECY-96-128, SECY-14-038) assessed for applicability and conformance, particularly regarding safe shutdown, station blackout, and fire protection. Referenced SECY documents addressing prototype decisions, regulatory treatment of non-safety systems, severe accident mitigation, and technical issue resolution have been incorporated into 10 CFR 52, NRC guidance, or are subject to rulemaking, with NuScale’s design conforming to these regulatory frameworks.

The provided passages detail the NuScale nuclear power plant’s conformance with seismic design and analysis requirements as specified in various regulatory standards and guidance documents. Site-specific ratios of peak ground acceleration (A), velocity (V), and displacement (D)—specifically V/A and AD/V²—are confirmed to be consistent with the controlling seismic events for the site-specific uniform hazard response spectra. Seismic design parameters, including ground motion and damping values, fully conform to DSRS 3.7.1, 3.7.2, and 3.7.3, Rev. 0, with no exceptions. The seismic system and subsystem analyses address analytical modeling, soil-structure interaction, modal response combination, interaction of non-seismic and seismic Category I SSCs, overturning moments, sliding forces, and frictional forces, all in compliance with DSRS 3.7.2 and 3.7.3. The Certified Seismic Design Response Spectra (CSDRS), though not developed using RG 1.60, envelopes the RG 1.60 spectrum anchored to 0.1g and serves as the Safe-Shutdown Earthquake (SSE); the Operating-Basis Earthquake (OBE) is set at one-third of the SSE, decoupling OBE from safety system design evaluation. Seismic design classification generally follows RG 1.29 and SRP 3.2.1, with a noted deviation: SSCs under Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i are classified as Seismic Category II instead of I. Structural analysis for Seismic Category I structures, including foundations, conforms to DSRS 3.8.4 and 3.8.5, Rev. 0, with no exceptions; masonry walls and concrete dams are not used in the design. Requirements for winter precipitation loads on roofs are met per DC/COL-ISG-7. Seismic instrumentation partially conforms to RG 1.12 and SRP 3.7.4, as detectors cannot be installed inside containment and are instead located in the Reactor Building, with full compliance deferred to the COL applicant. Regulatory Guide 1.93 and draft JLD-ISG-12-04 are not applicable. Overall, the NuScale design demonstrates comprehensive compliance with seismic regulatory requirements, with minor exceptions and COL items noted.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, details the design’s validation, regulatory conformance, and tailored application of industry standards for safety-related systems. NuScale partially conforms to several regulatory criteria, including accident monitoring instrumentation (IEEE Std 497-2002), use of computers in safety systems (RG 1.152), and software lifecycle processes (IEEE 1074-2006), adapting requirements to its I&C development lifecycle, which differs from the traditional waterfall model. Applicable tasks from IEEE standards such as 1012-2004, 1028-2008, 828-2005, 829-2008, 1008-1987, 830-1998, and 1074-2006 are mapped to NuScale’s processes, with exceptions to certain administrative requirements conflicting with established documentation practices. The design fully conforms to aircraft threat response strategies and environmental qualification for safety-related computer-based I&C systems in mild environments (RG 1.209), and addresses environmental effects on important-to-safety equipment. For electromagnetic and radio-frequency interference (RG 1.180) and environmental qualification (DSRS 3.11, RG 1.89), NuScale partially conforms, with some requirements designated for COL applicants. The Instrument Air Supply (IAS) system meets ANS/ISA S7.3-R1981 and Generic Letter 88-14, while setpoint methodology conforms to ISA-67.04.01-2006, addressing NRC RIS 2006-17. Safety classification of SSCs integrates deterministic and probabilistic methods, combining traditional definitions (10 CFR 50.2, RG 1.26, SRP 3.2.2) with an alternative risk-informed framework (10 CFR 50.69, RG 1.201, NEI 00-04), consistent with SECY-03-0047 and SECY-10-0034. The potable and sanitary water systems are designed to prevent flooding or adverse impacts on essential SSCs. NuScale’s electrical system partially conforms to NRC Generic Letter 88-15 and addresses SECY-91-078 by providing alternate power sources and direct offsite circuit connections to redundant safety divisions. The Fire Protection System contains no RTNSS equipment, and RTNSS guidance is partially incorporated into current regulations. Qualification of safety-related motor control centers and development of a risk-informed, performance-based inservice testing program (SRP 3.9.7) are not applicable, as these are either not used or are the responsibility of COL applicants.

The documentation section for the nuclear plant includes detailed information on plant location (1.1.1), power output (1.1.4), project schedule (1.1.5), and the report's format and content (1.1.6). The general plant description (1.2) addresses principal site characteristics (1.2.1), the general arrangement of major structures and equipment (1.2.2), and plant features of special interest (1.2.3). Additional sections provide comparisons with other facilities (1.3), identification of agents and contractors (1.4), division of responsibility (1.4.2), and principal consultants and participants (1.4.3), with Section 1.4.1 marked as "Not Used." Section 1.7 references drawings and detailed information. The documentation includes a series of figures depicting elevation and section views of key buildings: the Reactor Building at elevations of 24'-0", 35'-8", 50'-0", 62'-0", 75'-0", 86'-0", 100'-0", 126'-0", and 145'-6", with east, west, and south section views (Figures 1.2-10 through 1.2-20); the Control Building at elevations of 50'-0", 63'-3", 76'-6", 100'-0", and 120'-0", with North and West Section Views (Figures 1.2-21 through 1.2-27); and the Radioactive Waste Building at elevations of 71'-0", 82'-0", 100'-0", and 120'-0" (Figures 1.2-28 through 1.2-31). Each figure provides detailed visual documentation of the respective building's structural layout and configuration at the specified heights.

Section 1.7 of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, details the conventions, legends, and symbol definitions for Electrical and Instrumentation and Control (I&C) Drawings, including Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs), with system drawings summarized in Table 1.7-2. Electrical symbols are defined in Figures 1.7-1a and 1.7-1b, covering components such as 3-winding and 2-winding power transformers (with high/low side windings, MVA/kVA ratings, cooling methods, impedance), current and potential transformers (quantity, ratio, accuracy class), high voltage (>100 kV), medium voltage (1kV–100kV), and low voltage (<1kV) circuit breakers (ampere rating, N.O./N.C. status), inverters (DC input/AC output), multicell batteries (voltage, amp-hour, cell count), fuses (ampere rating, quantity), safety disconnect switches, transfer switches (ATS, STATIC), cable bus, surge capacitors, connection stabs, electrical heaters (kW rating), synchronous generators (frequency, RPM, % power factor), lightning arresters (voltage rating, quantity), relays/devices (NP-ES-0303-2923), various transformer types (delta, wye, delta-wye with resistance ground, double secondary, ratio, quantity), chargers (AC input/DC output), fused safety switches, VFDs, selector switches, monitoring devices (BM = battery monitor), local controllers, phasor indicators, meters (ammeter, frequency, synchroscope, voltmeter, VAR, watt, temperature, digital multimeter), power bus, rotary switches, and disconnect switches with interlocked grounding. Terminal numbering conventions are specified for starter, field terminal box, local control panel, DCS/PLC panel, MCC, and switchgear compartments. Abbreviations such as DCS (Distributed Control System), GTO (Gate Turn Off), NO (Normally Open), NC (Normally Closed), PLC (Programmable Logic Controller), and SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier) are defined. Figure 1.7-2 provides logic gate symbols (OR, AND, NOT) with output conditions. P&ID legends in Figures 1.7-3a through 1.7-3f specify that all vents and drains are 0.75" normally closed valves unless noted, and all inline valves are normally open (except butterfly valves), with symbols for fail-open, fail-locked-as-is, drift-open, control valve fail positions, and fail-closed. The I&C architectural design emphasizes clear interconnection interfaces, separation of safety and nonsafety systems, and functional simplification, with primary I&C systems described in Section 7.0. All tables and figures are sequentially numbered within each section and placed at the end of the relevant "X

The passages from the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, present a comprehensive table of acronyms and abbreviations used throughout the document, each with its corresponding definition. These cover a wide range of technical systems and components, such as the auxiliary AC power source (AAPS), emergency core cooling system (ECCS), decay heat removal system (DHRS), reactor protection system (RPS), containment vessel (CNV), reactor core (RXC), steam generator (SG), and spent fuel pool (SFP). Safety and accident-related terms include anticipated operational occurrence (AOO), anticipated transient without scram (ATWS), beyond design basis event (BDBE), station blackout (SBO), and small-break loss-of-coolant accident (SBLOCA). The list also includes regulatory and industry standards like the Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), General Design Criteria (GDC), and organizations such as the NRC, DOE, and INL. Instrumentation and monitoring systems (area radiation monitor (ARM), meteorological and environmental monitoring system (MEMS)), waste management systems (liquid, solid, and radioactive waste management systems: LWMS, SWMS, RWMS), and building designations (Annex Building (ANB), Radioactive Waste Building (RWB), Turbine Generator Building (TGB)) are also detailed. Analytical and operational terms such as core damage frequency (CDF), loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA), probabilistic risk assessment (PRA), and technical concepts like ALARA (as low as reasonably achievable), minimum critical heat flux ratio (MCHFR), and ultimate heat sink (UHS) are included. The table further covers testing and qualification procedures (inservice inspection (ISI), integrated leak rate testing (ILRT)), control systems, seismic considerations, and various plant subsystems (chemical and volume control system (CVCS), feedwater system (FWS), reactor coolant system (RCS)). This extensive list ensures clarity and consistency in terminology for all technical, operational, and regulatory aspects of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) and NuScale Power Plant (NPP) design and operation.

The passages comprehensively outline conventions and specifications for instrumentation and equipment identification in nuclear engineering documentation, particularly as applied to Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs) per the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. Instrument connections use structured codes (e.g., NP12-YY-DYY-E-SL-YYY-SYY) referencing equipment, drawing identifiers, and connection types, with tagging based on ANSI/ISA 5.1-2009 function identifiers and unique component numbers. Instrumentation tags specify component type, function, location (field/panel-mounted, operator-accessible), and accessibility, with temperature elements defaulting to thermocouples unless otherwise noted. Communication links are defined for fieldbus, intelligent/smart devices, and mechanical connections, while line symbols distinguish process/equipment connections, impulse lines, analyzer sample lines, and traced lines (ET for electrical, ST for steam, CW for chilled water). Equipment specifications require detailed parameters: capacity (gallons, tons), duty cycle (%), motor HP, flow rates (CFM, GPM), entering/leaving water temperatures (°F), power (kW), and heating/cooling capacities (BTU/hr, kW). Numbering formats for lines, components, and drawings are standardized, incorporating system abbreviations, module numbers, unique identifiers, and discipline codes, with consistent use of function identifier abbreviations. Pressure values (psig) are tabulated, ranging from 40 to 3459 psig for various process points, and equipment specification recommendations are provided, though final requirements are at the designer’s discretion. The P&ID legends detail system vent/drain labeling, structured letter codes for instrumentation (e.g., F for flow, P for pressure, T for temperature), and modifiers for function, readout, and output, covering controllers, indicators, switches, alarms, transmitters, solenoids, relays, computing devices, test points, safety devices, and final elements. Additional identifiers specify sensor types (e.g., FR for flame rod, UV for ultraviolet, n for neutron radiation, pH, DO, TDS), flow measurement devices (VENT, TUR, DP), and temperature sensors (TC, RTD, THRM). The legend categorizes a wide range of components—valves (gate, globe, ball, butterfly, etc.), actuators (manual, pneumatic, hydraulic, solenoid, etc.), specialty items (arrestors, strainers, filters, sample coolers), tanks, vessels, pumps (centrifugal, positive displacement, etc.), compressors, blowers, heat exchangers, HVAC components, drivers, and sample analysis accessories—with standardized symbols and operational status (NO, NC, FO, FC, FL, LO, LC). Instrumentation elements for flow, pressure, temperature, and level measurement are detailed, including orifice plates, venturi tubes, flowmeters (thermal mass, magnetic, vortex, turbine, wedge, ultrasonic, Coriolis

The passages comprehensively outline the applicability, responsibilities, and conformance of various regulatory guides (RGs), Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections, and technical requirements for nuclear plant licensing, focusing on Combined License (COL) applicants and the NuScale certified design as documented in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5. Regulatory Guide 1.178 and SRP 3.9.8 assign the development and implementation of risk-informed inservice inspection (RI-ISI) programs for piping to the COL applicant or licensee. SRP 16.1, Rev 1, and SRP 19.0/19.1, Rev 3, provide probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) guidelines for technical specification revisions, site-specific hazard evaluation, and PRA technical adequacy, with DC/COL-ISG-3 guidance now incorporated into SRP 19.0, Rev 3. Section 17.4 II.N requires external event analysis beyond the design basis in the plant PRA. Several SRP sections (e.g., 2.1.2, 13.1.1–13.1.3, 13.2.1–13.2.2, 13.3, 13.4, 13.5.1.1, 13.5.1.2, 13.6.1, 13.6.3, 13.6.6, 13.7.1) are not applicable to COL applicants, with no technical requirements specified. SRP 13.6.2, Rev 2, applies to physical security within the NuScale design boundary, and SRP 13.6.4, Rev 1, addresses access authorization for COL applicants. SRP 14.3.12, Rev 1, is partially conforming, with COL applicants responsible for ITAAC on physical security hardware outside the nuclear island. Emergency planning under SRP 13.3, including NRC notifications, communications, and offsite planning, is assigned to the COL applicant, with no changes to existing emergency response facilities. SRP 3.7.4, Rev 2 (Seismic Instrumentation), and SRP 12.1, Rev 4 (ALARA), are not applicable, though ALARA expectations per 10 CFR 20.1101 are noted. NuScale’s FSAR documents conformance with relevant SRP sections, Branch Technical Positions, and operational experience, but assigns responsibility for SRP 1.33, 13.3, 13.5.2.1, and 17.3 to the COL applicant. NuScale conforms to SRP 1.34 and 17.5 for Procurement Document Control, Test Control, and Audits, with no exceptions. RG 1.4 and RG 1.183 address LOCA assumptions for PWRs, with RG 1.183 specified

The passages provide a comprehensive summary of regulatory guidance, evaluation criteria, and conformance status for Combined License (COL) applicants and licensees under 10 CFR Part 52, with a focus on Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC) closure per 10 CFR 52.99, which is deemed acceptable for compliance. Containment structural integrity evaluations for internal pressure loadings above design-basis pressure fully meet requirements. Standard Review Plan (SRP) 14.3 (March 2007) addresses ITAAC scope, with partial or full conformity for various acceptance criteria across structural, piping, reactor, electrical, plant systems, human factors engineering, and emergency planning (SRP 14.3.2, 14.3.3, 14.3.4, 14.3.6, 14.3.7, 14.3.9, 14.3.10). Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) documents and SRP sections, such as DC/COL-ISG-13, NUREG-0800 SRP Section 11.2, and Branch Technical Position 11-6, partially conform regarding accidental radioactive releases from liquid waste tanks, with site-specific assessments and implementation (e.g., Offsite Dose Calculation Manual) delegated to COL applicants. Groundwater/surface water transport and exposure scenario calculations are not applicable at the design certification stage. DC/COL-ISG-17 (seismic input for site response and soil-structure interaction) is not applicable at design certification but is relevant for COL reviews, while DC/COL-ISG-28 (Advanced Light-Water Reactor probabilistic risk assessments) fully conforms for both design certification and COL applicants. Requirements for design certification detail (SECY-90-241, SECY-90-377), ITAAC guidance (SECY-91-178, SECY-91-210), and use of Design Acceptance Criteria (SECY-92-053) are incorporated into 10 CFR 52 and NRC guidance, with full conformance for ITAAC demonstration (II.L) and design reliability assurance program description (II.M).

The passages collectively summarize NuScale’s conformance with NRC regulatory requirements and guidance for advanced LWR designs, as documented in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, and Table 1.9-2. Full conformance is achieved for guidance on plugging degraded PWR steam generator tubes, beyond-design-basis aircraft impact (1.217), multiple steam generator tube failure (II.R), conversion of design certification PRA to plant-specific PRA (II.S), ALWR alarm systems (II.T), regulatory treatment of active nonsafety systems (III.A), redefinition of passive failures as active (III.B), periodic testing of protection system actuation (11.5, 1.22), bypassed/inoperable status indication (3.6, 5.2, 6.2, 9.3, 11.5, 14.2, 14.3, 1.47), welding preheat temperature control (1.50), electrical safety system independence (1.75), environmental qualification of safety-related I&C (1.209), QA list requirements (50.34(f)(3)(ii)), and safety-related logic circuit testing (GL 96-01). Partial conformance is noted for seismic floor response spectra (1.122), protective coatings (1.54), electric penetration assemblies (1.63), instrument sensing lines (1.151), station blackout (1.155), safety-related actuator qualification (1.73), initial startup/preoperational testing (14.2, 1.68.2, 1.68.3), fire protection (II.3, II.6), initial test programs and remote shutdown (3.13, 5.3, 1.68, 14.2, 1.68.2), RG 1.68.1, RG 1.79, RG 1.190 (with exceptions in TR-0116-20781), and categorization of SSCs by safety significance (10 CFR 50.69). Many requirements are not applicable, including those for SBWR thermal-hydraulic stability (III.C), meteorological monitoring (1.23), license termination (1.86), main steam isolation valve leakage (1.96), post-seismic restart (1.167), risk-informed licensing basis changes (1.174), RG 5.49 (internal SNM transfers), ice condenser systems, and elevated-temperature reactors. For operational, programmatic, and physical protection RGs—such as medical evaluation (1.134), respiratory protection (8.15), radiation protection training (8.27), design bases (1.186), implementation of changes (1.187), and most safeguards/security RGs (5.27, 5.54, 5.62, etc.)—responsibility is assigned to the COL applicant or licensee. The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) safeguards the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) during low temperature conditions by using three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, all constructed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A standards. The RSVs, each with a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, and design temperature of 650°F, are sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the 2310 psia design pressure, with a minimum relief capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with pressure setpoints ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, determined by linear interpolation based on downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation (up to 880 kW) and 2 MW core decay heat. The setpoints include margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure, and overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs), with the most severe AOO being a turbine trip at full power without bypass. RVVs and RSVs are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in referenced tables and figures, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, 10 CFR 50.61, and GDC 15 for a 60-year plant life. Additional details on valve qualification, design, operation, and testing are provided in Sections 6.3, 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report demonstrates that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, and clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The RCPB material selections, as specified in Table 5.2-4, include nickel-based Alloy 718 (UNS N07718) for threaded fasteners, SA-508 Grade 3, Class 1 and 2 for RPV sections, SA-533 Type B, Class 2 for support gussets and plates, and austenitic stainless steels (SA-479/SA-240 Type 304/304L, 316/316L) for core barrel guides, support blocks, and instrument assemblies.

Section 5.2 of the nuclear engineering document details the integrity of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), which includes all pressure-retaining components of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) up to and including the outermost containment isolation valve (CIV), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) reactor vent valves (RVVs), and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), as defined in 10 CFR 50.2. Key RCPB components—such as the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), pressurizer (PZR) heater bundles, RVVs, RRVs, actuator assemblies, and steam generator (SG) primary and secondary sides—are designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and maintained to ASME BPVC Section III, 2013 Edition, with Class 1 components classified as Quality Group A and subject to the highest quality standards per the Quality Assurance Program and 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 1, 4, 14, 30, 31, and 32. The pressurizer baffle plate, a critical safety-related internal RPV structure, forms the lower boundary of the pressurizer region, integrates with upper steam plenums, acts as a thermal barrier, and provides hydraulic communication via eight 4-inch flow holes. It also serves as the tubesheet for SG tube bundles, provides penetrations for control rod drive shafts and incore instrument guide tubes, and supports the upper riser assembly. The RPV is a cylindrical steel vessel (9 ft diameter, 58 ft high, 1,850 psia design), with a torispherical head, cylindrical sections, flanged connections, and penetrations for main steam, feedwater, pressurizer heaters, CVCS, and instrumentation. The RPV supports or attaches to CRDMs, seismic supports, heater bundles, in-core instrumentation, SG piping, RCS piping, RSVs, vent valves, and recirculation valves, and is certified per ASME BPVC Section III, with pressure-temperature curves per 10 CFR 50, Appendix G. Two 400 kW pressurizer heater bundles are mounted 180° apart through the RPV side. The containment vessel (CNV) is a 76 ft tall, 15 ft diameter cylindrical steel vessel with a bolted flange, supporting the RPV and housing the RPV, CRDMs, and associated piping, forming part of the RCPB and acting as a barrier to fission product release. The RPV contains the reactor core, reactor vessel internals (RVI), SGs, and pressurizer, with the RVI directing coolant flow and including sub-assemblies such as core support, surveillance capsules, riser assemblies, flow diverter, and pressurizer spray nozzles. RSVs provide overpressure protection for RCPB piping external to the RPV,

The passages detail the comprehensive inspection, testing, and fabrication requirements for the NuScale Nuclear Power Module (NPM) reactor vessel, reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), and associated systems, as specified in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report and in compliance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and NRC regulations. Reactor vessel and RCPB components are constructed from Type 3XX austenitic stainless steel (max 0.03 wt% C) to prevent sensitization and intergranular corrosion, with welding procedures qualified per ASME BPVC Section III (notably NB-4300, NB-4600, NB-4622), Section IX, and Regulatory Guides 1.43, 1.44, 1.50, and 1.71. Welders are qualified per ASME Section IX and RG 1.71. Austenitic stainless steel and Ni-Cr-Fe cladding (e.g., Alloy 52/152) are applied to low alloy steel surfaces, with specific layering (first layer 309L, subsequent 308L) and post-weld heat treatment at 1125°F ± 25°F; Alloy 600 and Alloy 82/182 are excluded. Nondestructive examination (NDE) of welds and tubular products follows ASME BPVC Section III and XI, with preservice ultrasonic and surface exams for Class 1 piping welds, and requirements varying by pipe size and location. Inspection and testing programs are based on ASME BPVC Section III (2013 Edition) for construction, Section XI for ISI, and ASME OM-2012 for operation and maintenance, as referenced in 10 CFR 50.55a and RGs 1.84, 1.147, and 1.192. ISI intervals, frequencies, and component selection are defined, with inspection elements for piping, supports, and valves detailed in Tables 5.2-6 through 5.2-9 and Figures 5.2-1, 5.2-2, 5.3-1, and 5.4-17. System leakage and VT-2 examinations are performed before startup, with continuous VT-2 monitoring of RPV leakage in containment. Valve position is verified every 24 months, set pressure every 5 years, and special seal verification tests are used for reactor pool valves. O-ring leakage and flange bolt inspections follow Section XI. Reactor vessel internals inspections, per MRP-227 and ASME BPVC Section XI, focus on core support blocks, plates, guide tubes, riser transitions, and fasteners, using VT-1 and VT-3, with augmented exams for critical components. The reactor vessel beltline is evaluated for Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (≥75 ft-lb initial, ≥50 ft-lb end-of-life), and plant procedures ensure compliance with pressure-temperature limits.
