The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, and supporting documentation emphasize that many regulatory criteria and guidance documents—especially those tailored to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs) and large Pressurized Water Reactors (PWRs)—are inapplicable to NuScale due to its unique, passive design. The plant eliminates forced reactor coolant flow, coolant loops, pumps, and several auxiliary systems, rendering guidance such as RG 1.79.1, BTP 3-2, SRP 4.6 II.8, BTP 10-1, SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5, and requirements for core spray and low pressure coolant injection systems (10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(viii)) non-applicable. The Strategic Water Chemistry Plan aligns with EPRI PWR Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines (e.g., EPRI #3002000505) and fuel vendor recommendations, specifying impurity limits (chloride, fluoride, sulfate ≤ 0.05 ppm; hydrogen 25–50 cc/kg; boron 0–2000 ppm), sampling frequencies, corrective actions, and chemistry parameters per RG 1.44, Rev 1. The plan ensures reactor pressure boundary and fuel integrity through alkaline-reducing chemistry, controlled pH, lithium, dissolved hydrogen, and zinc addition to mitigate PWSCC and radiation fields. Effluent release and coolant source term calculations follow NUREG-0017, PWR-GALE86, ANSI/ANS 18.1-1999, and NuScale technical reports, with decontamination factors and failed fuel fractions well below 0.25%. Partial conformance is noted with RG 1.110, RG 1.112, and 10 CFR 50.34(b)(3), 50.34a, and 52.79(a)(3), while DC/COL-ISG-5 and guidance for CAOC, SG blowdown, and once-through/top-feed/preheat steam generators (BTP 4-1, SRP 10.4.8, SRP 10.4.6, BTP 10-2) are not applicable; only OTSGD B.2 test acceptance criteria for once-through SGs are relevant. Flow Accelerated Corrosion is addressed per DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0. For LOCA and recirculation cooling (RG 1.82), NuScale meets the intent but differs in system design, supporting exemptions for 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) and portions of Appendix K, and partially conforming to radiological consequences of main steam line failures. The design omits reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, Class 1E motors, nonmetallic insulation, safety injection systems, auxiliary feedwater, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead

The NuScale reactor design incorporates several unique features and regulatory approaches, including a single-failure-proof crane that eliminates the need for load drop accident analyses for spent fuel casks or Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs). Containment atmosphere management minimizes oxygen during normal operations and uses continuous hydrogen and oxygen monitoring post-accident, meeting the intent of RG 1.7 without active combustible gas control systems; the design partially meets DSRS 6.2.5 for gas concentration analysis but fully meets requirements for equipment survivability, containment integrity, and atmosphere mixing, supporting exemptions from containment leakage rate testing (GDC 52, 10 CFR 50 Appendix J Type A). The Decay Heat Removal (DHR) System generally conforms to GDC 5 and related requirements, with exemptions for GDC 19 and 34, and supports NuScale-specific principal design criteria; for containment heat removal (DSRS 6.2.2), the design meets GDC 39 but departs from GDC 40, and for combustible gas control (DSRS 6.2.5), it supports an exemption from GDC 41 power provisions. Equipment survivability ensures radiation protection, and the design does not rely on prompt sampling for accident source term assessment (10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii)). The Control Rod Drive System (CRDS) conforms to GDCs 4, 23, 25, 28, and 29, clarifies GDC 26 does not require two safety-related reactivity controls, and departs from GDC 27 in favor of a design-specific PDC. Testing confirmed CRA drop time and impact force, with partial conformance to RG 1.77, SRP 6.5.3, BTP 6-1, SRP 9.2.4, and SRP 9.3.2, and full conformance to SRP 4.2, 9.2.6, 15.4.2, and 15.4.7; the condensate storage system is non-safety-related, and potable/sanitary water systems do not interface with radioactive systems. The FSAR documents non-applicability or non-conformance with several regulatory positions (e.g., C1.1.3, C1.1.4, C1.3.12, C.2 except 2.2, C.3), partial conformance to GDC 54 for DHRS, and exemptions from GDC 38, 42, and 43 due to design features. The design does not use exclusion zone/buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability and is similar to large PWRs for effluent release calculations, making certain interim guidance inapplicable. Containment purge systems vent directly to the environment, and SFP mitigating measures are not required due to design features (small containment, below-grade SFP). The Ultimate

The NuScale Power Plant’s compressed air and electric power systems are classified as non-safety and non-risk-significant, conforming to GDC 5 but not required to meet GDC 1 or 2, and only partially conforming to 10 CFR 50.63 for station blackout (SBO) coping. The design eliminates the need for alternate AC power sources and emergency diesel generators, consistent with Commission policy, and meets requirements for fuel cladding integrity, moderate frequency incidents, GDC 10 and 15, single failure analysis, and loss of AC power analysis per DSRS 15.2.6. Regulatory guide conformance is partial for RG 1.128, RG 1.129, and RG 1.212, full for RG 1.211, and not applicable for RG 1.157, RG 1.210, and RG 1.158, with DC system batteries being non-Class 1E. The FSAR Revision 5 demonstrates conformance with DSRS 6.2.4, 8.2, 8.3.1, 8.3.2, and SRP 9.4.1, with onsite AC power systems lacking Class 1E distribution but meeting Safety Guide 6 for independence and conforming to GDC 2, 4, 5 (partial), and 50. DC systems fully conform to GDC 2, 4, 5, and 50. NuScale supports exemptions from GDC 17, 18, 19, and 33, and complies with principal design criteria instead of GDCs 34, 35, 38, 41, and 44. Offsite power system requirements for Class 1E loads and grid stability studies are not applicable, with grid study responsibility assigned to the COL applicant. For DSRS 8.4, partial conformance with 10 CFR 50.63 and RG 1.155 is noted; all safety-related functions are maintained for 72 hours post-SBO without AC power, with RTNSS applied and EDSS independence addressed. The plant omits emergency diesel generators, ESF rooms, and pressurizer power operated relief valves, rendering several SRP sections and BTPs inapplicable. Nonsafety-related Turbine Generator Buildings (TGBs) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs) house turbine generators and backup diesel generators, none of which support safety-related systems. The Central Utility Building (CUB) and Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) provide common utility services and auxiliary support. The design does not credit ESF filter or ventilation systems for accident mitigation; nonsafety-related HVAC systems provide atmosphere cleanup per RG 1.140 but are not credited in dose analyses. Control room habitability is ensured by compressed air tanks supplying clean air during accidents, eliminating reliance on emergency filtration. The NuScale nuclear power plant design ensures protection of safety-related structures, systems, and components (SSCs) from internal flooding by siting the facility above the probable maximum flood height, including wind-induced wave run-up, making certain flood protection requirements inapplicable. The design conforms or partially conforms to NRC guidance (RG 1.102, SRP 3.4.2, 1.105), consistently assuming the plant is above the most severe flood and groundwater levels. Seismic design adheres to RG 1.61, with an alternative damping value for the NPM substructure, and includes a metal-lined concrete dike around the PSCS storage tank. Seismic parameters, including ground motion and damping, fully conform to DSRS 3.7.1, 3.7.2, and 3.7.3, with site-specific V/A and AD/V² ratios matching controlling seismic events. The Certified Seismic Design Response Spectra (CSDRS) envelopes RG 1.60 anchored to 0.1g and serves as the Safe-Shutdown Earthquake (SSE), with the Operating-Basis Earthquake (OBE) set at one-third SSE. Seismic system and subsystem analyses address analytical modeling, soil-structure interaction, modal response, and forces per DSRS 3.7.2 and 3.7.3. Seismic design classification generally follows RG 1.29 and SRP 3.2.1, except SSCs under Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i are classified as Seismic Category II. Structural analysis for Seismic Category I structures, including foundations, conforms to DSRS 3.8.4 and 3.8.5; masonry walls and concrete dams are not used. Winter precipitation loads on roofs are addressed per DC/COL-ISG-7. Seismic instrumentation partially conforms to RG 1.12 and SRP 3.7.4, with detectors in the Reactor Building and full compliance deferred to the COL applicant. For turbine missile hazards, the design verifies turbine missile spectrum, rotor design, and building wall integrity, but since no safety-related SSCs are in the Turbine Building, reliance on missile generation probabilities or rotor integrity is not required if RG 1.115 barriers are used. Protective barriers are analyzed using finite element methods for concrete and per SRP 3.5.3 for steel, ensuring physical separation of redundant safety-related equipment and not relying on turbine overspeed protection, per GDC 4. Tornado and hurricane hazards are addressed using bounding Region 1 characteristics (RG 1.76, RG 1.221) with a maximum tornado wind speed of 230 mph, conforming to SRP 3.3.2, 3.5.1.4, and DC/COL-ISG-24, with site-specific confirmation left to the COL applicant. The passages summarize the project's conformance to various Regulatory Guides (RGs), Generic Letters (GLs), and Standard Review Plan (SRP)/Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) sections regarding the qualification and regulatory compliance of mechanical and electrical equipment in nuclear power plants. Full conformance is stated for RG 1.152, RG 1.209, and GL 91-04 (setpoint methodology), as well as for DSRS 7.2.6, 7.2.8, 7.2.10, 7.2.4, 7.2.9, 7.2.11, and 7.2.12, referencing RGs 1.47, 1.75, 1.53, and 1.62, with details in Table 1.9-2 or 1.9-2.7.2.1. Partial conformance is reported for RGs 1.168, 1.169, 1.170, 1.171, 1.173, 1.151, 1.204, 1.105, 1.180 (DSRS 7.2.2), 1.32, 1.81, 1.118, 1.153 (DSRS 8.3.2), 1.63, 1.89, 1.97, 1.183, and 1.100, with non-applicability noted for RGs 1.158, 1.204, and certain SRP sections (e.g., SRP 3.7.4, RG 1.166). DSRS Table 8-1 summarizes NRC requirements and industry codes for electrical systems, with partial conformance in Sections 8.2, 8.3.1, 8.3.2, and 8.4. For DC power systems (DSRS 8.3.2), the design partially conforms to RGs 1.32, 1.81, 1.118, 1.153, and 1.63, and fully to RG 1.75; GL 91-06 and Generic Issue A30 are addressed for non-Class 1E EDSS design due to the absence of safety-related DC systems. Seismic and environmental qualification reference partial conformance to RG 1.100 (with exceptions for site-specific spectra and older plants), RG 1.183, and use of IEEE 344-2004 and IEEE 497-2002. Full conformance is noted for DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0, with RGs 1.73, 1.152, 1.211, and 1.156 applied to Class 1E valve operators, computer systems, cables, and connection assemblies. Installation and inspection fully conform to RG 1

The reviewed DSRS sections (6.2.1, 6.2.4, 11.5, and 11.6, Rev 0) confirm that containment functional design requirements are addressed in other SRP/DSRS sections, with the containment isolation system meeting instrument line isolation, leak detection, and isolation valve criteria, though many requirements are not applicable due to the absence of relevant lines, valves, or systems; relief valves are not used as containment isolation valves. For DSRS 11.5, process and effluent radiological monitoring instrumentation and sampling systems criteria are partially met, with exceptions related to guidance in RG 1.21, RG 1.33, RG 1.97, RG 4.1, RG 4.15, RG 4.21, and BTP 7-10, and some responsibilities deferred to the COL applicant. Full conformance is achieved for continuous sampling of radioactive iodines and particulates in gaseous effluents from all potential accident release points. DSRS 11.6 confirms compliance with GDC 63 and 64, 10 CFR 20.1406, and NEI guidance, but only partial conformance with 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xxvi) due to COL applicant responsibilities. Required instrumentation is provided for measuring and recording containment pressure, water level, hydrogen concentration, radiation intensity, and noble gas effluents at all potential accident release points, as required by 50.34(f)(2)(xvii). Installation of required instrumentation and sampling equipment, monitoring of gaseous and liquid release points, appropriate monitoring locations, and the software development process for digital systems are confirmed. RETS/SREC and ODCM setpoints are not applicable, and radiation monitoring and sampling conform to Technical Specifications, the Initial Test Program, and ITAAC. Reactor fuel storage area monitors are confirmed, with exceptions only where responsibilities are deferred to the COL applicant. The NuScale FSAR, Revision 5, and associated DSRS chapters confirm full conformance with SRP 12.1, Rev 4 (ALARA), ANSI/ANS/HPSSC-6.8.1-1981, ANSI/ANS-6.4-2006, NEI 97-06, NUREG-0737 II.B.2, ANSI/ANS 18.1, 10 CFR 50.49, RG 1.26, RG 1.29, RG 1.117, and DSRS 11.4, II.1, and 11.6, II.5/II.10. The NuScale Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive system that supplies clean, compressed air to the Main Control Room (MCR) during radioactive releases or loss of AC power, maintaining positive pressure and providing at least 72 hours of breathable air. The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, safety-related system designed for secondary side reactor cooling during non-LOCA events when normal feedwater or secondary cooling is unavailable. The DHRS uses redundant, single active failure-proof trains and operates via natural circulation without pumps, analogous to the Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system in large PWRs but tailored for NuScale reactor transients. It interfaces with the secondary system through the steam generators (SGs) and does not connect directly to the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), making certain regulatory requirements inapplicable. DHRS actuation is automatic via the Module Protection System (MPS), with manual initiation possible from the control room or MPS equipment rooms, though remote access is restricted. The system can be actuated without reducing RCS pressure or temperature and is designed to prevent flow disruption from noncondensible gas accumulation, with analyses considering heat transfer surface fouling, SG tube plugging, and maximum gas accumulation at 60°F. The DHRS maintains core cooling for 72 hours during a station blackout, meeting 10 CFR 50.63, and can transition the NuScale Power Module (NPM) to safe shutdown without external power, maintaining fuel and RCPB design limits. Leakage detection and control minimize radioactive contamination, with an all-welded design and facility programs to limit contamination and waste. In extended cooldown scenarios with low boron and unavailable CVCS, there is a potential for reestablishing criticality if the most reactive control rod remains stuck out. The DHRS has no direct interface or shared components with the RCPB and is monitored via system parameters rather than direct flow measurement. Key DHRS actuation valves (DHR-HOV-0101A/B, DHR-HOV-0201A/B) are normally closed and fail open upon actuation or loss of control power, using nitrogen pressure for operation without electrical power. Redundant parallel valves per train ensure continued function in case of single valve failure. Main steam and feedwater isolation valves (MS-HOV-0101, MS-HOV-0201, FW-HOV-0137, FW-HOV-0237) are normally open and fail closed to establish a closed DHRS loop, with downstream valves or temporary inventory addition mitigating failure to close. These valves are located outside the NPM atop the containment vessel and actuated automatically via ESFAS or manually via MPS, though manual actuation is not credited for design basis events. DHRS actuation valves are designed to fully open or close within 30 seconds at ≤50 psid differential pressure. The DHRS operates by natural circulation, driven by density differences

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) is a passive, safety-related, closed-loop system engineered to independently remove decay heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) using natural circulation through the steam generators (SGs) and passive condensers, with heat ultimately transferred to the reactor pool. The DHRS meets 10 CFR 50 Appendix A General Design Criteria (GDC 1, 2, 4, 5), is classified as Quality Group B, ASME BPVC Section III Class 2, and Seismic Category I, and is protected from both external and internal missiles by the Reactor Building and NPM bay configuration. Each NPM’s DHRS is fully independent, sharing only the reactor pool as the ultimate heat sink, and is unaffected by shared power supplies since active components fail-safe on power loss. The system is unavailable during NPM movement and refueling, during which decay heat is removed by conduction through the RPV and containment shell or direct pool contact, with protection provided by a single failure-proof Reactor Building crane. The DHRS is not affected by LOCA loads, as it is not connected to the RCS and its critical components are outside containment. The system is engineered to withstand natural phenomena, pipe whip, and flow-induced vibration, with preservice and inservice inspections per BPVC Section XI for Class 2 components. Four condensation-induced water hammer mechanisms were evaluated and found not applicable or acceptable for DHRS design life. DHRS actuation valves, piping, and passive condensers are Class 2, Quality Group B, and seismically qualified, with supports and welding per BPVC requirements. Instrumentation includes four level transmitters per train for noncondensible gas detection, eight steam pressure sensors per NPM for safety-related signals, and condensate temperature and pressure sensors, all classified as Type D accident monitoring variables per IEEE 497-2002 and RG 1.97. The DHRS is filled by condensation, with liquid fill pressure not exceeding saturation pressure, and noncondensible gases vented during normal operation; the maximum assumed noncondensible gas concentration is 1.22E+6 ppb (0.422 kg), limited by level sensor placement. Accumulation of noncondensible gas can impede heat transfer, but analysis shows it is unlikely to reach critical levels during operation. The DHRS provides overpressure protection without low-pressure interlocks or relief devices, and is controlled by on/off actuation via the MPS. The system is connected to the steam and feedwater piping between the MSIVs and FWIVs, forming a closed loop outside containment and providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and reactor pool. Each DHRS train is associated with one of the two NPM SGs, with actuation valves and orifices arranged to promote natural circulation and moderate flow. The DHRS is designed to maintain RCS average temperature below 420

The passages describe the structure and content of Table 1.9-3 from Section 1.9 of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) Revision 5, which systematically documents NuScale’s conformance with regulatory requirements from NUREG-0800 (Standard Review Plan, SRP) and the NuScale Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS). The table lists SRP and DSRS section numbers and revisions (e.g., 3.6, 4.4.7, 4.5.2, 4.6.2, 5.4.3, 6.1.1, 6.2.1, 6.2.4, 6.2.5, 6.3.1, 6.6.8, 9.2.5, 15.0, 15.0.2, 15.0.3, 15.1, 15.2, 15.5, 15.6, 15.6.5, 17.5), along with columns for Title, Acceptance Criteria (AC), AC Title/Description, Conformance Status, Comments, and Section. The table includes specific conformance outcomes, such as "Conforms," "Partially Conforms" (e.g., DSRS 7.2.1 to RG 1.172), and "Not Applicable" for sections irrelevant to the NuScale design (e.g., spent fuel reprocessing, special nuclear material transport, composite barrier design), with justifications provided (e.g., clean air supplied by compressed air tanks). Some sections, like SRP 1.0, have no specific AC, while others, such as DSRS 6.6 and 7.1.1, fully conform with no exceptions. The table also references improved standard technical specification guidance from DSRS NUREGs-1430 through -1434 and NUREG-2194. In compliance with 10 CFR 52.47(a)(21), Table 1.9-5 addresses unresolved or active medium- to high-priority generic safety issues (GSI) from NUREG-0933, referencing NRC SECY-07-0110 and Appendix B of NUREG-0933, while excluding resolved or non-applicable issues. Each entry in the tables includes conformance status, exceptions, and references to relevant FSAR sections. Additionally, the first passage mentions electrical ratings and component identifiers (e.g., 3000 A, 52 N.O.6A, 1200 A, N.O.200 A), likely referring to electrical switch ratings and normally open switch designations, though no further technical context is provided. Overall, the passages emphasize a methodical, comprehensive approach to documenting regulatory compliance for the NuScale nuclear facility design.

Tables 1.9-3 and 1.9-4 document conformance with NUREG-0800 (Standard Review Plan), the Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS), and Interim Staff Guidance (ISG) by listing relevant sections, acceptance criteria (AC) titles/descriptions, conformance status, and comments. In both tables, nearly all entries—including DSRS 3.5.1.3 and section 3.9.5—are marked "Not Applicable," with no specific ISG or SRP/DSRS sections, ACs, technical details, numerical values, or component names provided. The only detailed comment appears in Table 1.9-4, section 9.1.1, which states that, due to the spacing and the large number of assemblies in the base analysis model, there is no statistically significant increase in reactivity. Otherwise, the tables contain no additional technical content or conformance data.

The NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application (DCA) addresses emergency planning and regulatory requirements by conforming to SRP 13.3, Rev 3, providing an emergency response data system and technical support center, but leaves site-specific elements—such as evacuation time estimates, operational support center (OSC), emergency operations facility, and emergency planning ITAAC—to the Combined License (COL) applicant. The DCA applies regulatory guidance as needed to ensure COL applicants can meet requirements, with full implementation—including site-specific design, construction, testing, inspection, and record maintenance—remaining the COL applicant’s responsibility. The NuScale DCA (Revision 5, July 2020) details principal design criteria, safety features, emergency systems, instrumentation, controls, electrical and cooling systems, fuel handling, waste management, and auxiliary systems. COL applicants must develop a Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) referencing the NuScale FSAR and provide site-specific operational program details and schedules for inservice inspection, environmental qualification, fire protection, radiation protection, emergency planning, security, quality assurance, and operator training, per Regulatory Guide 1.33, Rev 3. Additional COL requirements include ultimate heat sink instrumentation (NEI 12-02, Rev 1), firefighting enhancements (10 CFR 50.155(b)(2)), and hazard evaluations for multi-unit sites (10 CFR 52.79(a)(31)). NuScale Power, LLC retains overall design responsibility, while COL applicants must address items such as flood mitigation (COL Item 3.4-2), fuel performance for non-baseload operations (4.2-1), module stability (7.0-1), critical load handling (9.1-5), operating procedures (13.5-5), a Startup Administration Manual (14.2-2), and human performance monitoring (18.12-1); items 1.4.1 and 5.2-1 are not used. Personnel qualification and training, safety-related diesel generators, and operator simulation facilities are site-specific or operational responsibilities of the COL applicant, and the NuScale design does not require safety-related emergency diesel generators or operator staffing per 10 CFR 50.54(m)(2)(i) and (iii). The design conforms to turbine missile protection (Section 1.115) and partially to requirements for locks, intrusion alarms, access controls, and vehicle protection, with site-specific implementation by the COL applicant or licensee, who must also comply with vehicle protection (F090 Site Layout Plan, 10 CFR 73.55) and liquid mass measurement systems (Section 5.48). The NRC inspected the test program using IP 35034, 35017, and 36100, and all design information is subject to NuScale Power LLC’s copyright. A Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must address extensive site-specific and programmatic requirements. The applicant must specify the plant location and implement programs for reactor vessel internals reliability and aging, such as the Comprehensive Vibration Assessment Program and steam generator programs. A Boric Acid Control Program, detailing inspection types and frequencies to ensure reactor coolant pressure boundary integrity, is required, along with a reactor vessel material surveillance program per NUREG 0800, Section 5.3.1.5.3, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, and RG 1.161. The applicant must verify containment vessel flange contact pressure under accident conditions and demonstrate that the as-built containment internal free volume provides adequate margin for design pressure and temperature, accounting for internal structures and manufacturing processes. Certified Overpressure Protection Reports per ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticles NB-7200 and NC-7200, are required for both the reactor coolant pressure boundary and secondary system, including low temperature overpressure protection. For piping systems, the applicant must complete routing outside containment and bioshield, identify high- and moderate-energy line locations, update Tables 3.6-1 and 3.6-2, and perform pipe rupture hazards analyses—including dynamic and environmental effects—for lines outside the reactor pool bay, considering multi-module impacts and subcompartment pressurization. Site-specific seismic demands for critical SSCs must be shown to be bounded by the certified design or the design must be modified, with structure-soil-structure interaction analyses for the Reactor Building, Control Building, Radioactive Waste Building, and Turbine Generator Buildings, and configuration analyses for the Reactor Building and NuScale Power Module layout. The applicant must demonstrate that seismic demands for empty dry dock conditions are bounded by FSAR capacity, determine interaction planes for soil-structure interaction, and establish bioshield lifting methods to ensure component integrity. A site-specific, strain-compatible soil profile must be developed, seismic site characteristics confirmed to be within certified design parameters, and nearby structures evaluated for collapse risk during a safe shutdown earthquake. Soil-structure interaction analyses using NuScale SASSI2010 models, a seismic monitoring system and program per RG 1.12 and RG 1.166, and post-earthquake procedures using seismic instrumentation data are required. Performance-based response spectra analyses at various depths must be provided, justifying V/H spectral ratios, and in-structure response spectra for the NuScale Power Module and building locations must be shown to be bounded by certified spectra; otherwise, the design must be modified.

The applicant must evaluate hazards from construction activities to safety-related and risk-significant SSCs at co-located sites per 10 CFR 52.79(a)(31), implement management and administrative controls to mitigate these hazards, and ensure limiting conditions for operation are not exceeded. The use of probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) and risk-informed applications must be described throughout the COL application,

Section 5.4 details the design and configuration of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components, focusing on the steam generators (SGs) integrated within the upper reactor pressure vessel (RPV). The reactor employs two independent, once-through, helical-coil SGs located inside the RPV, with primary coolant flowing downward around the SG tubes and secondary-side feedwater and main steam flowing inside the tubes. Feedwater is supplied via external piping through the containment vessel (CNV) and enters the SG tubes at their lowest point, penetrating the RPV wall into the lower SG plena. Each SG consists of interlacing helical tube columns connecting to two feed and two steam plena, forming an intertwined bundle around the upper riser assembly, resulting in four feed and four steam plena spaced 90° apart. The RPV acts as the SG shell and outer pressure boundary, while the SG tubes, tube-to-tubesheet welds, and tubesheets form part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) and serve as a fission product barrier. On the secondary side, preheated feedwater is converted to superheated steam in the SGs, collected in the steam plena, and routed via nozzles and piping to the main steam system and turbine generator. A turbine bypass line can direct up to 100% of rated main steam flow to the condenser for heat removal during load reductions or loss of electrical load. The SG system includes main steam and feedwater supply nozzles, tube supports, tube inlet flow restrictors, access ports, covers, and connections to the chemical and volume control system (CNTS) and decay heat removal system (DHRS), which operates through naturally circulating closed loops for decay heat removal. Overpressure protection is provided by thermal relief valves on each feedwater line and relief and safety valves (RSVs) for DHRS internal pressure. The SG design incorporates a 10% tube plugging margin, a fouling factor based on nuclear plant experience, and features to minimize tube damage from loose parts wear, with vertical bars and austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies providing vibration and seismic support at eight locations per tube. Inlet flow restrictors, mounted on plates in each feed plenum access port and attached to the tubesheets, ensure stable flow, prevent density wave oscillations, and are removable for maintenance. Flow-induced vibration analyses address fluid elastic instability, vortex shedding, and turbulent buffeting, with restrictors designed for high rigidity and low vibration amplitudes. Key SG component materials include SA-312 Type 304/304L, SA-182 Grade F304/F304L, SA-479 Type 304/304L, SB-166 Alloy 690, SB-637 Alloy 718, and SA-508 Grade 3 Class 2, with dual certification for stainless steel. The SGs are designed for ease of cleaning and maintenance, with inservice inspection (ISI) per ASME BPVC Section XI

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) steam generator (SG) is an integral, once-through, helical tube design located within the reactor pressure vessel, with two SGs per module. Each SG contains 1,380 seamless helical tubes in 21 columns, providing a total heat transfer area of 17,928 ft² and a 10% tube plugging margin. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with inlet and outlet temperatures of 299.7°F and 584.4°F, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. Both primary and secondary sides are rated at 2,100 psia and 650°F, while SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia and 550°F. Tubes have a 0.625-inch outer diameter, 0.050-inch wall thickness, and a minimum bend radius of 6.250 inches. Steam and feed tubesheets are 4.0 and 6.0 inches thick (without clad), increasing to 4.625 and 6.625 inches with clad (0.250 inches secondary, 0.375 inches primary). Eight austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies per column provide full-circumferential, non-continuous support, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion, and ensuring high vibration resistance. The design eliminates traditional tube support plates and uses full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore to minimize crevices, with all tube-to-tubesheet contact in the primary coolant. The once-through design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet, preventing impurity accumulation and eliminating the need for blowdown systems; impurities are managed by chemistry control and periodic cleaning. Secondary side surfaces use corrosion-resistant materials (nickel alloy or stainless steel), allowing safe cleaning and inspection. System monitoring is performed at ≥500 Hz, and preservice inspections include 100% volumetric tube and weld examination per BPVC. The SG tube support structure is within the primary coolant, preventing secondary system corrosion product deposition. Chemistry control follows industry guidelines with NPM modifications, and secondary water quality adheres to NEI 97-06. The fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. SG tube failure is indicated by pressure changes, and actuation valves have position indication.

Secondary side instability evaluation shows no in-phase oscillations and that out-of-phase oscillations cancel, resulting in no net oscillating secondary flow. Acceptable instabilities are limited to tube mass flow fluctuations that do not induce reactor power oscillations exceeding fuel design limits and comply with ASME Code. The vibration assessment program (per Regulatory Guide 1.20, Rev. 3) covers SGs, main steam piping (including MSIV), SG supports, and tube supports within the RPV. The NuScale nuclear plant design demonstrates broad conformance to regulatory and Standard Review Plan (SRP) requirements across multiple systems. The Light Load Handling System and Refueling Cavity Design fully meet GDC 2, 5, 61, and 62 (SRP 9.1.4, Rev 4), and the Overhead Heavy Load Handling Systems fully meet GDC 1, 2, 4, and 5 (SRP 9.1.5, Rev 1), all with no exceptions. Radioactive waste management (RG 1.143) criteria are generally met, except for non-applicability to steam generator blowdown systems. Solid radioactive waste management (BTP 11-3, Rev 4) conforms to processing and storage guidance, with exceptions for Process Control Program (PCP) and portable system controls, which are COL applicant responsibilities. The Liquid (DSRS 11.2, Rev 0) and Gaseous (DSRS 11.3, Rev 0) Waste Management Systems partially conform to dose design objectives, excluding site-specific cost-benefit analyses, but fully meet leakage control, operation, maintenance, and automatic control features; early site permit criteria are not applicable. The Solid Waste Management System (DSRS 11.4, Rev 0) partially conforms to leakage control and operation/maintenance, with long-term storage facility design not applicable. Both liquid and solid waste systems meet seismic design and equipment sizing standards. The Gaseous Waste Management System also meets HEPA/charcoal filter design, hydrogen explosion resistance, and postulated leakage criteria, with partial conformance for contamination minimization and decommissioning features due to COL applicant responsibilities. Solid waste criteria for stabilization, treatment, monitoring, packaging, storage, and disposal of Class A, B, C, and mixed wastes partially conform, with site-specific and programmatic aspects assigned to the COL applicant. The FSAR Tier 2, Rev 5, indicates partial conformance for effluent measurement and reporting, with many operational and site-specific requirements—such as environmental reporting, dosimetry, operational programs, radiological monitoring, and ALARA—delegated to the COL applicant or deemed not applicable to the certified design. For SRP 3.2.2, Rev 2 (System Quality Group Classification), the design meets GDC 1 and 10 CFR 50.55a, except for partial conformance regarding RG 1.85 (withdrawn) and references to non-applicable SSCs. Foundations testing, inservice surveillance, and specification of transients for mechanical components (SRP 3.9.1, Rev 3) fully conform. Control Rod Drive Systems (SRP 3.9.4, Rev 3) meet descriptive, construction, and material requirements, with a minor typographical error and RG 1.85 withdrawal noted. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, details the design’s conformance with a broad range of regulatory criteria, including NUREG-0800, SRP, DSRS, and various Branch Technical Positions (BTPs). Full conformance is achieved for systems such as station service water (SRP 9.2.1), condensate storage (SRP 9.2.6), reactor pressure vessel internals (SRP 3.9.5), nondestructive examination of reactor internals (SRP 4.5.2), overpressure protection (SRP 5.2.2), and upper-shelf energy/pressure-temperature limits (DSRS 5.3.2). The low-temperature overpressurization protection (LTOP) system aligns with BTP 5-2, Rev 3 intent by not relying on offsite power, but only partially conforms to interlock requirements due to design differences from large LWRs. Partial conformance is noted for emergency coolant water pH (BTP 6-1), potable and sanitary water (SRP 9.2.4), and certain BTP 11-5 and 11-6 sections, primarily due to the absence of referenced SSCs, non-applicability to NuScale’s design, or assignment of site-specific requirements to the COL applicant. Several SRPs and BTPs, such as those for chilled water (SRP 9.2.7), emergency diesel engine fuel oil storage (SRP 9.5.4), turbine generator inservice inspection (SRP 10.2), and main steam component classification outside the RCPB (BTP 3-1), are not applicable. The design does not use safety-related pumps, with overpressure relieved directly to containment, and excludes fiberglass-reinforced thermosetting resin piping in spray pond and UHS applications. Full conformance is demonstrated for protection against postulated piping failures (SRP 3.6.1, BTP 3-3, BTP 3-4), with detailed compliance for pipe rupture analysis, dynamic effects, and leak-before-break evaluations (SRP 3.6.2, 3.6.3). For vibration, thermal expansion, and dynamic effects testing (SRP 3.9.2), partial conformance is noted, with test performance and corrective actions assigned to the COL applicant. The report confirms full compliance with requirements for piping systems, supports, and analysis methods (SRP 3.12), valve qualification and inservice testing (SRP 3.10), and turbine/water systems (SRP 10.4.3, 10.4.4, 10.4.5), with exceptions or operational programs assigned to the COL applicant. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, demonstrates conformance with most regulatory criteria for air filtration, atmosphere cleanup, ventilation, and accident mitigation systems, with exceptions reflecting the unique NuScale design. Engineered Safety Feature (ESF) filter and atmosphere cleanup system guidance for post-design basis accident fission product removal (RG 1.52, SRP 9.4.5) is not applicable, as NuScale does not rely on ESF ventilation for accident mitigation, including in the spent fuel storage area (SRP 15.7.4). Control room habitability guidance (SRP 6.4) is not applicable for emergency ventilation during design basis accidents, since emergency standby filtration is not used for operator protection. The control room habitability system conforms to SRP 6.4, Rev 3 and SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3 for ventilation, pressurization, and radioactive material release control, except for ESF atmosphere cleanup. For toxic gas hazards (SRP 6.4.1 II.7), partial conformance is achieved, with programmatic requirements assigned to the COL applicant. During station blackout, compressed air tanks pressurize the control room envelope, which acts as a passive heat sink. The spent fuel pool area ventilation system (SRP 9.4.2) conforms to GDC 2, 5, and 61, except for ESF atmosphere cleanup, and partially conforms to cask drop accident criteria (SRP 15.7.5), with the reactor building crane meeting NUREG-0612 single-failure-proof requirements. The Equipment and Floor Drainage System (SRP 9.3.3, Rev 3), Control Room Area Ventilation System (SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3), and Lighting Systems (SRP 9.5.3, Rev 3) fully conform to requirements for protection against natural phenomena, environmental and dynamic effects, and lighting levels. Up to 12 modules share the same control room ventilation system during normal operations. The Auxiliary and Radwaste Area Ventilation System (SRP 9.4.3, Rev 3) conforms to GDC 2 and 5, but GDC 60 is not applicable to the Radwaste Building Ventilation (RWBV) system, as exhaust filtration is performed by the Reactor Building Ventilation (RBV) system.

NuScale’s Tier 2 FSAR demonstrates partial conformance with radiological safety and control room habitability criteria. For RG 1.196, only control room habitability design aspects within the standard plant design scope are applicable. SRP 15.1.5.A, 15.6.2, and 15.6.5.B are applicable only for specified portions, while SRP 15.4.8.A and 15.6.3 are relevant for technical specification

The passages describe the conformance of NuScale’s nuclear plant Instrumentation and Control (I&C) systems and related equipment to a comprehensive set of regulatory and industry standards. The I&C systems follow ISA-67.04.01 (2006) for instrument channel operability and IEEE 7-4.3.2-2003, with some test requirements inapplicable due to field programmable gate arrays. Digital I&C safety systems, including the Engineered Safety Feature Digital System (EDSS), meet multiple sections of IEEE Std 7-4.3.2-2003 as endorsed by RG 1.152, covering equipment qualification, reliability, integrity, interlocks, identification, and test/calibration, with partial conformance to RG 1.118. System architecture, independence, redundancy, predictability, repeatability, and quality comply with DSRS Appendices 7.0-B, 7.0-C, DSRS 7.1.2–7.2.1, Rev 0, and reference RG 1.75, RG 1.152, RG 1.53, and RG 1.28. Display and monitoring requirements conform to DSRS 7.2.13, Rev 0, with partial conformance to RG 1.97 and full conformance to RG 1.47. For Digital I&C-ISG-05, only the Minimum Inventory criterion is partially met, while ISG-06 and -07 are not applicable. Environmental qualification per DSRS 3.11, Rev 0, including IEEE Std. 323 and material suitability, is fully met, with maintenance and operational program implementation deferred to the COL applicant. Inservice Inspection and Testing of Class 2 and 3 Components per DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, is fully satisfied, with no exceptions or deviations. The Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) assessment aligns with NUREG/CR-6303 and DSRS 7.1.5, with D3 analyses of reactor protection systems meeting regulatory expectations. Guidance from Digital I&C-ISG-02, ISG-03, and ISG-04 is not applicable to the NuScale Design Certification Application, but the Module Protection System (MPS) provides internal diversity, meeting ATWS Rule and evolutionary plant criteria. Interdivisional communications are addressed in topical report TR-1015-18653-P-A, with conformance to Interim Staff Guidance documented in Table 1.9-4.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) defines the Certified Design Interface (CDI) using double brackets ([[]]), with security-related information marked by double braces {{ }} and withheld per 10 CFR 2.390(d)(1). Facility layout, CDI boundaries, and power generation systems are depicted in Figures 1.2-1 through 1.2-3, specifying that only clouded portions within CDI buildings—such as CDI cooling towers and certain circulating water systems—are included in the CDI. Table 1.8-1 details interfaces between the NuScale certified design and site-specific (COL) plant elements, listing CDI systems, structures, and components (e.g., turbine generator buildings, annex building, cooling towers, chemical treatment building, security buildings, central utility building, diesel generator buildings, offsite power transmission, main switchyard, transformer area, auxiliary AC power, site cooling water, circulating water, grounding/lightning protection, and plant exhaust stack), while potable/sanitary water, resin tanks, site drainage, raw water, site parameters, communications, turbine generators, and the Operational Support Center are site-specific. The FSAR references relevant sections for each interface and assesses operational experience conformance in Table 1.9-6, confirming that no 10 CFR 21 notifications have impacted NuScale-approved suppliers’ safety-related work. The NRC may reproduce NuScale reports for regulatory purposes, with proprietary or safeguards information handled per 10 CFR 2.390 and listed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2. The design documentation includes design bases, operational limits, safety analyses, and all information required by 10 CFR 52.47(a), addresses NRC DSRS, Interim Staff Guidance, 10 CFR 50.54(o), Appendix J/K requirements, and compliance with RGs and GDC 55/57, with some responsibilities assigned to the COL applicant. The design certification process used 21 main reports, Supplements 1-34, and NRC letter SECY-07-0110 to identify applicable generic issues, with Table 1.9-5 summarizing TMI requirements and NRC positions, and Table 1.9-6 evaluating operational experience. The design partially conforms to RG 1.189 (Fire Protection), entry/exit control, and technical/operational support center requirements, with site-specific updates required. NuScale identifies certain CFR regulations as literally applicable but seeks departures via exemption requests.

Section 1.7 of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, establishes detailed conventions, legends, and symbol definitions for Electrical and Instrumentation and Control (I&C) Drawings, including Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs). System drawings are summarized in Table 1.7-2, with electrical symbols defined in Figures 1.7-1a and 1.7-1b for components such as 3-winding and 2-winding power transformers (noting winding configuration, MVA/kVA ratings, cooling, impedance), current and potential transformers (quantity, ratio, accuracy), circuit breakers by voltage class (>100 kV, 1–100 kV, <1 kV) and ampere rating, inverters, multicell batteries (voltage, amp-hour, cell count), fuses, disconnect and transfer switches (ATS, STATIC), cable bus, surge capacitors, connection stabs, electrical heaters (kW), synchronous generators (frequency, RPM, % power factor), lightning arresters, relays/devices (NP-ES-0303-2923), transformer types (delta, wye, delta-wye with resistance ground, double secondary), chargers, VFDs, selector switches, monitoring devices (BM = battery monitor), local controllers, phasor indicators, meters (ammeter, frequency, synchroscope, voltmeter, VAR, watt, temperature, digital multimeter), power bus, rotary switches, and interlocked disconnect switches. Terminal numbering conventions are specified for starters, field terminal boxes, local control panels, DCS/PLC panels, MCC, and switchgear. Abbreviations such as DCS, GTO, NO, NC, PLC, and SCR are defined. Logic gate symbols (OR, AND, NOT) are provided in Figure 1.7-2. P&ID legends (Figures 1.7-3a–3f) specify that all vents and drains are 0.75" normally closed valves unless noted, and all inline valves are normally open except butterfly valves, with symbols for fail-open, fail-locked-as-is, drift-open, and fail-closed positions. The I&C architecture emphasizes clear interconnection interfaces, separation of safety/nonsafety systems, and functional simplification, with primary systems described in Section 7.0. Instrumentation and equipment identification uses structured codes (e.g., NP12-YY-DYY-E-SL-YYY-SYY) and ANSI/ISA 5.1-2009 function identifiers, specifying component type, function, location, and accessibility; temperature elements default to thermocouples unless noted. Communication links, line symbols, and equipment specifications (capacity, duty cycle, HP, flow rates, temperatures, power, heating/cooling capacities) are standardized, with numbering formats incorporating system abbreviations, module numbers, unique identifiers, and discipline codes. The NuScale reactor design utilizes the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to satisfy GDC 26, with its sole safety-related function being the prevention of inadvertent boron dilution in the reactor coolant system (RCS). The CVCS, including the Boron Recovery System, partially meets DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0 requirements, fulfilling criteria for functional performance, pumping capacity, defense-in-depth RCS makeup, single failure, GDC 5, contamination minimization, system design, leakage detection, and ITAAC, but only partially addresses holdup tank wall buckling/failure and venting/draining due to design differences. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) departs from conventional PWRs by omitting high- and low-pressure safety injection pumps, containment spray, and a sump, instead relying on steam condensation and natural recirculation of water within the containment vessel for core cooling, meeting ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46. The ECCS does not provide poison addition or makeup as required by GDC 27, and has departures in inservice inspection, operability testing, and combined reactivity control system capability, though actuation signal guidance is applied. The design ensures RCS pressure remains below 110% of design during inadvertent ECCS operation, maintains minimum DNBR, provides high-point venting for noncondensible gases, and includes control room instrumentation for clear indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(vi) and (xviii). The ECCS requires no personnel access, pumps, or containment pressure for recirculation, and lacks refueling water storage tanks; condensate storage is outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building with no safety risk from failure. Instrumentation and Controls review per DSRS 7.0 applies but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Gas accumulation is not expected to impact ECCS performance, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) interfaces only with the secondary system. Control room habitability meets SRP 6.4, Rev 3 for the emergency zone, and pre-operational ECCS testing will be performed to meet regulatory guidance.

The RCS, a subsystem of the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) within the containment vessel (CNV), consists of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) with integral pressurizer (PZR), reactor vessel internals (RVI), reactor safety valves (RSVs), RCS piping (injection, discharge, PZR spray supply, high-point degasification), PZR control cabinet, and associated instruments and cables. It transfers heat from the core to two once-through, helical-coil steam generators (SGs) by natural circulation, with SGs located in the annular space between the hot leg riser and RPV inner wall. The pressurizer (PZR) is an integral part of the NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS), located above the baffle plate within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and containing heater bundles, spray nozzles, and instrument lines. Its main function is to maintain RCS pressure within operational limits during normal and transient conditions, using electrical heaters (two bundles, each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to generate steam and increase pressure, and two 1-inch spray nozzles—supplied by the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS)—to condense steam and reduce pressure. The PZR water is heated to a temperature higher than the core outlet, ensuring a saturated water-steam interface, with minimal spray flow maintained to reduce thermal stresses and maintain chemistry equilibrium. The PZR is sized at approximately 23% of the RCS volume (578 ft³ total, 487 ft³ cylindrical) to accommodate expected RCS volume changes and prevent reactor trips or safety system actuation during transients. The nominal water level is controlled between 50–60% at full power, with analytical limits of 80% (upper) and 35% (lower), and is adjusted by the CVCS to absorb volume changes, especially between hot zero power and 15% power. Instrumentation monitors pressure and level, providing data to the Module Protection System (MPS), Module Control System (MCS), and control room, with the MCS automating RCS level and pressure control. During shutdown and refueling, decay heat is removed via the steam generator system (SGS) and decay heat removal system (DHRS), with the PZR not relied upon for safe shutdown. Overpressure protection is provided by reactor safety valves (RSVs) and relief valves, with the PZR, CVCS, and automatic isolation features preventing RCS solid conditions and protecting the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). During low-temperature operations, nitrogen is used for initial pressurization and bubble replacement, and the PZR is vented to match containment pressure before opening vent and relief valves. The MPS disconnects heater power if water level drops below the heater elements, using Class 1E circuit breakers for safety isolation. Heaters are grouped for steady-state control (Group 1) and backup during pressure drops (Groups 2 and 3). The PZR design ensures sufficient surge and steam volume to prevent liquid or two-phase flow to the RSVs during transients, with effective fluid mixing achieved through thermal effects and flow over SG tube bundles. All PZR regions are constructed for inspection and tested as part of RPV testing, with compliance to BPVC and seismic qualification. The RCS is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW thermal from the core to the SGS, with all major components—including the core, P

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) employs a robust containment leakage control and detection program, integrating initial and periodic leak rate testing for containment penetrations per ANSI/ANS 56.8, Regulatory Guide 1.163, and NEI 94-01, though the performance-based program is not applicable. Hydrogen control systems for a 100% fuel-clad metal-water reaction are not required by 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f), but the design minimizes ISLOCA probability, provides hydrogen measurement and mitigation, ensures containment performance, and includes a dedicated vent penetration. Containment leak rate testing partially meets Type C duration requirements, and post-accident sampling analyzes dissolved hydrogen, oxygen, and chloride. Leakage detection methods comply with RG 1.45 Positions C.2.1 and C.2.2, detecting unidentified leakage into containment at rates ≥0.05 gpm within one hour for rates >1 gpm. The Containment Evacuation System (CES), a nonsafety-related, module-specific system, maintains CNV pressure below saturation by removing water vapor and noncondensable gases via vacuum pumps, with Seismic Category I instrumentation providing real-time leakage data in the Main Control Room. Radiation detectors in the CES condenser vent line provide early RCS leakage indication, but all leakage is classified as unidentified until located and quantified, using RCS inventory balance and CES flow comparison. Primary detection methods include monitoring containment pressure and CES condensate, with technical specifications detailing requirements. The CES maintains sub-saturation pressure, making water level monitoring impractical; leakage from RSVs, ECCS valves, and actuators is exhausted into containment and counted as unidentified, while CVCS leakage outside the RCPB is identified and collected. RCCWS leakage is detected by expansion tank level and radiation monitoring, and primary-to-secondary leakage is monitored by radiation detectors and grab sampling. The CES sample vessel collects and monitors condensed water for pressure, temperature, level, and radioactivity, with alarms for operability limits and remote effluent monitoring. Accurate leakage rates are determined by condensate measurement with carryover correction, and the system can detect minimum RCS leak rates of 0.007 gpm, with a 1 gpm leak causing a 0.1 psi pressure rise in under a minute. Detection accuracy depends on maintaining containment pressure below the vapor pressure of the coldest internal wall, as internal condensation can affect results. Only pressure boundary failures within the CNV are detected by containment leakage monitoring instrumentation. Maintenance controls ensure ECCS functionality is not compromised by debris or coatings, with inspections verifying CNV cleanliness before power operation. Automatic containment isolation is actuated on low-low pressurizer level and high containment pressure, reliably preventing radiological release from the CES, with setpoints ensuring subatmospheric containment pressure and all design basis events meeting thermal and hydraulic criteria without relying on high radiation signals. Severe accident analyses confirm reliable containment isolation

The NuScale Power Plant consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each a modular, integral pressurized water reactor (PWR) rated at 160 MWt (about 50 MWe gross), for a total plant net output of approximately 570 MWe. Each NPM is partially immersed in a below-grade reactor pool, with the water surface about 6 feet below grade, and is enclosed by a high-pressure steel containment vessel (CNV) that operates at a vacuum and is submerged in water for passive fission product control. The reactor pool, spent fuel pool, most primary systems, and some radioactive waste equipment are located below grade, providing multiple barriers for fission product retention beyond the fuel, cladding, reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and containment. The NPM core contains 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, ~2 m rod length, <4.95% U-235 enrichment, Gd2O3 burnable absorber) and 16 control rod assemblies (CRAs), supported by five spacer grids and utilizing low-flow natural circulation for primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps and large-break LOCA scenarios. Each module features passive safety systems, including dedicated Emergency Core Cooling Systems (ECCS) and Decay Heat Removal Systems (DHRS), with three independent reactor vent valves and two independent reactor recirculation valves, capable of removing 100% of decay heat for at least 72 hours without water addition. The design eliminates external piping between major components, and the containment system meets DSRS 6.2.1.1.A requirements without using ice condenser or suppression pool systems. Refueling is independent for each NPM, with bioshields designed for removal and stacking, and the reactor core remains in the lower RPV during refueling. The plant includes a main steam system, turbine generator set, condenser, cooling tower, and condensate/feedwater system, with all hoisting, handling, and electrical connections above the pool water level. The module and plant control systems enable monitoring and control from the main control room, with dedicated panels for each NPM and integrated safety, alarm, and radiation monitoring. Critical heat flux (CHF) testing at Stern Laboratories and AREVA KATHY facility validated the NSP2 and NSP4 CHF correlations for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, supporting safety analysis and licensing. The reactor coolant system operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The containment lacks subcompartments, high-energy piping, or internal fuel handling, and does not require large vent openings. The design supports modular construction, incremental module operation, flexible siting, "island mode" operation, and off-site shipment of used NPMs, with extensive testing and documentation validating safety-related codes and

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, provides detailed technical and structural information on plant systems, operational parameters, and documentation. The Radioactive Waste Building is illustrated in multiple section and elevation views (Figures 1.2-28 through 1.2-33), while the radioactive waste management system—including liquid, gaseous, and solid waste—is described in Chapter 11, Sections 11.2–11.4, with system diagrams in Figures 11.2-1a through 11.4-2b. Instrumentation and controls are detailed in Table 1.7-1, covering system architecture, module protection, communication, reactor trip systems, power distribution, neutron monitoring, plant protection, safety display, and control system interfaces. Table 1.7-2 lists system drawings for major plant systems such as reactor coolant, steam generator, decay heat removal, containment, emergency core cooling, control room habitability, spent fuel pool cooling, pool cleanup, component cooling water, demineralized water, ultimate heat sink, condensate storage, site cooling water, chilled water, utility water, instrument and service air, nitrogen distribution, containment sampling, radioactive waste drain, chemical and volume control, boron addition, containment evacuation and flooding, HVAC, fire protection, power conversion, turbine generator, main steam, condenser, and auxiliary boiler. The plant design features digital control systems with strict independence between safety-related protection and nonsafety-related control systems; each reactor control display is dedicated to a specific reactor, with additional stations for shared systems, and security measures for access and tamper monitoring. Elevation and sectional views of the Reactor Building at various heights (Figures 1.2-9 through 1.2-19), the Control Building (Figures 1.2-21 through 1.2-27), and the Radioactive Waste Building (Figures 1.2-28 through 1.2-31) provide detailed visual documentation of structural layouts. Chapter 5 covers the Reactor Coolant System, with Table 5.1-1 detailing system volumes, Table 5.4-7 presenting pressurizer heater parameters, and Figures 5.1-2 and 5.1-3 providing schematic flow diagrams. The condenser design includes upper and lower headers with tubes forming the heat exchanger pressure boundary, containing secondary system fluid internally and exposed to reactor pool fluid externally. Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates at reactor powers from 0% (0 MWt) to 100% (160 MWt); at 100% power and best-estimate flow (587.0 kg/s), the core ΔT is 99.8°F, TCold is 496.6°F, Tavg is 543.3°F, and THot is 590.1°F, with minimum and maximum design flows of 538.5 kg/s and 660.5 kg/s

The criticality analysis follows DSS-ISG-2010-01, using a single fuel assembly design without burnup credit, zoning, loading patterns, or lumped fission products; all depletion, axial burnup, and interface analyses explicitly state no burnup credit is applied. The rack model includes an additional adjacent assembly under normal conditions. Code validation addresses area of applicability, trend analysis, bias, and uncertainty, with code-to-code comparisons and no reliance on precedents; all references are publicly available and cited, and assumptions are conservative or justified. The reactor design meets DSRS 4.4, Rev 0 and DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0 requirements for subchannel hydraulic analysis, core oscillations, thermal-hydraulic instabilities, CHF calculations, process monitoring, and core cooling instrumentation, with the only exception being the loose parts monitoring system, not required due to low primary system flow. Core stability during ATWS is not applicable due to diverse RTS signals that prevent ATWS and flow instabilities; DSRS 15.9.A II.1 has no requirements, II.2 is met via GDC 12, and II.3 and trip-before-CHF criteria are not applicable as reactor trip signals act before instabilities or CHF violations. For II.5, partial conformance is achieved as existing trip signals provide a more limiting exclusion zone than required for flow instability and SAFDL protection. DSRS 15.1.6, Rev 0, "Loss of Containment Vacuum," is fully met: reactor coolant pressure and cladding integrity are maintained, AOOs do not worsen conditions, instrument spans and setpoints follow RG 1.105, the limiting single failure is identified, a conservative scram is used, core burnup is considered, RCS-to-containment/pool heat transfer is maximized, and setpoint inaccuracies are addressed per RG 1.105, with no exceptions. Containment heat removal capability and LOCA design margin (DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0, II.3) meet requirements with no exceptions; mass and energy release analysis for postulated LOCAs (DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, II.3) and its ITAAC are not applicable. Containment heat removal systems (DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, II.5 and II.6) comply with 10 CFR 50.46(b)(5) for long-term cooling and water level margin in the presence of LOCA-generated and latent debris. The LOCA acceptance criteria use a temperature limit more restrictive than 2,200°F, conforming to DSRS 15.0, Rev 0, I. Review of NuScale’s transient and accident analyses (DSRS 15.0, Rev 0; SRP 15.0.2) shows general conformance with regulatory guidance for categorization,

The inservice inspection program for Class 2 and 3 components encompasses pre-service and periodic inspections and tests to ensure safety-related and risk-significant systems, structures, and components can perform their intended functions, with detailed requirements specified in Section 6.6 (referencing DSRS 6.6, Rev 0) and implementation milestones assigned to the COL applicant. The Inservice Test Program (Section 3.9.6) complies with 10 CFR 50.55a(f)(3)(iii)(B) and (iv)(B), while operational inservice testing and inspection programs (Sections 5.2.4 and 5.4.1.6) establish post-startup requirements, including the Steam Generator program. DSRS 5.2.4, Rev 0, review confirms conformance for system boundary, accessibility, and augmented ISI, with partial conformance for other inspection programs due to the absence of a boric acid control program; operational and risk-informed ISI programs are COL applicant responsibilities. ITAAC partially conforms, with some criteria applicable only to COL applicants. The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing program (DSRS 5.2.4, Rev 0) meets requirements for examination categories, intervals, evaluation, code exemptions, relief requests, and code cases, with no exceptions. Leakage detection (DSRS 5.2.5, Rev 0) meets GDC 14 and 30, and reactor vessel integrity (DSRS 5.3.3, Rev 0) conforms for operating conditions and inservice surveillance. Relief and safety valve position indication in the control room is provided per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi), and ATWS conditions are not required for the valve test program. The Residual Heat Removal System design (DSRS BTP 5-4, Rev 0) conforms for pressure relief and implementation, with operational procedures partially conforming as site-specific COL applicant responsibilities; materials and fabrication for Engineered Safety Features (SRP 6.1.1, Rev 2) also conform. DSRS 6.2.1.4, Rev 0, Mass and Energy Release Analysis for Postulated Secondary System Pipe Ruptures, conforms for energy sources, mass and energy release rate, and single-failure analyses, with no exceptions. Compliance with DSRS 5.3.2, Rev 0, is confirmed for pressure-temperature limits, upper-shelf energy, and pressurized thermal shock, as well as the regulatory basis and reactor vessel integrity design (DSRS 5.3.3, Rev 0), with no exceptions. The steam generator materials and design fully conform to DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0, including design, fabrication and processing of ferritic and austenitic stainless steel materials, material compatibility and cleanliness control, and secondary side access provisions, with no exceptions
